Addresses the destruction of longstanding cultural, institutional, and social structures in France and beyond, and the revolution's causes in Enlightenment thought, fiscal crisis, and social inequality. Connects to modern democratic governance, rationality in public affairs, the history of political violence, European political development, and the relationship between revolution and institutional reform.

titles.title: Philosophy
titles.author: Edward Craig
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Philosophy: A Very Short Introduction looks at different schools of philosophy and classic philosophical problems, via readings from Plato, Hume, Hobbes, Descartes, and early Buddhist writers. Could a philosopher today be asking the same questions about the self as David Hume or the early Buddhists? What were Descartes and Nietzsche trying to prove? Most people have a philosophy, in terms of a general picture of what they think the world is like and what values they live by. Are we all philosophers, and why are we more comfortable with some philosophical ideas than others? How should we live? What should we do, and how do we make these choices?
titles.summaryAI: Philosophy as a discipline addressing fundamental questions about knowledge, reality, ethics, and the self. Covers major thinkers including Plato, Descartes, Hume, Hobbes, Nietzsche, and early Buddhist writers, spanning Western and Eastern traditions. Examines core problems: the nature of the self, free will, moral reasoning, how we should live, and the relationship between philosophical ideas and everyday beliefs. Distinguished from specialized disciplines by its generality and foundational character. Connects to epistemology, ethics, political philosophy, philosophy of mind, and the cross-cultural dialogue between Western and Asian philosophical traditions.

titles.title: Barthes
titles.author: Jonathan Culler
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: Roland Barthes: A Very Short Introduction elucidates the varied theoretical contributions of Roland Barthes, whose lifelong fascination was with the way people make their world intelligible. It describes the many projects, which Barthes explored and which helped to change the way we think about a range of cultural phenomena — from literature, fashion, wrestling, and advertising to notions of the self, of history, and of nature. To some, he is the structuralist who outlined a ‘science of literature’, and the most prominent promoter of semiology; to others he stands not for science but pleasure, espousing a theory of literature which gives the reader a creative role.
titles.summaryAI: Roland Barthes (1915-1980), French literary theorist and semiotician whose work transformed the study of cultural phenomena. Covers structuralism, semiology (the science of signs), the nouveau roman, and post-structuralist textual theory. Examines Barthes' analyses of literature, fashion, wrestling, advertising, and concepts of the self, history, and nature. Addresses his notion of the death of the author, the pleasure of the text, and the creative role of the reader. Influenced by Maurice Blanchot. Connects to cultural studies, literary criticism, media studies, philosophy of language, and the relationship between meaning-making and cultural production.

titles.title: Animal rights
titles.author: David DeGrazia
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Animal Rights: A Very Short Introduction explores the implications for how we should treat animals in connection with our diet, zoos, and research. Most people are opposed to cruelty and sense that animals have moral significance. At the same time, traditional views that sanction animal use with few constraints have heavily influenced beliefs and everyday practices. How should we understand the moral status of animals vis-à-vis human beings? Do animals have moral rights? If so, what does this mean? What kinds of beings are animals, what sorts of mental lives do they have, and how should we understand welfare?
titles.summaryAI: Animal rights and moral philosophy examining the ethical treatment of animals in relation to diet, zoos, and scientific research. Covers theories of moral status including Kantian deontology and utilitarianism, and the concept of animal welfare. Examines animal cognition, mental lives, sentience, and whether animals possess moral rights. Addresses the tension between traditional views sanctioning animal use and contemporary moral arguments for constraint. Connects to bioethics, environmental philosophy, philosophy of mind, cognitive science, agricultural ethics, and the legal and social dimensions of the human-animal relationship.

titles.title: Kierkegaard
titles.author: Patrick Gardiner
titles.subject: Philosophy/Biography
titles.abstract: Kierkegaard: A Very Short Introduction shows how Kierkegaard developed his views in emphatic opposition to prevailing opinions. It describes his reaction to the ethical and religious theories of Kant and Hegel, and contrasts his position with doctrines advanced by people like Feuerbach and Marx. Kierkegaard's seminal diagnosis of the human condition, which emphasizes the significance of individual choice, has arguably been his most striking philosophical legacy, particularly for the growth of existentialism. What did Kierkegaard conceive of as the basis of religious belief? How did he come about his views on the nature of the human condition? How has Kierkegaard influenced contemporary thought?
titles.summaryAI: Soren Kierkegaard (1813-1855), Danish philosopher who developed his views in opposition to Hegelianism, Kantian ethics, and the systematic philosophies of his era. Covers his emphasis on subjectivity, individual choice, the nature of the self, and the basis of religious belief. Examines his relationship to Hume, Marx, and Feuerbach, and concepts of fear, practical reason, and the a priori. Addresses Kierkegaard's seminal diagnosis of the human condition as foundational to existentialism. Connects to philosophy of religion, ethics, phenomenology, and 20th-century existentialist thought from Heidegger to Sartre.

titles.title: Russell
titles.author: A. C. Grayling
titles.subject: Philosophy/Biography
titles.abstract: Russell: A Very Short Introduction provides an account of the life of Bertrand Russell (1872–1970), one of the most famous and important philosophers of the twentieth century. It introduces both his technical contributions to logic and philosophy, and his wide-ranging views on education, politics, war, and sexual morality. Russell is credited with being one of the prime movers of Analytic Philosophy, and with having played a part in the revolution in social attitudes witnessed throughout the twentieth-century world. This VSI gives a clear survey of Russell's achievements across their whole range.
titles.summaryAI: Bertrand Russell (1872-1970), British philosopher and public intellectual who cofounded analytic philosophy and contributed to mathematical logic, philosophy of language, epistemology, and philosophy of mind. Covers his logical contributions including the theory of descriptions and Principia Mathematica, his views on perception and the nature of mind, and his positions on education, politics, war, pacifism, and sexual morality. Addresses Russell's role in 20th-century social and political reform. Connects to the foundations of mathematics, the philosophy of science, political activism, and the development of analytic philosophy as a tradition.

titles.title: William Shakespeare
titles.author: Stanley Wells
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: William Shakespeare: A Very Short Introduction provides a guide to the life and writings of one of the world’s greatest and best-known dramatists: William Shakespeare. Looking at his early life and education, it explores Shakespeare’s social and intellectual background and the literary traditions on which Shakespeare drew. Examining the theatres and theatrical profession of the time, it also considers how Shakespeare experienced this world, both as an actor and as a writer. Examining Shakespeare’s narrative poems, sonnets, and all of his plays, this VSI outlines their sources, style, and originality over the course of Shakespeare’s career, to consider the fundamental impact his work has had for subsequent generations.
titles.summaryAI: William Shakespeare (1564-1616), English playwright and poet from Stratford-upon-Avon. Covers his complete dramatic works spanning comedy, tragedy, tragicomedy, and the history plays, plus the sonnets and narrative poems. Examines Shakespeare's social and intellectual background, the Elizabethan and Jacobean theatrical profession, and his dual role as actor and writer. Addresses literary sources, stylistic development across his career, and his enduring impact on world literature and performance. Connects to English Renaissance culture, the history of theater, poetics, and the transmission and interpretation of literary texts across centuries.

titles.title: Clausewitz
titles.author: Michael Howard
titles.subject: History/Politics/Biography
titles.abstract: Clausewitz: A Very Short Introduction explains Clausewitz’s ideas in terms both of his experiences as a professional soldier in the Napoleonic Wars, and of the intellectual background of his time. Karl von Clausewitz’s study On War was described by the American strategic thinker Bernard Brodie as ‘not simply the greatest, but the only great book about war’. Even though he wrote his only major work at a time when the range of firearms was 50 yards, much of what he had to say remains relevant today. This VSI outlines Clausewitz’s enduring theory of war and how his theory relates to his actual experience in the Napoleonic Wars.
titles.summaryAI: Carl von Clausewitz (1780-1831), Prussian military officer and theorist, produced On War, the foundational text of Western strategic thought. His theory addresses war as a continuation of politics, the fog of war, friction in military operations, and the distinction between absolute and limited war. Rooted in the Napoleonic Wars and early 19th-century European conflict, his ideas influenced Marx, Engels, Lenin, Mao Zedong, and Hitler. Covers civil war, strategy, tactics, and the relationship between military force and political objectives. Connects military theory to political philosophy, international relations, and the intellectual currents of German idealism and Enlightenment thought.

titles.title: Schopenhauer
titles.author: Christopher Janaway
titles.subject: Philosophy/Biography
titles.abstract: Schopenhauer is the most readable of German philosophers. Schopenhauer: A Very Short Introduction gives a succinct explanation of his metaphysical system, concentrating on the original aspects of his thought, which inspired many artists and thinkers including Nietzsche, Wagner, Freud, and Wittgenstein. Schopenhauer's central notion is that of the will — a blind, irrational force that he uses to interpret both the human mind and the whole of nature. Seeing human behaviour as that of a natural organism governed by the will to life, Schopenhauer developed radical insights concerning the unconscious and sexuality which influenced both psychologists and philosophers.
titles.summaryAI: Arthur Schopenhauer (1788-1860), German philosopher whose metaphysical system centers on the will as a blind, irrational force underlying all of nature and human experience. Building on Kant's distinction between appearance and reality, he developed theories of representation, the principle of sufficient reason, and the self as embodied will. His pessimistic philosophy addressed suffering, aesthetics, ethics, and the denial of the will to life. Pioneered ideas about the unconscious mind and sexuality that prefigured Freud's psychoanalysis. Influenced Nietzsche, Wagner, Wittgenstein, and existentialism. Connects 19th-century German idealism to psychology, Eastern philosophy (Buddhism), philosophy of mind, and aesthetic theory.

titles.title: The Russian Revolution
titles.author: S. A. Smith
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: The Russian Revolution: A Very Short Introduction provides an analytical narrative of the main events and developments in Soviet Russia between 1917 and 1936. What impact did the revolution have on society as a whole—on different classes, ethnic groups, the army, men and women, and youth? How was it that one structure of domination was replaced by another? Political developments during this tumultuous period sit firmly in the context of massive economic, social, and cultural change. Since the fall of Communism there has been much reflection on the significance of the Russian Revolution. Is there an alternative interpretation to the currently influential, liberal one, one that sees the revolution as rooted in the contradictions of a backward society that sought modernization and enlightenment and ended in political tyranny?
titles.summaryAI: The Russian Revolution of 1917 and its aftermath through 1936, encompassing the February and October revolutions, civil war, War Communism, the New Economic Policy, and the rise of Stalinism. Covers the political, economic, social, and cultural transformation of Russia into the Soviet Union, including impacts on class structure, ethnic groups, gender relations, youth, literacy campaigns, trade, and urbanization. Examines how communist ideology was implemented and how revolutionary ideals gave way to political tyranny. Addresses competing historiographical interpretations including liberal and revisionist perspectives. Connects to modernization theory, comparative revolution studies, and the contradictions of rapid industrialization in a predominantly agrarian society.

titles.title: Hobbes
titles.author: Richard Tuck
titles.subject: Philosophy/Politics/Biography
titles.abstract: Hobbes's reputation was paradoxical. He was hostile to dogmatism of every kind, yet he was seen as a brusque dogmatist. He was hostile to the intellectual authorities of the churches, but yet he wanted his own philosophical works to be the authoritative texts within the universities. The Conclusion tries to explain this paradox by placing Hobbes within a wider paradox, one which is possibly inherent to scepticism or liberalism. A central question remains: if we lose all confidence in the truth of existing beliefs, how can we live? Instead of scepticism, Hobbes offered a type of science: looking closer we find that his science is of an extremely exiguous kind.
titles.summaryAI: Thomas Hobbes (1588-1679), English philosopher whose Leviathan established foundational concepts in political philosophy including the state of nature, social contract theory, and sovereign authority. His thought addresses ethical relativism, scepticism, materialism, and the relationship between science and politics. Hobbes challenged ecclesiastical authority while seeking to ground political obligation in rational self-interest rather than divine right. His influence extends to Hume, Locke, and modern liberal theory. Covers tensions between dogmatism and scepticism, the problem of political order in the absence of shared moral certainty, and the philosophical foundations of the modern secular state. Connects political philosophy to epistemology, ethics, and early modern scientific method.

titles.title: World music
titles.author: Philip V. Bohlman
titles.subject: Music
titles.abstract: World Music: A Very Short Introduction looks at the history of world music and its many definitions. ‘World music’ is more than a marketing term for the music industry. During the Enlightenment, the idea of the ‘folk song’ encouraged European audiences to imagine music from around the world. Technology helped to create the ‘audio moment’—the transformation of sound into material which could be recorded and distributed worldwide. Throughout history, music has been used to express unity and national pride. World music both foregrounds and transgresses borders. Ideas in different cultures about world music, and indeed about music, are as diverse as ever.
titles.summaryAI: World music as a category encompassing non-Western and folk musical traditions from every continent, shaped by Enlightenment-era European concepts of the folk song and by recording technology that created the audio moment. Covers the music industry’s role in marketing and distributing diverse musical traditions, the use of music to express nationalism and cultural identity, and how music crosses and reinforces geographic and cultural borders. Addresses ethnomusicology, oral traditions, sound recording history, and the tension between authenticity and commercialization. Distinguished from Western classical and popular music by its global scope. Connects to cultural anthropology, postcolonial studies, globalization, and the politics of cultural representation.

titles.title: Mathematics
titles.author: Timothy Gowers
titles.subject: Philosophy/Mathematics
titles.abstract: The aim of Mathematics: A Very Short Introduction is to explain, carefully but not technically, the differences between advanced, research-level mathematics, and the sort of mathematics we learn at school. It offers readers an insight into such seemingly paradoxical concepts as infinity, imaginary numbers, and curved space. The first few chapters are concerned with general aspects of mathematical thought and are followed by chapters on more specific topics such as limits and infinity, dimension, geometry, and estimates and approximations. It concludes with some answers to common sociological questions about the mathematical community.
titles.summaryAI: Mathematics as a discipline of abstract reasoning, covering foundational concepts including number theory, geometry (from Euclid onward), infinity, limits, imaginary numbers, curved space, dimension, and estimates and approximations. Addresses the nature of mathematical proof, abstraction, and the distinction between school-level and research-level mathematics. Explores commutative and associative properties, square roots, and the philosophical underpinnings of mathematical truth. Touches on the sociology of the mathematical community and how mathematicians work. Distinguished from applied mathematics and physics by its emphasis on pure logical structure. Connects to philosophy of mathematics, computer science, and the role of mathematical thinking in scientific discovery.

titles.title: Philosophy of science
titles.author: Samir Okasha
titles.subject: Philosophy/History of Science
titles.abstract: What is science? Is science objective? Can science explain everything? Philosophy of Science: A Very Short Introduction explores the main themes and theories of contemporary philosophy of science. Beginning with a concise history of science, it examines the nature of scientific reasoning, scientific explanation, and the theory/evidence relation. Looking at scientific revolutions and scientific change, it asks whether there is a discernible pattern to the way scientific ideas develop over time and discusses realist versus anti-realist attitudes towards science. It concludes by considering the role of science in society, and ethical questions surrounding modern science.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophy of science as a branch of philosophy examining the foundations, methods, and implications of scientific inquiry. Covers scientific reasoning (induction, deduction, inference to best explanation), the theory-evidence relationship, scientific explanation, and the demarcation problem. Addresses scientific revolutions and paradigm change, realism versus anti-realism, objectivity, and underdetermination. Draws on examples from physics and biology. Examines the social role of science and ethical dimensions of scientific practice. Distinguished from history of science by its normative focus on what counts as good scientific reasoning. Connects to epistemology, logic, ethics of technology, and debates over scientific authority in public policy.

titles.title: Cryptography
titles.author: Fred Piper, Sean Murphy
titles.subject: Mathematics/Computing
titles.abstract: We are far removed from the historical situation in which cryptography was used only by militaries and governments. With vast quantities of sensitive information transferred online by individuals, companies, organizations, and nation-states, cryptography is increasingly important to everyone. Most of us, often without realizing, use cryptography on a daily basis. Cryptography: A Very Short Introduction demystifies the art of cryptography by tracing its historical use, explaining how it works, and providing examples of its practical use. This second edition highlights the important advances both in academic cryptography research and in its everyday use. In non-technical language and without assuming advanced mathematical knowledge, Cryptography: A Very Short Introduction introduces symmetric and public-key cryptography. The book then provides a detailed discussion of the design of cryptographic algorithms that are secure against quantum computers and of the development of cryptographic algorithms with advanced functionalities. It also considers new applications of cryptography including blockchain, secure messaging apps, and electronic voting.
titles.summaryAI: Cryptography as the science of securing information, spanning from ancient ciphers and military codes to modern digital applications. Covers symmetric-key and public-key cryptography, algorithmic design, identity verification, and information security fundamentals. Addresses post-quantum cryptography and algorithms resistant to quantum computing attacks. Modern applications include blockchain technology, secure messaging, electronic voting, and cyber security for individuals, corporations, and nation-states. Distinguished from pure mathematics by its applied focus on secrecy and authentication. Connects to computer science, number theory, digital commerce, national security, privacy law, and the ongoing arms race between code-makers and code-breakers.

titles.title: Quantum Theory
titles.author: John Polkinghorne
titles.subject: Chemistry/Physics
titles.abstract: Quantum Theory is the most revolutionary discovery in physics since Newton. Quantum Theory: A Very Short Introduction gives a lucid, exciting, and accessible account of the surprising and counterintuitive ideas that shape our understanding of the sub-atomic world. It does not disguise the problems of interpretation that still remain unsettled 75 years after the initial discoveries. The main text makes no use of equations, but there is a Mathematical Appendix for those desiring stronger fare. Uncertainty, probabilistic physics, complementarity, the problematic character of measurement, and decoherence are among the many topics discussed.
titles.summaryAI: Quantum theory as the fundamental framework of modern physics describing subatomic phenomena. Core concepts include the uncertainty principle (Heisenberg), wave-particle duality, the superposition principle, the Schrodinger equation, probabilistic physics, complementarity, and decoherence. Addresses the measurement problem and competing interpretations, particularly the Copenhagen interpretation (Bohr) and alternatives. Covers quantum field theory and its relationship to classical physics. Key figures include Einstein, Bohr, Heisenberg, and Schrodinger. Distinguished from classical mechanics by its inherently probabilistic nature and rejection of determinism at the subatomic scale. Connects to philosophy of physics, chemistry, particle physics, and foundational questions about the nature of reality and observation.

titles.title: Spinoza
titles.author: Roger Scruton
titles.subject: Philosophy/Biography
titles.abstract: Spinoza: A Very Short Introduction presents a systematic analysis of Spinoza's thought by describing the contours of this complex system of thought, and shows its relevance to today's intellectual preoccupations. Benedict de Spinoza (1632–77) was at once the father of the Enlightenment and the last sad guardian of the medieval world. In his brilliant synthesis of geometrical method, religious sentiment, and secular science, he attempted to reconcile the conflicting moral and intellectual demands of his epoch, and to present a vision of humanity as simultaneously bound by necessity and eternally free.
titles.summaryAI: Benedict de Spinoza (1632-1677), Dutch-Jewish philosopher whose Ethics employed geometrical method to construct a comprehensive metaphysical system. Central concepts include substance monism (God or Nature as the single substance), causa sui, the mind-body problem, natural law, and the ontological argument. Engaged with Hobbes, Grotius, and Bayle. His thought synthesized medieval scholasticism with early modern science, addressing determinism, freedom, and the sub specie aeternitatis perspective. Distinguished from Descartes by rejecting mind-body dualism. Pioneered secular biblical criticism and influenced Enlightenment rationalism, political liberalism, and later figures including Hegel and Einstein. Connects metaphysics, ethics, political theory, and philosophy of religion.

titles.title: Choice Theory
titles.author: Michael Allingham
titles.subject: Economics
titles.abstract: We make choices all the time — about trivial matters, about how to spend our money, about how to spend our time, about what to do with our lives. Choice Theory: A Very Short Introduction introduces ideas from economics, philosophy, and other areas, showing how the theory applies to everyday decisions, and to particular situations such as gambling and resource allocation. But what kind of criteria are we applying when we say that a choice is rational? What guides our own choices? What strategies should be applied in making decisions that affect a lot of people? This VSI explores what it means to be rational in all these contexts.
titles.summaryAI: Choice theory (rational choice theory) as a formal framework for analyzing decision-making under certainty, risk, and uncertainty. Core concepts include cardinal and ordinal utility, expected utility theory, expected value, marginal utility, risk aversion, risk premium, certainty equivalents, and transitive preferences. Covers applications to gambling, insurance, resource allocation, cooperation, and collective decision-making. Draws on economics, philosophy, and game theory. Distinguished from behavioral economics by its normative emphasis on rational criteria. Addresses what constitutes rational choice when decisions affect multiple people. Connects to welfare economics, social choice theory, public policy, moral philosophy, and the foundations of microeconomic theory.

titles.title: Architecture
titles.author: Andrew Ballantyne
titles.subject: Architecture
titles.abstract: Architecture: A Very Short Introduction provides a highly original and sophisticated look at architecture and helps us to understand the cultural significance of the buildings that surround us. It avoids the traditional style-spotting approach in favour of giving an idea of what it is about buildings that moves us, and what it is that makes them important artistically and culturally. The VSI begins by looking at how architecture acquires meaning through tradition, and concludes with the exoticism of the recent avant garde. Illustrations of particular buildings help to anchor the general points with specific examples, from ancient Egypt to the present day.
titles.summaryAI: Architecture as both art form and cultural practice, spanning from ancient Egypt through Renaissance (Alberti, Brunelleschi), Gothic Revival, Arts and Crafts (Mackintosh), modernism (Le Corbusier, Frank Lloyd Wright), to postmodern and avant-garde movements (Philip Johnson). Focuses on how buildings acquire cultural meaning through tradition, context, and aesthetic experience rather than mere stylistic classification. Covers the relationship between form and function, architectural symbolism, and the emotional and intellectual responses buildings evoke. Distinguished from engineering by its emphasis on artistic and cultural significance. Connects to urban planning, art history, cultural identity, aesthetics, and the social function of built environments across civilizations.

titles.title: Poststructuralism
titles.author: Catherine Belsey
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Poststructuralism challenges traditional ways of thinking about the relations between human beings, culture, and the world. Language and meaning are reappraised, and with them assumptions about what it is possible for us to know. Poststructuralism resists certainties and offers ways to consider our place in the world that compete with conventional explanations. In this Very Short Introduction, Catherine Belsey examines key figures such as Roland Barthes, Jacques Derrida, Michel Foucault, Julia Kristeva, Gilles Deleuze, and Sarah Kofman, drawing examples from sources as diverse as M. C. Escher, politics, Shakespeare, climate change, love, primatology, and pandemics.
titles.summaryAI: Poststructuralism as a philosophical and critical movement challenging fixed meanings, stable identities, and binary oppositions in language, culture, and knowledge. Key thinkers include Barthes (semiotics, death of the author), Derrida (deconstruction, differance), Foucault (power-knowledge, discourse), Kristeva (intertextuality, abjection), Deleuze (difference, rhizomatic thought), and Kofman. Emerged in 1960s-70s France as a critique of structuralism. Covers applications to literary criticism, gender theory, psychoanalysis, politics, and epistemology. Distinguished from structuralism by rejecting stable underlying structures. Connects to linguistics, philosophy of language, critical theory, cultural studies, and contemporary debates on identity, climate change, and pandemic politics.

titles.title: Postmodernism
titles.author: Christopher Butler
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: How can postmodernism be defined? Postmodernism: A Very Short Introduction challenges and explores the key ideas of postmodernists, and their engagement with theory, literature, the visual arts, film, architecture, and music. A artists, intellectuals, critics, and social scientists are treated ‘as if they were all members of a loosely constituted and quarrelsome political party’ — a party which includes such members as Cindy Sherman, Salman Rushdie, Jacques Derrida, Walter Abish, and Richard Rorty — creating a vastly entertaining framework in which to unravel the mysteries of the ‘postmodern condition’, from the politicizing of museum culture to the cult of the politically correct.
titles.summaryAI: Postmodernism as an intellectual and cultural movement emerging in the late 20th century, spanning philosophy, literature, visual arts, film, architecture, and music. Key thinkers include Derrida, Nietzsche, Freud, Marx, Wittgenstein, and Rorty, alongside artists like Cindy Sherman and writers like Salman Rushdie and Umberto Eco. Encompasses deconstruction, anti-foundationalism, identity politics, political correctness, and the politicization of museum culture. Distinguished from modernism by its skepticism toward grand narratives and universal truths. Connects to cultural studies, aesthetics, social theory, and debates over relativism and the nature of knowledge.

titles.title: Democracy
titles.author: Bernard Crick
titles.subject: Politics/History
titles.abstract: Democracy: A Very Short Introduction shows the distinction between the shared concept of democracy and different conceptions or instances of democracy. The key idea of democracy is that those governed—the demos—have a say in the government. However, until the 19th century, different conceptions of democracy did not extend to democratic society, resulting in the exclusion of many groups and individuals. The ideas of social and political thinkers including Plato, Aristotle, J. S. Mill, Marx, Rawls, and Sen provide the intellectual history of democracy. This VSI explores past and present forms of democratic government and considers traditional and inclusive historical approaches to democracy.
titles.summaryAI: Democracy as a political system and concept traced from ancient Greek origins through Enlightenment political philosophy to contemporary forms. Examines the distinction between the concept of democracy and its varied conceptions across history. Key thinkers include Plato, Aristotle, J. S. Mill, Marx, Rawls, and Amartya Sen. Covers the historical exclusion of groups from democratic participation, normative ideals of self-governance, representative and direct democracy, and the evolution of democratic society. Distinguished from authoritarianism and oligarchy. Connects to political philosophy, human rights, social justice, and theories of government legitimacy.

titles.title: Empire
titles.author: Stephen Howe
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: There is fierce argument, and much speculation, about what has replaced the old territorial empires in world politics. Empire: A Very Short Introduction asks: Do the United States and its allies, transnational companies, financial and media institutions, or more broadly the forces of ‘globalization’, constitute a new imperial system? Spanning the history of empires, from ancient Rome to the modern British empires, it explores the meaning of the idea of ‘empire’ through the ages and examines the aftermath of imperialism on the contemporary world, disentangling the multiple uses and abuses of the labels ‘empire’ and ‘colonialism’.
titles.summaryAI: Empire as a political formation spanning from ancient Rome and the Ottoman Empire through the British, Dutch, and French colonial empires to contemporary debates about neo-imperialism and globalization. Examines whether the United States, transnational corporations, and international financial institutions constitute a new imperial system. Covers the intellectual history of empire including Edward Gibbon, theories of colonialism and decolonization, and the aftermath of imperialism on the contemporary world. Distinguished from nation-states by territorial expansion and hierarchical governance over diverse peoples. Connects to international relations, postcolonialism, economic exploitation, and global power structures.

titles.title: Fascism
titles.author: Kevin Passmore
titles.subject: History/Politics
titles.abstract: What is fascism? Is it revolutionary? Or is it reactionary? Can it be both? Fascism: A Very Short Introduction unravels the paradoxes of one of the most important phenomena in the modern world — tracing its origins in the intellectual, political, and social crises of the late nineteenth century, the rise of fascism following World War I, including fascist regimes in Italy and Germany, and the fortunes of ‘failed’ fascist movements in Eastern Europe, Spain, and the Americas. It also considers fascism in culture, the new interest in transnational research, and the progress of the far right since 2002.
titles.summaryAI: Fascism as a political ideology and movement originating in the intellectual, political, and social crises of late 19th-century Europe, with major manifestations in Mussolini's Italy and Hitler's Germany. Covers corporatism, social Darwinism, Nazism, the Ku Klux Klan, and far-right movements in Eastern Europe, Spain, and the Americas. Examines fascism's paradoxical relationship between revolutionary and reactionary impulses, its class dynamics, and its cultural dimensions. Includes the post-2002 resurgence of far-right politics and figures like Jean-Marie Le Pen. Distinguished from authoritarian conservatism by its mass mobilization and totalitarian aspirations. Connects to nationalism, propaganda, and comparative political ideology.

titles.title: Terrorism
titles.author: Charles Townshend
titles.subject: Sociology/Terrorism and National Security Law/ Warfare and Defense
titles.abstract: Terrorism: A Very Short Introduction examines the historical, ideological, and local roots of terrorist violence and explores terrorism in relation to revolutionary power, nationalism, and religious extremism. Is one person’s terrorist another’s freedom fighter? Is terrorism crime or war? What can we do to stop it? For many, the terrorist attacks of September 2001 changed the face of the world, pushing terrorism to the top of many political agendas. This VSI considers recent changes in terrorism, such as the emergence of ISIS and upsurge in individual suicide action, looking at the impact and consequences of several recent terrorist attacks, and outlining the complex issues related to antiterrorist and counterterrorist measures.
titles.summaryAI: Terrorism as a political phenomenon encompassing revolutionary violence, nationalist movements, and religious extremism from the 19th century to the present. Covers the ideological roots of terrorist violence, the emergence of ISIS, individual suicide attacks, and the September 2001 attacks. Examines terrorism in relation to genocide, propaganda, state violence, and conflicts in Northern Ireland and Yugoslavia. Addresses antiterrorist and counterterrorist measures, the League of Nations and United Nations frameworks, and the contested definitions of terrorism versus freedom fighting. Distinguished from conventional warfare by its targeting of civilians and psychological aims. Connects to international law, security policy, and political legitimacy.

titles.title: Plato
titles.author: Julia Annas
titles.subject: Classical Studies/Philosophy/Biography
titles.abstract: Plato: A Very Short Introduction explores the philosophy and argument of Plato's writings, looking at Plato as a thinker grappling with philosophical problems in a variety of ways, rather than a philosopher with a fully worked-out system. It discusses Plato's style of writing: his use of the dialogue form, his use of what we today call fiction, and his philosophical transformation of myths. It looks at his discussions of love and philosophy, his attitude to women and to homosexual love, and his claim that virtue is sufficient for happiness. It also examines his arguments for the immortality of the soul, and his ideas about the nature of the universe.
titles.summaryAI: Plato's philosophy as developed in his dialogues, addressing questions of knowledge, ethics, metaphysics, and political thought in 5th-4th century BC Athens. Covers the Socratic method, the theory of Forms, arguments for the immortality of the soul, the nature of the universe, and the relationship between virtue and happiness. Examines Plato's literary techniques including dialogue form, philosophical use of fiction, and transformation of myths. Addresses views on gender, homosexual love, and the philosophical significance of eros. Distinguished from later systematic philosophy by its exploratory, non-dogmatic approach. Connects to epistemology, ethics, political philosophy, cosmology, and the foundations of Western philosophical tradition.

titles.title: Ethics
titles.author: Simon Blackburn
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Ethics: A Very Short Introduction highlights the importance of an understanding of approaches to ethics and its foundations. Today, we are confronted with an uncertain world of eroding trust, swirling conspiracy theories, and a dismaying loss of respect in public discourse. Our self-image as moral, well-behaved creatures is dogged by scepticism, relativism, hypocrisy, and nihilism, and by the fear that science has unmasked us as creatures fated by our genes to be selfish, competitive, and aggressive. This VSI tackles the major moral questions surrounding birth, death, happiness, desire, and freedom. It shows us how we should think about the meaning of life.
titles.summaryAI: Ethics as a branch of philosophy addressing fundamental moral questions about birth, death, happiness, desire, and freedom. Covers major ethical frameworks including consequentialism, deontology, and virtue ethics, alongside challenges from skepticism, relativism, nihilism, and evolutionary psychology. Examines whether morality is objective or constructed, the relationship between self-interest and altruism, and the implications of scientific accounts of human nature as genetically selfish and competitive. Addresses trust, hypocrisy, and the meaning of life. Distinguished from applied ethics by its focus on foundational principles. Connects to psychology, evolutionary biology, political philosophy, and debates about moral responsibility.

titles.title: Emotion
titles.author: Dylan Evans
titles.subject: Anthropology/Psychology
titles.abstract: Was love invented by European poets in the Middle Ages or is it part of human nature? Will winning the lottery really make you happy? Is it possible to build robots that have feelings? Emotion: A Very Short Introduction explores the latest thinking about emotions, drawing on a wide range of scientific research, from anthropology and psychology to neuroscience and artificial intelligence. It discusses the evolution of emotions and their biological basis, the science of happiness, and the role that emotions play in memory and decision-making. This new edition has been updated to incorporate new developments in our understanding of emotions, including the neural basis of empathy and the emotional impact of films.
titles.summaryAI: Emotion as studied across anthropology, psychology, neuroscience, and artificial intelligence. Covers the evolutionary origins and biological basis of emotions, the neural mechanisms of empathy, the science of happiness, and the role of emotions in memory and decision-making. Examines cross-cultural questions about whether love is universal or culturally constructed, the emotional impact of film, Stoic philosophy of emotion, and Martha Nussbaum's capabilities approach. Addresses the possibility of robot emotions and artificial affective systems. Distinguished from mood and temperament by its specificity and triggering mechanisms. Connects to cognitive science, philosophy of mind, social psychology, and human-computer interaction.

titles.title: Northern Ireland
titles.author: Marc Mulholland
titles.subject: History/Human Geography
titles.abstract: Northern Ireland: A Very Short Introduction explores the pivotal moments in Northern Irish history: the rise of republicanism in the 1800s, Home Rule and the civil rights movement, the growth of Sinn Féin and the Provisional IRA, and the DUP, before bringing the story up to date, drawing on newly available memoirs by paramilitary militants to offer previously unexplored perspectives, as well as recent work on Northern Irish gender relations. This VSI also includes a new chapter on the state of affairs in 21st-century Northern Ireland, considering the question of Irish unity in the light of both Brexit and the approaching anniversary of the 1921 partition, and drawing new lessons for the future.
titles.summaryAI: Northern Ireland's political and social history from the rise of republicanism in the 1800s through partition in 1921 to the 21st century. Covers the civil rights movement, the IRA and Provisional IRA, Sinn Fein, the Democratic Unionist Party, and Ian Paisley. Examines Home Rule, the Troubles, paramilitary militancy, gender relations, and the peace process. Addresses Brexit's impact on the Irish border and the question of Irish unity. Distinguished from broader Irish history by focus on sectarian conflict. Connects to British constitutional history, decolonization, and conflict resolution.

titles.title: Art Theory
titles.author: Cynthia Freeland
titles.subject: Art
titles.abstract: In today's art world many strange, even shocking, things qualify as art. Art Theory: A Very Short Introduction discusses beauty, blood, culture, money, museums, sex, and politics, clarifying contemporary and historical accounts of the nature, function, and interpretation of the arts. It examines art on the Web, video art, museum CD-ROMs, and various theorists of the new media and of postmodern art. It surveys the latest research on the brain's role in perceiving art and engages with the big debates surrounding our responses to art, examining why innovation and controversy are valued in the arts.
titles.summaryAI: Art theory as a philosophical discipline examining the nature, function, and interpretation of the arts from classical aesthetics to contemporary practice. Covers beauty, taste, significant form, and institutional definitions of art. Examines video art, web-based art, new media theory, and postmodern discourse. Includes neuroscience research on art perception. References artists from Botticelli and Leonardo to Francis Bacon, Van Gogh, and Damien Hirst. Distinguished from art history by its focus on conceptual frameworks. Connects to philosophy of aesthetics, cognitive science, cultural criticism, and museum studies.

titles.title: Locke
titles.author: John Dunn
titles.subject: Philosophy/Biography
titles.abstract: Locke: A Very Short Introduction unravels the meaning of John Locke's political thought and his contribution to English ideology. One of the greatest English philosophers of the late seventeenth and early eighteenth centuries, he argued in his masterpiece, An Essay Concerning Human Understanding, that our knowledge is founded in experience and reaches us principally through our senses; but its message has been curiously misunderstood. This VSI shows how Locke arrived at his theory of knowledge, and how his exposition of the liberal values of toleration and responsible government formed the backbone of enlightened European thought of the eighteenth century.
titles.summaryAI: John Locke's philosophy spanning epistemology, political theory, and religious toleration in late 17th and early 18th-century England. Central work is An Essay Concerning Human Understanding, arguing that knowledge is founded in sensory experience rather than innate ideas. Covers Locke's empiricist theory of knowledge, his defense of liberal values including toleration and responsible government, and his influence on Enlightenment thought across Europe. Distinguished from continental rationalism of Descartes and Leibniz by its empiricist foundations. Connects to political liberalism, social contract theory, the philosophy of mind, religious freedom, and the intellectual origins of constitutional democracy.

titles.title: Modern Ireland
titles.author: Senia Paseta
titles.subject: Human Geography
titles.abstract: Modern Ireland: A Very Short Introduction attempts to answer the Irish Question. The term has become something of a catch-all, a convenient way to encompass numerous issues and developments which pertain to the political, social, and economic history of modern Ireland. No other issue brought down as many nineteenth-century governments and no comparable twentieth-century dilemma has matched its ability to frustrate the attempts of British cabinets to find a solution; this inability to find a lasting answer to the Irish Question is especially striking when seen in the context of the massive shifts in British foreign policy brought about by two world wars, decolonization, and the cold war.
titles.summaryAI: Modern Ireland's political, social, and economic history from the Act of Union through the 19th and 20th centuries. Covers the Irish Republican Brotherhood, Charles Stewart Parnell, Home Rule, the Orange Order, land tenure reform, Eamon De Valera, and Northern Ireland's partition. Examines the Church of Ireland's role and House of Commons debates on Irish governance. Addresses how the Irish Question shaped British politics through world wars, decolonization, and the Cold War. Distinguished from ancient Irish history by focus on post-Union political struggles. Connects to imperial history, nationalism, and constitutional politics.

titles.title: Globalization
titles.author: Manfred Steger
titles.subject: Economics/Politics
titles.abstract: Globalization: A Very Short Introduction looks at the interconnected and accelerated processes changing how we see and experience the world. Is globalization really a new phenomenon? Is increased connection between people and nations inevitable, or are we witnessing the beginning of an era of ‘deglobalization’ or ‘anti-globalization’? Updated with new developments including advancing climate change, the Trump presidency, and the Mexico–USA border, this VSI explores the history and impact of globalization. Chapters on the cultural, economic, political, and ecological dimensions of globalization investigate the impact of new technologies, economic deregulation, and mass migration on our world and consider what we might expect from the future of globalization.
titles.summaryAI: Globalization as interconnected economic, political, cultural, and ecological processes accelerating worldwide integration. Covers the history of global trade and migration, economic deregulation, new technologies, the Trump presidency, the Mexico-USA border, and advancing climate change. Examines anti-globalization and deglobalization movements alongside mass migration and its impacts. Addresses the cultural dimensions including identity and homogenization, the political implications for national sovereignty, and ecological consequences of global economic integration. Distinguished from internationalism by its emphasis on transnational flows beyond state control. Connects to trade policy, environmental politics, cultural exchange, and debates about the future of the nation-state.

titles.title: The Cold War
titles.author: Robert J. McMahon
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: The Cold War: A Very Short Introduction discusses the Cold War, which dominated international life from the end of the Second World War to the fall of the Berlin Wall. But how did the dispute begin, and why did it move from its origins in post-war Europe to encompass virtually every corner of the globe? This VSI considers these questions and more, providing a truly international history of the Cold War and examining its enduring legacy. It draws on the most recent scholarship and documents to offer a full analysis of all aspects of the war. These include the Vietnam War and the changing global politics since the 1970s.
titles.summaryAI: The Cold War as a global geopolitical conflict from the end of World War II (1945) to the fall of the Berlin Wall (1989). Covers the origins in post-war Europe, expansion to encompass virtually every global region, NATO's formation, the Vietnam War, and shifting global politics from the 1970s onward. Examines the superpower rivalry between the United States and the Soviet Union, nuclear deterrence, proxy wars, and ideological competition between capitalism and communism. Distinguished from conventional wars by its avoidance of direct superpower military confrontation. Connects to decolonization, international institutions, arms control, and the post-Cold War international order.

titles.title: The History of Astronomy
titles.author: Michael Hoskin
titles.subject: History of Science/Physics
titles.abstract: The History of Astronomy: A Very Short Introduction traces the history of Western astronomy, from prehistoric times to the origins of astrophysics in the mid-nineteenth century and the technical developments since the Second World War. Astronomy, perhaps the first of the sciences, was already well developed by the time of Christ — the arithmetical astronomy of the Babylonians was merged with the Greek geometrical approach. This legacy was transmitted to the West via Islam and led to the Copernican revolution, which in turn led to Kepler and Newton, who provided the principles on which the exploration of the solar system and the stars continued in the eighteenth- and nineteenth centuries.
titles.summaryAI: The history of Western astronomy from prehistoric observations through Babylonian arithmetical methods, Greek geometrical approaches, and Islamic transmission to the Copernican revolution and beyond. Key figures include Aristotle, Ptolemy (Almagest), Tycho Brahe, Kepler, Isaac Newton, and William Herschel. Covers calendar development, the heliocentric model, gravitational theory, solar system exploration, and stellar astronomy of the 18th-19th centuries. Traces the emergence of astrophysics in the mid-19th century and post-WWII technical developments. Distinguished from cosmology by its observational and instrumental focus. Connects to the history of science, mathematics, navigation, and religious-scientific debates.

titles.title: Schizophrenia
titles.author: Chris Frith, Eve C. Johnstone
titles.subject: Psychology/Social Work
titles.abstract: Schizophrenia: A Very Short Introduction considers a common mental disorder that has a devastating effect on sufferers and their families. While patients typically hear voices in their heads and hold bizarre beliefs, the schizophrenic patient presented to the public in sensational press reports and lurid films bears little resemblance to the reality of the illness. This VSI describes what schizophrenia is really like, how the illness progresses, and the treatments that have been applied. It summarizes the most up-to-date knowledge about the biological bases of schizophrenia and attempts to convey what it is like to suffer from it and what this disorder reveals about the relationship between mind and brain.
titles.summaryAI: Schizophrenia as a major mental disorder characterized by hallucinations, delusions, and negative symptoms. Covers the biological basis of schizophrenia including brain imaging through MRI, neurotransmitter systems, and pharmacological treatments. Examines the disorder's progression, diagnostic criteria, and the subjective experience of sufferers. Addresses misconceptions from sensationalized media portrayals and distinguishes schizophrenia from conditions like Alzheimer's disease and multiple sclerosis. Includes behaviour therapy approaches alongside drug treatments. Reveals what schizophrenia discloses about the relationship between mind and brain. Connects to neuroscience, psychiatric classification, psychopharmacology, and the philosophy of mental illness.

titles.title: The Earth
titles.author: Martin Redfern
titles.subject: Earth Sciences and Geography/Environment
titles.abstract: The Earth: A Very Short Introduction provides an introduction to the processes that govern the changing geology of the Earth. About 30 years ago, two things happened that began to revolutionize the understanding of these processes. First, geologists realized that the continents themselves were drifting across the surface of the globe and that the oceans were being created and destroyed. Secondly, satellites in space returned images of the entire planet and Earth began to be viewed as a single, dynamic entity. This VSI looks at how those ideas have transformed our understanding of the geological processes that are at work on our planet.
titles.summaryAI: Earth science encompassing plate tectonics, geological processes, and planetary formation within the solar system. Covers continental drift (Alfred Wegener), convection currents (Arthur Holmes), chemical weathering, radioactive decay as a dating tool, and ocean systems including the Gulf Stream conveyor belt. Examines how satellite imaging transformed Earth into a single dynamic system and how plate tectonics revolutionized geology since the 1960s. Addresses solar wind interactions, Earth's magnetic field, and Charles Darwin's geological observations. Distinguished from planetary science by its focus on terrestrial processes. Connects to oceanography, atmospheric science, geophysics, and the history of geological discovery.

titles.title: Engels
titles.author: Terrell Carver
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: Engels: A Very Short Introduction examines the man who invented Marxism. Engels' work did more than Marx to make converts to the most influential political movement of modern times. He was not only the father of dialectical and historical materialism (the official philosophies of history and science in many communist countries), he was also the first Marxist historian, anthropologist, philosopher, and commentator on early Marx. In his later years, Engels developed his materialist interpretation of history, his chief intellectual legacy. This VSI traces its source and its effect on the development of Marxist theory and practice, assesses its utility, and discusses the difficulties which Marxists have encountered in defending it.
titles.summaryAI: Friedrich Engels's intellectual contributions to Marxism, including the development of dialectical materialism, historical materialism, and political economy. Covers Engels's relationship with Karl Marx, his interpretation of Ludwig Feuerbach, and his role as the first Marxist historian, anthropologist, and philosopher. Examines Engels's analysis of class dynamics between the working class and middle class, his materialist interpretation of history as his chief intellectual legacy, and the adoption of his ideas as official philosophy in Soviet Union and other communist states. Distinguished from Marx's own writings by Engels's systematizing and popularizing role. Connects to socialist theory, philosophy of history, and 20th-century communist ideology.

titles.title: British Politics
titles.author: Tony Wright
titles.subject: Politics – U.K.
titles.abstract: British Politics: A Very Short Introduction explores the history of British politics, looking at whether the present instability is an aberration, the result of long-standing fault lines, or both. Current events are placed within a longer, larger perspective, focusing on Britain’s constitution, its polarized political culture of debates and disagreements, the importance of party politics, and the meaning of representative democracy now. Following the financial crisis, a peacetime coalition government, and the fallout from the 2016 referendum, Britain’s political future is uncertain. However, even after the momentous changes leading up to and including the 2019 general election, it remains uncertain whether the character of British politics will fundamentally change.
titles.summaryAI: British politics as a constitutional and institutional system, covering parliamentary democracy, party politics, and political culture from historical foundations to the post-2019 era. Examines the 2016 Brexit referendum, the 2019 general election, the 2010 coalition government, and the aftermath of the financial crisis. Covers Britain's unwritten constitution, the polarized culture of political debate, representative democracy, and questions about fundamental political change. Distinguished from comparative politics by its focus on specifically British institutions and conventions. Connects to constitutional law, European integration, electoral systems, and debates about democratic legitimacy and political polarization.

titles.title: Linguistics
titles.author: P. H. Matthews
titles.subject: Linguistics
titles.abstract: Linguistics: A Very Short Introduction considers a subject that falls in the gap between arts and science, on the edges of which fascinating discoveries and important problems are found. Beginning with the oldest, ‘arts’ end of the subject, it moves chronologically through to the newest research — the ‘science’ aspects. Themes included are: the prehistory of languages and their common origins; language and evolution; language in time and space (the nature of change inherent in language) grammars and dictionaries (how systematic is language?) and phonetics. Explication of the newest discoveries relating to language in the brain completes the coverage of major aspects of linguistics.
titles.summaryAI: Linguistics as the scientific study of language, spanning historical linguistics, phonetics, syntax, semantics, and neurolinguistics. Covers the prehistory and common origins of languages, language evolution, language change over time and across geographic space, and the relationship between speech and writing. Examines grammar and dictionaries as systematizations of language, with examples from French, German, and Spanish. Includes discoveries about language processing in the brain. Distinguished from literary studies by its empirical and structural approach to language. Connects to cognitive science, anthropology, evolutionary biology, and the philosophy of language.

titles.title: The Celts
titles.author: Barry Cunliffe
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: The Celts: A Very Short Introduction seeks to reveal the true nature of the Celts. Were they savage and bloodthirsty, or civilized and peaceable? The Celts have long been a subject of enormous fascination, speculation, and misunderstanding. From the ancient Romans to the present day, their real character has been obscured by a tangled web of preconceived ideas and stereotypes. This VSI aims to show what the Celts were really like, exploring subjects such as trade, migration, and the evolution of Celtic traditions. Along the way, it exposes how society's needs have shaped our visions of the Celts, and examines such colourful characters as St Patrick, Cú Chulainn, and Boudica.
titles.summaryAI: The Celts as an archaeological and linguistic cultural group spanning from the Iron Age through the Roman period to modern Celtic identities across Britain, Ireland, and continental Europe. Covers trade networks, migration, material culture including pottery and weapons, and the evolution of Celtic traditions. Examines Julius Caesar's accounts, Boudica, Cu Chulainn, and St Patrick. Addresses how stereotypes have shaped interpretations of Celtic culture from Roman ethnography to modern nationalism. Distinguished from Germanic and Roman cultures by language family and artistic style. Connects to archaeology, mythology, and ethnic identity.

titles.title: Ideology
titles.author: Michael Freeden
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: Ideology: A Very Brief Introduction is an examination of the major theories of ideology and the ways they have enriched our comprehension of ideology. Ideology is one of the most controversial terms in the political vocabulary. It creates both revulsion and inspiration. This VSI examines the reasons for this reaction and explains why ideologies deserve respect as a major form of political thinking. It explores the changing understandings of ideology as a concept and the arguments of the main ideologies. It draws on a range of disciplines in order to show the potency of ideology as a resource at the disposal of societies.
titles.summaryAI: Ideology as a concept in political thought, covering liberalism, Marxism (Marx, Engels), fascism (Hitler), and other major political ideologies. Examines theories of ideology from Karl Mannheim's sociology of knowledge to contemporary analysis, including the concept of false consciousness. Addresses how ideology functions through language, political parties, and institutional structures. Explores why ideology generates both revulsion and inspiration as a form of political thinking. Distinguished from political philosophy by its focus on how ideas function in political practice rather than abstract normative theory. Connects to sociology of knowledge, political party systems, propaganda, and the relationship between ideas and political power.

titles.title: Prehistory
titles.author: Chris Gosden
titles.subject: History/Archaeology/Art
titles.abstract: Prehistory covers the period of some four million years before the start of written history, when our earliest ancestors, the Australopithecines, existed in Africa. Prehistory: A Very Short Introduction invites us to think about who we are by considering who we have been. There have been many archaeological discoveries over the last ten years, with a new framework for prehistory emerging. Greater understanding of Chinese and central Asian prehistory shows Eurasian prehistory in a different light, changing the traditional view of human progress around the invention of agriculture and development of cities. This VSI explores the new landscape of our prehistory, considering the way the different geographical locations weave together.
titles.summaryAI: Prehistory spanning approximately four million years from the earliest Australopithecines in Africa to the beginning of written records. Covers the Holocene epoch, the invention of agriculture, the development of cities, and the Clovis Culture in the Americas. Examines new archaeological frameworks incorporating Chinese and central Asian evidence that reframe traditional Eurasian narratives. Addresses Darwin's influence on evolutionary thinking about human origins and the different geographical trajectories of prehistoric development. Distinguished from ancient history by the absence of written records and reliance on material evidence. Connects to archaeology, physical anthropology, agricultural origins, and the comparative study of early human societies.

titles.title: Political philosophy
titles.author: David Miller
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Political Philosophy: A Very Short Introduction introduces the concepts of political philosophy — authority, democracy, freedom and its limits, justice, feminism, multiculturalism, and nationality. It asks such questions as: Is it really true that what governments do profoundly affects the quality of our lives? Do states need to be able to coerce their subjects if they are to have political authority? What role should ordinary citizens play in a democracy? It looks at the reasons why we need politics at all, the limitations of politics, and whether there are areas of life that shouldn't be governed by politics.
titles.summaryAI: Political philosophy as a discipline examining authority, democracy, freedom, justice, human rights, feminism, multiculturalism, and nationality. Covers social contract theory from Hobbes and Locke, civil disobedience, the state of nature, and the moral foundations of political obligation. Addresses why political institutions are necessary, the limits of legitimate political authority, and areas of life that should remain free from political governance. Distinguished from political science by its normative rather than empirical approach. Connects to ethics, constitutional theory, international law, theories of distributive justice, and debates about the proper scope and limits of government power.

titles.title: Postcolonialism
titles.author: Robert J.C. Young
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract: Postcolonialism: A Very Short Introduction describes how people around the world have increasingly challenged the idea that Western perspectives are the only ones that count. It examines the history of that challenge, outlining the ideas behind it, and exploring how the histories and cultures of the world can be rethought in new, productive directions. This VSI situates the discussion in wide cultural and geographical contexts. It draws on examples such as the situation of indigenous peoples, of those dispossessed from their lands, Algerian raï music, and global social and ecological movements. This new edition also includes updated material on race, slavery, decoloniality, and the postcolonial politics of gender and sexualities.
titles.summaryAI: Postcolonialism as an intellectual and political movement challenging Western-centric perspectives on history, culture, and knowledge. Covers the experiences of indigenous peoples, dispossessed populations, and colonized societies across Africa, Asia, and the Americas. Examines decoloniality, race, slavery, and gender politics within postcolonial frameworks, alongside cultural expressions such as Algerian rai music and global social movements. Addresses environmental and ecological dimensions of colonial legacies. Distinguished from postmodernism by its grounding in material histories of colonial exploitation. Connects to literary criticism, cultural studies, global history, racial justice, and the politics of knowledge production.

titles.title: Atheism
titles.author: Julian Baggini
titles.subject: Religion/Philosophy
titles.abstract: Atheism: A Very Short Introduction discusses the case for atheism. Atheism is often seen as simply a rejection of theism, but it encompasses so much more. Atheists are typically naturalists, who believe that meaning and morality are possible in a finite, natural world. ‘New Atheism’, a powerful new movement in atheism in the early 21st century, driven by books from authors such as Richard Dawkins and Sam Harris, has left a legacy. There is an important question to consider: whether East Asia has been historically atheist or not. Atheism can be located in recent European history. What is the position of atheists around the world today?
titles.summaryAI: Atheism as a philosophical position and cultural phenomenon encompassing naturalism, secular ethics, and the rejection of theistic belief. Covers the New Atheism movement of the early 21st century driven by Richard Dawkins and Sam Harris, the historical roots of atheism in European Enlightenment thought, and the question of whether East Asian traditions constitute historical atheism. Examines how atheists construct meaning and morality within a finite, natural world without supernatural foundations. Distinguished from agnosticism by its affirmative denial of deities. Connects to philosophy of religion, secularism, comparative religion, the relationship between science and faith, and global religious demographics.

titles.title: Evolution
titles.author: Brian Charlesworth, Deborah Charlesworth
titles.subject: Biology/Anthropology
titles.abstract: Evolution: A Very Short Introduction explains the crucial role of evolutionary biology in transforming our view of human origins and relation to the universe, and the impact of this idea on traditional philosophy and religion. It introduces some of the most important findings, concepts, and procedures of evolutionary biology, as it has developed since the publications of Darwin and Wallace on the subject, over 140 years ago. Evolution provides a unifying set of principles for the whole of biology. In addition, many aspects of evolution have practical importance; for instance, the rapid evolutionary resistance by bacteria to antibiotics and of HIV to antiviral drugs are pressing medical problems.
titles.summaryAI: Evolution as a unifying biological framework explaining the diversity and adaptation of life through natural selection, genetic drift, and other mechanisms. Covers Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace's foundational work, artificial selection, speciation, kin selection, and molecular evolution including protein and bacterial genetics. Examines practical implications including antibiotic resistance in bacteria and HIV antiviral resistance. Addresses the relationship between evolutionary theory and traditional philosophy and religion, including human origins. Uses Darwin's finches as a case study. Distinguished from creationism and intelligent design. Connects to genetics, medicine, ecology, anthropology, and the philosophy of science.

titles.title: Molecules
titles.author: Philip Ball
titles.subject: Biology/Chemistry
titles.abstract: Molecules: A Very Short Introduction investigates the nature of molecules and how molecular structures and activities underlie the properties of materials and the processes of life. A living cell is like a city teeming with molecular inhabitants that move, communicate, cooperate, and compete. This VSI considers how a single fertilized egg can grow into a multi–celled Mozart, what makes spider's silk insoluble in the morning dew, and how our understanding and synthesis of molecules has led to exciting new areas of research into the development of molecular machines and powerful yet tiny molecular computers.
titles.summaryAI: Molecular science spanning biochemistry, organic chemistry, materials science, and nanotechnology. Covers chemical bonding (covalent bonds, hydrogen bonds), the alpha helix and other protein structures, cellular metabolism, and molecular recognition. Examines how molecular structures underlie both the properties of materials (such as spider silk) and biological processes from fertilization to organismal development. Addresses emerging research in molecular machines and molecular computing. Distinguished from atomic physics by its focus on multi-atom structures and interactions. Connects to cell biology, genetics, pharmacology, polymer science, and the development of nanoscale technologies.

titles.title: Art history
titles.author: Dana Arnold
titles.subject: Art/History
titles.abstract: Art history encompasses the study of the history and development of painting, sculpture, and the other visual arts. Art History: A Very Short Introduction considers the issues, debates, and artefacts that make up art history. It explores the emergence of social histories of art and, using a wide range of images, it discusses key aspects of the discipline including how we write about, present, read, and look at art, and the impact this has on our understanding of art history. This second edition includes a new chapter on global art histories, considering how the traditional emphasis on periods and styles in art originated in Western art and can obscure other critical approaches and artwork from non-Western cultures.
titles.summaryAI: Art history as a discipline studying the development of painting, sculpture, and visual arts from ancient times (Apollo Belvedere) through the International Gothic and Renaissance (Gentile da Fabriano, Ghiberti, Cennini) to Impressionism (Monet) and contemporary art (Damien Hirst). Covers methods of writing about, presenting, and interpreting art, including the camera obscura's influence on representation. Examines the emergence of social histories of art and global art histories that challenge Western-centric emphasis on period styles. Distinguished from art theory by its historical and contextual approach. Connects to cultural history, museum studies, visual culture, and debates about canon formation and non-Western artistic traditions.

titles.title: Presocratic Philosophy
titles.author: Catherine Osborne
titles.subject: Philosophy/Classical Studies
titles.abstract: Presocratic Philosophy: A Very Short Introduction introduces the first thinkers to explore ideas about the nature of reality, time, and the origin of the universe. It begins by discussing a new papyrus fragment of Empedocles, and uses the story of its discovery and interpretation to highlight the way our understanding of early philosophers is marked by their presentation in later sources. Generations of philosophers have traced their inspiration back to the Presocratics. The fragmentary remains of thinkers from Thales to Pythagoras, and from Heraclitus to Protagoras, are explored to try to fill in the bits of a jigsaw that has been rejigged many times and in many different ways.
titles.summaryAI: Presocratic philosophy encompassing the earliest Greek thinkers from Thales to Protagoras (roughly 6th-5th centuries BC), who explored the nature of reality, time, and the origin of the universe. Key figures include Parmenides, Heraclitus, Democritus, Anaxagoras, Empedocles, and Pythagoras. Covers first principles, the relationship between reason and perception, early atomic theory, and proto-scientific inquiry. Examines how fragmentary sources and later reinterpretation (including Nietzsche's) shape understanding of these thinkers. Distinguished from classical Athenian philosophy (Plato, Aristotle) by its cosmological focus. Connects to the history of science, metaphysics, epistemology, and the foundations of Western philosophical inquiry.

titles.title: The Elements
titles.author: Philip Ball
titles.subject: Chemistry/History of Science
titles.abstract: The Elements: A Very Short Introduction traces the history and cultural impact of the elements on humankind, and examines why people have long sought to identify the substances around them. Looking beyond the Periodic Table, our relationship with matter is examined, from the uncomplicated vision of the Greek philosophers who believed there were four elements — earth, air, fire, and water — to the work of modern–day scientists in creating elements such as hassium and meitnerium. This VSI is an exploration of the fundamental question: what is the world made from?
titles.summaryAI: The chemical elements traced from the ancient Greek four-element theory (earth, air, fire, water) through Lavoisier's modern chemical nomenclature to contemporary synthesis of transuranic elements like hassium and meitnerium. Covers atomic structure, atomic weight, isotopes, noble gases, quantum mechanics, and the periodic table as an organizing framework. Examines the Big Bang as the origin of elements, heavy water and nuclear science, and the cultural significance of elements like gold and oxygen. Distinguished from molecular chemistry by its focus on fundamental atomic species. Connects to nuclear physics, cosmology, materials science, the history of chemistry, and quantum theory.

titles.title: Dada and Surrealism
titles.author: David Hopkins
titles.subject: Art
titles.abstract: Dada and Surrealism: A Very Short Introduction focuses on the many debates surrounding these movements: the Marquis de Sade's Surrealist deification, issues of quality, the idea of the ‘readymade’, attitudes towards the city, the impact of Freud, attitudes to women, and primitivism. Where does the Dadaist obsession with the body-as-mechanism come from? Why are the Surrealists obsessed with the fetishized/eroticized body? Dada and Surrealism continue to have a huge influence on cultural practice, especially in contemporary art, with its obsession with sexuality, fetishism, and shock tactics. A huge range of media is employed by both movements (collage, painting, found objects, performance art, photography, film), but there are aesthetic differences between them.
titles.summaryAI: Dada and Surrealism as avant-garde art movements from the 1910s through the mid-20th century influencing contemporary practice. Key figures include De Chirico, Magritte, Miro, Gorky, and the Young British Artists. Examines the readymade concept, Freudian psychoanalysis in art, the eroticized body, primitivism, and Sade's role in Surrealist thought. Covers collage, painting, found objects, performance art, photography, and film. Distinguished from each other by Dada's nihilistic anti-art stance versus Surrealism's exploration of the unconscious. Connects to psychoanalysis, modernism, sexuality, and shock in contemporary art.

titles.title: Egyptian Myth
titles.author: Geraldine Pinch
titles.subject: Classical Studies
titles.abstract: Egyptian Myth: A Very Short Introduction explores the cultural and historical background behind a wide variety of sources and objects on Egyptian mythology, from Cleopatra's Needle and Tutankhamun's golden statue, to a story on papyrus of the gods misbehaving. The world of Egyptian myth is complex. Mythology was an integral part of Egyptian culture for much of its timespan. What did the myths mean, and how have they been interpreted? How have the myths of deities such as Isis and Osiris influenced contemporary culture and become part of our cultural heritage?
titles.summaryAI: Egyptian mythology as an integral component of ancient Egyptian religion and culture spanning thousands of years. Covers major deities including Osiris, Horus, and Seth, with key texts such as the Coffin Texts and the Contendings of Horus and Seth. Examines mythological narratives found on papyri, temples, and artifacts from sites including Deir El-Medina, connected to pharaohs Amenhotep III and Ramesses II.
