Addresses the cultural significance of deities like Isis in contemporary reception. Distinguished from Greek and Roman mythology by its integration with ritual practice and royal ideology. Connects to Egyptology, comparative religion, ancient literature, and cultural heritage studies.

titles.title: Christian art
titles.author: Beth Williamson
titles.subject: Religion/Art
titles.abstract: Christian Art: A Very Short Introduction explores the work of major artists such as Memling, Holbein, El Greco, and Rossetti, and well-known examples including the frescoes of St Francis at Assisi. How do didactic and consciously devotional works compare with the controversial work of contemporary artists such as Andres Serrano and Chris Ofili? Christian images have a long history within the Western art tradition, from the narrative and devotional works of the Medieval and Renaissance periods, to the radical new interpretations of the twenty-first century. Over the centuries the nature of the representation of key themes and subjects found in Christian art such as the Eucharist, the crucifixion, the Virgin Mary, and the saints has changed considerably.
titles.summaryAI: Christian art spanning from early medieval narrative and devotional works through the Renaissance to radical contemporary interpretations. Covers major artists including Memling, Holbein, El Greco, and Rossetti, alongside controversial contemporary figures like Andres Serrano and Chris Ofili. Examines key themes including the Eucharist, crucifixion, Virgin Mary, saints, the Fall, St Francis of Assisi frescoes, and the Virgin of Guadalupe. Addresses the relationship between Christian imagery and pagan and Jewish visual traditions. Distinguished from secular Western art by its liturgical and devotional functions. Connects to theology, iconography, the history of Western visual culture, and debates about sacred imagery and blasphemy.

titles.title: Capitalism
titles.author: James Fulcher
titles.subject: Politics/Economics
titles.abstract: The word ‘capitalism’ is heard and used frequently, but what is capitalism about, and what does it mean? Capitalism: A Very Short Introduction discusses the history and development of capitalism through several detailed case studies, ranging from the ‘tulipomania’ of 17th-century Holland, the Great Depression of the 1930s, and the impact of the global financial crisis that started in 2007–8. It looks at the different forms that capitalism takes in Britain, Japan, Sweden, and the United States, and explores whether capitalism has escaped the nation-state by going global. It asks whether there is an alternative to capitalism, discussing socialism, communal and cooperative experiments, and the alternatives proposed by environmentalists.
titles.summaryAI: Capitalism as an economic system traced from 17th-century Dutch tulipomania through industrialization to the 2007-8 global financial crisis. Covers market forces, capital accumulation, production, trade, ownership structures, and financial systems including Wall Street. Examines comparative capitalisms in Britain, Japan, Sweden, and the United States. Addresses the Great Depression of the 1930s, economic deregulation, and globalization of capital beyond nation-states. Considers alternatives including socialism, communal and cooperative experiments, and environmentalist proposals. Distinguished from feudalism by its reliance on market exchange and wage labor. Connects to political economy, economic history, industrial development, and debates about economic inequality.

titles.title: Particle physics
titles.author: Frank Close
titles.subject: Physics
titles.abstract: Particle Physics: A Very Short Introduction takes a journey into the atom and presents an overview of the fundamental particles and forces that make up the universe. The VSI discusses the features and functions of particles such as quarks, electrons, and neutrinos, and of gluons and bosons. It includes the key role and significance of the Higgs boson in moulding these foundational elements and giving structure to matter. It highlights how discoveries in particle physics were made and have radically changed understanding of the material universe. The book concludes by considering the implications of the discovery of the Higgs boson, and asks whether massive neutrinos might be key to resolving the mystery surrounding the absence of antimatter.
titles.summaryAI: Particle physics examining the fundamental constituents of matter and forces of the universe. Covers quarks, electrons, neutrinos, gluons, bosons, and the Higgs boson's role in giving mass to particles. Examines atomic structure, antimatter-matter asymmetry, and whether massive neutrinos explain the absence of antimatter. Traces experimental discoveries that transformed understanding of the material universe. Distinguished from nuclear physics by its focus on subatomic constituents rather than atomic nuclei. Connects to cosmology, quantum field theory, high-energy experimental methods, and fundamental questions about matter.

titles.title: Free will
titles.author: Thomas Pink
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Free Will: A Very Short Introduction asks: Are our choices really free? Every day we seem to make and act upon all kinds of free choices. Are these choices ours, or are we compelled to act the way we do by factors beyond our control? Is the feeling that we could have made different decisions just an illusion? And if our choices are not free, why should we be held morally responsible for our actions? This VSI looks at a range of issues surrounding this fundamental philosophical question, exploring it from the ideas of the Greek and medieval philosophers through to the thoughts of present-day thinkers.
titles.summaryAI: Free will as a central problem in philosophy spanning from ancient Greek thought through medieval theology to modern debates. Covers determinism, compatibilism, and libertarian free will, with key thinkers including Hobbes, Kant, and contemporary philosophers. Examines the relationship between free will and moral responsibility, the role of randomness versus self-determination in human action, and skeptical challenges to the experience of genuine choice. Addresses whether the subjective sense of choosing freely is illusory. Distinguished from political freedom by its focus on metaphysical and psychological dimensions. Connects to ethics, philosophy of mind, neuroscience of decision-making, and theories of moral and legal responsibility.

titles.title: Myth
titles.author: Robert A. Segal
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Where do myths come from? What is their function and what do they mean? Myth: A Very Short Introduction introduces a wide array of approaches to understanding myth from disciplines as varied as anthropology, sociology, psychology, literary criticism, philosophy, science, and religious studies. It uses the famous ancient myth of Adonis to analyse the ideas and individual approaches and theories of theorists such as Sigmund Freud, Claude Lévi-Strauss, Albert Camus, and Roland Barthes. This new edition considers the interactions of myth theory with cognitive science, the implications of the myth of Gaia, and the differences between story-telling and myth, as well as the future study of myth.
titles.summaryAI: Myth as a subject studied across anthropology, sociology, psychology, literary criticism, philosophy, science, and religious studies. Covers theoretical approaches from Freud's psychoanalytic interpretation to Levi-Strauss's structuralism, Camus's existentialism, Barthes's semiotics, and Propp's narrative morphology. Uses the ancient Adonis myth as a central analytical case. Examines the distinction between myth, legend, and story-telling, the relationship between myth and cognitive science, and the Gaia hypothesis as modern myth. Distinguished from folklore by its cosmological and explanatory functions. Connects to comparative religion, narrative theory, cultural anthropology, and the intersection of scientific and mythological worldviews.

titles.title: Ancient Egypt
titles.author: Ian Shaw
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: Ancient Egypt: A Very Short Introduction explores the history and culture of pharaonic Egypt, including ideas about Egyptian kingship, religion, ethics, customs, and writing systems. The ancient Egyptians are an enduring source of fascination and mystery; for generations, mummies and pyramids, curses and rituals have captured the imagination. This VSI draws on the latest archaeological discoveries and scholarship on ancient Egypt. It considers issues relating to the history of Egyptology, ethnicity, race, gender, and sexual relations. Moreover, it examines the impact of the Arab Spring on approaches to Egyptian museums and cultural heritage all over the world.
titles.summaryAI: Ancient Egypt spanning pharaonic civilization from the earliest dynasties through the New Kingdom and beyond. Covers Egyptian kingship, religion, ethics, customs, and writing systems, alongside the history of Egyptology as a discipline. Examines issues of ethnicity, race, gender, and sexual relations in ancient Egyptian society. Addresses the impact of the Arab Spring on approaches to Egyptian museums and cultural heritage worldwide. Distinguished from other ancient Near Eastern civilizations by its distinctive monumental architecture, hieroglyphic script, and religious practices. Connects to archaeology, museum studies, postcolonial approaches to heritage, and the comparative study of ancient civilizations and their modern political contexts.

titles.title: Hieroglyphs
titles.author: Penelope Wilson
titles.subject: Classical Studies
titles.abstract: Hieroglyphs: A Very Short Introduction explores the cultural significance of this script with an emphasis on previously neglected areas such as cryptography and the continuing decipherment into modern times. It examines the powerful fascination hieroglyphs still hold for us today. The people of Ancient Egypt left behind many monuments and objects, covered in the writing now known as Egyptian hieroglyphs. Hieroglyphs were far more than a language. They were an omnipresent and all-powerful force in communicating the messages of ancient Egyptian culture for over three thousand years; used as monumental art, as a means of identifying Egyptianness, and for rarefied communication with the gods.
titles.summaryAI: Egyptian hieroglyphs as a writing system spanning over three thousand years from the Early Dynastic Period through the Old, Middle, and New Kingdoms. Covers the Rosetta Stone and modern decipherment, the Pyramid Texts, Coffin Texts, and their significance as monumental art and ritual communication. Examines hieroglyphic cryptography, the relationship between hieroglyphs and Egyptian cultural identity, and their function in communicating with the gods. Distinguished from other ancient scripts (cuneiform, Linear B) by their pictographic aesthetic and sacral function. Connects to epigraphy, Egyptology, linguistics, art history, and the study of ancient literacy and textual transmission.

titles.title: Medical ethics
titles.author: Tony Hope
titles.subject: Philosophy/Medical Ethics
titles.abstract: Medical ethics—from thorny moral questions such as euthanasia and the morality of killing to political questions such as the fair distribution of healthcare resources—is rarely out of today’s media. Medical Ethics: A Very Short Introduction explores the ethical reasoning used to approach medical ethics, introducing the most important ‘tools’ of ethical reasoning, and discussing how argument, thought experiments, and intuition can be combined in the consideration of medical ethics. It also considers its practical application by health professionals in clinical settings and the increasingly important place of medical ethics in the wider social context, in healthcare policy, discussions in the media, pressure group and activism settings, and in legal judgements.
titles.summaryAI: Medical ethics as an applied branch of philosophy addressing moral questions in healthcare. Covers euthanasia, consent, fair distribution of healthcare resources, and health policy. Draws on ethical reasoning tools including thought experiments, intuitive moral judgments, and systematic argumentation, with references to Socrates and Isaiah Berlin's value pluralism. Examines medical ethics in clinical settings, public discourse, media, pressure group activism, and legal judgments. Distinguished from bioethics by its traditional emphasis on physician-patient relationships. Connects to political philosophy of justice, law, public health policy, and debates about autonomy, beneficence, and the limits of medical intervention.

titles.title: Kafka
titles.author: Ritchie Robertson
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: Attending both to Kafka's crisis-ridden life and to the subtleties of his art, Kafka: A Very Short Introduction shows how his work explores such characteristically modern themes as the place of the body in culture, the power of institutions over people, and the possibility of religion after Nietzsche had proclaimed ‘the death of God’. Kafka is among the most intriguing and influential writers of the 20th century. During his lifetime he worked as a civil servant and published only a few short stories. All three of his novels, The Trial, The Castle, and The Man Who Disappeared [Amerika], were published after his death and helped to found Kafka's reputation as a uniquely perceptive interpreter of the 20th century.
titles.summaryAI: Franz Kafka's literary works and their significance as interpretations of 20th-century modernity. Covers major novels The Trial, The Castle, and The Man Who Disappeared (Amerika), alongside his short stories. Examines themes of institutional power over individuals, the body in culture, and religion after Nietzsche's proclamation of the death of God. Contextualizes Kafka among literary figures including Dickens, Flaubert, Tolstoy, Thomas Mann, and Byron. Addresses Kafka's civil service career and posthumous publication through Edwin Muir's translations. Distinguished from expressionism by Kafka's precise realist prose style. Connects to existentialism, modernist literature, bureaucratic theory, and the philosophy of absurdity.

titles.title: Anarchism
titles.author: Alex Prichard
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: Anarchism: A Very Short Introduction explores key anarchist thinkers from Kropotkin to Chomsky and considers anarchism from a variety of perspectives: theoretical, historical, and international. What exactly is anarchism and who are the anarchists? Can anarchy ever function effectively as a political force? Is it more ‘organized’ and ‘reasonable’ than is currently perceived? The word ‘anarchism’ tends to conjure up images of aggressive protest against government, and — recently — of angry demonstrations against bodies such as the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund. But is anarchism inevitably linked with violent disorder? Do anarchists adhere to a coherent ideology?
titles.summaryAI: Anarchism as a political philosophy advocating the abolition of the state and hierarchical authority. Covers key thinkers including Bakunin, Kropotkin, Godwin, Goldman, Paine, Stirner, Tolstoy, and Chomsky. Examines anarchist theory spanning individualist, collectivist, anarcho-communist, and federalist traditions. Addresses anarchism's relationship to Marxism, its historical manifestations from 19th-century labor movements to contemporary anti-globalization protests against the World Bank and IMF. Explores whether anarchism requires violence or can function as organized political practice. Distinguished from libertarianism by its anti-capitalist orientation. Connects to socialist theory, direct democracy, mutual aid, and debates about the necessity of governmental authority.

titles.title: Ancient warfare
titles.author: Harry Sidebottom
titles.subject: Classical Studies
titles.abstract: Ancient Warfare: A Very Short Introduction examines all aspects of ancient warfare, from philosophy and strategy to the technical skills needed to fight. How did wars shape classical society? How was the individual's identity constructed by war? Can a war be just? Why was siege warfare particularly bloody? What role did divine intervention play in the outcome of a battle? Greek and Roman warfare differed from other cultures and was unlike any other forms of warfare. The key difference is often held to be that the Greeks and Romans practised a ‘Western Way of War’, where the aim is an open, decisive battle, won by courage instilled in part by discipline. What is this ‘Western Way of War’?
titles.summaryAI: Ancient warfare in Greek and Roman civilizations, covering military philosophy, strategy, tactics, and combat experience. Examines the Western Way of War emphasizing open decisive battle, discipline, and courage. Covers siege warfare, divine intervention in battle, and just war theory. Key figures include Alexander the Great, Julius Caesar, and religious-military thinkers including Muhammad and Origen. Addresses how warfare shaped classical identity and society. Distinguished from modern military history by its integration with civic and religious life. Connects to political theory, ethics of war, and comparative military studies.

titles.title: Climate change
titles.author: Mark Maslin
titles.subject: Earth Sciences and Geography/Environment
titles.abstract: Climate Change: A Very Short Introduction examines the science, the history, and the politics of climate change. Drawing on the latest science from the recent IPCC reports, this VSI examines the potential catastrophic impacts of climate change in the future. Global awareness of climate change has grown very rapidly, as shown by the wide support for campaigners like Greta Thunberg and groups like Extinction Rebellion, and the declaration by many governments that we are now in a climate emergency. It is a threat that forces us to examine the whole basis of modern society. This VSI explores the geopolitical, economic, technological, and social solutions to climate change, and argues for new modes of thinking in tackling the climate crisis.
titles.summaryAI: Climate change science, history, and politics covering global warming, extreme weather events, and the climate emergency. Draws on IPCC reports for scientific evidence on temperature rise, sea level change, and ecological disruption. Examines geopolitical dimensions, global health impacts, and economic consequences of climate change. Covers climate activism including Greta Thunberg and Extinction Rebellion, alongside government declarations of climate emergency. Addresses technological, economic, and social solutions to decarbonization. Distinguished from natural climate variability by its focus on anthropogenic causes. Connects to energy policy, environmental justice, international diplomacy, public health, and the restructuring of industrial economies.

titles.title: Christianity
titles.author: Linda Woodhead
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: At a time when Christianity is flourishing in the Southern hemisphere but declining in much of the West, Christianity: A Very Short Introduction offers an important overview of the world's largest religion. Exploring the cultural and institutional dimensions of Christianity, and tracing its course over two millennia, it provides a candid portrait of Christianity's past and present. Addressing topics including the competition for power between different forms of Christianity, the churches' use of power, and its struggles with modernity, this VSI includes up-to-date information on the growth and geographical spread of Eastern Christianity, reflecting the global nature of Christianity in our ever-shifting contemporary culture.
titles.summaryAI: Christianity as a world religion spanning two millennia, covering its doctrinal development, institutional forms, and global spread. Examines the Bible (Old and New Testaments), the Gospels (Mark, Luke, John), Acts of the Apostles, and key figures including Jesus, St Thomas Aquinas, and Martin Luther. Covers the Roman Catholic Church, Orthodox Church, and Protestant traditions including the Second Vatican Council and Brethren of the Free Spirit. Addresses the growth of Eastern Christianity, competition for power among denominations, and Christianity's contemporary flourishing in the Southern hemisphere while declining in the West. Connects to theology, church history, ethics, and the sociology of religion.

titles.title: Modern art
titles.author: David Cottington
titles.subject: Art
titles.abstract: Modern Art: A Very Short Introduction examines many key aspects of this subject, including the issue of controversy in modern art, from Manet's Dejeuner sur L'Herbe (1863) to Picasso's Les Demoiselles, and Tracey Emin's Bed, (1999); and the role of the dealer. Public interest in modern art continues to grow, as witnessed by the spectacular success of Tate Modern and the Bilbao Guggenheim. What makes a work of art qualify as modern (or fail to)? How is this selection made? What is the relationship between modern and contemporary art? Is ‘postmodernist’ art no longer modern, or just no longer modernist? Why, and what does this claim mean, both for art and the idea of ‘the modern’?
titles.summaryAI: Modern art from the mid-19th century through the 20th century, covering avant-garde movements, institutional frameworks, and critical debates about what qualifies as modern. Examines key artists including Manet, Picasso, Van Gogh, Braque, Leger, Mondrian, Marinetti, Tatlin, Oldenburg, and the Young British Artists including Tracey Emin. Covers the role of museums (MoMA, Tate Modern, Bilbao Guggenheim), dealers, and critics like Clement Greenberg. Addresses the relationship between modern and contemporary art, modernism versus postmodernism, and the definition of artistic modernity. Distinguished from academic art by its rejection of traditional conventions. Connects to aesthetics, cultural institutions, art market economics, and cultural modernity.

titles.title: Consciousness
titles.author: Susan Blackmore
titles.subject: Philosophy/Psychology
titles.abstract: Consciousness is ‘the last great mystery for science’. How can a physical brain create our experience of the world? What creates our identity? Do we really have free will? Could consciousness itself be an illusion? Exciting new developments in brain science are continuing these debates, and the field has now expanded to include biologists, neuroscientists, psychologists, and philosophers. Consciousness: A Very Short Introduction clarifies the potentially confusing arguments, and the major theories, whilst also outlining the amazing pace of neuroscience discoveries. Covering areas such as construction of self in the brain, mechanisms of attention, neural correlates of consciousness, and physiology of altered states of consciousness, it highlights the latest findings.
titles.summaryAI: Consciousness as studied across philosophy, psychology, and neuroscience. Covers the hard problem of consciousness, neural correlates of consciousness, mechanisms of attention, and construction of self in the brain. Examines the grand illusion theory, whether consciousness itself may be illusory, the evolution of consciousness, and the relationship between free will and subjective experience. Addresses altered states of consciousness and their physiological basis. Distinguished from mere wakefulness or responsiveness by its subjective experiential quality (qualia). Connects to the philosophy of mind, cognitive neuroscience, artificial intelligence, meditation research, and debates about the explanatory gap between physical processes and subjective experience.

titles.title: Foucault
titles.author: Gary Gutting
titles.subject: Philosophy/Biography
titles.abstract: Foucault: A Very Short Introduction presents a wide-ranging, but non-systematic exploration of some highlights of Foucault’s life and thought. Beginning with a brief biography of the philosopher to set the social and political stage, it then tackles Foucault’s thoughts on literature, in particular the avant-garde scene; his philosophical and historical work; his treatment of knowledge and power in modern society; and his thoughts on sexuality. This new edition includes feminist criticisms of Foucault’s apparently sexist treatment of the Jouy case, as well as a new chapter offering a unified overview of the Collège de France lectures, now a major focus of interest in Foucault.
titles.summaryAI: Michel Foucault's philosophy spanning literature, history, knowledge, power, and sexuality. Covers Foucault's analysis of discourse, institutional power, and the construction of the modern subject through disciplines and surveillance. Examines his engagement with avant-garde literature, philosophical and historical methodology, and the relationship between knowledge and power in modern society. Addresses feminist criticisms of his treatment of the Jouy case and his College de France lectures on governmentality and biopolitics. Key intellectual context includes Kant, Spinoza, and Adam Smith. Distinguished from traditional philosophy by its genealogical method. Connects to social theory, queer studies, criminology, medical history, and poststructuralism.

titles.title: The Spanish Civil War
titles.author: Helen Graham
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: The Spanish Civil War: Very Short Introduction offers an explanation of the war's origins and course, explores its impact on a personal and international scale, and provides an ethical reflection on the war. How has the war inspired some of the greatest writers of our time? In what ways does it continue to resonate today in Britain, continental Europe, and beyond? The war can be seen as an arena of social change where ideas about culture were forged or resisted, and in which both Spaniards and non-Spaniards participated alike. During the Second World War, these conflicts would stretch from Franco's regime, which envisaged itself as part of the Nazi new order, to Europe and beyond.
titles.summaryAI: The Spanish Civil War (1936-1939) as a military conflict, social revolution, and international crisis. Covers the war's origins in Spanish political polarization, the Catholic Church's role, international intervention including Mussolini's Italy, and propaganda campaigns. Examines personal and civilian impact, the war's influence on major writers, and its connection to rising fascism. Addresses Franco's regime and its alignment with the Nazi order during World War II. Distinguished from other interwar conflicts by attracting international volunteers. Connects to European fascism, anti-fascist movements, cultural production, and Cold War McCarthyism.

titles.title: The Marquis de Sade
titles.author: John Phillips
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: The Marquis de Sade: A Very Short Introduction aims to disentangle the ‘real’ Marquis de Sade from his mythical and demonic reputation of the past two hundred years by examining Sade's libertine novels, his championing of atheism, and his uniqueness in bringing the body and sex back into philosophy. Were it not for the Sade's explicit use of language and complete disregard for the artificially constructed taboos of a religious morality he despised, the novelty and profundity of his thought, and above all, its fundamental modernity, would have long since secured him a place alongside the greatest authors and thinkers of the European Enlightenment.
titles.summaryAI: The Marquis de Sade (1740-1814) as a writer and philosopher of the French Enlightenment. Covers his libertine novels, radical atheism, and philosophical project of reintegrating the body and sexuality into philosophical discourse. Examines his relationship to Enlightenment thinkers including Diderot and Rousseau, and his influence on later figures including Freud, Bataille, and Pasolini. Addresses the construction of Sade's mythical and demonic reputation over two centuries versus his actual philosophical contributions. Distinguished from pornography by his systematic philosophical materialism. Connects to the history of sexuality, Enlightenment philosophy, censorship, moral philosophy, and the relationship between literature and transgression.

titles.title: Habermas
titles.author: Gordon Finlayson
titles.subject: Philosophy/Sociology/Biography
titles.abstract: Habermas: A Very Short Introduction provides an overview of Jürgen Habermas's forbiddingly complex philosophy using concrete examples. Habermas is arguably the most influential German philosopher alive today. He has influenced a variety of fields, including philosophy, political and social theory, cultural studies, sociology, and literary studies. He has commented widely on subjects such as Marxism, the importance and effectiveness of communication, the reunification of Germany, and the European Union. What are the theoretical underpinnings of Habermas's social theory? How can his social theory be applied to the fields of ethics, politics, and law? How have Habermas's social and political theories informed his writing on contemporary, political, and social problems?
titles.summaryAI: Jurgen Habermas's social and political philosophy spanning critical theory, discourse ethics, and democratic theory. Covers the Frankfurt School intellectual tradition, communicative action theory, the relationship between validity and meaning, and the application of discourse ethics to law and politics. Examines Habermas's engagement with Marxism, German reunification, the European Union, and contemporary political problems. Addresses the concept of civil society and the public sphere. Distinguished from poststructuralism by its commitment to rational consensus and universal norms. Connects to sociology, philosophy of language, political theory, legal philosophy, and debates about modernity and communicative rationality.

titles.title: Socialism
titles.author: Michael Newman
titles.subject: Politics/Economics/History
titles.abstract: Socialism: A Very Short Introduction looks at the history of socialism and its contemporary relevance. Is socialism an outdated ideology today? This VSI provides an overview of socialism’s origins, its relationships to communism and social democracy, its different manifestations around the world and throughout history, and its intersections with issues of ethnicity and gender. Examples of the practical implementation of socialist values are provided by case studies of social democracy in Sweden and communism in Cuba. The rise of the ‘New Left’ midway through the 20th century included feminist and green movements, followed up by radical anti-capitalist and climate justice protests and political movements.
titles.summaryAI: Socialism as a political ideology and movement traced from its 19th-century origins through communism and social democracy to contemporary progressive movements. Covers case studies of Swedish social democracy and Cuban communism. Examines socialism's intersections with feminism, the green movement, ethnicity, gender, and anti-capitalist movements including climate justice protests. Addresses the New Left of the mid-20th century and socialism's relationship to communist ideology. Distinguished from liberalism by its emphasis on collective ownership and economic equality. Connects to labor history, welfare state theory, revolutionary politics, environmentalism, and debates about economic alternatives to capitalism.

titles.title: Dreaming
titles.author: J. Allan Hobson
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: Dreaming: A Very Short Introduction provides an increasingly complete picture of how dreaming is created by the brain. It explores how the new science of dreaming is affecting psychoanalysis theories and how it is helping our understanding of the causes of mental illness. Dreams are investigated to illustrate and explain some of the fascinating discoveries of modern sleep science, while challenging some of the traditionally accepted theories about dream meaning. How dreaming maintains and develops the mind, why we go crazy in our dreams in order to avoid doing so when we are awake, and why sleep is not just good for health, but essential for life is revealed.
titles.summaryAI: Dreaming as studied through modern sleep science, neuroscience, and its relationship to psychoanalytic theory. Covers the brain mechanisms that generate dreams, including the limbic system and reticular activating system, examined through brain imaging and MRI. Addresses the function of dreams in maintaining mental health, the relationship between dreaming and mental illness, and why dream content appears bizarre. Challenges traditional psychoanalytic dream interpretation with neurobiological evidence. Examines how dreaming supports memory consolidation and cognitive function. Distinguished from waking consciousness by its neurochemical profile. Connects to psychiatry, cognitive neuroscience, sleep medicine, and the philosophy of consciousness.

titles.title: Dinosaurs
titles.author: David Norman
titles.subject: Biology/Earth Sciences/Geography
titles.abstract: Dinosaurs: A Very Short Introduction discusses how dinosaurs were first discovered and interpreted, and how our understanding of them has changed over the past 200 years. It looks at some of the amazing discoveries that have enabled us to gain new and unexpected insights into dinosaurs as animals with natural histories and behaviours, and considers some of the biggest questions in dinosaur biology, such as the implications of their having warm blood. It also shows how research on dinosaurs has been enriched, particularly in recent decades, by technological break-throughs which complement the informed speculation and luck that have played a part in many of the major discoveries.
titles.summaryAI: Dinosaurs as a biological group spanning the Mesozoic Era, covering their discovery, classification, and evolving scientific understanding over 200 years. Examines warm-bloodedness hypotheses, behavioral reconstruction, and natural history. Covers the mass extinction event evidenced by the iridium anomaly and cosmic dust, linking to nuclear winter scenarios. Includes Richard Owen's foundational taxonomy and iconic species like Tyrannosaurus Rex. Addresses how technological advances including CT scanning and isotopic analysis have transformed paleontological research. Distinguished from other prehistoric reptiles by hip structure and upright gait. Connects to geology, evolutionary biology, extinction studies, and the history of natural science.

titles.title: Renaissance Art
titles.author: Geraldine A. Johnson
titles.subject: Art
titles.abstract: Renaissance Art: A Very Short Introduction provides a broad cultural-historical context for some of the Renaissance's most famous artists and works of art, introducing both famous and lesser-known artists, patrons and works of art within the cultural and historical context of Renaissance Europe. Botticelli, Holbein, Leonardo, Dürer, and Michelangelo are familiar names, but who were these artists, why did they produce such memorable images, and how would their original beholders have viewed these objects? Was the Renaissance only about great masters and masterpieces, or were ‘mistresses’ also involved, such as women artists and patrons? And what about the ‘minor’-pieces that Renaissance men and women would have encountered in homes, churches and civic spaces?
titles.summaryAI: Renaissance art in Europe from roughly the 14th through 16th centuries, covering painting, sculpture, and decorative arts in their cultural and historical context. Examines major artists including Botticelli, Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, Durer, Jan van Eyck, and Carpaccio, alongside theorists Leon Battista Alberti and Pliny the Elder. Covers patronage systems, women as both artists and patrons, the role of art in churches, homes, and civic spaces, and Jacob Burckhardt's historiography. Addresses Northern and Southern European traditions. Distinguished from medieval art by its emphasis on naturalism, perspective, and classical revival. Connects to cultural history, social hierarchy, gender studies, and art theory.

titles.title: Buddhist ethics
titles.author: Damien Keown
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Buddhist Ethics: A Very Short Introduction brings together two largely independent fields of knowledge: Buddhism and ethics. It begins by explaining Buddhist ethical teachings and then asking how these teachings might be classified in terms of more familiar Western theories of ethics. Following this overview, the remainder of the book considers how Buddhism might respond to six specific issues of contemporary concern that have proved controversial in the West. A separate chapter is devoted to each of the following topics: animals and the environment; sexuality and gender; war, violence, and terrorism; abortion; suicide and euthanasia; and biomedical technologies and Transhumanism. While Buddhism shares many moral values with other religions like Christianity, some aspects of its moral teachings—like karma and rebirth—are less familiar and result in positions that are more nuanced than might at first be imagined.
titles.summaryAI: Buddhist ethics as a system of moral thought integrating traditional Buddhist teachings with contemporary Western ethical concerns. Covers the Buddhist moral code, karma and rebirth as ethical foundations, and Buddhist positions on six controversial issues: animals and the environment, sexuality and gender, war and terrorism, abortion, suicide and euthanasia, and biomedical technologies including Transhumanism. Compares Buddhist ethical frameworks with Western theories including utilitarianism and deontology. Distinguished from secular ethics by its grounding in karma and spiritual development. Connects to comparative religion, bioethics, environmental ethics, just war theory, and cross-cultural moral philosophy.

titles.title: Tragedy
titles.author: Adrian Poole
titles.subject: Classical Studies
titles.abstract: Tragedy: A Very Short Introduction addresses questions of tragedy about belief, blame, mourning, revenge, pain, witnessing, timing, and ending and demonstrates the age-old significance of our attempts to make sense of terrible suffering. What do we mean by ‘tragedy’ in present-day usage? When we turn on the news, does a report of the latest atrocity have any connection with the masterpieces of Sophocles, Shakespeare, and Racine? What has tragedy been made to mean by dramatists, story-tellers, critics, philosophers, politicians, and journalists over the last two and a half millennia? Why do we still read, re-write, and stage these old plays?
titles.summaryAI: Tragedy as a literary and dramatic genre spanning from ancient Greek theater to contemporary literature and media. Covers Sophocles, Shakespeare, Racine, Corneille, Brecht, Homer, and Philip Roth. Examines the philosophical dimensions of tragic drama including questions of belief, blame, revenge, mourning, pain, and the meaning of suffering. Addresses the satyr-play tradition, the relationship between fictional tragedy and real-world atrocity, and how critics, philosophers, politicians, and journalists have defined tragedy across two and a half millennia. Distinguished from melodrama by its exploration of irresolvable moral conflict. Connects to aesthetics, performance studies, moral philosophy, and the cultural function of narrative suffering.

titles.title: Sikhism
titles.author: Eleanor Nesbitt
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: The Sikh religion has a following of around twenty-four million people worldwide, and is ranked as the world’s fifth largest religion. Sikhism: A Very Short Introduction introduces newcomers to the meaning of the Sikh religious tradition, its teachings, practices, rituals, and festivals. It highlights and contextualizes the key threads in the history of Sikhism, from the first Gurus to martyrdom, codes of conduct, militarization, and the increasingly significant diaspora. Examining gender, caste, and the changes that are currently underway in the faith, this VSI considers contemporary Sikh identities and their role in our world.
titles.summaryAI: Sikhism as the world's fifth largest religion with approximately twenty-four million followers worldwide. Founded by Guru Nanak in 15th-century Punjab, with ten Gurus culminating in Gobind Singh. Covers Sikh teachings, practices, rituals, festivals, codes of conduct, and the significance of the turban. Examines the history of Sikh martyrdom, militarization, caste relations, vegetarianism, and yoga within Sikh practice. Addresses the growing Sikh diaspora and contemporary identity questions. Distinguished from Hinduism and Islam by its monotheistic rejection of caste and its distinctive initiated community (Khalsa). Connects to South Asian religious history, diaspora studies, gender, and the politics of religious identity.

titles.title: The History of Time
titles.author: Leofranc Holford-Strevens
titles.subject: Science/History
titles.abstract: Why do we measure time in the way that we do? Why is a week seven days long? At what point did minutes and seconds come into being? Why are some calendars lunar and some solar? The History of Time: A Very Short Introduction explores these questions using a range of fascinating examples from Ancient Rome and Julius Caesar's imposition of the Leap Year, to the 1920s' project for a fixed Easter. The organisation of time into hours, days, months and years seems immutable and universal, but is actually far more artificial than most people realise.
titles.summaryAI: The history of time measurement and calendar systems from ancient civilizations to modernity. Covers the development of hours, days, weeks, months, and years as artificial constructs, the seven-day week's origins, and the distinction between lunar and solar calendars. Examines Julius Caesar's leap year reform, the Gregorian calendar, Bede's dating systems, John Dee's proposals, and the 1920s fixed Easter project. Distinguished from the physics of time by its focus on social and institutional timekeeping. Connects to astronomy, religious practice, chronology, and the standardization of civil life.

titles.title: Nationalism
titles.author: Steven Grosby
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: Nationalism: A Very Short Introduction examines the political and moral challenges that face the vast majority of human beings who consider themselves to be members of various nations. It explores nationality through the difficulties and conflicts that have arisen throughout history, from ancient civilizations to the present day, and discusses nations and nationalism from social, philosophical, and anthropological perspectives. This VSI looks at the nation in history, the territorial element in nationality, and the complex ways nationality has co-existed with religion, and shows how closely linked the concept of nationalism is with being human.
titles.summaryAI: Nationalism as a political ideology and social phenomenon spanning from ancient civilizations to the contemporary world. Covers the concept of the nation as a kinship-based territorial community, patriotism, and the Declaration of Independence as foundational national expression. Examines nationalism's relationship with religion, the formation of nation-states, and its role in World Wars I and II. Addresses cases including Kashmir, the European Union, and the United States. Engages with social, philosophical, and anthropological perspectives, including Adam Smith on national sentiment. Distinguished from mere patriotism by its political program of national self-determination. Connects to empire, international relations, ethnic identity, and sovereignty.

titles.title: The World Trade Organization
titles.author: Amrita Narlikar
titles.subject: Economics/Politics
keywords: General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade
titles.abstract: The World Trade Organization: A Very Short Introduction explanation of what the WTO is, what it does, and how it goes about executing its tasks. The World Trade Organization (WTO) is scarcely ten years old, but even in these early years of its existence it has generated debate, controversy and even outrage. The deep and far-ranging impact of the WTO on peoples' everyday lives means that it is not just an institution of interest to economists, but to everyone. A clear understanding of the mandate, structure and functioning of the WTO is essential to appreciate the controversy behind the organization, and whether it deserves the reputation that it has acquired.
titles.summaryAI: The World Trade Organization as an international institution governing global trade, established in 1995 as successor to the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade. Covers the WTO's mandate, structure, dispute resolution mechanisms, and the Uruguay Round negotiations. Examines trade principles including most favoured nation status, comparative advantage, and Trade-Related Intellectual Property Rights (TRIPS). Addresses the Bretton Woods system, the IMF's relationship to trade, the EU's Common Agricultural Policy, and balance of payments issues. Distinguished from bilateral trade agreements by its multilateral framework. Connects to international economics, development policy, intellectual property law, agricultural policy, and globalization debates.

titles.title: Design
titles.author: John Heskett
titles.subject: Art
titles.abstract: Design: A Very Short Introduction transforms the way we think about design. How does design affect our everyday activities? Do we appreciate its place in our daily lives? Simple objects, such as a spoon, can have their design modified to meet the needs of specific cultural behaviour. Different cultures and individuals personalize objects. Good design combines ‘need’ and ‘desire’ in the form of a practical object that reflects the user's identity and aspirations. How do major companies such as Nokia, Ford, and Sony approach design? How can design humanize new technology?
titles.summaryAI: Design as a discipline and practice encompassing industrial, product, and cultural design. Covers the Arts and Crafts Movement, the Bauhaus tradition (Peter Behrens), and contemporary designers including Philippe Starck and Michele De Lucchi. Examines how major companies like Nokia, Ford, Sony, General Motors, Fiskars, and Lego approach product design. Addresses the relationship between form and function, how cultural context shapes design, and personalization of objects across cultures. Distinguished from fine art by its integration of utility, identity, and mass production. Connects to industrial manufacturing, consumer culture, ergonomics, cultural anthropology, and the humanization of technology.

titles.title: The Vikings
titles.author: Julian D. Richards
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: The Vikings: A Very Short Introduction reveals the history, society, and culture of the Vikings, and their expansion overseas for trade, colonization, and plunder. How did the Vikings express their identity through their artistic expression, rune stones, their ships, and their religion? How has their legacy influenced the medieval Icelandic sagas, nationalism of the 19th century, Wagner, and the Nazis? The Viking reputation is of bloodthirsty seafaring warriors, repeatedly plundering the British Isles and the North Atlantic throughout the early Middle Ages. Yet Vikings were also traders, settlers, and farmers, with a complex artistic and linguistic culture, whose expansion overseas led people to cross the Atlantic for the first time in European history.
titles.summaryAI: The Vikings as Scandinavian seafarers, traders, settlers, and warriors active from the 8th to 11th centuries across the North Atlantic, British Isles, and continental Europe. Covers ship technology, ship burials, artistic expression, rune stones, Old English influence, and Norse religion. Examines Viking trade networks, colonization, and the first European Atlantic crossing. Addresses saga literature, Adam of Bremen's accounts, and Viking legacy in 19th-century nationalism and Nazi appropriation. Distinguished from other medieval cultures by their maritime reach. Connects to medieval history, maritime archaeology, and cultural identity.

titles.title: Fossils
titles.author: Keith Thomson
titles.subject: Biology/Earth Sciences/Geography
titles.abstract: How did Darwin use fossils to support his theory of evolution? What are ‘living fossils’? What fossils will we leave behind for future generations to examine? Fossils: A Very Short Introduction shows how fossils have been vital to our understanding of the formation of the earth and the origins of all life on it. However, their impact has not been limited to debates about geology and evolution; attempts to explain their existence has shaken religion at its very roots, and they have remained a subject of ceaseless fascination for people of all ages and backgrounds.
titles.summaryAI: Fossils as preserved remains of ancient organisms, central to understanding Earth's geological history and biological evolution. Covers key discoveries including the Burgess Shale fauna, the contributions of William Buckland, Georges Cuvier, Charles Darwin, Robert Hooke, and Othniel Charles Marsh. Examines plate tectonics as context for fossil distribution, the concept of living fossils, the fossil record of Homo sapiens, and Tyrannosaurus Rex. Addresses how fossil interpretation challenged religious accounts of Earth's origins. Distinguished from subfossils and trace fossils by preservation through mineralization. Connects to stratigraphy, evolutionary biology, paleontology, geology, and the history of science-religion debates.

titles.title: Journalism
titles.author: Ian Hargreaves
titles.subject: Journalism/Media Studies
titles.abstract: Journalism has a central and profound impact on our world view; we find it everywhere from newspapers and television, to radio and the Internet. Journalism: A Very Short Introduction examines the world of contemporary journalism and reflects on the growth of social media, online, and electronic journalism. By looking not only at what journalism has been in the past, but also what it is becoming in the digital age, it examines the big issues relating to reportage, warfare, celebrity culture, privacy, ethics, and technology worldwide. It also explores the impact of issues such as the banking and financial crisis as well as the aftermath of the Leveson inquiry in the UK.
titles.summaryAI: Journalism as a profession and institution encompassing newspapers, broadcasting, television, radio, and digital media. Covers the transformation brought by social media and online journalism, celebrity culture, privacy ethics, and the relationship between journalism and public relations. Examines reporting on warfare, the banking and financial crisis, and the aftermath of the Leveson inquiry into press standards in the UK. Addresses tabloid culture and the tension between investigative reporting and entertainment. Distinguished from public relations and propaganda by its editorial independence and truth-seeking function. Connects to media studies, democratic accountability, freedom of the press, technology, and the economics of news production.

titles.title: The Crusades
titles.author: Christopher Tyerman
titles.subject: History/Religion
titles.abstract: The Crusades: A Very Short Introduction examines this topic by bringing together issues of colonialism, cultural exchange, economic exploitation, and the relationship between past and present. Crusading fervour gripped Europe for over 200 years, creating one of the most extraordinary, vivid episodes in world history. The legacy of the ideas and imagery of the Crusades has resonated through the centuries, inspiring Hollywood movies and great works of literature. Despite their powerful hold on our imaginations, our knowledge of them remains obscured and distorted by time. Were the Crusaders motivated by spiritual rewards, or by greed? Were the Crusades an experiment in European colonialism, or a manifestation of religious love? How were they organized and founded?
titles.summaryAI: The Crusades as military-religious campaigns spanning over 200 years (primarily 11th-13th centuries), driven by papal authority including Urban II, Innocent III, and Gregory IX. Covers crusading in the Holy Land, Baltic States, and Iberia (Las Navas de Tolosa), alongside Peter the Hermit and Simon de Montfort. Examines the Byzantine Empire's role, Christian-Islamic cultural exchange, and economic exploitation. Addresses motivations from spiritual reward to colonial greed. Distinguished from other medieval warfare by religious sanction and cross-cultural scope. Connects to colonialism, interfaith relations, and modern political rhetoric.

titles.title: Feminism
titles.author: Margaret Walters
titles.subject: History/Philosophy/Sociology
titles.abstract: Feminism: A Very Short Introduction provides an historical account of feminism, exploring its earliest roots as well as key issues including voting rights, the liberation of the sixties, and its relevance today. How much have women's lives really changed? In the West women still come up against the ‘glass ceiling’ at work, most earning considerably less than their male counterparts. What are we to make of the now commonplace insistence that feminism deprives men of their rights and dignities? And how does one tackle the issue of female emancipation in different cultural and economic environments — in, for example, the Middle East, the Indian sub-continent, and Africa?
titles.summaryAI: Feminism as a political, philosophical, and social movement from its earliest roots in Enlightenment thought (Mary Astell, 18th century) through suffrage campaigns, John Stuart Mill and Harriet Taylor on women's liberty, to second-wave liberation in the 1960s and contemporary third-wave debates. Covers employment inequality, the glass ceiling, housework as unpaid labor, marriage reform, and misogyny. Geographic scope includes Western Europe, North America, the Middle East, the Indian subcontinent, and Africa. Connects to political philosophy, labor economics, human rights discourse, and cross-cultural comparisons of gender emancipation strategies.

titles.title: Human evolution
titles.author: Bernard Wood
titles.subject: Anthropology/Archaeology
titles.abstract: Human Evolution: A Very Short Introduction traces the history of palaeoanthropology from its beginnings in the 18th century to the very latest fossil finds. In this new edition it discusses how ancient DNA studies have revolutionized how we view the recent (post-550,000 years ago) human evolution, and the process of speciation. The combination of ancient and modern human DNA has contributed to discoveries of new taxa, as well as the suggestion of ‘ghost’ taxa whose fossil records still remain to be discovered. Considering the contributions of related sciences such as palaeoclimatology, geochronology, systematics, genetics, and developmental biology, this VSI explores our latest understandings of our own evolution.
titles.summaryAI: Human evolution spanning roughly seven million years from early hominins (Australopithecus) through the genus Homo to anatomically modern humans. Covers paleoanthropology, fossil taxonomy, geochronology, speciation events, and ancient DNA analysis revealing interbreeding with Neanderthals and Denisovans, plus hypothetical ghost taxa. Integrates developmental biology, genetics, palaeoclimatology, and systematics. Geographic focus on African origins with dispersal across Eurasia. Distinguished from general biology by emphasis on hominin phylogeny and from archaeology by focus on biological rather than cultural evolution. Connects to molecular biology, population genetics, and climate science.

titles.title: The Dead Sea Scrolls
titles.author: Timothy Lim
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: The Dead Sea Scrolls: A Very Short introduction discusses the cultural significance of the discovery of the Dead Sea Scrolls and the religious, political, and legal controversies during the seventy years of study since they were found. It looks at the contribution the scrolls have made to our understanding of the Old Testament or Hebrew Bible, and the origins of early Christianity. Exploring the most recent scholarly discussions on the archaeology of Khirbet Qumran, and the study of the biblical texts, the canon, and the history of the Second Temple Period, it considers what the scrolls reveal about the communities closely associated with the scrolls and sectarianism in early Judaism.
titles.summaryAI: The Dead Sea Scrolls discovered near Khirbet Qumran from 1947 onward, comprising Hebrew and Aramaic manuscripts dating to the Second Temple Period (roughly 3rd century BC to 1st century AD). Covers their significance for Old Testament textual criticism, biblical canon formation, early Jewish sectarianism, and the origins of Christianity. Addresses the archaeology of Qumran, controversies over scroll access and publication, and debates about the community (possibly Essenes) that produced them. Connects to biblical studies, ancient Judaism, early Christian history, and Near Eastern archaeology.

titles.title: The Brain
titles.author: Michael O'Shea
titles.subject: Biology/Neuroscience - Clinical/Neurology/Psychology
titles.abstract: How does the brain work? The Brain, A Very Short Introduction provides a non-technical introduction to the main issues and findings in current brain research, and gives a sense of how neuroscience addresses questions about the relationship between the brain and the mind. Our knowledge of subjects such as brain processes, perception, memory, motor control and the causes of ‘altered mental states’ have been hugely improved by modern neurological research. These insights may pave the way for future developments, such as artificial intelligence, gene therapy, drugs by design, as well as revolutionizing transplants.
titles.summaryAI: The brain as the central organ of the nervous system, covering neuroanatomy (cerebral cortex, basal ganglia, spinal cord), neurophysiology (ion channels, sensory processing), and the historical shift from humoral theories to modern neuroscience. Addresses perception, memory, motor control, and altered mental states. Explores the mind-brain relationship, consciousness, and emerging applications including artificial intelligence, gene therapy, designer drugs, and neural transplantation. Distinguished from psychology by emphasis on biological substrates and from clinical neurology by broader scope. Connects to cognitive science, philosophy of mind, pharmacology, and biomedical engineering.

titles.title: Geophysical and Climate Hazards
titles.author: Bill McGuire
titles.subject: Earth Sciences and Geography/Environment/Environmental Science
titles.abstract: These are tempestuous times, and barely a day goes by without news of more geophysical mayhem splashed across our Twitter feeds, television screens, and front pages. Earthquakes and volcanic eruptions, floods and storms, and other deadly phenomena have always been with us and have taken a toll on communities for many thousands of years. But now things are changing—and fast. Global heating, unequivocally a consequence of humankind’s carbon-polluting activities, is building rapidly, and in recent years has translated faster than anyone could have imagined into an explosion of extreme weather. As the heat builds and sea levels climb, storms, floods, and wildfires are taking an increasing toll on lives and livelihoods, making our world a far more dangerous place. If we are to cope, we need to better understand the geophysical and climate hazards that now assail us, so as to both limit their impacts and build the resilience needed for people and communities to endure on a hotter and more dynamic planet.
titles.summaryAI: Geophysical and climate hazards including earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, floods, storms, wildfires, and extreme weather events. Covers the physical mechanisms of tectonic and atmospheric phenomena, their historical toll on human communities over millennia, and the accelerating impact of anthropogenic global heating on hazard frequency and severity. Addresses rising sea levels, carbon emissions, community resilience, and disaster risk reduction. Geographic scope is global with emphasis on vulnerable regions. Connects earth sciences, atmospheric science, and climate science to emergency management, urban planning, environmental policy, and sustainable development.

titles.title: Contemporary art
titles.author: Julian Stallabrass
titles.subject: Art
titles.abstract: Contemporary Art: A Very Short Introduction presents a radical analysis of the global art scene. Contemporary art has never been so popular—but the art world is changing. Today, there is growing interest in questions over the nature and use of contemporary art, and over who controls its future. This VSI studies how new regions and nations, such as China, have leapt into astonishing prominence, overturning the old Euro-American dominance. It also examines the powerful influence of big business and the super-rich on the art world, and the reinvention of artists and museums as brands.
titles.summaryAI: Contemporary art from the late 20th century to the present, encompassing the global art scene, art market dynamics, museum branding, and the biennials circuit. Examines the shift from Euro-American dominance to the prominence of new regions including China. Addresses the influence of big business and the super-rich on artistic production and institutional practices, the commodification of art, and artists as brands. Distinguished from art history by focus on living practice and market forces rather than historical periods. Connects to cultural economics, globalization, institutional critique, and the sociology of taste and cultural capital.

titles.title: Philosophy of Law
titles.author: Raymond Wacks
titles.subject: Philosophy/Law
titles.abstract: The concept of law lies at the heart of our social and political life. Legal philosophy, or jurisprudence, explores the notion of law and its role in society, illuminating its meaning and its relation to the universal questions of justice, rights, and morality. Philosophy of Law: A Very Short Introduction analyses the nature and purpose of the legal system, and the practice by courts, lawyers, and judges. Referring to significant works by Ronald Dworkin, Scott Shapiro, John Finnis, and Neil McCormick, it reveals the intriguing and challenging nature of legal philosophy with clarity and enthusiasm, providing an enlightening guide to the central questions of legal theory.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophy of law (jurisprudence) examining foundational questions about the nature, purpose, and legitimacy of legal systems. Covers natural law theory (John Finnis), legal positivism, Ronald Dworkin's rights-based theory, Scott Shapiro's planning theory, and Neil McCormick's institutional theory. Addresses the relationship between law, morality, and justice, the role of courts and legal practitioners, and concepts of rights and social order. Distinguished from constitutional law by its theoretical orientation and from political philosophy by its focus on legal institutions. Connects to moral philosophy, political theory, sociology of law, and practical legal reasoning.

titles.title: The Renaissance
titles.author: Jerry Brotton
titles.subject: Art/History/Philosophy
titles.abstract: The Renaissance: A Very Short Introduction sees the period of the Renaissance as a time of unprecedented intellectual excitement cultural experimentation and interaction on a global scale. However, there was a darker side of religious discord, slavery, and massive inequality of wealth and status. The Renaissance stands as one of the defining periods in world history. Between 1400 and 1600, European perceptions of society, culture, politics, and even humanity changed in ways that affect not only Europe but the entire world. What were the key issues that defined the period, in its art, architecture, literature, advancements in the fields of science, trade, and travel?
titles.summaryAI: The Renaissance spanning roughly 1400-1600, encompassing transformations in European art, architecture, literature, science, trade, and global exploration. Covers the influence of the Byzantine and Ottoman Empires, the Hundred Years War, church-state relations, humanism, and classical revival (Leon Battista Alberti, Alexander the Great as cultural icon). Geographic focus on Italy, the Low Countries, and broader European-global interactions. Addresses the darker dimensions including religious discord, slavery, and wealth inequality. Distinguished from medieval studies by emphasis on cultural experimentation and global exchange. Connects to art history, political history, philosophy, early modern science, and economic history.

titles.title: Anglicanism
titles.author: Mark Chapman
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Anglicanism: A Very Short Introduction highlights the diversity of contemporary Anglicanism by exploring its history, theology, and structures. Although originally united by location and a common belief, Anglicanism has gradually lost its pre-eminence as the English state religion due to increasing pluralism and secularization. Whilst there are distinctive themes and emphases which emerge from its early history and theology, there is little sense of unity in Anglicanism today. Putting the history and development of the religion into context, this VSI reveals what holds Anglicanism together despite the recent crises that threaten to tear it apart.
titles.summaryAI: Anglicanism as a Christian tradition originating with the Church of England, tracing its history from the English Reformation through colonial expansion to its present global communion. Covers Anglican theology, liturgy (prayer traditions), church governance, evangelicalism, and the relationship to Catholicism. Addresses contemporary crises including debates over homosexuality, secularization, and pluralism. Geographic scope encompasses England and the worldwide Anglican Communion. Distinguished from other Protestant traditions by its via media between Catholic and Reformed theology. Connects to English political history, ecumenism, colonial history, and contemporary debates on religious identity and institutional unity.

titles.title: The Roman Empire
titles.author: Christopher Kelly
titles.subject: Classical Studies
titles.abstract: The Roman Empire: A Very Short Introduction covers the history of the empire from Augustus (the first Emperor) to Marcus Aurelius, describing how the empire was formed, how it was run, its religions and its social structure. It examines how local cultures were ‘romanized’ and how people in faraway lands came to believe in the emperor as a god. This VSI also examines how the Roman Empire has been considered and depicted in more recent times, from the writings of Edward Gibbon, to the differing attitudes of the Victorians and recent Hollywood blockbuster films.
titles.summaryAI: The Roman Empire from Augustus (27 BC) through Marcus Aurelius (180 AD), covering imperial administration, military expansion, the Roman Senate, social structure, and provincial governance. Geographic scope spans the Mediterranean, Gaul, Asia Minor, and North Africa (Lepcis Magna). Addresses Romanization of local cultures, imperial cult and religion, the Punic Wars as foundational context, and veteran settlement. Examines reception history from Edward Gibbon through Victorian interpretations to modern Hollywood depictions. Distinguished from Roman Republic studies by focus on imperial institutions. Connects to ancient religion, provincial archaeology, cultural assimilation, and historiography.

titles.title: Photography
titles.author: Steven Edwards
titles.subject: Art
titles.abstract: Photography: A Very Short Introduction examines the definition, importance, and meaning of photography by combining a sense of the historical development of photography with an analysis of its purpose and meaning within a wider cultural context. Photographs are everywhere, in print and online. They are an integral part of our daily lives from sensationalist images in tabloid papers, to snapshots, to art-photography displayed in galleries and sold through international art markets. How important are photographs to our cultural lives? How do we look and think about photographs? How truthful are photographs?
titles.summaryAI: Photography as a visual medium from its 19th-century origins (William Henry Fox Talbot, daguerreotype) through modernist art photography to digital-era image culture. Covers photographic theory (Roland Barthes, Walter Benjamin on mechanical reproduction), documentary tradition (Robert Capa, Henri Cartier-Bresson, Walker Evans), political photomontage (John Heartfield), landscape photography (Ansel Adams), and critical theory (Allan Sekula). Addresses questions of truth, representation, the art market for photography, and the cultural impact of ubiquitous images in print and online media. Connects to visual culture, semiotics, journalism, and aesthetics.

titles.title: Psychiatry
titles.author: Tom Burns
titles.subject: Medicine - Psychiatry/Psychology/Social Work
titles.abstract: Psychiatry: A Very Short Introduction explores the nature of psychiatry, focusing on what it can and cannot do, and discussing why its history has been beset by dramatic shifts in emphasis and types of treatment. Considering the main disorders that have shaped its practice (such as schizophrenia and manic depression), it analyses how it differs from (and overlaps with) psychology and psychotherapy. Discussing philosophical issues of psychiatry’s legitimacy, this VSI explores the mistakes psychiatry has made and the blind alleys in its history, before looking forward to the problems associated with ageing populations and the likely changes in its practice with the coming of artificial intelligence and virtual reality.
titles.summaryAI: Psychiatry as a medical specialty addressing mental illness, covering major disorders including schizophrenia and bipolar disorder (manic depression), alongside conditions related to aging, gender-specific presentations, and suicide risk. Traces the discipline's historical shifts between biological, psychoanalytic, and community-based approaches. Addresses the distinction between psychiatry, psychology, and psychotherapy, as well as philosophical debates about psychiatric legitimacy and classification. Examines genetics in mental illness, confidentiality ethics, and emerging technologies including artificial intelligence and virtual reality in clinical practice. Connects to neuroscience, medical ethics, social work, and public health.

titles.title: Existentialism
titles.author: Thomas Flynn
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Existentialism: A Very Short Introduction examines one of the leading philosophical movements of the twentieth century. Focusing on its seven leading figures: Sartre, Nietzsche, Heidegger, Kierkegaard, de Beauvoir, Merleau–Ponty, and Camus, this VSI considers the key themes of the movement, which emphasized individuality, free will, and personal responsibility in the modern world. Drawing on the movement's varied relationships with the arts, humanism, and politics, it clarifies the philosophy and original meaning of ‘existentialism’ — which has tended to be obscured by misappropriation. By placing the philosophy in its political context, this VSI highlights the continuing relevance of existentialism.
titles.summaryAI: Existentialism as a philosophical movement of the 19th and 20th centuries, centered on seven major thinkers: Kierkegaard, Nietzsche, Husserl, Heidegger, Sartre, de Beauvoir, and Camus, with additional attention to Merleau-Ponty and Karl Jaspers. Core themes include authenticity, individual freedom, personal responsibility, the death of God, and the nature of human existence. Covers existentialism's relationship to humanism, phenomenology, the arts (literature, theater), and political engagement. Distinguished from analytic philosophy by emphasis on lived experience. Connects to ethics, feminist philosophy, theology, political theory, and 20th-century European intellectual history.

titles.title: The First World War
titles.author: Michael Howard
titles.subject: History/Military
titles.abstract: The First World War: A Very Short Introduction is a concise history of the ‘Great War’, focusing on why it happened, how it was fought, and why it had the consequences it did. It examines the state of Europe in 1914 and the outbreak of war; the onset of attrition and crisis; the role of the USA; the collapse of Russia; and the weakening and eventual surrender of the Central Powers. It also investigates the historical controversies surrounding the causes and conduct of war, and describes how peace was ultimately made and the potent legacy of resentment left to Germany.
titles.summaryAI: The First World War (1914-1918), covering the causes rooted in European alliance systems, the outbreak and initial campaigns, trench warfare and attrition on the Western Front, the Eastern Front and Russian collapse, American entry under Woodrow Wilson, and the surrender of the Central Powers. Key figures include Lord Kitchener, Sir John French, David Lloyd George, and John Maynard Keynes on economic consequences. Addresses propaganda, military technology (mortars), the Paris Peace Settlement, and the legacy of German resentment. Connects to diplomatic history, military strategy, economic history, and the origins of the interwar crisis.

titles.title: Fundamentalism
titles.author: Malise Ruthven
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Fundamentalism: A Very Short Introduction investigates its historical, social, religious, political, and ideological roots, and tackles the polemic and stereotypes surrounding this complex phenomenon — one that eludes simple definition, yet urgently needs to be understood. Since it was coined by American Protestant evangelicals in the 1920s, the use of the term ‘fundamentalist’ has expanded to include a diverse range of radical conservatives and ideological purists, not all religious. Fundamentalism could now mean both militant Israeli settlers as well as the Islamist radicals who oppose them, it can mean Christians, Hindus, animal liberationists, and even Buddhist nationalists.
titles.summaryAI: Fundamentalism as a cross-religious phenomenon originating with American Protestant evangelicals in the 1920s but extending to militant movements within Christianity, Islam, Judaism (Gush Emunim), Hinduism, and Buddhism. Covers the ideological roots in scriptural literalism, anti-modernism, and moral absolutism, with applications to issues including abortion and homosexuality. Addresses the relationship between fundamentalism and New Religious Movements, polytheism, and Nietzsche's critique of religion. Distinguished from conservative religion by its political activism and from extremism by its doctrinal specificity. Connects to political ideology, secularization theory, comparative religion, and social movements.

titles.title: Economics
titles.author: Partha Dasgupta
titles.subject: Economics
titles.abstract: Economics: A Very Short Introduction describes the lives of two children who live very different lives in different parts of the world: in the Mid-West USA and in Ethiopia. It compares the obstacles facing them, and the processes that shape their lives, their families, and their futures. This VSI shows how economics uncovers these processes, finds explanations for them, and how it forms policies and solutions, along the way providing an intelligent and accessible introduction to key economic factors and concepts such as: individual choices, national policies, efficiency, equity, development, sustainability, dynamic equilibrium, property rights, markets, and public goods.
titles.summaryAI: Economics as a social science analyzing resource allocation, market mechanisms, and development outcomes. Covers microeconomic concepts (opportunity cost, adverse selection, moral hazard) and macroeconomic measures (gross domestic product, economic development). Addresses division of labor, economies of scale, human capital formation, property rights, market structures, public goods, research and development, and natural resource management. Contrasts economic conditions in the developed world (United States) with sub-Saharan Africa (Ethiopia). Distinguished from business studies by theoretical foundations. Connects to development studies, environmental sustainability, policy analysis, and institutional economics.

titles.title: International Migration
titles.author: Khalid Koser
titles.subject: Sociology
titles.abstract: In recent years, global migration has transformed in terms of its rising numbers and reach, its political significance, and its impact. The political and media attention on migration and greater public interest and concern feed into an international debate that is all too often poorly informed and one-sided. International Migration: A Very Short Introduction looks at the phenomenon of international human migration—both legal and illegal—and offers an objective stance on the topic, revealing the benefits and challenges that migration presents. Using interviews with migrants from around the world, it presents the human side of issues such as asylum, human trafficking, migrant smuggling, and the international labour force.
titles.summaryAI: International migration as a global phenomenon encompassing legal immigration, undocumented migration, asylum seeking, refugee flows, human trafficking, and migrant smuggling. Covers the economic drivers (labor markets, employment), social dimensions (cultural integration, rights), and political implications (policy debates, media representation). Addresses globalization and technology as enablers of increased mobility. Geographic scope is worldwide with attention to diverse migration corridors. Distinguished from demography by focus on cross-border movement and from refugee studies by broader scope. Connects to labor economics, human rights law, cultural sociology, and international development.

titles.title: Newton
titles.author: Rob Iliffe
titles.subject: History of Science/Physics/Biography
titles.abstract: Newton: A Very Short Introduction introduces Newton's scientific studies in fields ranging from physics, astronomy, and mathematics, to alchemy. It also examines his controversial theological beliefs and his politics, and explores the forces that shaped his life. Although Newton's science was largely revered — his reputation reached near-immortal status with the publication of the Principia — theologically, his beliefs were very controversial. Newton's views on alchemy, mathematics, physics, and religion are shown to be the very different obsessions of an extremely complex man whose beliefs at the time dominated England's political, religious, and intellectual landscape.
titles.summaryAI: Isaac Newton (1642-1727), covering his contributions to physics (mechanics, optics, the Principia), mathematics (calculus), and astronomy, alongside his extensive work in alchemy and controversial theological beliefs including anti-Trinitarianism. Examines his intellectual rivalries with Robert Hooke, Christiaan Huygens, and engagement with Cartesian philosophy (Descartes) and Robert Boyle's chemistry.
