Explores ethical questions including labor practices, sustainability, and consumer culture. Connects fashion to visual and material culture, art, photography, and media representation.

titles.title: Forensic science
titles.author: Jim Fraser
titles.subject: Science
titles.abstract: Forensic Science: A Very Short Introduction introduces the concept of forensic science and explains how it is used in the investigation of crime. In forensic science, a criminal case can often hinge on a piece of evidence such as a hair, a blood trace, half a footprint, or a tyre mark. High profile cases have attracted enormous media attention and enhanced this interest in recent years. However, the public understanding of forensic science is poor, and largely based on TV shows. This VSI explains the principles of crime scene management, explores how forensic scientists work, and considers the techniques they use.
titles.summaryAI: Forensic science as applied to criminal investigation, covering crime scene management, evidence collection, and laboratory analysis. Examines physical evidence types including DNA profiling, fingerprints, hair and fiber analysis, blood pattern interpretation, footprint and tire mark comparison, and digital forensics. Addresses the forensic identification of individuals, chain of custody procedures, and expert witness testimony in court. Explores the gap between public perception shaped by television and the actual capabilities and limitations of forensic methods. Connects to criminal justice principles, legal standards of evidence admissibility, quality assurance in forensic laboratories, and the role of science in achieving justice.

titles.title: Puritanism
titles.author: Francis J. Bremer
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Puritanism: A Very Short Introduction traces the development, triumph, and decline of the Puritan world and examines the Puritan legacy in American history. It charts the rise of Puritanism in the English Reformation, the struggle of the reformers to purge what they viewed as the corruptions of Roman Catholicism from the Elizabethan church, and the struggle with the Stuart monarchs that led to a brief Puritan triumph under Oliver Cromwell. It also explores puritan theology, views on family and community, beliefs about the proper relationship between religion and public life, the limits of toleration, and the balance between individual rights and one's obligation to others.
titles.summaryAI: Puritanism as a Protestant reform movement within English Christianity from the Elizabethan era through the Stuart period, Oliver Cromwell's Commonwealth, and its transplantation to colonial New England. Covers Calvinist theology, covenant theology, and the thought of William Perkins and Richard Baxter. Examines Puritan views on family, community, prayer, education, and the relationship between religion and public life. Addresses the Half-Way Covenant controversy, John Bunyan's literary contributions, John Milton's political thought, and the Protestant work ethic. Explores Puritan attitudes toward toleration, individual rights versus communal obligation, and the lasting Puritan legacy in American culture and identity.

titles.title: The Reformation
titles.author: Peter Marshall
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: The Reformation transformed Europe and left an indelible mark on the modern world. But what was the Reformation? A force for progress, liberty, and modernity, or for conflict, division, and repression? Over the course of two centuries the relationships between Church and state, between subjects and rulers, and between men and women were changed fundamentally and permanently. In this Very Short Introduction, now thoroughly revised and updated in a second edition, Peter Marshall argues that the Reformation was not solely a European phenomenon, but one that transformed Christianity into today’s world religion. Exploring the effects it had on politics, society, art, gender relations, and the treatment of minorities, Marshall reveals the legacies left by the Reformation to the present day.
titles.summaryAI: The Protestant Reformation as a transformative movement in European Christianity from the early 16th through 17th centuries. Covers Lutheran, Calvinist, Anabaptist, and Anglican traditions alongside the Catholic Counter-Reformation. Examines the reshaping of church-state relations, political authority, gender roles, and minority treatment. Addresses the Reformation's impact on art, literacy (vernacular Bible translation), education, and social organization. Explores how the Reformation spread beyond Europe through missionary activity, becoming a global phenomenon that shaped modern Christianity. Connects to the emergence of religious pluralism, individual conscience, capitalism, and the secularization processes that define Western modernity.

titles.title: Thomas Aquinas
titles.author: Fergus Kerr
titles.subject: Religion/Biography
titles.abstract: Thomas Aquinas: A Very Short Introduction is an introduction to this early thirteenth century Italian Catholic priest's work. Placing Aquinas in an historical context, it explores the Church and culture into which he was born. A controversial figure, often engaged in conflict, Aquinas was, and still is, considered to be one of the great Christian thinkers. He continues to have a profound influence on Western thought. Recently scholars have begun to insist on the importance of studying his biblical commentaries. This VSI is centred on The Summa Theologiae, his greatest and most discussed single work, and some of the debates it has given rise to.
titles.summaryAI: Thomas Aquinas (1225-1274), Italian Dominican friar and medieval Catholic theologian whose Summa Theologiae remains foundational for Western Christian philosophy. Covers Aquinas's synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy with Christian doctrine, his arguments for God's existence (Five Ways), natural law theory, and theological anthropology. Examines his relationship with teacher Albert the Great and engagement with Pseudo-Dionysius and Islamic philosophy. Addresses creation theology, the nature-grace distinction, and debates about pantheism. Explores Aquinas's influence on Calvinist theology, Catholic scholasticism, and modern revivals of Thomistic thought, including recent scholarly emphasis on his biblical commentaries.

titles.title: Deserts
titles.author: Nick Middleton
titles.subject: Geography
titles.abstract: Deserts: A Very Short Introduction explores the nature of deserts and the extent of their variety, climates, landscapes, wildlife, and human habitation. Typified by drought and extremes of temperature, deserts can be harsh and hostile; but many are also beautiful and, on occasion, teem with life. The uniqueness of deserts through an examination of their plants, animals, climatic conditions, scenery, resources, human occupants, and future potential are outlined. The beauty, biodiversity, and importance of deserts within a global context are highlighted and a case made for their preservation, protection, and further research.
titles.summaryAI: Desert environments characterized by aridity, extreme temperatures, and distinctive geomorphological processes including erosion, sand dune formation, and evaporation dynamics. Covers desert classification by rain shadow, continental interior, coastal, and polar types. Examines desert flora and fauna adaptations, biodiversity, and ecological resilience. Addresses human habitation including nomadic pastoralism, oasis agriculture, and modern desert cities. Explores water resource management, desertification processes, and climate change impacts on arid regions globally. Covers the Sahara, Arabian, Gobi, Atacama, and other major deserts. Connects desert geography to resource extraction, conservation challenges, and the importance of preservation and further scientific research.

titles.title: The Norman Conquest
titles.author: George Garnett
titles.subject: History – U.K.
titles.abstract: The Norman Conquest: A Very Short Introduction considers one of the most profound turning points in English history. The Norman Conquest in 1066 was the last time England was successfully invaded, cataclysmically transforming a disparate collection of small nations into a European state. But what actually happened? How was the invasion viewed by those who witnessed it? And how has its legacy been seen by generations since? This VSI reveals how dramatically English life was changed, from its language to its law, and focuses on the differing ways the conquest has been viewed by historians and in folklore ever since.
titles.summaryAI: The Norman Conquest of England in 1066, from the Battle of Hastings through William the Conqueror's consolidation of power. Covers the political context of Anglo-Saxon England, the succession crisis, and rival claims of Harold Godwinson, William of Normandy, and Harald Hardrada. Examines primary sources including the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle, Domesday Book, and chroniclers William of Malmesbury, Orderic Vitalis, Henry of Huntingdon, and William of Jumieges. Addresses the Conquest's transformative effects on English law, language (Norman French influence), land tenure, church organization, and castle architecture. Explores East Anglia's resistance and the Conquest's contested legacy in English historical memory and national identity.

titles.title: Biblical archaeology
titles.author: Eric H. Cline
titles.subject: History/Archaeology
titles.abstract: Biblical Archaeology: A Very Short Introduction examines the origins of biblical archaeology as a discipline and the major controversies that first prompted explorers to go in search of objects and sites that would “prove” the Bible. The most well-known biblical archaeologists are discussed, such as Kathleen Kenyon and Yigael Yadin. The sites that are essential sources of knowledge are described, along with some of the most important discoveries that have been made, such as the Dead Sea Scrolls. Also examined are the archaeological finds that shed further light on the Hebrew Bible and the New Testament, and the issue of potential frauds and forgeries.
titles.summaryAI: Biblical archaeology as a discipline examining material evidence related to the Hebrew Bible and New Testament across the ancient Near East. Covers key excavation sites including Megiddo, Jerusalem's Temple Mount, and Jericho. Examines major discoveries such as the Dead Sea Scrolls, Sennacherib's prism, Nebuchadnezzar's inscriptions, and silver amulet scrolls. Addresses pioneering archaeologists including Kathleen Kenyon and Yigael Yadin. Explores methodological debates about using archaeology to verify biblical narratives, the problem of frauds and forgeries, and the evolution from apologetic "proof" archaeology to modern scientific approaches integrating stratigraphy, ceramic typology, and textual analysis.

titles.title: The Reagan Revolution
titles.author: Gil Troy
titles.subject: History – U.S.
titles.abstract: Nearly two decades after that 1989 speech, debate continues to rage over just how revolutionary those Reagan years were. The Reagan Revolution: A Very Short Introduction identifies and tackles some of the controversies and historical mysteries that continue to swirl around Reagan and his legacy, while providing an illuminating look at some of the era's defining personalities, ideas, and accomplishments. Love him or hate him, Ronald Reagan remains the most influential president since Franklin D. Roosevelt, and one of the most controversial. This VSI situates the reception of Reagan's actions within the contemporary liberal and conservative political scene.
titles.summaryAI: The Reagan Revolution (1981-1989) as a conservative political transformation in American history, examining Ronald Reagan's presidency and its ideological roots in Barry Goldwater's 1964 campaign. Covers supply-side economics, tax cuts, deregulation, military buildup, and Cold War strategy. Addresses domestic policy shifts including opposition to affirmative action, reduction of Great Society and New Deal programs, and executive power expansion. Examines Reagan's relationship to the conservative movement, Newt Gingrich's subsequent Congressional revolution, and comparisons with Jefferson, the Fair Deal, New Frontier, and Jimmy Carter's presidency. Explores Reagan's enduring influence on American political culture and liberal-conservative debates.

titles.title: The Book of Mormon
titles.author: Terryl Givens
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: The Book of Mormon: A Very Short Introduction examines the Book of Mormon primarily in terms of the claims that its narrators make for its historical genesis, its purpose as a sacred text, and its meaning for an audience which shifts over the course of the history it unfolds. Five governing themes — revelation, Christ, Zion, scripture, and covenant — serve as vectors for analysis of the Book's central doctrines and teachings, particularly in relation to familiar nineteenth-century religious preoccupations. This VSI samples characters and representative passages from the Book and surveys its contested origins and addresses questions of the record's historicity.
titles.summaryAI: The Book of Mormon as a sacred text of the Latter-day Saint tradition, examining its claimed ancient origins, narrative structure, and doctrinal content. Covers five governing themes: revelation, Christ, Zion, scripture, and covenant. Examines the book's relationship to the Bible, New Testament theology, and 19th-century American religious concerns. Addresses the narrative of ancient peoples migrating from the Tower of Babel and Jerusalem to the Americas. Explores the Urim and Thummim as translation instruments, the Tree of Life vision, and contested historicity. Connects to broader questions of scriptural authority, faith, and the relationship between Mormonism and Catholic and Protestant Christianity.

titles.title: Islamic History
titles.author: Adam J. Silverstein
titles.subject: History/Religion
titles.abstract: Why does Islamic history matter? Islamic History: A Very Short Introduction answers this question by introducing the story of Islamic history, the controversies surrounding its study, and the significance that it holds for Muslims and non-Muslims alike. The rise and spread of Islam and the evolution into an international religion with over a billion adherents is charted. The peoples — Arabs, Persians, and Turks — who shaped Islamic history and three representative and diverse institutions — the mosque, jihad, and the caliphate — are presented. The roles that Islamic history has played in both religious and political contexts are also analysed, stressing the unique status that history enjoys amongst Muslims.
titles.summaryAI: Islamic history from the 7th-century Arabian Peninsula through medieval caliphates to the modern era, covering the rise and global spread of Islam as a world religion with over one billion adherents. Examines the foundational roles of Arabs, Persians, and Turks in shaping Islamic civilization. Covers three central institutions: the mosque as community center, jihad as religious and political concept, and the caliphate as governance system. Addresses Mecca, Medina, the Dome of the Rock, Hadith literature, and the Ottoman Empire. Explores Arab nationalism, contemporary political Islam, and the unique importance Muslims attach to historical consciousness.

titles.title: Privacy
titles.author: Raymond Wacks
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: While no brief book can hope to provide a comprehensive account of the manifold features of a concept as complex and controversial as privacy, this one introduces readers to the main features of what has become one of the most important rights or interests in contemporary society. In Britain the Leveson Inquiry Report of 2012 is the most comprehensive investigation into the ethics and practice of the media, with a significant section devoted to privacy and media intrusion. Its recommendations relating to media self-regulation continue to engender heated debate in Britain. The extraordinary revelations by the whistleblower, Edward Snowden, of the extensive surveillance conducted by the National Security Agency (NSA) in the United States (US) continue to generate an enormous international debate about the legitimate balance between security and privacy. And the courts—particularly in England and Strasbourg—have reshaped the protection of privacy afforded to individuals under Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights. New questions are routinely spawned about privacy on Facebook, Twitter, and other social media sites. And, of course, novel forms of electronic and technological surveillance devices pose almost daily challenges to privacy both online and in the ‘real’ world. The book attempts to describe the essential features of both the invasion and the protection of privacy in our digital world.
titles.summaryAI: Privacy as a legal, political, and ethical concept in contemporary society. Examines the tension between national security and individual privacy, including mass surveillance by intelligence agencies such as the NSA revealed by Edward Snowden. Covers data protection law, the European Convention on Human Rights Article 8, media intrusion and press regulation following the Leveson Inquiry in Britain. Explores privacy challenges posed by social media platforms, electronic surveillance technologies, and digital communications. Addresses freedom of expression, balancing competing rights, and privacy in both online and offline contexts.

titles.title: Neoliberalism
titles.author: Manfred Steger, Ravi K. Roy
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: Neoliberalism: A Very Short Introduction explores the considerable variations of neoliberalism around the world. It also discusses the origins, evolution, and core ideas of neoliberalism. Neoliberalism emerged as the world's dominant economic paradigm in the 1990s. The global financial crash of 2008 and the subsequent emergence of more nationalist ideologies have challenged both neoliberal assumptions and related financial systems—a development most spectacularly reflected in 2016's pro-Brexit referendum in the UK and the Trump election victory in the same year. This new edition asks whether changing versions of neoliberalism might succeed in drowning out the calls for a return to territorial sovereignty and national greatness.
titles.summaryAI: Neoliberalism as a dominant economic paradigm from the 1990s onward, examining its origins, intellectual evolution, and core ideological commitments including free markets, deregulation, and privatization. Covers global variations in neoliberal policy implementation. Analyzes the 2008 global financial crisis and its challenge to neoliberal assumptions and financial systems. Explores the rise of nationalist and populist responses including Brexit and the 2016 Trump election. Considers whether adapted forms of neoliberalism can survive challenges from movements advocating territorial sovereignty and economic nationalism.

titles.title: Progressivism
titles.author: Walter Nugent
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: Progressivism: A Very Short Introduction offers an overview of progressivism in America — its origins, guiding principles, major leaders and major accomplishments. Progressivism emerged as a response to the excesses of the Gilded Age, an era that plunged working Americans into poverty while a new class of ostentatious millionaires flaunted their wealth. As capitalism ran unchecked and more and more economic power was concentrated in fewer hands, a sense of social crisis was pervasive. This VSI shows that the progressives — with the glaring exception of race relations — shared a common conviction that society should be fair to all its members and that governments had a responsibility to see that fairness prevailed.
titles.summaryAI: Progressivism as a political and social reform movement in the United States, emerging in response to Gilded Age inequality, concentrated economic power, and working-class poverty. Covers key leaders including Theodore Roosevelt, Woodrow Wilson, Jane Addams, and William McKinley. Examines guiding principles of social fairness, government responsibility, and opposition to unchecked capitalism. Discusses settlement houses, civil service reform, and social Darwinism. Addresses the movement's significant failure on race relations. Spans the late nineteenth century through World War I, touching on its legacy through figures like Ronald Reagan.

titles.title: Epidemiology
titles.author: Rodolfo Saracci
titles.subject: Medicine
titles.abstract: Epidemiology: A Very Short Introduction explains what epidemiology is and its importance to the discovery, control, and prevention of disease in world populations. It looks at health hazards identified by the study of epidemiology — such as the relationship between tobacco smoking and lung cancer and between various aspects of diet, environment, and exercise — and the origin and spread of new epidemics. This VSI dispels some of the myths surrounding the study of epidemiology. It explores the principles behind clinical trials and explains the nature of the basic statistics concerning disease. It also considers the ethical and political issues related to obtaining and using information concerning patients and trials involving placebos.
titles.summaryAI: Epidemiology as a discipline for studying disease patterns, health hazards, and public health in world populations. Covers methods for discovering, controlling, and preventing disease, including clinical trial design, biostatistics, and evidence evaluation. Examines established health hazards such as tobacco smoking and lung cancer, dietary and environmental risk factors, blood pressure, heart attack, and myocardial infarction. Discusses the origin and spread of epidemics, the role of the World Health Organization, and selection bias in research. Addresses ethical and political issues surrounding patient data, informed consent, and placebo-controlled trials.

titles.title: Information
titles.author: Luciano Floridi
titles.subject: Philosophy/Science
titles.abstract: Information: A Very Short Introduction explores the concept of information, central to modern science and society, from thermodynamics and DNA to our use of the mobile phone and the Internet. It moves from a brief look at the mathematical roots of information — its definition and measurement in ‘bits’ — to its role in genetics, and its social meaning and value, before considering the ethics of information, including issues of ownership, privacy, and accessibility; copyright and open source. This VSI also considers concepts such as ‘Infoglut’ (too much information to process) and the emergence of an information society.
titles.summaryAI: Information as a foundational concept spanning science, technology, philosophy, and society. Covers mathematical and computational foundations including definition, measurement in bits, probability, entropy, and Alan Turing's contributions. Examines information in genetics and DNA, Maxwell's demon in thermodynamics, and information's role in understanding nature and life. Explores social dimensions including communication, language, mobile technology, and the Internet. Addresses the ethics of information encompassing ownership, privacy, accessibility, copyright, and open source. Discusses information overload and the emergence of an information society as a cultural and economic phenomenon.

titles.title: The Laws of Thermodynamics
titles.author: Peter Atkins
titles.subject: Physics
titles.abstract: The Laws of Thermodynamics: A Very Short Introduction explains how the laws of thermodynamics establish fundamental concepts such as temperature and heat, and the nature of energy itself. From the sudden expansion of a cloud of gas or the cooling of a hot metal, to the unfolding of a thought in our minds and even the course of life itself, everything is governed by the four Laws of Thermodynamics. This VSI explains the basis and deeper implications of each law, introducing concepts such as entropy, free energy and absolute zero.
titles.summaryAI: The four laws of thermodynamics and their fundamental role in physics and natural processes. Covers the zeroth law establishing temperature, the first law on conservation of energy and internal energy, the second law on entropy and the directionality of natural processes, and the third law concerning absolute zero. Examines key concepts including heat, energy conversion, free energy, and the thermodynamic behavior of gases and metals. Explores deeper implications for understanding irreversibility, energy availability, and the governance of all physical and biological processes from gas expansion to life itself.

titles.title: Innovation
titles.author: Mark Dodgson, David Gann
titles.subject: Science/Business
titles.abstract: What is innovation? How is innovation used in business? How can we use it to succeed? Innovation: A Very Short Introduction looks at what innovation is and why it affects us so profoundly. It examines how it occurs, who stimulates it, how it is pursued, its outcomes—both positive and negative—and how it plays an essential role in economic and social development. Considering innovation today, and discussing future disruptive technologies such as AI, which have important implications for work and employment, this VSI considers the extent to which our understanding of innovation has developed over the past century and how it might be used to interpret the global economy.
titles.summaryAI: Innovation as a process driving economic and social development, examined through business strategy, industrial production, and technological change. Covers how innovation occurs, who stimulates it, and its positive and negative outcomes. Discusses creative destruction, economies of scale, disintermediation, intellectual property, marketing, risk management, and project management. Examines innovation across banking, industry, and commercial sectors. Addresses disruptive technologies including artificial intelligence and their implications for work and employment. Considers the historical evolution of understanding innovation over the past century and its role in interpreting the contemporary global economy.

titles.title: Witchcraft
titles.author: Malcolm Gaskill
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Witchcraft: A Very Short Introduction explores why witches still feature so heavily in our cultures and consciousness. From childhood most of us develop a sense of the mysterious, malign person, usually an old woman. Tracing the history of witchcraft from the ancient world to contemporary paganism, this VSI reveals how it has meant different things to different people and that in every age it has raised questions about the distinction between fantasy and reality, faith and proof. It uses stories, court records, and myths to examine the witch-hunts of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries, and explores the reinvention of witchcraft — as history, religion, fiction, and metaphor.
titles.summaryAI: Witchcraft from the ancient world through early modern Europe to contemporary paganism, spanning beliefs, practices, and cultural representations. Examines the sixteenth and seventeenth century witch-hunts using court records, stories, and myths, with attention to Scotland and broader Western Europe. Explores connections to magic, alchemy, astrology, prophecy, and sacrifice. Discusses the reinvention of witchcraft through Wicca, goddess spirituality, and modern paganism. Considers anthropological perspectives, the influence of Freudian psychology, and Spinoza's rationalism. Addresses distinctions between fantasy and reality, faith and proof, and the persistent cultural significance of the witch figure.

titles.title: The New Testament
titles.author: Luke Timothy Johnson
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: The New Testament: A Very Short Introduction locates the New Testament in its historical and literary context and explains why the religious experiences of the first Christians forced them to reinterpret their Jewish and Greek heritage and reshape their symbolic world. It provides the broad conceptual and factual framework for the New Testament, describing the process by which distinct compositions became a sacred book as well as providing a detailed examination of specific compositions that have had a particularly strong influence, such as Paul's letters to the Corinthians and Romans, the four Gospels, and the Book of Revelation.
titles.summaryAI: The New Testament as a collection of early Christian writings, examining its historical, literary, and religious context within the Roman Empire. Covers the process of canon formation by which distinct compositions became sacred scripture. Analyzes key texts including Paul's letters to the Corinthians and Romans, the four Gospels, and the Book of Revelation. Explores how first-century Christian religious experiences led to reinterpretation of Jewish and Greek heritage, reshaping symbolic worlds. Discusses Jesus, John the Baptist, the Kingdom of God, the Holy Spirit, and the relationship between Old Testament Hebrew scripture and New Testament writings.

titles.title: French literature
titles.author: John D. Lyons
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract: French Literature: A Very Short Introduction examines texts that portray protagonists whose adventures and conflicts reveal shifts in literary and social practices. It explores the notion of the ‘problematic hero’ and introduces a number of major works from a wide variety of periods, styles, and genres, examining how each fits into society and reflects its concerns and values. The first great works of this repertory were written in the twelfth century in northern France. Now, at the beginning of the twenty-first century, French literature includes authors writing in many parts of the world, from the Caribbean to Western Africa.
titles.summaryAI: French literature from its twelfth-century origins in northern France through the Middle Ages to the twenty-first century, including Francophone authors from the Caribbean and Western Africa. Examines the concept of the problematic hero across periods, styles, and genres. Covers major figures including Pierre Corneille, the Marquis de Sade, Marcel Proust, and Bernardin de Saint-Pierre. Explores shifts in literary and social practices, questions of character and identity, the nouveau roman movement, and literature under Louis XIV. Analyzes how literary works reflect societal concerns, values, and changing conceptions of nature and selfhood.

titles.title: Film music
titles.author: Kathryn Kalinak
titles.subject: Music
titles.abstract: Film Music: A Very Short Introduction focuses on the most central issues in the practice of film music. What is film music? How is it composed? How does film music work? Why does film music work? The rich and deeply moving sounds of film music are as old as cinema. The very first projected moving images were accompanied by music around the globe as a variety of performers—from single piano players to small orchestras—brought images to life. Film music has since become its own industry, an aesthetic platform for expressing creative visions, and a commercial vehicle for generating increased revenue. The second edition updates coverage to 2022 and includes attention to recent developments in global film music, women in film music, and African American and minority composers.
titles.summaryAI: Film music from the silent film era to contemporary cinema, covering composition techniques, aesthetic theory, and the commercial music industry. Examines how music accompanied the earliest projected moving images globally, performed by solo pianists and small orchestras. Discusses film music theory, the relationship between music and narrative in both silent and sound films, and how composers craft scores. Covers recent developments through 2022 including global film music traditions, contributions of women in film music, and African American and minority composers. Explores film music as creative expression and revenue-generating commercial vehicle.

titles.title: Druids
titles.author: Barry Cunliffe
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: The Druids: A Very Short Introduction sets out to answer the questions: Who were the Druids? What do we know about them? Do they still exist today? The Druids first came into focus in Western Europe — Gaul, Britain, and Ireland — in the 2nd century bc. This VSI examines the origins of the Druids and the evidence for their beliefs and practices, asking what can and cannot be known about them. It considers why the nature of the Druid caste changed quite dramatically over time, and how successive generations have interpreted the phenomenon in very different ways.
titles.summaryAI: Druids as a priestly caste in ancient Western Europe, first documented in the second century BC across Gaul, Britain, and Ireland. Examines archaeological and literary evidence including accounts by Julius Caesar and Pliny the Elder. Covers druid beliefs and practices involving prophecy, sacrifice, magic, and their relationship to agriculture and language. Discusses how the druid caste changed over time and how successive generations reinterpreted druidism, from John Aubrey and William Stukeley's antiquarian studies to modern neo-druidic movements. Explores the role of women in druidic traditions and connections to broader Celtic society.

titles.title: German philosophy
titles.author: Andrew Bowie
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: German Philosophy: A Very Short Introduction discusses the idea that that German philosophy forms one of the most revealing responses to the problems of ‘modernity’, addressing questions such as the relationships between knowledge and faith, reason and emotion, and scientific, ethical, and artistic ways of seeing the world. It examines Germany's transition from a traditional feudal society to a modern, industrialized one, exploring how philosophy relates to social and historical developments. All the significant thinkers of the eighteenth, nineteenth, and twentieth centuries are examined, as well as some of the lesser-known philosophers.
titles.summaryAI: German philosophy as a tradition responding to problems of modernity from the eighteenth through twentieth centuries. Covers German idealism, rationalism, neo-Kantianism, critical theory, and aesthetics. Examines relationships between knowledge and faith, reason and emotion, and scientific, ethical, and artistic ways of understanding the world. Explores how philosophy relates to Germany's transition from feudal to modern industrialized society. Addresses questions of language, meaning, nature, truth, and science. Discusses major and lesser-known thinkers across the Enlightenment, Romantic, and modern periods within broader social, economic, and political contexts.

titles.title: Advertising
titles.author: Winston Fletcher
titles.subject: Business
titles.abstract: Advertising: A Very Short Introduction dispels the myths surrounding the advertising industry. Advertising is a diverse entity, and campaigns work (or fail to work) in a plethora of ways. How does the advertising industry work? What part do all the key players (the advertisers, the media, and the agencies) play? Where does the public sit in the world of advertising? This VSI examines how advertisers determine whether they have been successful, how they know if the money they have spent has been wasted, and what the benefits of advertising are and what the future may hold for the industry.
titles.summaryAI: Advertising as an industry and practice encompassing strategy, media channels, and market research. Examines the roles and interactions of advertisers, agencies, and media organizations. Covers diverse advertising formats including print media, direct mail, outdoor advertising, and internet-based campaigns. Discusses account management, account planning, target market identification, and measuring advertising effectiveness. Addresses the economics of advertising expenditure, determining return on investment, and understanding when campaigns succeed or fail. Explores the relationship between advertising and the public, the benefits and criticisms of the industry, and future trends in commercial communication.

titles.title: Forensic psychology
titles.author: David Canter
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: Forensic Psychology: A Very Short Introduction discusses the aspects of psychology that are relevant to the legal and criminal process. It includes explanations of criminal behaviour and criminality, including the role of mental disorder in crime, and discusses how forensic psychology contributes to helping investigate the crime and catching the perpetrators. It also explains how psychologists provide guidance to those involved in civil and criminal court proceedings and what expert testimony can be provided by a psychologist about the offender at the trial. Finally, this VSI examines how forensic psychology is used, particularly in prisons, to help in the management, treatment and rehabilitation of offenders, once they have been convicted.
titles.summaryAI: Forensic psychology as a discipline applying psychological knowledge to legal and criminal justice processes. Covers explanations of criminal behavior and criminality, including the role of mental disorders in crime. Examines psychological methods for criminal investigation and offender profiling. Discusses expert testimony in civil and criminal court proceedings, psychological assessment tools including the thematic apperception test, and evaluation of post-traumatic stress disorder. Explores the management, treatment, and rehabilitation of convicted offenders within prison systems. Addresses family dynamics in criminal behavior and the use of psychological information in legal decision-making.

titles.title: Modernism
titles.author: Christopher Butler
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract: Modernism: A Very Short Introduction examines how and why modernism began, what it is, how it has gradually informed all aspects of 20th and 21st century life. Modernism expressed a new way of thinking of the self, subjectivity, irrationalism, people and machines, and politics. Is a tower block, an unmade bed, a lavatory basin, or the bicycle chained to a gate a work of art? Why should a novel have a beginning, a middle, and an end; or even a story? Whether we recognise it or not, virtually every aspect of our life today has been influenced in part by the aesthetic legacy of modernism. What does modernism mean today?
titles.summaryAI: Modernism as an aesthetic and cultural movement transforming art, literature, architecture, and thought in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries. Examines modernism's origins and development, including movements such as Dada and surrealism associated with Andre Breton. Explores new conceptions of selfhood, subjectivity, irrationalism, and the relationship between people and machines. Covers modernist innovations in the novel, challenging traditional narrative structure with beginnings, middles, and ends. Discusses the role of myth and the break with tradition. Addresses modernism's engagement with politics and its lasting aesthetic legacy across visual arts, literature, and everyday contemporary life.

titles.title: Leadership
titles.author: Keith Grint
titles.subject: Business/Psychology
titles.abstract: What is leadership? How does it differ from management and command? Are leaders born or bred? Leadership: A Very Short Introduction prompts the reader to rethink their understanding of what leadership is. It examines the way leadership has evolved from its earliest manifestations in ancient societies, highlighting the beginnings of leadership writings through Plato, Sun Tzu, Machiavelli and others, to consider the role of social, economic, and political contexts undermining particular modes of leadership. Exploring the idea that leaders cannot exist without followers, and recognising that we all have diverse experiences and assumptions of leadership, this VSI looks at the practice of management, its history, future, and influence on society.
titles.summaryAI: Leadership as a concept and practice examined historically from ancient societies to the present, distinguishing leadership from management and command. Covers early leadership writings by Plato, Sun Tzu, and Machiavelli. Explores whether leaders are born or developed, the relationship between leaders and followers, and the role of social capital in leadership effectiveness. Examines how social, economic, and political contexts shape particular modes of leadership. Discusses decision-making processes, presidential leadership, and the evolution of management practice. Addresses diverse cultural experiences and assumptions about leadership and its influence on organizational and societal outcomes.

titles.title: Christian ethics
titles.author: D. Stephen Long
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Christian Ethics: A Very Short Introduction presents a discussion of the relationship between Christian ethics, modern, and postmodern ethics, and explores practical issues including sex, money, and power. What are the inherent difficulties in bringing together ‘Christian’ and ‘ethics’? Christian ethics is not a precise science; rather, it is the cultivation of practical wisdom from a range of sources. This VSI looks at the failures of the Christian tradition, including the crusades, the conquest, slavery, inquisitions, and the Galileo affair. These events raise challenges for modern Christian ethics. They have implications in the modern era, that affect our lives in the present age.
titles.summaryAI: Christian ethics as the cultivation of practical wisdom from diverse theological and philosophical sources, examining the relationship between Christian moral thought and modern and postmodern ethical frameworks. Covers practical moral issues including sexuality, homosexuality, abortion, economic justice, money, and power. Discusses historical failures of the Christian tradition including the Crusades, colonial conquest, slavery, inquisitions, and the Galileo affair. Engages thinkers including Friedrich Nietzsche and Sallie McFague. Explores virtue ethics, the concept of the Fall, the role of prayer in moral life, and ongoing implications of historical Christian moral failings for contemporary ethics.

titles.title: Tocqueville
titles.author: Harvey Mansfield
titles.subject: Politics/Biography
titles.abstract: Tocqueville: A Very Short Introduction uncovers the man behind the landmark Democracy in America a book still considered one of the best sources for anyone trying to understand American democracy. Tocqueville was a liberal and a thinker whose life's experiences — his aristocratic ancestry, his ventures in politics, and his voyages abroad — shaped his writings. His work expressed his passion for political liberty and insistence on human greatness. He opposed abstraction and theory, and his emphasis on the practice of self-government in America was a reflection of his hopes and fears for America, and his disappointment with his native France.
titles.summaryAI: Alexis de Tocqueville as a political thinker and author of Democracy in America, one of the most influential analyses of American democratic society. Examines Tocqueville's aristocratic background, political career, and travels shaping his thought. Covers his emphasis on political liberty, self-government, democratic institutions, and human greatness. Discusses his opposition to abstraction and theory in favor of practical political observation. Situates Tocqueville among thinkers including Rousseau, Kant, Marx, Mill, and Spinoza. Explores connections to the Declaration of Independence, social contract theory, revolution, state of nature concepts, and comparative analysis of American democracy and French political disappointments.

titles.title: Landscapes and Geomorphology
titles.author: Andrew Goudie, Heather Viles
titles.subject: Geology
titles.abstract: Landscapes and Geomorphology: A Very Short Introduction presents an introduction to landscapes and the methods and techniques used to study it. Examining what landscapes are, and how we use a range of ideas and techniques to study them, it is demonstrated how geomorphologists have built on methods pioneered by 19th-century scientists. Using examples from around the world, key controls on landscapes today, such as tectonics, climate, humans, and the living world, are explored. Concluding with the cultural importance of landscapes, and how this has led to the conservation of much 'earth heritage', this VSI looks at how we can predict the response of landscapes to climate change in the future.
titles.summaryAI: Geomorphology as the scientific study of landscapes, landforms, and the processes shaping the Earth's surface. Covers methods and techniques including radiometric dating, building on nineteenth-century scientific pioneers such as Louis Agassiz. Examines key controls on landscape formation including tectonics, climate, human impact, and biological processes. Discusses specific geomorphic phenomena including mass movement, wind erosion, sea level change, and the Dust Bowl. Explores the relationship between climate change and landscape response, including prediction of future changes. Addresses the cultural importance of landscapes, earth heritage conservation, and examples of geomorphic processes from around the world.

titles.title: Spanish Literature
titles.author: Jo Labanyi
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract: Spanish Literature: A Very Short Introduction explores the ways in which it has been read, in and outside Spain, explaining misconceptions, outlining the insights of recent scholarship, and suggesting new readings. Spanish literature has given the world the figures of Don Quixote and Don Juan, and is responsible for the ‘invention’ of the novel in the 16th century. The medieval period produced literature in Castilian, Catalan, Galician, Latin, Arabic, and Hebrew, and today there is a flourishing literature in Catalan, Galician, and Basque as well as in Castilian — the language that has become known as ‘Spanish’. A multi-layered history of exile has produced a transnational literary production, while writers in Spain have engaged with European cultural trends.
titles.summaryAI: Literary traditions of Spain from the medieval period to the present, spanning Castilian, Catalan, Galician, Basque, Latin, Arabic, and Hebrew literatures. Covers the Golden Age, the development of the novel in the sixteenth century, and iconic figures such as Don Quixote and Don Juan. Addresses multilingualism, transnational literary production shaped by histories of exile, engagement with European cultural movements, and the distinction between Castilian as a national language and broader Iberian literary diversity.

titles.title: Diplomacy
titles.author: Joseph M. Siracusa
titles.subject: Politics/History
titles.abstract: Diplomacy: A Very Short Introduction introduces the subject of diplomatic history, the critical study of the management of relations between nation-states. Based on significant historical case studies—the diplomacy of the American Revolution, the diplomatic origins of the Great War and its aftermath Versailles, the personal summitry behind the night Stalin and Churchill divided Europe, George W. Bush and the coming of the Iraq War, and diplomacy in the age of globalization—there are concrete examples of diplomacy in action while locating the universal role of negotiations. Through these examples we can see what the key element of success is. The lessons learned provide a road map to navigating the challenges of 21st-century diplomacy.
titles.summaryAI: History and practice of diplomacy as the management of relations between nation-states, from the eighteenth century to the twenty-first. Case studies include the American Revolution, the origins and aftermath of World War I including Versailles, wartime summitry dividing Europe between Stalin and Churchill, the lead-up to the Iraq War under George W. Bush, and diplomacy in the age of globalization. Emphasizes negotiation as a universal mechanism, lessons from historical successes and failures, and evolving challenges in international relations.

titles.title: North American Indians
titles.author: Theda Perdue, Michael D. Green
titles.subject: History/Archaeology
titles.abstract: North American Indians: A Very Short Introduction describes how nomadic bands of hunter-gatherers travelled over the Bering land mass between Asia and what is now Alaska from 25,000 to 15,000 years ago, settling throughout North America. When Europeans arrived in North America, between five and eight million indigenous people were already there. There was a great diversity of indigenous peoples in America, who spoke more than 400 different languages and whose ways of life varied according to the environments they settled in and adapted to. But how did they come to be there? What were their agricultural, spiritual, and hunting practices? How did their societies evolve and what challenges do they face today?
titles.summaryAI: Indigenous peoples of North America from their migration across the Bering land bridge twenty-five thousand to fifteen thousand years ago through European contact and into the present. Covers the diversity of over four hundred languages and varied ways of life adapted to different environments across the continent. Addresses agricultural practices, spiritual traditions, hunting, societal evolution, and contemporary challenges facing Native American communities. Distinguishes between pre-contact population estimates and post-contact demographic and cultural transformations.

titles.title: The U.S. Congress
titles.author: Donald Ritchie
titles.subject: Politics – U.S.
titles.abstract: The U.S. Congress: A Very Short Introduction provides an analytical insight into the work of Congress. The framers of the Constitution intended Congress to be the preeminent branch of government. What is it like to campaign for Congress? How do you attract large donors? What are the roles of staffers and lobbyists, and how do committees work? This VSI explores all these questions and also examines the creation of Congress by the constitutional convention, the checks and balances to the work of Congress and the floor proceedings, parliamentary rules, coalition building, and the key players on Capitol Hill.
titles.summaryAI: Structure, functions, and historical development of the United States Congress as a legislative institution. Covers its creation at the constitutional convention, the Great Compromise establishing bicameral representation, checks and balances with other branches, and the roles of the House of Representatives and Senate. Examines committee systems including Armed Services and Ways and Means, floor proceedings, parliamentary rules, coalition building, campaigning, campaign finance, the influence of staffers and lobbyists, and key figures on Capitol Hill.

titles.title: Romanticism
titles.author: Michael Ferber
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract: Romanticism: A Very Short Introduction explores the definition of the word ‘Romantic’ and considers its origins. What is Romanticism? Who were the Romantics? and What did they have in common? The ideas, beliefs, commitments and tastes of the Romantics are explored. The birth and growth of Romanticism throughout Europe and the Americas is described, and the various expressions of Romanticism in literature, music, painting, religion, and philosophy are surveyed. This VSI looks at the ‘Sensibility’ movement, which preceded Romanticism; the importance of poets to the movement; Romanticism as a religious trend; Romantic philosophy and science; Romantic responses to the French Revolution; and Romanticism and the condition of women.
titles.summaryAI: Pan-European and American cultural movement of the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries spanning literature, music, painting, religion, and philosophy. Traces origins in the Sensibility movement and responses to the French Revolution. Covers key figures including Wordsworth, Coleridge, Shelley, Schiller, and Kant. Addresses Romantic philosophy and science, the centrality of poetry, Romanticism as a religious trend, and questions of gender and the condition of women within the movement. Distinguishes Romanticism from Enlightenment rationalism and classicism.

titles.title: Utopianism
titles.author: Lyman Tower Sargent
titles.subject: Literature/Philosophy
titles.abstract: Utopianism: A Very Short Introduction examines utopianism and its history by discussing its role in literature, the development of colonies, and in immigration. The idea of utopia has become commonplace in social and political thought, both negatively and positively. Some thinkers see a trajectory from utopia to totalitarianism with violence an inevitable part of the mix. Others see it as directly connected to freedom and as necessary in the fight against totalitarianism. In Christianity utopia is labelled as both heretical and a fundamental part of Christian belief. Such debates are also central to architecture, town and city planning, and sociology. What constitutes a utopia? What is the relationship between utopia and ideology?
titles.summaryAI: Concept and history of utopianism across literature, political theory, architecture, urban planning, and sociology. Examines utopian colonies, immigration driven by utopian ideals, and the contested relationship between utopia and totalitarianism versus freedom. Addresses utopianism within Christianity, Buddhism, Judaism, and secular ideologies including communism. Covers the connection between utopia and ideology as theorized by Mannheim, third-world utopianism, and globalization. Distinguishes positive and negative deployments of utopian thought in social and political contexts.

titles.title: The Blues
titles.author: Elijah Wald
titles.subject: Music
titles.abstract: The Blues: A Very Short Introduction surveys a genre which lies at the heart of American culture. Blues is more of a broad musical tradition than a musical genre, with a constantly evolving pop culture. It explores its roots in work and praise songs, and its popularization by W. C. Handy. It follows its evolution from Ma Rainey and Bessie Smith to Bob Dylan and Jimi Hendrix; identifies the impact of rural field recordings of Blind Lemon Jefferson and Charley Patton; explores the influence of blues on country music and jazz; and looks at the rhythm and blues trends of the 1940s and 1950s, from T–Bone Walker to Muddy Waters.
titles.summaryAI: History and evolution of blues as a broad American musical tradition from its roots in work and praise songs through its twentieth-century transformations. Covers W. C. Handy's popularization, classic female blues singers Ma Rainey and Bessie Smith, rural field recordings of Blind Lemon Jefferson and Charley Patton, rhythm and blues of the 1940s and 1950s from T-Bone Walker to Muddy Waters, and crossover influence on country music, jazz, and rock through Bob Dylan and Jimi Hendrix. Addresses blues as evolving pop culture rather than a fixed genre.

titles.title: Keynes
titles.author: Robert Skidelsky
titles.subject: History of Science/Economics/Biography
titles.abstract: Keynes: A Very Short Introduction provides an insight into hils life and works, and explores Keynes's ethical and practical philosophy, and his monetary thought. John Maynard Keynes (1883–1946) was a central thinker of the twentieth century, not just an economic theorist and statesman, but also in economics, philosophy, politics, and culture. Keynes's fundamental insight was that we do not know, and cannot calculate, what the future will bring. In the recent financial crisis, Keynes's theories have become more relevant than ever, and remain at the centre of political and economic discussion. What did Keynes contribute to twentieth-century economic thought? What can we say about his legacy for today's economy and society?
titles.summaryAI: Life, thought, and legacy of John Maynard Keynes (1883-1946) spanning economics, philosophy, politics, and culture. Covers his ethical and practical philosophy, monetary thought, and contributions to twentieth-century economic theory including his fundamental insight about irreducible uncertainty regarding the future. Addresses his roles as economic theorist and statesman, his relationship to predecessors like Alfred Marshall, the gold standard debate, the founding of the International Monetary Fund, and the renewed relevance of Keynesian economics during financial crises.

titles.title: English literature
titles.author: Jonathan Bate
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract: English Literature: A Very Short Introduction considers such diverse topics as the birth of the novel, the brilliance of English comedy, the deep Englishness of landscape poetry, and the ethnic diversity of Britain's Nobel literature laureates. English literature is known for its major literary movements such as Romanticism and Modernism, and influential authors including Chaucer, Donne, Johnson, Wordsworth, Austen, Dickens, and Woolf. The study of English literature brings up fascinating questions. Why does literature matter? How does narrative work? What is distinctly English about English literature? How do literary texts change as they are transmitted from writer to reader?
titles.summaryAI: Survey of English literature from its oral tradition origins through major movements including Romanticism and Modernism. Covers influential authors such as Chaucer, Shakespeare, Donne, Johnson, Wordsworth, Austen, Dickens, and Woolf. Addresses the birth and development of the novel, English comedy, landscape poetry and its deep connection to Englishness, and the ethnic diversity among British Nobel literature laureates. Explores questions of narrative mechanics, literary transmission from writer to reader, and the cultural distinctiveness of English literary traditions.

titles.title: Agnosticism
titles.author: Robin Le Poidevin
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Agnosticism: A Very Short Introduction asks if there is more to agnosticism than merely the absence of belief. What is agnosticism? Who were the first to call themselves ‘agnostics’? Agnosticism is something we encounter in science and ethics and should be considered as an historical and cultural phenomenon. It is not confined to the religious sphere. Some of the preconceptions and assumptions amongst both believers and non-atheists are examined and the philosophical case for agnosticism is set out. Agnosticism is a more complex attitude than simply a failure to either commit to, or reject, religious belief.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophical examination of agnosticism as a complex epistemological position extending beyond mere absence of religious belief. Traces the historical origins of self-identified agnostics and examines agnosticism as a cultural phenomenon applicable to science and ethics, not solely religion. Addresses the philosophical case for agnosticism, challenges preconceptions held by both believers and non-believers, and distinguishes agnosticism from atheism. Considers the relationship between knowledge, certainty, and belief in theological and secular contexts within Western philosophical tradition.

titles.title: Aristocracy
titles.author: William Doyle
titles.subject: Politics/History
titles.abstract: Aristocracy: A Very Short Introduction shows how ideas of aristocracy originated in ancient times, were transformed in the middle ages, and have only fallen apart over the last two centuries. The myths in which aristocracies have always sought to shroud themselves are stripped away, but the true sources of their enduring power are also revealed. Their outlook and behaviour affected the rest of society in innumerable and sometimes surprising ways, but perhaps most surprising was the way in which a centuries-old aristocratic hegemony has crumbled away over the last two hundred years. This VSI considers why this happened and what remains today.
titles.summaryAI: History of aristocracy as a social and political institution from ancient origins through medieval transformation to modern decline over the past two centuries. Covers the true sources of aristocratic power, the myths aristocracies constructed, and the ways aristocratic outlook and behavior shaped broader society. Addresses feudalism, patronage, the roles of institutions like the House of Lords, and the impact of the French Revolution. Examines why centuries-old aristocratic hegemony collapsed in Europe and what vestiges remain today.

titles.title: Martin Luther
titles.author: Scott H. Hendrix
titles.subject: Religion/Biography
titles.abstract: Martin Luther: A Very Short Introduction details Luther's life, writings, world, and legacy. It explains the religious reformation and his importance, emphasizing the political and cultural forces that led the reformation down paths Luther could neither foresee nor influence. How do historians view Martin Luther? What effect did his self-righteous attitude have on contemporaries? What place did his anti-Judaism play in Nazi propaganda? This introduction presents Martin Luther as historians now see him and emphasizes the context in which Luther worked, the colleagues who supported him, and the opponents who adamantly opposed his agenda for change.
titles.summaryAI: Life, writings, and legacy of Martin Luther within the context of the sixteenth-century Protestant Reformation. Covers the political and cultural forces that shaped the Reformation beyond Luther's control, his relationships with colleagues and opponents, and his self-righteous temperament. Addresses the connections between Lutheranism, Calvinism, Anabaptism, and Catholicism. Examines historiographical debates including the use of Luther's anti-Judaism in Nazi propaganda and the broader relationship between religious reform and political authority under figures like Charles V.

titles.title: Michael Faraday
titles.author: Frank A.J.L. James
titles.subject: History of Science/Physics/Biography
titles.abstract: Michael Faraday: A Very Short Introduction explores the life of one of the best-known scientific figures of the nineteenth century. Faraday's achievements — his discovery of electro-magnetic induction, the principle behind the electric generator and transformer — are set in the context of the rapidly changing place that science had in society. This VSI dispels the myth of Faraday as an isolated, experimental genius working in a basement laboratory. Instead it shows his place as a key figure in the developing scientific institutions of the time and his legacy as a theorist of the physical world and his influence on later physicists such as Kelvin, Maxwell, and Einstein.
titles.summaryAI: Life and scientific contributions of Michael Faraday in nineteenth-century Britain, centered on his discovery of electromagnetic induction, the principle underlying electric generators and transformers. Covers his role in developing scientific institutions, his theoretical contributions to understanding matter, fields, and physical forces, and his influence on later physicists including Kelvin, Maxwell, and Einstein. Addresses the changing place of science in Victorian society and challenges the myth of Faraday as an isolated experimental genius working alone.

titles.title: Planets
titles.author: David A. Rothery
titles.subject: Astronomy
titles.abstract: Planets: A Very Short Introduction demonstrates the excitement, uncertainties, and challenges faced by planetary scientists, and provides an overview of our Solar System and its origins, nature, and evolution. Terrestrial planets, giant planets, dwarf planets and various other objects such as satellites (moons), asteroids, trans-Neptunian objects, and exoplanets are discussed. Our knowledge about planets has advanced over the centuries, and has expanded at a rapidly growing rate in recent years. Controversial issues are outlined, such as What qualifies as a planet? What conditions are required for a planetary body to be potentially inhabited by life? Why does Pluto no longer have planet status? And Is there life on other planets?
titles.summaryAI: Overview of planetary science covering the origins, nature, and evolution of the Solar System. Addresses terrestrial planets, giant planets, dwarf planets, satellites, asteroids, trans-Neptunian objects, and exoplanets. Covers planetary classification controversies including the reclassification of Pluto, conditions required for potential habitability, and methods of detection such as radial velocity measurements. Discusses the Kuiper belt, Oort cloud, solar nebula theory, and how knowledge of planetary systems has expanded through technologies like the Hubble Space Telescope.

titles.title: Pentecostalism
titles.author: William K. Kay
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Pentecostalism: A Very Short Introduction outlines the origins and growth of Pentecostalism, looking at the theological aspects of the movement and also the sociological influences of its political and humanitarian viewpoints. In religious terms Pentecostalism was probably the most vibrant and rapidly-growing religious movement of the 20th century. Starting as a revivalistic and renewal movement within Christianity, it encircled the globe in less than 25 years and grew in North America and then in those parts of the world with the highest birth-rates. Characterised by speaking in tongues, miracles, television evangelism and megachurches, it is also noted for its small-group meetings, empowerment of individuals, liberation of women, and humanitarian concerns.
titles.summaryAI: Origins, growth, and global expansion of Pentecostalism as the most rapidly growing Christian movement of the twentieth century. Covers theological aspects including speaking in tongues and miracles, sociological dimensions including political engagement and humanitarian concerns, and organizational features from megachurches and television evangelism to small-group meetings. Addresses Pentecostalism's roots in the Holiness movement, its relationship to Methodism, Lutheranism, and Calvinism, its empowerment of individuals and liberation of women, and its rapid spread through high-birth-rate regions worldwide.

titles.title: Humanism
titles.author: Stephen Law
titles.subject: Religion/Philosophy
titles.abstract: Humanism: A Very Short Introduction explores how humanism uses science and reason to make sense of the world, looking at how it encourages individual moral responsibility and shows that life can have meaning without religion. Are atheism and humanism ‘faith positions’? Without God is there morality? Without God do we have a purpose? Religion is currently gaining a higher profile. The number of faith schools is increasing, and religious points of view are being aired more frequently. As religion's profile rises, those who reject religion can find themselves misunderstood and misrepresented. This VSI examines the arguments about the existence of God, and explores the role humanism plays in moral and secular societies.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophical and ethical framework of humanism as a worldview grounded in science, reason, and individual moral responsibility without reliance on religion. Addresses arguments about the existence of God, whether morality requires a divine foundation, and whether life can have meaning and purpose in a secular framework. Covers the relationship between humanism and atheism, the charge that both are faith positions, and the role of humanism in contemporary debates about education, secularism, and the rising public profile of religion.

titles.title: Folk music
titles.author: Mark Slobin
titles.subject: Music
titles.abstract: Folk Music: A Very Short Introduction explores a remarkably diverse musical genre. It considers folk musical traditions from across the globe, everything from music to accompany a Russian wedding near the Arctic Circle, to a group song in a small rainforest village in Brazil, and an Uzbek dance tune in Afghanistan. Three poignant songs from three widely separated regions — northern Afghanistan, Jewish Eastern Europe, and a ballad from the Anglo–American world — are living examples of the richness of this ever–changing musical genre. Musical notation and lyrics are included throughout.
titles.summaryAI: Global survey of folk music traditions spanning diverse cultures and geographies, from Russian Arctic wedding music to Brazilian rainforest village songs to Uzbek dance tunes in Afghanistan. Examines three detailed case studies from northern Afghanistan, Jewish Eastern Europe, and the Anglo-American ballad tradition. Addresses the richness and constant evolution of folk musical genres, cross-cultural comparison of folk traditions, and the relationship between folk music and broader categories like world music. Includes musical notation and lyrics as analytical tools.

titles.title: Late Antiquity
titles.author: Gillian Clark
titles.subject: Classical Studies
titles.abstract: Late Antiquity: A Very Short Introduction debates the concept of and the events that took place in late antiquity. Was late antiquity a period of decline or transformation, conflict or interaction? Late antiquity was the period (c. 300—c.800) during which barbarian invasions defeated the Roman Empire in Western Europe by the 5th century and Arab invasions ended Roman rule over the eastern and southern Mediterranean coasts by the 7th century. It was a time of cultural clashes, political restructurings, geographical controversies, and competing religions. Late antiquity is discussed in relation to Byzantium and the Fall of Rome, and the relationship between late antiquity and the modern world is considered.
titles.summaryAI: Period of transition from roughly 300 to 800 CE, encompassing the fall of the Western Roman Empire to barbarian invasions by the fifth century and Arab conquests ending Roman rule over eastern and southern Mediterranean coasts by the seventh century. Covers cultural interactions, political restructurings, competing religions including Christianity and Manichaeism, and geographic controversies. Addresses debates over whether this era represents decline or transformation, relationships to Byzantium, the roles of Greek, Latin, Arabic, and Syriac linguistic cultures, and connections to the modern world.

titles.title: Genius
titles.author: Andrew Robinson
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: Homer, Leonardo da Vinci, Mozart, Shakespeare, and Tolstoy; Curie, Darwin, Einstein, Galileo, and Newton. What do these world-famous artists and scientists have in common? Most of us would probably say all ten possessed something we call genius, which in each instance permanently changed the way that humanity perceived the world. Genius: A Very Short Introduction uses the life and work of familiar geniuses, and some less familiar, to illuminate both the individual and the general aspects of genius. In particular: the roles of talent, heredity, parenting, education, training, hard work, intelligence, personality, mental illness, inspiration, eureka moments, and luck, in the making of genius.
titles.summaryAI: Psychological and historical analysis of genius examining the roles of talent, heredity, parenting, education, training, hard work, intelligence, personality, mental illness, inspiration, eureka moments, and luck. Draws on case studies of renowned figures in art and science including Leonardo da Vinci, Darwin, Einstein, Galileo, Newton, Mozart, Shakespeare, and Tolstoy. Addresses both individual and general aspects of genius, the relationship between creativity and mental health, and the contributions of Francis Galton to the study of exceptional ability.

titles.title: Numbers
titles.author: Peter M. Higgins
titles.subject: Mathematics
titles.abstract: Numbers: A very Short Introduction unravels the world of numbers; demonstrating its richness, and providing a comprehensive view of the idea of the number. This VSI paints a picture of the number world, considering how the modern number system matured over centuries. Explaining the various number types and showing how they behave, it introduces key concepts such as integers, fractions, real numbers, and imaginary numbers. By approaching the topic in a non–technical way and emphasising the basic principles and interactions of numbers with mathematics and science, this VSI also demonstrates practical interactions and modern applications, such as encryption of confidential data on the internet.
titles.summaryAI: Historical development and mathematical properties of number systems, from ancient origins to modern applications. Covers integers, fractions, real numbers, and imaginary numbers, explaining how the number system matured over centuries. Addresses foundational concepts including sequences, ratios, square roots, infinity, and prime number theory including twin primes and contributions of Euclid and Euler. Explores practical applications of number theory in modern contexts such as internet cryptography and data encryption, and the interactions between numbers and science.

titles.title: Muhammad
titles.author: Jonathan A.C. Brown
titles.subject: Religion/Biography
titles.abstract: Muhammad: A Very Short Introduction provides an introduction to the major aspects of Muhammad's life and its importance, giving both the Muslim and Western historical perspectives. As the founder of Islam, Muhammad is one of the most influential figures in history. The furore surrounding the publication of The Satanic Verses and the Danish cartoon crisis reminded the world of the tremendous importance of the prophet of Islam. Learning about his life and understanding its importance, however, has always proven difficult. Our knowledge of Muhammad comes from the biography written by his followers, but Western historians have questioned the reliability of this story in their quest to uncover the ‘historical Muhammad’.
titles.summaryAI: Life and historical significance of Muhammad as the founder of Islam, examining both Muslim biographical traditions and Western historical-critical approaches. Covers key events and figures including Abu Bakr, Abu Sufyan, and Zaynab bint Jahsh. Addresses the challenges of reconstructing the historical Muhammad from follower-authored sources, the reliability debates in Western scholarship, and the enduring cultural sensitivity surrounding depictions of the Prophet as illustrated by the Satanic Verses controversy and the Danish cartoon crisis.

titles.title: Beauty
titles.author: Roger Scruton
titles.subject: Philosophy/Art
titles.abstract: Beauty: A Very Short Introduction explores the concept of beauty, asking what makes an object — either in art, in nature, or the human form — beautiful. It strongly refutes the notion that judgements of beauty are purely subjective and relative, and that we can learn little from art criticism and study. Arguing that our experience of beauty is rationally founded, and that beauty is a real and universal value, this VSI shows how our sense of beauty has an indispensable part to play in the way we shape our world.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophical investigation of beauty as a real and universal value across art, nature, and the human form. Argues against purely subjective and relativistic accounts of aesthetic judgment, contending that the experience of beauty is rationally grounded. Draws on thinkers including Plato, Aquinas, Rousseau, Coleridge, Ruskin, and Greenberg. Addresses the relationship between aesthetic criticism and philosophical understanding, the role of taste in judgment, and how the human sense of beauty shapes cultural and environmental engagement with the world.

titles.title: Critical theory
titles.author: Stephen Eric Bronner
titles.subject: Literature/Philosophy
titles.abstract: Critical Theory: A Very Short Introduction explores the concepts and themes that distinguished critical theory from its more traditional philosophical competitors. Critical theory emerged in the 1920s from the work of the Frankfurt School, the circle of German–Jewish academics who sought to diagnose and cure the ills of society. Sketches of leading representatives of this critical tradition, such as Georg Lukács and Ernst Bloch, Theodor W. Adorno and Walter Benjamin, Herbert Marcuse and Jürgen Habermas, as well as many of its seminal texts and empirical investigations, are presented. Concepts such as method and agency, alienation and reification, the culture industry and repressive tolerance, non-identity, and utopia are explained and discussed.
titles.summaryAI: Origins and development of critical theory from the Frankfurt School of German-Jewish academics in the 1920s through its later evolution. Covers key figures including Lukacs, Bloch, Adorno, Benjamin, Marcuse, and Habermas. Addresses central concepts such as method and agency, alienation and reification, the culture industry, repressive tolerance, non-identity, and utopia. Distinguishes critical theory from traditional philosophy through its diagnostic and emancipatory aims, its engagement with social ills, and its seminal texts and empirical investigations.

titles.title: Organizations
titles.author: Mary Jo Hatch
titles.subject: Business
titles.abstract: Organizations are everywhere, but have you ever considered where they came from, what they are today, and what they may become in the future? Organizations: A Very Short Introduction introduces the concept of organizations by presenting definitions and ideas drawn from the a variety of subject areas including the physical sciences, economics, sociology, psychology, anthropology, literature, and the visual and performing arts. Drawing on examples from prehistory and everyday life, from the animal kingdom as well as from business, government, and other formal organizations, this VSI provides a lively and thought provoking introduction to the process of organization.
titles.summaryAI: Interdisciplinary analysis of organizations drawing on physical sciences, economics, sociology, psychology, anthropology, literature, and the performing arts. Covers the origins and evolution of organizations from prehistory to contemporary forms, including business, government, and formal institutions. Addresses organizational concepts such as contingency theory, management, competition, innovation, information technology, outsourcing, and supply chain dynamics. Examines how organizations function in developing countries and considers future trajectories of organizational forms and processes.

titles.title: Early Music
titles.author: Thomas Forrest Kelly
titles.subject: Music
titles.abstract: Early Music: A Very Short Introduction explores the music of the Medieval, Renaissance, and Baroque periods. The revival of Gregorian chant in the early nineteenth century, the ‘Cecilian movement’ in later nineteenth century Germany, and Mendelssohn's revival of Bach, are just some of the efforts made to restore early music. In recent years this interest has taken on particular meaning, representing a rediscovery of little-known underappreciated repertories and an effort to recover lost performing styles. Much has been gained in the twentieth century from the study and revival of instruments, playing techniques, and repertories.
titles.summaryAI: History and revival of Western music from the Medieval, Renaissance, and Baroque periods.
