titles.abstract: Ethnomusicology: A Very Short Introduction describes this growing discipline, showing how modern researchers go about studying music from around the world, looking for insights into both music and humanity. Ethnomusicologists believe that all humans, not just those we call musicians, are musical, and that musicality is one of the essential touchstones of the human experience. This insight raises big questions about the nature of music and the nature of humankind, and ethnomusicologists argue that to address these questions properly, we must study music in all its geographical and historical diversity. Ethnomusicologists today not only examine traditional forms of music, but also explore more contemporary musical forms.
titles.summaryAI: Ethnomusicology as a discipline studying music across geographic and historical diversity, encompassing traditional and contemporary forms. Covers methodologies for researching music worldwide, drawing on fieldwork, cultural analysis, and comparative approaches. Examines the premise that musicality is a universal human trait and essential aspect of human experience. Addresses relationships between music and culture, the ethics of musical borrowing as in Deep Forest, and connections between ethnomusicology and classical music, jazz, rock, popular music, and dance. Bridges musicology with anthropology and cultural studies.

titles.title: African Religions
titles.author: Jacob K. Olupona
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: African Religions: A Very Short Introduction offers a wide-ranging look at the myriad indigenous religious traditions on the African continent, drawing on archeological research, historical evidence, ethnographic studies, and archival materials. African religious belief-exploring myths, gods and local deities, ancestor worship, rites of passage, festivals, and divination are considered and the role these religions play in everyday African life, from birthing and death, marriage and family dynamics, to diet, dress and grooming, health care, and even governance, is discussed. Christianity and Islam has spread throughout Africa, but the more extreme fundamentalist sects—which decry traditional religion as paganism—have driven a near total collapse of indigenous practice.
titles.summaryAI: Indigenous religious traditions across the African continent, drawing on archaeological, historical, ethnographic, and archival evidence. Covers myths, gods, local deities, ancestor worship, rites of passage, festivals, divination, and cosmology among peoples including the Akan and Asante. Examines religion's role in daily life: birth, death, marriage, diet, dress, health care, and governance. Addresses the impact of Christianity and Islam on indigenous practices, including fundamentalist movements driving collapse of traditional religion. Distinguishes African religions from racialized interpretive frameworks.

titles.title: Humour
titles.author: Noël Carroll
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract: Humour: A Very Short Introduction considers the relation of humour to emotion and cognition, explores the value of humour in its social functions, and examines the leading humour theories including The Superiority Theory and The Incongruity Theory. Humour, known in every human culture, can serve many functions; it can be used to relieve stress, to promote goodwill among strangers, to dissipate tension within a fractious group, and to display intelligence. This VSI argues that humour, and the comic amusement that follows it, has a crucial role to play in the construction of communities. It also demonstrates that the social aspect of humour raises questions such as ‘When is humour immoral?’.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophical and psychological analysis of humor, examining its cognitive and emotional dimensions. Covers major theories including the Superiority Theory and Incongruity Theory. Explores humor's social functions: stress relief, group cohesion, tension dissipation, and intelligence display. Examines humor's role in community construction and its relationship to comic amusement. Addresses moral questions about when humor becomes immoral, the cultural universality of humor across human societies, and connections to black humor traditions. Bridges aesthetics, social psychology, and ethics through analysis of comedic phenomena.

titles.title: Family law
titles.author: Jonathan Herring
titles.subject: Law - U.K.
titles.abstract: Family Law: A Very Short Introduction provides insight not only into what family law is, but why it is the way it is. How have laws had to respond to social changes in family life? The last few decades have seen rising divorce rates and an increase in the use of surrogate mothers. How do family courts deal with the chaos of modern family life? Family law has recently been challenged to keep up-to-date with the social and scientific changes which affect it. What is a family? What makes someone a parent? What rights should children have? What will families look like in the years ahead? What new dilemmas will the courts face?
titles.summaryAI: Legal frameworks governing family relationships in the United Kingdom, covering marriage, civil partnership, divorce, and forced marriage. Examines children's rights, parental definitions, child abuse protections, and domestic violence legislation. Addresses the legal status of surrogacy and evolving family structures. Explores how family courts manage disputes amid social change, including rising divorce rates and new reproductive technologies. Connects family law to human rights frameworks and criminal law, examining tensions between individual autonomy, child welfare, and state regulation of intimate relationships.

titles.title: The Ice Age
titles.author: Jamie Woodward
titles.subject: Geology
titles.abstract: The study of the Quaternary ice age has revolutionized ideas about Earth system change and the pace of landscape and ecosystem dynamics. The Ice Age: A Very Short Introduction looks at evidence from the continents, the oceans, and the ice core records, and the human stories behind it all. It examines the remarkable environmental shifts that took place during the Great Ice Age of the Quaternary Period. This VSI explores the evolution of ideas, evaluates the contributions of the leading players in the great debates, and presents some of the ingenious methods that have been used to retrieve information about the recent geological past.
titles.summaryAI: Quaternary ice age environmental history, covering evidence from continental glacial deposits, ocean sediment records, and ice core data. Examines the development of glacial theory from Louis Agassiz, William Buckland, Jean de Charpentier, and Charles Lyell. Covers ice sheet dynamics, erratic boulder distribution, ecosystem responses to glacial-interglacial cycles, and climate change during the Quaternary Period. Addresses methodological innovations for reconstructing past climates and landscapes. Connects geological interpretation to broader understanding of Earth system change and the pace of environmental transformation.

titles.title: Revolutions
titles.author: Jack A. Goldstone
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: From 1789 in France to 2011 in Cairo, revolutions have shaken the world. In their pursuit of social justice, revolutionaries have taken on the assembled might of monarchies, empires, and dictatorships. Revolutions: A Very Short Introduction illuminates the revolutionaries, their strategies, their successes and failures, the major theories of revolution, and the ways in which revolutions continue to dominate world events and the popular imagination. Starting with the city-states of ancient Greece and Rome, this VSI traces the development of revolutions through the Renaissance and Reformation, the Enlightenment and liberal constitutional revolutions such as in America, and their opposite—the communist revolutions of the 20th century.
titles.summaryAI: Theory and history of revolutions from ancient Greek and Roman city-states through the Arab Spring of 2011. Covers the French Revolution, American Revolution, Russian Revolution, and communist revolutions of the twentieth century. Examines revolutionary strategies, leadership, successes, and failures across diverse political contexts including monarchies, empires, and dictatorships. Traces revolutionary thought through the Renaissance, Reformation, and Enlightenment. Addresses relationships between revolution and social justice, civil war, guerrilla warfare, and international relations, including the role of the Soviet Union.

titles.title: Classical Literature
titles.author: William Allan
titles.subject: Classical Studies
titles.abstract: From popular histories through to reworkings of classical subject matter by contemporary poets, dramatists, and novelists, the classical world and the masterpieces of its literature continue to fascinate readers and audiences in a huge variety of media. Classical Literature: A Very Short Introduction explores what the ‘classics’ are and why they continue to shape our Western concepts of literature. It provides a concise, lucid, yet broad overview of classical literature. Presenting a range of material from both Greek and Latin literature, it illustrates the variety and sophistication of these works, and considers examples from all the major genres.
titles.summaryAI: Greek and Latin literary traditions across major genres including epic, lyric, satire, tragedy, and comedy. Covers canonical authors Homer, Virgil, Catullus, Ennius, and Pliny the Younger, and works such as Medea. Examines the variety and sophistication of classical literary production from archaic Greece through imperial Rome, including the concept of golden ages in Latin literature. Addresses the enduring influence of classical literature on Western conceptions of literary form and genre, and its continued adaptation in contemporary poetry, drama, and fiction.

titles.title: Accounting
titles.author: Christopher Nobes
titles.subject: Business
titles.abstract: If you read the ‘business pages’ of a newspaper or if you listen to the financial news on the television or radio, you will often hear terms such as ‘liability’, ‘balance sheet’ or ‘earnings’. Accounting: A Very Short Introduction provides a guide to understanding and using accounting information. It examines the key concepts and the main types of accounting work, from bookkeeping and financial reporting to auditing and management accounting. It traces the development of accounting over the centuries; examines the building blocks of financial reports, such as assets and liabilities; and looks at how accounting differs between countries and the importance of international standards.
titles.summaryAI: Accounting principles, practices, and institutions covering financial accounting, management accounting, auditing, and bookkeeping. Examines core concepts including assets, liabilities, balance sheets, consolidated financial statements, and earnings. Traces the historical development of accounting across centuries and compares practices across countries. Addresses international standardization through the International Accounting Standards Board and the Financial Accounting Standards Board, the concept of true and fair view, and the role of the Securities and Exchange Commission. Connects accounting to corporate governance and economic decision-making.

titles.title: Teeth
titles.author: Peter S. Ungar
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: Teeth are the product of half a billion years of evolution. They provide fuel for the body by breaking apart other living things; and they must do it again and again over a lifetime without themselves being broken in the process. Teeth: A Very Short Introduction presents the story of teeth from the earliest tooth-bearing fishes hundreds of millions of years ago through amphibians, reptiles and, ultimately, mammals. It considers the anatomy and variety of teeth and the important role they play, explores the variety of tooth forms in living animals, and looks at how mammal teeth have evolved and how recent changes to our diet are now affecting dental health.
titles.summaryAI: Evolutionary biology of teeth across half a billion years, from early tooth-bearing fishes through amphibians, reptiles, and mammals. Covers dental anatomy including enamel, dentine, canines, and molars, and the functional demands of food processing. Examines the diversity of tooth forms in living animals and the evolutionary adaptations linking dental morphology to feeding ecology. Addresses mammalian dental evolution, natural selection pressures on tooth design, and the impact of recent dietary changes on human dental health. Connects paleontology with comparative anatomy and nutrition science.

titles.title: Physical chemistry
titles.author: Peter Atkins
titles.subject: Chemistry
titles.abstract: With the development of a variety of exciting new areas of research involving computational chemistry, nano- and smart materials, and applications of the recently discovered graphene, there can be no doubt that physical chemistry is a vitally important field. It is also perceived as the most daunting branch of chemistry, being grounded in physics and mathematics and drawing on quantum mechanics, thermodynamics, and statistical thermodynamics. Physical Chemistry: A Very Short Introduction provides a non-technical insight into the central concepts of the field, and explains its relationship to other branches of chemistry, and its important contributions to our understanding of the natural world.
titles.summaryAI: Core concepts of physical chemistry integrating physics and mathematics with chemical understanding. Covers thermodynamics including entropy and absolute zero, quantum mechanics applied to electron behavior, and statistical thermodynamics. Examines states of matter, phase transitions, equations of state, activation energy and reaction kinetics, and nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy. Addresses computational chemistry, nano-materials, smart materials, and graphene applications. Connects molecular-level understanding to chemical bonding, synthesis processes, and the relationship between physical chemistry and other chemical subdisciplines.

titles.title: Microeconomics
titles.author: Avinash Dixit
titles.subject: Economics
titles.abstract: Microeconomics — individuals' choices of where to live and work, how much to save, what to buy, and firms' decisions about location, hiring, firing, and investment — involves issues that concern us on a daily basis. But when people think about economics, they tend to place importance on the bigger picture — macroeconomics — including issues such as unemployment, inflation, and the competitiveness of nations. Microeconomics: A Very Short Introduction argues that the microeconomy has a large impact on the economic world. Using real-life examples from around the world, this VSI provides insights into economics from psychology and sociology to explain economic behaviour and rational choice.
titles.summaryAI: Individual and firm-level economic decision-making covering consumer choice, labor markets, savings, investment, and business location. Examines market structures including monopoly, competition, and antitrust regulation. Covers Pareto efficiency, risk assessment, cost analysis, and supply dynamics. Addresses agricultural policy through the Common Agricultural Policy and price mechanisms for goods distribution. Integrates insights from psychology and sociology to explain economic behavior and rational choice. Contrasts microeconomic analysis with macroeconomic concerns including unemployment and inflation, using international real-world examples.

titles.title: Landscape architecture
titles.author: Ian Thompson
titles.subject: Architecture
titles.abstract: Landscape architecture plays an important role in shaping the places in which we live and work. But what is it? Landscape architects are involved in the layout of business parks, the reclamation of derelict industrial sites, the restoration of historic city parks, and the siting and design of major pieces of infrastructure such as motorways, dams, power stations, and flood defences, as well as the planning of parks and gardens. Landscape Architecture: A Very Short Introduction looks at both the roots of landscape architecture and the people that established it. It also explores some of the misconceptions about landscape architecture and considers the discipline’s origins in landscape gardening.
titles.summaryAI: Landscape architecture as a discipline encompassing the design of parks, gardens, business parks, infrastructure, and urban environments. Covers historical development from landscape gardening traditions through figures including Frederick Law Olmsted, Thomas Church, and Andrew Jackson Downing. Examines the reclamation of industrial sites, historic park restoration, and infrastructure design including motorways, dams, and flood defenses. Addresses relationships with urban design, architecture, and the influence of modernist designers like Ludwig Mies van der Rohe and Martha Schwartz. Distinguishes landscape architecture from garden design.

titles.title: The Eye
titles.author: Michael F. Land
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: The eye is one of the most remarkable achievements of evolution. In humans, vision is the most important sense, and much of the brain is given over to the processing of visual information. The Eye: A Very Short Introduction describes the evolution of vision and the variety of eyes found in both humans and animals. It explores the evolution of colour vision in primates and the workings of the human eye. It explains how we see in three dimensions and the basic principles of visual perception, including our impressive capacity for pattern recognition and the ability of vision to guide action.
titles.summaryAI: Evolution, structure, and function of eyes across animal species, with emphasis on human vision. Covers retinal anatomy including fovea, receptors, lens, and optic nerve connections to the brain cortex. Examines the evolution of color vision in primates, color blindness, and three-dimensional perception. Addresses eye movements, pattern recognition capabilities, and vision-guided action. Explores the developmental biology of eye formation and the diversity of visual systems across the animal kingdom. Connects evolutionary biology with neuroscience, optics, and perceptual psychology.

titles.title: The Etruscans
titles.author: Christopher Smith
titles.subject: Classical Studies
titles.abstract: This book explores Etruscan history, culture, language, and customs and examines the controversial debates about their origins, placing them within the geographical, economic, and political context of the time. From around 900 to 400 bc, the Etruscans were the most innovative, powerful, wealthy, and creative people in Italy. Their settlements were powerful, and their influence extended into northern and southern Italy, including Rome. Their archaeological record is both substantial and fascinating, including tomb paintings, sculpture, jewellery, and art. However no literature of their own has survived, so we have to understand them entirely through the eyes of contemporary and later ancient writers. This account seeks to understand the way the Etruscans were perceived and described, and also to insist on the possibility of a diachronic historical understanding of their civilization. The volume ends with an account of the influence of the Etruscans from the middle ages on, showing how the distinct discipline of Etruscology has grown up, and what may be its future.
titles.summaryAI: Etruscan civilization in Italy from approximately 900 to 400 BCE, covering their origins, culture, language, political organization, and artistic achievements including tomb paintings, sculpture, and jewelry. Examines their influence on Rome and broader Italian peninsula, connections with Greek and Phoenician civilizations, and the Villanovan cultural precursor. Addresses debates about Etruscan origins drawing on Herodotus and Dionysius of Halicarnassus. Covers the absence of surviving Etruscan literature and reliance on external ancient sources. Traces the development of Etruscology from the medieval period onward.

titles.title: Nutrition
titles.author: David Bender
titles.subject: Medicine
titles.abstract: Nutrition is a topic of wide interest and importance. In spite of a growing understanding of the underlying biochemistry, and health campaigns such as ‘five-a-day’, increasing obesity and reported food allergies and eating disorders, as well as the widely advertised ‘supposed’ benefits of food supplements mean that a clear explanation of the basic principles of a healthy diet are vital. Nutrition: A Very Short Introduction explains the basic elements of food, the balance between energy intake and exercise, the problems of over- and under-nutrition, and raises the question of the safety of nutritional supplements.
titles.summaryAI: Biochemical and physiological principles of human nutrition, covering macronutrients including protein and dietary fiber, vitamins, and metabolic processes. Examines the balance between energy intake and physical activity, using body mass index as a health metric. Addresses the spectrum from undernutrition and fasting to obesity and overconsumption. Explores food allergies, eating disorders, and the evidence base for nutritional supplements. Connects nutritional science to public health campaigns, dietary guidelines, and the underlying biochemistry of how food components support or impair human health.

titles.title: Coral Reefs
titles.author: Charles Sheppard
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: Reefs and the coral life that builds them were for centuries a source of mystery to naturalists and hazard to seafarers. Many ideas were developed of what built them and why they all existed so close to sea level but never above it. Darwin developed the theory of how they were built, which was proven a century later. The coral polyp is central to each coral colony and to the reef. Each houses countless symbiotic algal cells that provide the energy that supports the coral reef ecosystem, and the energy needed to extract minerals from seawater to deposit as solid limestone. These are the ocean’s most biodiverse ecosystem. The islands perched on them include many entire nations, and reefs provide land, food, and protection to these as well as parts of many others. The diversity and abundance of other species, from microbial systems that are key to nutrient and energy transfer, to the large predatory fish, are similarly vast, and various components of the reef system have been researched intensively since the advent of scuba techniques. Today, however, local impacts and pressures from pollution to overfishing have degraded and damaged many, and more recently, warming of ocean water resulting from climate change is causing an existential threat to the survival of this rich ecosystem. Arresting the decline is no longer a scientific problem but one for society and governments, and failure to do so will result, indeed already is, in untold damage to human societies that depend on coral reefs.
titles.summaryAI: Coral reef biology, ecology, and conservation, covering coral polyp anatomy, symbiotic algal relationships, and limestone deposition processes. Examines Darwin's subsidence theory of reef formation, reef biodiversity as the ocean's richest ecosystem, and microbial nutrient cycling systems. Covers the dependence of island nations on reef structures for land, food, and coastal protection. Addresses threats including pollution, overfishing, ocean warming, and climate-driven reef degradation. Connects marine biology with biogeochemistry, conservation policy, and the socioeconomic consequences of reef decline for human communities.

titles.title: Complexity
titles.author: John H. Holland
titles.subject: Science
titles.abstract: From the movement of flocks of birds to the Internet, environmental sustainability, and market regulation, the study and understanding of complex non-linear systems has become highly influential over the last thirty years. Complexity: A Very Short Introduction introduces the key elements and conceptual framework of complexity. From complex physical systems such as fluid flow and the difficulties of predicting weather, to complex adaptive systems such as the highly diverse and interdependent ecosystems of rainforests, it combines simple, well-known examples—Adam Smith’s pin factory, Charles Darwin’s comet orchid, and Herbert Simon’s “watchmaker”—with an account of the approaches, involving agents and urn models, taken by complexity theory.
titles.summaryAI: Complex systems theory covering both complex physical systems such as fluid dynamics and weather prediction, and complex adaptive systems including rainforest ecosystems and market regulation. Examines key concepts: emergence, nonlinearity, agent-based modeling, network interactions, and urn models. Draws on examples from Adam Smith's division of labor, Darwin's coevolution, and Herbert Simon's hierarchical systems. Addresses applications in environmental sustainability, Internet networks, and economic systems. Bridges mathematics, physics, biology, and economics through frameworks for understanding self-organizing, interdependent systems.

titles.title: Alexander the Great
titles.author: Hugh Bowden
titles.subject: Classical Studies/History/Biography
titles.abstract: Alexander the Great: A Very Short Introduction demonstrates the profound influence the legends of Alexander's life have had on our historical understanding of him by focusing on the evidence from his own time. Aged just 20, Alexander the Great became king of Macedon in 336 bc; he died aged 32. During his reign, he conquered the Achaemenid Persian Empire, the largest empire that had ever existed, leading his army from Greece to Pakistan, and from the Libyan desert to the steppes of Central Asia. He had a profound effect on the world he moved through and his achievements became legendary both in his lifetime and in the centuries that followed.
titles.summaryAI: Alexander III of Macedon's reign from 336 to 323 BCE, covering his conquest of the Achaemenid Persian Empire from Greece to Pakistan and from Libya to Central Asia. Examines the historical evidence from Alexander's own time versus later legendary accounts by Curtius Rufus, Plutarch, and Ptolemy I. Covers his relationship with Philip II, campaigns against Darius III, destruction of Persepolis, and movements through Asia Minor, Babylon, and the Persian Gulf. Addresses his profound impact on the ancient Mediterranean and Near Eastern world.

titles.title: Hormones
titles.author: Martin Luck
titles.subject: Medicine
titles.abstract: Hormones balance and coordinate the workings of the body. There are many misconceptions about the endocrine system and few people are aware of its nature and importance. Hormones: A Very Short Introduction explains what hormones are, where they come from, what they do, how they work, and how they evolved. It describes how the endocrine system operates, explaining how hormones regulate water, salt, and calcium in the body, how they affect reproduction and our appetites, and how they help us adjust to different environments, such as travel across time zones. The ethical and moral issues surrounding research methods, testing on animals, and hormone misuse are also considered.
titles.summaryAI: Endocrine system physiology covering hormone types, sources, mechanisms of action, and evolutionary development. Examines specific hormones including insulin, growth hormone, adrenocorticotrophic hormone, and thyroid hormones. Covers hormonal regulation of water, salt, and calcium balance, reproductive cycles, appetite control, and circadian adjustment across time zones. Addresses the pituitary gland and releasing hormone cascades, connections between hormonal dysfunction and conditions including gigantism and cancer. Explores ethical issues surrounding animal testing, hormone research methods, and hormone misuse in society.

titles.title: Confucianism
titles.author: Daniel K. Gardner
titles.subject: Religion/Philosophy
titles.abstract: To understand China, it is essential to understand Confucianism. First formulated in the sixth century bce, the teachings of Confucius would come to dominate Chinese society, politics, economics, and ethics. Confucianism: A Very Short Introduction explores the major philosophical ideas of the Confucian tradition, showing their profound impact on state ideology, imperial government, the civil service examination system, domestic life, and social relations over the course of twenty-six centuries. It focuses on two of the Sage's most crucial philosophical problems—what makes for a good person, and what constitutes good government—and demonstrates the enduring significance of these questions today and how the Confucian tradition sits alongside Western ideas and technology.
titles.summaryAI: Confucian philosophical tradition from its sixth-century BCE formulation through twenty-six centuries of influence on Chinese civilization. Covers core texts including the Analerta and the Book of Changes, and central questions about moral cultivation and good governance. Examines Confucianism's impact on state ideology, imperial government, civil service examinations, domestic life, and social relations through the Shang, Tang, Qing, and modern periods. Addresses twentieth-century upheavals including the May Fourth Movement, the Cultural Revolution under Mao, and Confucianism's contemporary relationship with Western ideas under Deng Xiaoping.

titles.title: American Slavery
titles.author: Heather Andrea Williams
titles.subject: History – U.S.
titles.abstract: American Slavery: A Very Short Introduction traces the development of American slavery, from the Portuguese capture of Africans in the 1400s until its abolition following the Civil War, and explores its effects on the American colonies and the United States of America. It examines legislation that differentiated American Indians and Africans from Europeans as the ideology of white supremacy flourished. These laws reflected the contradiction of America's moral and philosophical ideology that valorized freedom on one hand and justified the enslavement of a population deemed inferior on another. The tense and often violent relationships between the enslaved and the enslavers are explored, as well as those between abolitionists and pro-slavery advocates.
titles.summaryAI: Development of chattel slavery in the Americas from Portuguese capture of Africans in the 1400s through abolition after the American Civil War. Covers the transatlantic slave trade, the emergence of white supremacy ideology, and legislation differentiating Africans and Native Americans from Europeans. Examines enslaver-enslaved relationships, fugitive slave experiences, and the abolitionist movement including William Lloyd Garrison and Lydia Maria Child. Addresses the contradiction between American freedom ideals and slavery, Uncle Tom's Cabin, Jim Crow laws, and slavery's lasting legacies in American society.

titles.title: African American Religion
titles.author: Eddie S. Glaude Jr.
titles.subject: Religion
keywords: National Association for the Advancement of Colored People
titles.abstract: African American Religion: A Very Short Introduction explores the history and circumstances of African American religion through three examples: conjure, African American Christianity, and African American Islam. It argues that the phrase “African American religion” is meaningful only insofar as it describes how through religion, African Americans have responded to oppressive conditions. This bold claim frames the interpretation of the historical record of diverse religious experiences in the African American community. The common tendency to racialize African American religious experiences as an inherent proclivity towards religiousness is rejected and instead the focus is on how religious communities and experiences have developed in the African American community and the context in which these developments took place.
titles.summaryAI: Religious experience in the African American community examined through three traditions: conjure, African American Christianity, and African American Islam including the Nation of Islam. Covers the relationship between religion and responses to racial oppression from slavery through the civil rights movement. Examines institutions including the National Baptist Convention, Church of God in Christ, and organizations like the NAACP and Southern Christian Leadership Conference. Challenges racialized interpretations of inherent religiousness, instead contextualizing religious development within specific historical conditions of discrimination and resistance.

titles.title: God
titles.author: John Bowker
titles.subject: Religion/Philosophy
titles.abstract: Who or what is God? How can we know God? Many people believe in God; not just throughout history but also in the present day. But who or what is it they believe in? Many different and sometimes conflicting answers have been suggested to this question. God: A Very Short Introduction explores some of the answers provided by philosophers, poets, and theologians, and considers why some people believe in God and others do not. It considers how each major religion established their own distinctive beliefs about God and how they each interpret His existence, and concludes by looking at how our understanding of God continues to evolve.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophical and theological conceptions of God across major world religions, examining arguments for and against divine existence. Covers the five ways of proving God's existence, the problem of evil, the God of the gaps argument, and the anthropic principle in cosmology. Examines distinctive beliefs about God in traditions addressing dharma, the Holy Spirit, and creation narratives. Explores perspectives from philosophers, poets, and theologians on divine nature, religious experience, and the death of God movement. Addresses the evolving understanding of God incorporating quantum mechanics and human nature.

titles.title: Genes
titles.author: Jonathan Slack
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: What are genes? What do they say about us? Genes: A Very Short Introduction explores the concept of the gene and looks at the discovery, nature, and role of genes in both evolution and development. Explaining the nature of genetic variation in the human population, how hereditary factors were identified as molecules of DNA, and how certain specific mutations can lead to disease, it highlights how DNA variants are used to trace human ancestry and migration, and can also be used by forensic scientists to identify individuals in crime. It also explores issues such as genetic heritability and IQ.
titles.summaryAI: Gene concept from discovery through modern molecular biology, covering DNA structure, sequencing, and the polymerase chain reaction. Examines genetic variation in human populations, hereditary mechanisms including sex linkage, and how specific mutations cause diseases such as cystic fibrosis. Covers the role of genes in evolution through natural selection and the Red Queen hypothesis, and in developmental biology through cell division and protein expression. Addresses applications in forensic identification, ancestry tracing, and debates about genetic heritability of traits including IQ.

titles.title: Knowledge
titles.author: Jennifer Nagel
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: What is knowledge? How does it differ from mere belief? Do you need to be able to justify a claim in order to count as knowing it? Knowledge: A Very Short Introduction considers these epistemological questions alongside new puzzles arising from recent discoveries about humanity, language, and the mind. It explains the formation of major historical theories of knowledge, and shows how contemporary philosophers have developed new ways of understanding knowledge, using ideas from logic, linguistics, and psychology. Covering topics ranging from relativism and the problem of scepticism to the trustworthiness of internet sources, this VSI uses everyday examples to explain the key issues and debates.
titles.summaryAI: Epistemological investigation of knowledge, belief, and justification, covering historical theories from Montaigne's skepticism through rationalism and empiricism to contemporary analytic approaches. Examines the causal theory of knowledge, externalism, and challenges from relativism. Addresses the problem of skepticism, the role of testimony and trust, and the reliability of internet sources. Draws on methods from logic, linguistics, and psychology to analyze how humans acquire and validate knowledge. Connects epistemology to philosophy of mind, philosophy of language, and practical reasoning about evidence.

titles.title: Structural engineering
titles.author: David Blockley
titles.subject: Engineering
titles.abstract: Every object has structure. Structure is the difference between a random pile of components and a fully functional object. Through structure the parts connect to make the whole. Natural structures vary from the very smallest part of an atom to the entire cosmology of the universe. Man-made structures include buildings, bridges, dams, ships, aeroplanes, rockets, trains, cars, and even large artistic sculptures. Different industries in which structural engineers work include construction, transport, manufacturing, and aerospace. Structural Engineering: A Very Short Introduction explores what structural engineering is all about, including examples ranging from the Shard in London and the Golden Gate Bridge in San Francisco to jumbo jets and the Queen Elizabeth cruise liner.
titles.summaryAI: Principles and practice of structural engineering across construction, transport, manufacturing, and aerospace industries. Covers fundamental elements including beams, bridges, buttresses, catenaries, concrete, and timber structures. Examines the relationship between structural engineering and architecture from Vitruvius through Antoni Gaudi and Isambard Kingdom Brunel. Addresses both natural structures at atomic and cosmic scales and man-made structures including buildings, bridges, dams, ships, aircraft, and large sculptures. Explores structural modeling, material properties, and the integration of form and function in engineered design.

titles.title: Theatre
titles.author: Marvin Carlson
titles.subject: Art
titles.abstract: From before history was recorded to the present day, theatre has been a major artistic form around the world. From puppetry to mimes and street theatre, this complex art has utilized all other art forms such as dance, literature, music, painting, sculpture, and architecture. Every aspect of human activity and human culture can be, and has been, incorporated into the creation of theatre. Theatre: A Very Short Introduction moves through Ancient Greece and Rome, to medieval Japan and Europe, and to America and beyond. It looks at how the various forms of theatre have been interpreted and enjoyed, and it explores the role that theatre artists play.
titles.summaryAI: Theatre as a global artistic form from ancient Greece and Rome through medieval Europe and Japan to modern performance, encompassing puppetry, mime, street theatre, and musical theatre. Covers the integration of dance, literature, music, painting, sculpture, and architecture within theatrical production. Examines figures including Adam de la Halle, Jean Racine, and Lope de Vega, and forms such as the Theatre of the Absurd. Addresses the roles of theatre artists, audience interpretation, and the incorporation of all aspects of human culture into theatrical creation.

titles.title: Ancient Egyptian Art and Architecture
titles.author: Christina Riggs
titles.subject: Art
titles.abstract: What did Egyptian art and architecture mean to the people who first made and used it—and why has it had such an enduring appeal? Ancient Egyptian Art and Architecture: A Very Short Introduction explores the visual arts produced in Egypt over a span of some 4,000 years. The stories behind these objects and buildings have much to tell us about how people in ancient Egypt lived their lives in relation to each other, the natural environment, and the world of the gods. Demonstrating how ancient Egypt has fascinated Western audiences over the centuries, this VSI considers the relationship between ancient Egypt and the modern world.
titles.summaryAI: Egyptian visual arts and architecture spanning approximately four thousand years, covering sculpture, painting, decorative objects, and monumental building. Examines the cultural meanings of art for ancient Egyptians in relation to daily life, social organization, the natural environment, gender roles, fertility, and religious cosmology. Addresses figures including Amenhotep III and Alexander the Great's impact on Egyptian artistic traditions. Covers the Western reception of Egyptian art through institutions including the British Museum, Ashmolean Museum, and Metropolitan Museum of Art, and the legacy of archaeologists like Flinders Petrie.

titles.title: The Middle Ages
titles.author: Miri Rubin
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: The Middle Ages: A Very Short Introduction explores the variety, change, dynamism, and sheer complexity of the period of European history between 500 and 1500 and the impact that this period has had on the modern day world. From the provinces of the Roman Empire, which became barbarian kingdoms after c.450–650, to the northern and eastern regions, which were increasingly integrated into Europe, this volume describes the emergence of a truly global system of communication, conquest, and trade. The Middle Ages also considers the challenges of life in Europe at that time—at all levels of society—by looking at the various facets of kinship and family, agriculture and trade, and groups and individuals.
titles.summaryAI: European history from approximately 500 to 1500 CE, covering the transition from Roman provinces to barbarian kingdoms and the integration of northern and eastern regions into European frameworks. Examines kinship, family structures, agriculture, trade, and the emergence of global communication and commerce networks. Covers kingship and political authority including Frederick I, Henry III, and the Norman Conquest. Addresses the Black Death, the Chanson de Roland as literary culture, the role of Pope Gregory I, and the Teutonic Order. Spans all levels of medieval society.

titles.title: Materials
titles.author: Christopher Hall
titles.subject: Engineering
titles.abstract: The study of materials and their properties forms a major area of research that supports innovation and technology. Using modern scientific techniques, material scientists can explore and manipulate materials, and create new materials with remarkable properties. For example, material scientists now have the possibility of creating new materials that: combine hardness with flexibility; have the ability to store digital data; that can respond to their environment; and that can be scaffolds for the growth of new biological tissue. Materials: A Very Short Introduction begins with gold, sand, and string—representing the families of metals, ceramics, and polymers—and considers the properties and processes involved in their fabrication into objects, to show how materials science brings together engineering and technology with physics, chemistry, and biology.
titles.summaryAI: Materials science integrating engineering, physics, chemistry, and biology to study metals, ceramics, and polymers. Covers crystal lattice structures, chemical bonding, X-ray crystallography, and X-ray fluorescence analysis. Examines material properties including hardness, flexibility, and digital data storage capacity. Addresses innovations in smart materials, responsive materials, and biological tissue scaffolds. Traces scientific foundations from the periodic table and quantum theory through Einstein's contributions to modern computational and nano-material developments. Connects fabrication processes to material properties using gold, cement, sand, and carbon compounds as exemplars.

titles.title: Minerals
titles.author: David Vaughan
titles.subject: Geology
titles.abstract: Minerals existed long before any forms of life, playing a key role in the origin and evolution of life; an interaction with biological systems that we are only now beginning to understand. Exploring the traditional strand of mineralogy, which emphasizes the important mineral families, the well-established analytical methods (optical microscopy and X-ray diffraction) and the dramatic developments made in techniques over recent decades, Minerals: A Very Short Introduction also introduces the modern strand of mineralogy. This explores the role minerals play in the plate tectonic cycle and how they can be critical for human health and illness by providing essential nutrients and releasing poisons.
titles.summaryAI: Mineralogy covering major mineral families including quartz, feldspar, calcite, pyrite, and clay minerals such as montmorillonite. Examines crystal structures, unit cells, solid solutions, and fractional crystallization processes. Covers analytical methods from optical microscopy and X-ray diffraction to electron microscopy and synchrotron radiation techniques. Addresses minerals' role in plate tectonics, their pre-biotic existence, and interactions with biological systems including essential nutrients and toxic elements affecting human health. Connects traditional classification approaches with modern geochemical and biomedical mineralogy.

titles.title: Peace
titles.author: Oliver P. Richmond
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: Peace: A Very Short Introduction explores the evolution of peace in practice and in theory, exploring modern assumptions about peace and the different interpretations of its application. The concept of peace has always attracted radical thought, action, and practices. A term that has been taken to mean merely an absence of overt violence or war is, in the contemporary era, often used interchangeably with ‘peacemaking’, ‘peacebuilding’, ‘conflict resolution’, and ‘statebuilding’. The modern concept of peace has therefore broadened from the mere absence of violence to something more complicated.
titles.summaryAI: Evolution of peace as concept and practice, from the absence of violence to contemporary frameworks encompassing peacemaking, peacebuilding, conflict resolution, and statebuilding. Covers just war theory, the social contract tradition, and peacekeeping operations including the UN Security Council's role. Examines relationships between peace, democracy, social justice, and civil society. Addresses Cold War legacies, the World Bank's role in development-oriented peace, and tensions between security-focused and justice-focused approaches. Connects political theory with international relations, governance, and the structural conditions enabling or preventing violence.

titles.title: Iran
titles.author: Ali Ansari
titles.subject: History - Iran/Human Geography
titles.abstract: Iran has rarely been out of the headlines. Yet extensive media interest has tended to hinder rather than help our understanding of Iran as an idea, an identity, and a people, leading to a superficial understanding of what is a complex and nuanced political culture and civilization. Iran: A Very Short Introduction presents a radical reinterpretation of Iranian history and politics, placing the Islamic Revolution in the context of a century of political change and social transformation. It considers the various factors that have contributed towards the construction of the idea of Iran and the complex identity of Iranians themselves, to provide a more realistic understanding for us all.
titles.summaryAI: Iranian civilization, identity, and political culture from ancient Persia through the modern Islamic Republic. Covers the Constitutional Revolution, twentieth-century political transformation, and the 1979 Islamic Revolution in historical context. Examines the interplay of Islam, pre-Islamic Persian heritage, and modernization in constructing Iranian national identity. Addresses Cold War geopolitics, relations with major powers, and figures including Mahmoud Ahmadinejad. Explores Iran as both idea and political entity, challenging superficial Western media portrayals by analyzing the complex, nuanced factors shaping Iranian society and governance.

titles.title: World War II
titles.author: Gerhard L. Weinberg
titles.subject: Military
titles.abstract: World War II: A Very Short Introduction examines the origins, course, and impact of the Second World War on those who fought and the ordinary citizens who lived through it. Starting with the inter-war years and the German invasion of Poland in September 1939, it examines how the war progressed by examining a number of key events: the war in the West in 1940, Barbarossa, the German invasion of the Soviet Union, the expansion of Japan's war with China, developments on the home front, and the Allied victory from 1944–45. Exploring the costs and effects of the war, this VSI considers the long-lasting mark World War II has left on society today.
titles.summaryAI: Global military conflict from 1939 to 1945 encompassing origins in interwar diplomacy and the German invasion of Poland. Covers the Western Front campaigns of 1940, Operation Barbarossa against the Soviet Union, Japan's expansion in the Pacific and China, the Battle of the Atlantic, and Allied victory in 1944-45. Addresses home front mobilization, civilian experiences, wartime leadership under Hitler and Mussolini, and the war's lasting geopolitical, social, and demographic consequences for postwar international order.

titles.title: Child Psychology
titles.author: Usha Goswami
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: Child Psychology: A Very Short Introduction examines modern child psychology, tracing its development from birth up until early adolescence. Child Psychology studies the process of attachment and ‘bonding’, and it considers how secure attachments will enable the child to progress in the development of self-understanding. The volume also considers an individual’s psychological development during the adolescent years. It poses and discusses a number of questions: how do babies and toddlers develop an understanding of the physical, biological, and social worlds that surrounds them? How do they develop complex abilities and senses such as language and morality? How specifically do children learn languages? How do they develop relationships with siblings and friends?
titles.summaryAI: Developmental psychology from birth through early adolescence covering attachment, bonding, and the formation of secure relationships as foundations for self-understanding. Examines cognitive development in physical, biological, and social domains including language acquisition, moral reasoning, imagination, and inner speech. Addresses brain development and neurobiology underlying learning processes. Explores social development through sibling and peer relationships, memory formation, and the psychological changes characteristic of adolescent identity formation.

titles.title: Sport
titles.author: Mike Cronin
titles.subject: Sport
titles.abstract: Sport is big business, international in nature, and the focus of much media and cultural attention. Sport: A Very Short Introduction charts the history of sport, from its traditional origins in folk football and cock fighting to its position as a global phenomenon today. Looking at a variety of sports from team games such as rugby, cricket, and football to games for individuals such as golf, tennis, and skiing, it considers how these first emerged and captivated the interest of ordinary people, and how sport has been transformed within our daily lives. This VSI also explores the relationship between sport and class, gender, commerce, identity, and ethics.
titles.summaryAI: Historical development of sport from folk origins including traditional games and blood sports to modern global phenomena. Covers team sports such as rugby, cricket, football, baseball, and ice hockey alongside individual pursuits like golf, tennis, and skiing. Examines the Olympic Games, Tour de France, and professional leagues. Addresses intersections of sport with social class, gender, commercial enterprise, national and cultural identity, ethics and fair play, and the role of media attention in transforming athletic competition into mass entertainment.

titles.title: Exploration
titles.author: Stewart A. Weaver
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: For as long as there have been civilizations, there has been the urge to venture outside of them, either in search of other civilizations or in search of novelty. But what accounts for this exploration urge? Exploration: A Very Short Introduction surveys this quintessential human impulse, tracing it from pre-history to the present, from east to west around the globe, and from the depths of volcanoes to the expanses of space. It arranges the history of world explorations into thematic chapters, each of which isolates the distinctive qualities and characteristics of exploration in a particular era, period, or place.
titles.summaryAI: History of global exploration from prehistory to the present organized thematically by era and region. Covers ancient expeditions including Alexander the Great, Portuguese navigation around the Cape of Good Hope under Henry the Navigator and Vasco da Gama, Columbus and Spanish conquest, and Enlightenment-era scientific voyages by Cook and Humboldt. Addresses the Lewis and Clark Expedition, Arctic Northwest Passage searches, African interior exploration, and extends to volcanic and space exploration, examining motivations and cultural significance across civilizations.

titles.title: Microbiology
titles.author: Nicholas P. Money
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: In recent decades we have come to realize that the microbial world is hugely diverse, and can be found in the most extreme environments. Fungi, single-celled protists, bacteria, archaea, viruses, and sub-viral particles far outnumber plants and animals. Microbes play a critical role in ecosystems, in the chemistry of the atmosphere and oceans, and within our bodies. Microbiology: A Very Short Introduction explores not only the traditional methods of microscopy and laboratory culture, but also the modern techniques of genetic detection and DNA sequencing, genomic analysis, and genetic manipulation. In turn, it demonstrates how advances in microbiology have had a tremendous impact on the areas of medicine, agriculture, and biotechnology.
titles.summaryAI: Study of microorganisms including bacteria, archaea, fungi, algae, protists, viruses, and sub-viral particles. Covers traditional microscopy and laboratory culture alongside modern techniques of DNA sequencing, genomic analysis, polymerase chain reaction, and genetic manipulation. Examines microbial ecology including nitrogen fixation, soil communities, and roles in atmospheric and oceanic chemistry. Addresses microbial evolution, diversity in extreme environments, and practical applications in medicine, agriculture, and biotechnology including the historical contributions of Louis Pasteur.

titles.title: Corporate social responsibility
titles.author: Jeremy Moon
titles.subject: Business
titles.abstract: Corporate social responsibility (CSR) has been defined as ‘the responsibility of enterprises for their impacts on society’. Is CSR a contradiction in terms? Corporate Social Responsibility: A Very Short Introduction shows that CSR holds much more value than it first appears, and shows how it has come of age in recent years. Illustrating the sorts of CSR investments companies make, the ways in which they practice CSR, and the challenges this brings, it considers how the principles migrated from their US roots to become a global business phenomenon. Exploring the place of CSR in different economic, social, political, and managerial contexts, this VSI considers the many positives that CSR can present.
titles.summaryAI: Corporate social responsibility as a global business phenomenon examining how enterprises address their societal impacts. Traces CSR's migration from American origins to international adoption across different economic and political contexts. Covers corporate governance, stakeholder engagement, transparency, human rights, gender equity, health and safety, and labor practices. Addresses CSR investment strategies, the relationship between business and socialization, scientific management principles applied to human resources, and challenges of implementing responsible practices within private equity and corporate remuneration structures.

titles.title: Love
titles.author: Ronald de Sousa
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Do we love someone for their virtue, their beauty, or their moral qualities? Are love’s characteristic desires altruistic or selfish? What do the sciences tell us about love? Many of the answers given to such questions are determined not so much by the facts of human nature as by the ideology of love. Love: A Very Short Introduction considers some of the many paradoxes raised by love, looking at the different kinds of love—affections, affiliation, philia, storge, agape—before focusing on eros, or ‘romantic’ love. It considers whether our conventional beliefs about love and sex are deeply irrational and argues that alternative conceptions of love and sex may be worth striving for.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophical analysis of love examining its varieties including affection, philia, storge, agape, and erotic or romantic love. Explores paradoxes of love concerning virtue, beauty, desire, altruism, and selfishness. Addresses Kantian ethics, Freudian psychology, and scientific perspectives on love. Critically examines the ideology of love and its cultural construction, questioning conventional beliefs about love and marriage. Considers whether prevailing assumptions about romantic relationships and sexuality are irrational and whether alternative conceptions of love offer more rational frameworks for human connection.

titles.title: Psychotherapy
titles.author: Tom Burns (Author), Eva Burns-Lundgren (Editor)
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: Psychotherapy: A Very Short Introduction traces the development of psychotherapy from its origins in Freud’s psychoanalysis to the range of different approaches—counselling, cognitive behaviour therapy, other time-limited therapies, mindfulness, and group and family therapies. Describing the processes central to them all and highlighting their differences, it demonstrates what problems each therapy is best suited for. The principles behind the most commonly available types of psychotherapies are explained, and examples of what patients can expect when they seek such help are provided. This VSI concludes by examining the practice of psychotherapy: the types of psychotherapist training, the safeguards that exist to keep practice reliable, and how to choose a psychotherapist.
titles.summaryAI: Development and practice of psychotherapy from Freudian psychoanalysis through contemporary approaches. Covers cognitive behaviour therapy, counselling, time-limited therapies, mindfulness-based interventions, and group and family therapies. Examines core processes shared across therapeutic modalities and distinguishes which approaches suit specific conditions including depression. Addresses attachment theory in therapeutic relationships, practitioner training requirements, professional safeguards and regulatory standards, and practical guidance for selecting appropriate psychotherapeutic treatment and qualified practitioners.

titles.title: Chemistry
titles.author: Peter Atkins
titles.subject: Chemistry
titles.abstract: Chemistry: A Very Short Introduction encourages us to look at chemistry anew, through a chemist's eyes, in order to understand its central concepts and to see how it contributes towards our material comfort and to human culture. It shows how chemistry provides the infrastructure of our world, through the chemical industry, the fuels of heating, power generation, and transport, as well as the fabrics of our clothing and furnishings. By considering the remarkable achievements that chemistry has made, and examining its place between both physics and biology, this VSI presents a fascinating, clear, and rigorous exploration of the world of chemistry—its structure, core concepts, and exciting contributions to new cutting-edge technologies.
titles.summaryAI: Foundational principles of chemistry spanning inorganic, organic, and physical chemistry. Covers the periodic table, molecular structure, chemical bonding, quantum mechanical foundations, spectroscopy, energy transfer, and entropy. Examines chemistry's position between physics and biology as an interdisciplinary bridge. Addresses industrial applications including fuels for heating and transport, power generation, and materials for clothing and furnishings. Explores contributions to cutting-edge technologies and the chemical infrastructure underlying modern material civilization and human culture.

titles.title: Human Anatomy
titles.author: Leslie Klenerman
titles.subject: Medicine
titles.abstract: Knowledge of the basic construction of the human body—the skeleton, the organs of the chest and abdomen, the nervous system, the head and neck with its sensory systems and anatomy for breathing and swallowing—is vital for anyone studying medicine, biology, and health studies. Human Anatomy: A Very Short Introduction provides a clear, concise, and accessible introduction to the structure, function, and main systems of the human body, including a number of clear and simple illustrations to explain the key areas. Some aspects of human evolution are also considered to show how and why the human body has developed as it has.
titles.summaryAI: Structure and function of the human body covering the skeletal system, organs of the chest and abdomen, the central and peripheral nervous systems, head and neck anatomy including sensory organs, and structures for breathing and swallowing. Addresses the lymphatic system, carpal tunnel and other clinical conditions, and the pelvis. Incorporates evolutionary perspectives explaining how and why human anatomical structures developed. Relevant to medicine, biology, and health studies, tracing anatomical knowledge from Andreas Vesalius to modern understanding.

titles.title: The American West
titles.author: Stephen Aron
titles.subject: History - U.S.
titles.abstract: The American West: A Very Short Introduction tracks “the West” from the building of the Cahokia Mounds around 900 ad, through early encounters with Europeans, to the Newlands Reclamation Act in 1902, and the post-World War II migration to California. It stretches the chronology, enlarges the geography, and varies the casting, providing a history of the American West that is longer, larger, and more complicated than popular culture has previously suggested. It is a history of how portions of North America became Wests, how parts of these became American, and how ultimately American Wests became the American West.
titles.summaryAI: History of the American West from Cahokia Mounds around 900 AD through post-World War II California migration. Covers early Native American civilizations, European encounters, frontier expansion under Jefferson and Jackson, the Lewis and Clark expedition, gold rushes, and the Newlands Reclamation Act of 1902. Examines how portions of North America became contested western frontiers and were incorporated into the United States. Challenges popular cultural narratives by expanding the chronology, geography, and diverse peoples involved in western American development.

titles.title: American Political History
titles.author: Donald Critchlow
titles.subject: History - U.S./Politics
titles.abstract: American Political History: A Very Short Introduction explores key turning points in American political history, the nature of the two-party system, representative presidential and congressional elections, struggles to expand the electorate, critical social protest and third-party movements, and the emergence of a democratic political culture within a republican form of government. Despite fierce debates over the meaning of the U.S. Constitution, a belief in the importance of constitutional order persists among political leaders and voters. There have been deep divisions about the extent of federal power, slavery, citizenship, immigration policy, civil rights, and economic, financial, and social policies. New immigrants, racial minorities, and women have also joined the electorate and the debates.
titles.summaryAI: Evolution of American political institutions and democratic culture from the constitutional founding to the present. Covers the two-party system, presidential and congressional elections, expansion of the electorate to include women, racial minorities, and immigrants. Examines debates over federal power, slavery, citizenship, civil rights, immigration policy, and economic regulation. Addresses social protest movements, third-party challenges, and the persistent tension between constitutional order and competing interpretations of governance within a republican framework.

titles.title: Ritual
titles.author: Barry Stephenson
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Ritual is part of what it means to be human. It defines and enriches culture, but what is ritual? What are the various kinds of ritual? Is ritual tradition bound and conservative or innovative and transformational? Ritual: A Very Short Introduction describes a number of specific rites and explores ritual from theoretical and historical perspectives. It focuses on the places where ritual touches everyday life; shows how ritual is an important vehicle for group and identity formation; how it generates and transmits beliefs and values; how it can be used to exploit and oppress; and how it has served as a touchstone for thinking about cultural origins and historical change.
titles.summaryAI: Cross-cultural analysis of ritual as a fundamental human activity encompassing rites of passage, sacrifice, birth, marriage, and death ceremonies. Examines ritual from theoretical perspectives including liminality and ritualization concepts associated with Mircea Eliade and others. Addresses how ritual functions in group identity formation, transmission of beliefs and values, and cultural reproduction. Explores tensions between ritual as conservative tradition and as vehicle for innovation and transformation, including its potential for both social cohesion and exploitation or oppression.

titles.title: American Women's History
titles.author: Susan Ware
titles.subject: History – U.S.
titles.abstract: What does U.S. history look like with women at the center of the story? From Pocahantas to military women serving in the Iraq war, American Women's History: A Very Short Introduction chronicles the contributions, recognized and unrecognized, that women have made to the American experience. It recognizes the diversity of American women's experiences defined by race, ethnicity, and class, but also geography, sexual orientation, age, and religion. At the core of the narrative is the recognition that gender—the changing historical and cultural constructions of roles assigned to the biological differences of the sexes—is central to understanding the history of American women's lives, and the history of the United States.
titles.summaryAI: History of women in the United States from colonial encounters through contemporary military service, centering gender as an analytical category. Covers women's suffrage movement, evolving legal rights, and political participation. Examines diversity of experience across race, ethnicity, class, geography, sexual orientation, age, and religion. Addresses changing cultural constructions of gender roles, recognized and unrecognized contributions to American economic and social life, and how biological sex differences have been assigned shifting cultural meanings throughout American history.

titles.title: Dante
titles.author: Peter Hainsworth, David Robey
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: Dante: A Very Short Introduction examines the main themes and issues that run through all of Dante’s work, ranging from autobiography, to understanding God, and the order of the universe. In doing so, it highlights what has made Dante a vital point of reference for modern writers and readers. It emphasizes the distinctive and dynamic interplay in Dante's writing between argument, ideas, and analysis on the one hand, and poetic imagination on the other. Dante was highly concerned with the political and intellectual issues of his time, demonstrated most powerfully in his masterpiece, The Divine Comedy. The key figures and episodes in the poem are discussed, bringing out the originality and power of Dante's writing.
titles.summaryAI: Life and literary works of Dante Alighieri in medieval Italy, focusing on autobiographical themes, theological engagement, and political thought. Analyzes the Divine Comedy including its depictions of hell, repentance, and cosmic order, examining key figures and episodes. Explores the interplay between rational argument and poetic imagination across Dante's writings. Addresses connections to Old and New Testament traditions, medieval intellectual debates, and Dante's experience of exile. Considers Dante's enduring influence as a reference point for modern writers and readers.

titles.title: Ancient Assyria
titles.author: Karen Radner
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: Assyria was one of the most influential kingdoms of the Ancient Near East. Ancient Assyria: A Very Short Introduction sketches the history of Assyria from city state to empire, from the early 2nd millennium bc to the end of the 7th century bc. Since the archaeological rediscovery of Assyria in the mid-19th century, its cities have been excavated extensively in present-day Iraq, Syria, Turkey, and Israel, with further sites in Iran, Lebanon, and Jordan providing important information. The Assyrian Empire was one of the most geographically vast, socially diverse, multicultural, and multi-ethnic states of the early first millennium bc. After a brief introduction the book explores the geography and the history of Ancient Assyria. It continues by examining the lives not only of some of its key rulers, but also the lives of some typical citizens, and slaves too, are selected. In this way the culture and diverse ways of life of those who lived in the empire are vividly brought to life.
titles.summaryAI: History of Assyria from city-state origins in the early second millennium BC to imperial collapse in the late seventh century BC. Covers geographic scope across modern Iraq, Syria, Turkey, Israel, Iran, Lebanon, and Jordan. Examines Assyrian kingship, imperial administration, temple culture, inscriptions, and agricultural systems. Addresses social diversity including the lives of rulers, ordinary citizens, and slaves within a multicultural, multi-ethnic empire. Discusses archaeological rediscovery in the mid-nineteenth century and the role of museums in preserving Assyrian material culture.

titles.title: Plate tectonics
titles.author: Peter Molnar
titles.subject: Geology
titles.abstract: A revolution occurred in geology in the 1960s: plate tectonics. The idea that continents had drifted with respect to one another had been proposed decades before, but now the mechanism became clear. The Earth’s crust and upper mantle is divided into a small number of rigid, moving plates (approximately twenty of them). Plate tectonics explains much of the structure and phenomena seen today: how ocean basins form, widen, and disappear; why earthquakes and volcanoes are concentrated at plate boundaries; and how the great mountain ranges of the world were built. Plate Tectonics: A Very Short Introduction explains the nature and mechanisms of plate tectonics and its impact on our understanding of the ongoing processes that continue to change Earth. The significant historical breakthroughs in plate tectonics, by both individuals and teams, are discussed. Some of the practical uses of the subject in forecasting future events and in discovering Earth’s precious mineral resources are also highlighted.
titles.summaryAI: Theory of plate tectonics explaining how the Earth's lithosphere is divided into approximately twenty rigid plates whose movements drive geological processes. Covers continental drift, ocean basin formation, subduction, and the relationship between plate boundaries and earthquakes and volcanoes. Examines mountain building, crustal structure, and mantle dynamics. Addresses the historical development of plate tectonic theory in the 1960s, key scientific breakthroughs by individuals and teams, and practical applications in forecasting geological events and discovering mineral resources.

titles.title: Corruption
titles.author: Leslie Holmes
titles.subject: Law
keywords: Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development
titles.abstract: Corruption is one of the biggest global issues, ahead of extreme poverty, unemployment, the rising cost of food and energy, climate change, and terrorism. It is thought to be one of the principal causes of poverty around the globe. Its significance in the contemporary world cannot be overestimated. Corruption: A Very Short Introduction notes that corruption is as old as humanity itself, and then considers why the international community has only highlighted it as a problem in the past two decades. It explores the phenomenon from several different perspectives, from the cultural differences affecting how corruption is defined, its impact, its various causes, and the possible remedies.
titles.summaryAI: Analysis of corruption as a global phenomenon examining its definitions across cultural contexts, causes, impacts, and potential remedies. Addresses corruption's role as a principal driver of poverty and its relationship to unemployment, terrorism, and economic instability. Covers the involvement of international organizations including the Asian Development Bank, Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development, and World Trade Organization in anti-corruption efforts. Examines why the international community only prioritized corruption as a systemic problem in recent decades despite its ancient origins.

titles.title: Pilgrimage
titles.author: Ian Reader
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Pilgrimage is found in most religious cultures and pilgrimage sites around the world—including Mecca in Saudi Arabia, Lourdes in France, and Shikoku in Japan—attract millions of pilgrims annually, while a flourishing 'spiritual tourism' industry has grown to promote the practice. In the present day, new pilgrimage locations, including 'secular' ones with no official affiliation, such as Graceland, Elvis Presley's house, continue to emerge across the world. Pilgrimage: A Very Short Introduction explores the key issues and themes of pilgrimage through early history to the present, looking at its various forms, how people take part, what is learnt from the journeys, and why pilgrimage remains popular in an increasingly secular age.
titles.summaryAI: Cross-cultural study of pilgrimage from early history to the present spanning major world religions and secular contexts. Covers sacred sites including Mecca, Lourdes, Shikoku, Canterbury, and the Ganges alongside secular destinations like Graceland. Examines forms of participation, the experience of walking and physical journey, miraculous claims, and commercialization of pilgrimage. Addresses Catholic Church traditions, the Virgin Mary, spiritual tourism as an industry, and the persistence and growth of pilgrimage practices in an increasingly secular age.

titles.title: Taxation
titles.author: Stephen Smith
titles.subject: Economics
titles.abstract: Taxation is crucial to the functioning of the modern state. Tax revenues pay for public services—roads, the courts, defence, welfare assistance, and in many countries much of health care and education too. More than one third of national income in the industrialized (OECD) countries is, on average, taken in taxation. Taxes affect individuals in many ways. Taxation: A Very Short Introduction explains the role that taxes play in a modern economy and describes some of the main taxes used by most developed and developing countries. It considers the key issues: the question of where the burden of taxation really lies, how taxation affects the economy, and the effects of tax evasion and tax avoidance.
titles.summaryAI: Economics of taxation covering income tax, capital gains tax, corporation tax, sales tax, and other major tax instruments used in developed and developing countries. Examines how tax revenues fund public services including roads, courts, defense, welfare, healthcare, and education. Addresses tax burden distribution, effects of taxation on economic behavior, tax base design, and the challenges of tax avoidance and evasion. Analyzes taxation's role in modern state functioning, noting that OECD countries collect over one-third of national income through taxation.

titles.title: Crime Fiction
titles.author: Richard Bradford
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract: Crime Fiction: A Very Short Introduction explores the history of ‘crime fiction’ and the various definitions of the genre and considers how it has developed over time. Discussing the popularity of crime fiction worldwide and its various styles; the role that gender plays within the genre; spy fiction, legal dramas, and thrillers; it explores how the crime novel was shaped by the work of British and American authors in the 18th and 19th centuries. Highlighting the works of Edgar Allan Poe, Arthur Conan Doyle, Agatha Christie, and Raymond Chandler, the role of the crime novel in modern popular culture is considered and it asks whether crime fiction can be considered serious ‘literature’.
titles.summaryAI: History and development of crime fiction as a literary genre from the eighteenth century to the present. Covers foundational authors including Edgar Allan Poe, Arthur Conan Doyle, Agatha Christie, and Raymond Chandler. Examines subgenres including mystery, spy fiction, legal dramas, and thrillers. Addresses the role of gender within the genre, the influence of British and American literary traditions, Wilkie Collins's contributions, and crime fiction's place in popular culture. Considers whether crime fiction qualifies as serious literature and its global popularity.

titles.title: Microscopy
titles.author: Terence Allen
titles.subject: Science
titles.abstract: Microscopy is a dynamic area of science, incorporating both basic classroom microscopes and sophisticated research style instruments that can be driven by light, electrons, or X-rays. Microscopy: A Very Short Introduction describes the scientific principles behind the main forms of microscopy and the exciting new developments and technological advances in the field. It introduces the power of what is achievable today using microscopes and demonstrates how microscopy impinges on almost every aspect of our daily lives—from medical diagnosis to quality control in manufacture.
