titles.abstract: Molecular biology is the story of the molecules of life, their relationships, and how these interactions are controlled. Its applications are wide and growing; the power of molecular biology can now be harnessed to treat diseases, solve crimes, map human history, and produce genetically modified organisms and crops. Starting with the building blocks established by Darwin, Wallace, and Mendel, and the discovery of the structure of DNA in 1953, Molecular Biology: A Very Short Introduction considers the wide range of applications for molecular biology today, including the development of new drugs and DNA fingerprinting, and looks forward to two key areas of evolving research: personalized medicine and synthetic biology.
titles.summaryAI: Science of biological molecules and their interactions at the cellular and subcellular level. Covers the discovery of DNA structure by Watson and Crick building on work by Franklin, and foundational contributions of Darwin, Wallace, and Mendel to hereditary theory. Examines nucleic acid and protein structure, gene expression, and molecular mechanisms of cellular processes. Addresses applications including pharmaceutical drug development, DNA fingerprinting for forensic science, genetic engineering, personalized medicine, and synthetic biology. Connects genetics, biochemistry, and cell biology to medicine and biotechnology.

titles.title: Blood
titles.author: Christopher Cooper
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: Blood is vital to most animals. In mammals it transports oxygen and food, carries away waste, and contains the white cells that attack invading microbes. Playing a central role in life, blood has profound cultural and historical significance and plays an important role in religious ritual. Blood was one of the four humours in early Western medicine and is still a major diagnostic tool in the doctor’s armoury. Blood: A Very Short Introduction analyses the components of blood, explains blood groups, and looks at transfusions, blood tests, and blood-borne diseases. It considers the future, including the possibility of making artificial blood, and producing blood from stem cells in the laboratory.
titles.summaryAI: Biology and medical science of blood in mammals, covering its composition, functions, and clinical significance. Examines hemoglobin and oxygen transport, nutrient delivery, waste removal, and immune function through white blood cells. Covers blood groups, transfusion medicine, blood-borne diseases, diagnostic blood testing, and blood pressure physiology. Addresses historical and cultural significance of blood including its role in early Western humoral medicine and religious ritual. Explores future developments including artificial blood and stem cell-derived blood production. Connects physiology, immunology, and hematology to medical practice and cultural history.

titles.title: Copernicus
titles.author: Owen Gingerich
titles.subject: History of Science/Science/Biography
titles.abstract: Nicolaus Copernicus (1473–1543) was the astronomer whose shocking vision of a sun-centered universe turned out to be the essential blueprint for a physical understanding of celestial motions. Copernicus: A Very Short Introduction offers a fascinating portrayal of the man who launched the modern vision of the universe. It sets Copernicus in the context of a rapidly changing world, where the recent invention of printing with movable type not only made sources more readily available to him, but also fueled Martin Luther’s transformation of the religious landscape. Copernicus’s heliocentric revolution is revealed as an aesthetic achievement not dictated by observational “proofs,” but another new way of looking at the ancient cosmos.
titles.summaryAI: Nicolaus Copernicus (1473-1543) and the heliocentric revolution in astronomy during the Renaissance. Covers the development of the sun-centered cosmological model as an aesthetic and mathematical achievement rather than one driven by observational proof. Examines the intellectual context of early modern Europe including the impact of movable-type printing on scientific communication and Martin Luther's religious reformation. Traces influence on later astronomers including Kepler and Galileo. Connects the history of astronomy to the history of science, technology, philosophy, and the broader cultural transformation of the early modern period.

titles.title: Military Justice
titles.author: Eugene R. Fidell
titles.subject: Law
titles.abstract: Military Justice: A Very Short Introduction presents an accessible and honest assessment of the strengths and weaknesses of military justice in both common law countries and those with other legal traditions, with particular emphasis on the United States, the United Kingdom, and Canada. It integrates a description of the US military justice system with a comparative view of civilian and foreign models for the administration of justice, including the increasingly important focus on human rights. The military is the rare part of contemporary society that enjoys the privilege of policing the behavior of its own members, with special courts and a separate body of rules.
titles.summaryAI: Legal systems governing military personnel, including courts-martial, military tribunals, and separate bodies of military law. Focuses primarily on common law traditions in the United States, United Kingdom, and Canada while incorporating comparative perspectives from other legal systems. Examines the unique privilege of military self-policing, human rights considerations, terrorism-related legal challenges, and the role of institutions like the International Criminal Court. Addresses the Guantanamo Bay detention facility. Connects military law to constitutional law, international humanitarian law, and broader questions of civil-military relations and due process.

titles.title: Cognitive Neuroscience
titles.author: Richard Passingham
titles.subject: Neuroscience/Psychology
titles.abstract: Cognitive neuroscience was born in the late 1970s when the study of patients who had suffered brain damage or injury to limited parts of the brain revealed different components and the processes taking place in the brain. Cognitive Neuroscience: A Very Short Introduction outlines the nature and scope of this relatively new field and the techniques available, focusing on investigation of the human brain. It explains what brain imaging shows, points out common misconceptions, and gives a brief overview of the different aspects of human cognition: perceiving, attending, remembering, reasoning, deciding, and acting. It concludes with a discussion of the exciting advances that may lie ahead.
titles.summaryAI: Interdisciplinary study of the neural basis of mental processes, emerging in the late 1970s from brain lesion research. Covers brain imaging techniques and their interpretation, including common misconceptions about neuroimaging data. Examines the neuroscience of perception, attention, memory, reasoning, decision-making, and motor action. Analyzes how brain damage reveals distinct cognitive components and processing pathways. Connects neurology, experimental psychology, and computational approaches to understanding human cognition. Distinguishes cognitive neuroscience from behavioral psychology and philosophy of mind while addressing future research directions.

titles.title: Leibniz
titles.author: Maria Rosa Antognazza
titles.subject: Philosophy/Biography
titles.abstract: Leibniz: A Very Short Introduction discusses the philosophy of the German polymath Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (1646–1716) and his desire for systematic reform and advancement of all sciences and establishment of a stable and just political order. It introduces the fundamental principles of Leibniz’s thought and his theories of truth and knowledge. Exploring Leibniz’s contributions to logic, mathematics, physics, and metaphysics, it considers how his theories sat alongside his concerns with politics, diplomacy, and a range of practical reforms. Discussing Leibniz’s theories of possible worlds, it concludes by looking at what is ultimately real in the world we experience, and Leibniz’s response to the problem of evil through his theodicy.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophy of Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (1646-1716), German polymath who sought systematic reform across all sciences and a just political order. Covers Leibniz's fundamental principles, theories of truth and knowledge, contributions to logic and the characteristica universalis, mathematics including calculus, physics, and metaphysics. Examines the theory of possible worlds, monads, and the theodicy as a response to the problem of evil. Addresses Leibniz's engagement with politics, diplomacy, and practical reforms. Connects rationalist philosophy to early modern science, theology, and political thought.

titles.title: War and Technology
titles.author: Alex Roland
titles.subject: Military/Engineering
titles.abstract: Warfare and its technologies predate human history. Their dynamic relationship drives the transformation of war and countless realms of technological innovation. Yet even now the newest, best, and highest technology cannot guarantee victory. Rather, technology and warfare remain in a timeless dialectic, driving change without ever stabilizing a military balance of power or capping a trajectory of technological creativity. New technologies continue to push warfare in unexpected directions, while warfare pulls technology into new realms and new possibilities. War and Technology: A Very Short Introduction explores this continually evolving story from the ancestors of Homo sapiens to the age of net-centric warfare and autonomous weapons.
titles.summaryAI: Dialectical relationship between military conflict and technological innovation from prehistoric ancestors of Homo sapiens to contemporary net-centric and autonomous warfare. Covers the co-evolution of weapons systems, military tactics, and technological capabilities across land, sea, air, and digital domains. Examines nuclear weapons, information warfare, and autonomous weapons systems. Analyzes why technological superiority does not guarantee military victory and how warfare drives innovation while technology transforms combat. Connects military history to engineering, industrial development, and the broader trajectory of technological change across civilizations.

titles.title: Pandemics
titles.author: Christian W. McMillen
titles.subject: Medicine
titles.abstract: The 2014 Ebola epidemic demonstrated the power of pandemics and their ability not only to destroy lives locally but also to capture imaginations worldwide. Pandemics: A Very Short Introduction provides a concise yet comprehensive account of pandemics throughout human history, illustrating the ways in which pandemic disease—including plague, tuberculosis, malaria, smallpox, cholera, influenza, HIV/AIDS, and Covid 19—has shaped history and how human history has shaped pandemic disease. By considering the explosion of medical research and the varying state responses, such as quarantine and travel restrictions, this Very Short Introduction shows why pandemics are both interesting from a medical standpoint and provide insight into the culture and politics of their time.
titles.summaryAI: History and science of pandemic diseases across human civilization, covering plague, tuberculosis, malaria, smallpox, cholera, influenza, HIV/AIDS, and Covid-19. Examines how pandemic disease has shaped demographic, political, and social history, and how human activity has reciprocally influenced disease emergence and spread. Analyzes medical research responses, public health interventions including quarantine and travel restrictions, and varying state policies. Connects epidemiology, microbiology, and immunology to cultural attitudes, political decision-making, and international cooperation in disease control across different historical periods and geographic regions.

titles.title: Translation
titles.author: Matthew Reynolds
titles.subject: Linguistics
titles.abstract: Translation is everywhere, and matters to everybody. Translation doesn’t only give us foreign news, dubbed films, and instructions for using the microwave: without it, there would be no world religions, and our literatures, our cultures, and our languages would be unrecognizable. Translation: A Very Short Introduction provides an authoritative and thought-provoking account of the field, from ancient Chinese to World English, from St Jerome to Google Translate. It shows how translation determines meaning; how it matters in commerce, empire, conflict, and resistance; and why it is fundamental to literature and the arts.
titles.summaryAI: Theory and practice of translation across languages, cultures, and historical periods from ancient Chinese traditions to contemporary machine translation. Covers how translation determines and transforms meaning, its role in the development of world religions, literature, and cross-cultural exchange. Examines translation in commerce, empire, political conflict, and resistance movements. Addresses sign language translation and the impact of technologies from printing to automated systems. Connects linguistics, literary studies, philosophy of language, and communication theory to the practical challenges of rendering meaning across linguistic and cultural boundaries.

titles.title: Modern Italy
titles.author: Anna Cento Bull
titles.subject: Human Geography
titles.abstract: Modern Italy: A Very Short Introduction addresses the question of what modernity means to Italy and explores the extent to which modernity still represents a shared vision among Italian intellectuals, political leaders, and ordinary people. It covers the history of Italy from the Risorgimento (Resurgence), the movement leading to the Italian Unification in 1861, to the present. Italy’s political system and style of government is considered along with its economic modernization and issues with emigration, internal migration, and immigration. This volume concludes by looking at Italian culture and lifestyle, including modern art and architecture, cinema, literature, gastronomy, fashion, and sport.
titles.summaryAI: Italian history and society from the Risorgimento and 1861 unification to the present, examining modernity as a contested concept among Italian intellectuals, political leaders, and citizens. Covers political systems and governance including the Berlusconi era, economic modernization and industrial development, and patterns of emigration, internal migration, and immigration. Examines Italian cultural contributions in modern art, architecture, cinema including Fellini, literature, gastronomy, fashion, and sport. Addresses the role of the Roman Catholic Church in Italian society. Connects national identity formation to political, economic, and cultural transformation.

titles.title: Eugenics
titles.author: Philippa Levine
titles.subject: History of Science
titles.abstract: The word “eugenics” was coined in 1883 by Francis Galton to express his dream of perfecting the human race by applying the laws of genetic heredity. Eugenics: A Very Short Introduction tackles the intricate and controversial history of eugenics. It synthesizes the enormous range of policies and experiments carried out in the name of eugenics around the world throughout the twentieth century. It questions the widespread belief that eugenics disappeared after World War II and evaluates the impact of eugenics on current reproductive and genetic sciences. Charting the development of such controversial practices as artificial insemination, sperm donation, and population control, this VSI offers a timely reflection on the frequent interplay between genetics and ethics.
titles.summaryAI: History and legacy of eugenics from Francis Galton's 1883 coinage through twentieth-century global implementation and its continuing influence on reproductive and genetic sciences. Covers the range of eugenic policies including selective breeding, forced sterilization, artificial insemination, sperm donation, and population control programs carried out worldwide. Examines the relationship between genetic heredity, intelligence, race, and social engineering. Challenges the assumption that eugenics ended after World War II. Connects genetics, ethics, public policy, and human rights to ongoing debates about reproductive technology, genetic screening, and the boundaries of scientific intervention.

titles.title: Ageing
titles.author: Nancy Pachana
titles.subject: Medicine
titles.abstract: Ageing: A Very Short Introduction discusses the lifelong dynamic changes in biological, psychological, and social functioning involved in ageing. Increased lifespans have created an urgent need to find ways to enhance our functioning and well-being in the later decades of life. This is reflected in relevant policies and action plans from the WHO and the UN. Looking to the future, this VSI considers advancements in provision for our ageing populations, including revolutionary models of nursing-home care. Understanding the process of ageing is not only important for individuals but also for societies and nations if the full potential of those entering later life is to be realized.
titles.summaryAI: Biological, psychological, and social dimensions of human ageing across the lifespan, viewed through the lens of gerontology. Covers physiological changes in later life, cognitive aging, mental health including suicide risk, employment and retirement, family relationships and marriage, and institutionalization. Examines WHO and UN policy frameworks addressing aging populations and increased life expectancy. Addresses innovative nursing-home care models and strategies for enhancing well-being and functioning in later decades. Connects developmental psychology, medicine, social policy, and demographics to the challenges and opportunities of population aging.

titles.title: Asian American History
titles.author: Madeline Y. Hsu
titles.subject: History – U.S.
titles.abstract: Asian American History: A Very Short Introduction explores the complex history of Asian immigrants in the United States, from their arrival during the Gold Rush to their role in constructing the Transcontinental Railroad. Despite being perceived as highly successful and well-assimilated in modern America, Asian Americans have historically faced exclusion and discrimination. This VSI examines key moments such as the incarceration of Japanese Americans during World War II and the rise of the “model minorities” stereotype in the 1960s. It traces the evolution of their struggle for full acceptance and integration. The VSI provides a narrative interpretation of key themes within the broader history of Asian migrations to North America.
titles.summaryAI: History of Asian immigration and Asian American communities in the United States from the mid-nineteenth-century Gold Rush era to the present. Covers labor contributions including Transcontinental Railroad construction, exclusion laws, discrimination, and the World War II incarceration of Japanese Americans. Examines the emergence of the model minority stereotype in the 1960s and ongoing struggles for acceptance and integration. Traces migration patterns from multiple Asian countries to North America. Connects immigration policy, racial politics, labor history, and cultural identity to the broader narrative of American social history and civil rights.

titles.title: Home
titles.author: Michael Allen Fox
titles.subject: Philosophy/Sociology
titles.abstract: Thoughts and feelings about home traditionally provided people of all cultures with a firm sense of where they belonged, and why. But the world is rapidly changing: populations are constantly on the move, seeking better opportunities and living conditions, or an escape from violence and war. Despite this, the concept of home remains a central organizing influence in human life. Home: A Very Short Introduction considers the complex meaning of home and the essential importance of place to human psychology. It considers what a dwelling is, the variety of dwellings, and also looks at the politics of the concept of ‘home’, homelessness, refugeeism and migration, and the future of home.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophical and sociological examination of home as a concept central to human psychology, identity, and cultural belonging. Covers the nature and variety of dwellings, the meaning of place and dwelling in human experience, and the psychological significance of domestic space. Examines the politics of home including homelessness, refugee displacement, migration, and the impact of globalization on traditional place-attachment. Draws on thinkers including Bachelard and Wittgenstein. Connects philosophy, psychology, sociology, and political theory to questions of belonging, displacement, and the future of habitation in a mobile world.

titles.title: Calvinism
titles.author: Jon Balserak
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Calvinism: A Very Short Introduction explores major ideas associated with the Calvinist system of thought. Beginning during the Protestant Reformation in cities like Zurich, Geneva, and Basel, Calvinism—also known as Reformed Theology—spread rapidly throughout Europe and the New World, eventually making its way to the African Continent and the East. This VSI examines how Calvinism spread and took root, helping shape church, society, and much of contemporary thought, especially Western thought, on everything from theology to civil government, economics, the arts, work and leisure, education, and the family. The common misconceptions and objections to Calvinism are examined, along with a Calvinist understanding of God, the world, humankind, and the meaning of life.
titles.summaryAI: Reformed theological tradition originating during the Protestant Reformation in Zurich, Geneva, and Basel, spreading across Europe, the Americas, Africa, and East Asia. Covers core doctrines including covenant theology, predestination, conversion, and Calvinist understandings of God, humanity, and the world. Examines Calvinism's influence on civil government, economics, education, the arts, family life, and work and leisure. Addresses common misconceptions and objections. Connects Reformation theology to the shaping of Western political thought, social institutions, and cultural values across multiple continents and centuries.

titles.title: Measurement
titles.author: David J. Hand
titles.subject: Science
titles.abstract: Measurement is a fundamental concept central to the sciences, social sciences, medicine, economics, government, and indeed also to everyday life. The history of measurement goes back to the ancient world. Its story has been one of gradual standardization, although different types of measurement, levels of accuracy, and systems of units, apply in different contexts. Measurement: A Very Short Introduction explains the common mathematical framework underlying all measurement, the main approaches to measurement, and the challenges involved. Following a brief historical account of measurement, it discusses measurement as used in the physical sciences and engineering, the life sciences and medicine, the social and behavioural sciences, economics, business, and public policy.
titles.summaryAI: Theory and practice of measurement across scientific, social, and practical domains, from ancient origins to modern standardization. Covers the mathematical framework underlying all measurement, including ordinal and ratio scales, SI units, accuracy, and precision. Examines measurement applications in physical sciences, engineering, life sciences, medicine, behavioral and social sciences, economics, business, and public policy. Addresses challenges of standardization across different contexts and the philosophical foundations of quantification. Connects metrology to the history of science, epistemology, and the role of quantification in governance and knowledge production.

titles.title: Telescopes
titles.author: Geoffrey Cottrell
titles.subject: Science
titles.abstract: Telescopes have made dramatic revelations about the Universe and our place in it. Galileo’s observations of the Moon’s cratered surface and discovery of Jupiter’s four big satellites profoundly altered the perception of the heavens. Over the past century, the rapid development of computer technology and sophisticated materials has allowed enormous strides in telescope construction. Modern telescopes range from large Earth-based optical telescopes and radio arrays linking up across continents, to space-based telescopes capturing the Universe in infrared, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays. Telescopes: A Very Short Introduction describes the basic physics of telescopes, the challenges of overcoming turbulence and distortion from the Earth’s atmosphere, the special techniques used in space telescopes, and looks towards the new generation of telescopes.
titles.summaryAI: Optical and electromagnetic telescope design, physics, and technological evolution from Galileo's early instruments to modern space-based observatories. Covers the basic optics of lenses and mirrors, challenges of atmospheric turbulence and distortion, adaptive optics, and radio interferometry across continental baselines. Examines space telescopes operating in infrared, ultraviolet, X-ray, and gamma-ray wavelengths. Addresses advances in computer technology, detector materials, and mirror fabrication. Connects observational astronomy to physics, materials science, and engineering, and discusses the next generation of telescope technology and their implications for understanding the universe.

titles.title: Rocks
titles.author: Jan Zalasiewicz
titles.subject: Geology
titles.abstract: Rocks underpin our lives: they make up the solid structure of the Earth. We live on the rocky surface of the planet; grow our food on weathered debris derived from rocks; and we obtain nearly all of the raw materials with which we founded our civilization from rocks. Rocks: A Very Short Introduction looks at the nature and variety of rocks and the processes by which they are formed. It considers what we know about the deep rocks of the mantle and core, and what rocks can tell us about the evolution of the Earth; and it looks at rocks found in outer space and on other planets.
titles.summaryAI: Geology of rocks as the solid structural materials of Earth, covering the three major rock types: igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic. Examines rock formation processes including volcanic activity, sedimentation, and pressure-temperature transformation. Covers deep Earth geology including mantle and core composition. Analyzes what rocks reveal about Earth's evolutionary history through the geological record. Addresses extraterrestrial rocks including meteorites and planetary geology. Connects petrology and mineralogy to resource extraction, civilization development, and planetary science, spanning from practical human uses to fundamental questions about Earth and solar system origins.

titles.title: Banking
titles.author: John O. S. Wilson, John Goddard
titles.subject: Economics
titles.abstract: Banks are of central importance for economic growth, the allocation of capital, competitiveness, and financial stability. Propelled by technological advances in financial analysis and financial deregulation, the banking industry’s investment played a key role in enhancing national economic growth in the early 21st century. Banking: A Very Short Introduction explores the world of banking, describing the role of central banks in national and global economies, and analysing the increasing supervision and regulation imposed on the banking industry in light of the recent global financial crisis. Looking to the future, it considers proposals for reform of the banking industry, and the prospects of a resolution of the closely-related banking and sovereign debt crises.
titles.summaryAI: Banking systems and their role in economic growth, capital allocation, and financial stability. Covers central bank functions including monetary policy, the structure of commercial banking, and the impact of financial deregulation and technological advances on the industry. Examines the 2008 global financial crisis, its causes in excessive risk-taking and inadequate regulation, and the resulting sovereign debt crises. Analyzes evolving supervision and regulatory frameworks, proposals for banking reform, and the relationship between banking stability and national economic competitiveness. Connects monetary economics, financial regulation, and macroeconomic policy.

titles.title: Depression
titles.author: Mary Jane Tacchi, Jan Scott
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: Covering melancholia, depression, manic depression, and bipolar disorder, Depression: A Very Short Introduction gives a brief account of the history of these concepts, before focusing on the descriptions and understanding of depression and bipolar disorders today. It examines the symptoms and signs of clinical depression. It looks at the introduction of modern treatments for individuals with depression, recounting the stories behind the development of antidepressants and mood stabilizers. Exploring the importance of depression and bipolar disorder in society, it also looks at the link between mental health and economic prosperity and between creativity and mood disorders, and concludes by discussing future research and potential new treatments for those with depression.
titles.summaryAI: Clinical depression and bipolar disorder examined through historical, diagnostic, and therapeutic perspectives. Covers the evolution of concepts from melancholia to modern psychiatric classifications, symptoms and signs of depressive illness, and the distinction between unipolar depression and bipolar disorder. Examines the development of antidepressant medications and mood stabilizers. Analyzes societal impact including links between mental health and economic prosperity and between creativity and mood disorders. Addresses ongoing research into new treatments. Connects psychiatry, neuroscience, pharmacology, and public health to the understanding and management of affective disorders.

titles.title: Behavioural Economics
titles.author: Michelle Baddeley
titles.subject: Economics
titles.abstract: Behavioural Economics: A Very Short Introduction introduces the field of behavioural economics, analysing the motivations behind economic decisions and showing the relevance of behavioural economics to our own lives and everyday economic and financial problems. It explores the reasons why we make irrational decisions; how we decide quickly; why we make mistakes in risky situations; our tendency to procrastination and short-termism; and how we are affected by social influences, personality, mood, and emotions. The implications of understanding the rationale for our own financial behaviour are huge. Behavioural economics could help policy-makers to understand the people behind their policies, enabling them to design more effective policies, while also improving their marketing.
titles.summaryAI: Integration of psychological insights into economic theory, analyzing why human economic decisions deviate from rational choice models. Covers cognitive biases, heuristics for rapid decision-making, errors in risk assessment, procrastination, short-termism, and the influence of social factors, personality, mood, and emotions on financial behavior. Examines applications in public policy design, taxation, marketing, and government nudge strategies. Connects psychology, economics, and decision science to explain irrational behavior in markets, personal finance, and consumer choices, challenging classical assumptions of homo economicus.

titles.title: Weather
titles.author: Storm Dunlop
titles.subject: Science
titles.abstract: Weather: A Very Short Introduction explains what weather is, how it differs from climate, what causes weather, and how we measure it. It shows how the different properties of the atmosphere are directly related to the weather experienced on the ground, and to specific weather phenomena and extreme weather events. It describes how the global patterns of temperature and pressure give rise to the overall circulation within the atmosphere, the major wind systems, and the major oceanic currents, and how features such as mountains and the sea affect local weather. It concludes by looking at how weather observations have been used to develop increasingly sophisticated long- and short-range weather forecasting.
titles.summaryAI: Atmospheric science of weather phenomena, distinguished from long-term climate patterns. Covers the physical properties of the atmosphere including temperature, pressure, humidity, and their relationship to surface weather conditions. Examines global circulation patterns, major wind systems, oceanic currents, and local effects of topography and maritime influence. Addresses extreme weather events, El Nino oscillations, and chaos theory in atmospheric prediction. Covers the history of weather observation and the development of numerical weather prediction for both short-range and long-range forecasting. Connects meteorology to atmospheric physics and climatology.

titles.title: Zionism
titles.author: Michael Stanislawski
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Zionism: A Very Short Introduction explores the history of Zionism from the origins of modern Jewish nationalism in the 1870s to the present. While situating the history of Zionism within the wider context of Middle Eastern politics, British colonialism, and the policies of the State of Israel since its founding in 1948, this VSI focuses on its internal intellectual history and ideological developments and divides. It illuminates the development of the Zionist ideology from the appearance of Herzl to the Weizmann Era, from the creation of the state of Israel and the Six Day War of 1967 to the rise of the Peace Now movement.
titles.summaryAI: History of modern Jewish nationalism from its origins in the 1870s to the present, situated within Middle Eastern politics and British colonialism. Covers the ideological development from Herzl through the Weizmann era, the creation of the State of Israel in 1948, the Six-Day War of 1967, and the Peace Now movement. Examines internal intellectual divisions within Zionism including political, labor, religious, and revisionist strands. Addresses the relationship between Zionism, Judaism, emancipation, and the concept of Ingathering of the Exiles. Connects nationalist ideology to international diplomacy and Israeli state formation.

titles.title: Intellectual property
titles.author: Siva Vaidhyanathan
titles.subject: Law
titles.abstract: Intellectual property is the most pervasive yet least understood way we regulate expression. Despite its importance to so many aspects of the global economy and daily life, intellectual property policy remains a confusing and arcane subject. Intellectual Property: A Very Short Introduction clarifies both the basic terms and the major conflicts surrounding this area of law, offering an introduction to copyright, patents, trademarks, and other forms of knowledge that are subject to global law and regulation. It illustrates the powers and limits of intellectual property, distilling the complex tangle of laws, policies, and values governing the dissemination of ideas, expressions, inventions, creativity, and data collection in the modern world.
titles.summaryAI: Legal frameworks governing the ownership and regulation of ideas, expressions, inventions, and creative works in the global economy. Covers the major branches of intellectual property law including copyright, patents, trademarks, and related regulatory regimes. Examines the powers and limitations of intellectual property rights, conflicts between creators, consumers, and corporations, and the impact of global harmonization of IP law. Addresses challenges posed by digital technology, data collection, and international trade. Connects legal theory, economics, innovation policy, and cultural production to the governance of knowledge dissemination.

titles.title: The Industrial Revolution
titles.author: Robert C. Allen
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: The Industrial Revolution was a pivotal point in British history that occurred between the mid-18th and mid-19th centuries, and led to far reaching transformations of society. The Industrial Revolution: A Very Short Introduction analyses the key features of the Industrial Revolution in Britain, and the spread of industrialization to other countries. It considers the factors that combined to enable industrialization at this time, including Britain’s position as a global commercial empire, and discusses the changes in technology and business organization, and their impact on different social classes and groups. It looks at how the changes were reflected in evolving government policies, and what contribution these made to the economic transformation.
titles.summaryAI: Economic and social transformation in Britain from the mid-eighteenth to mid-nineteenth century, and the subsequent spread of industrialization to other countries. Covers the technological innovations of Newcomen, Watt, Hargreaves, Crompton, Cartwright, and Darby in steam power, textiles, and iron production. Examines enabling factors including Britain's global commercial empire, changes in business organization, and evolving government policies. Analyzes differential impacts on social classes and groups. Connects economic history, technology, trade, labor, and political economy to explain the origins and consequences of industrial capitalism.

titles.title: Populism
titles.author: Cas Mudde, Cristsbal Rovia Kalwasser
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: What is populism? What is the relationship between populism and democracy? Populism: A Very Short Introduction presents populism as an ideology that divides society into two antagonistic camps: the “pure people” versus the “corrupt elite,” and that privileges popular sovereignty above all else. It illustrates the practical power of this ideology by describing populist movements of the modern era—European right-wing parties, left-wing presidents in Latin America, and the Tea Party movement in the United States—and charismatic populist leaders such as Juan Domingo Péron, H. Ross Perot, Silvio Berlusconi, and Hugo Chávez. Although populism is ultimately part of democracy, populist forces constitute an increasing challenge to democratic politics.
titles.summaryAI: Political ideology dividing society into antagonistic camps of pure people versus corrupt elite, privileging popular sovereignty. Covers populist movements across regions including European right-wing parties, Latin American left-wing presidents such as Peron and Chavez, and the US Tea Party movement. Examines charismatic leadership, the relationship between populism and democracy, and populism as both a democratic corrective and a threat to democratic institutions. Analyzes populism as a thin-centered ideology adaptable to different political contexts. Connects political theory, comparative politics, and democratic governance.

titles.title: Voltaire
titles.author: Nicholas Cronk
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: Voltaire (1694–1778), best remembered as the author of Candide, is one of the central actors of the European Enlightenment. Voltaire: A Very Short Introduction explores Voltaire’s remarkable career, his most important works, and demonstrates how his thinking is pivotal to our notion and understanding of the Enlightenment. It examines the nature of Voltaire’s literary celebrity, demonstrating the extent to which his work was reactive and practical, and therefore made sense within the broader context of the debates to which he responded. It concludes by looking not only at Voltaire’s impact in literature and philosophy, but also at his influence on French political values and modern French politics.
titles.summaryAI: Francois-Marie Arouet, known as Voltaire (1694-1778), a central figure of the European Enlightenment and author of Candide. Covers his literary career, major philosophical and literary works, and his role in defining Enlightenment values. Examines the nature of Voltaire's celebrity, the reactive and practical character of his writings within contemporary intellectual debates, and his engagement with philosophy, history, and political criticism. Analyzes his lasting influence on French political values, modern French politics, and Enlightenment thought. Connects eighteenth-century literature, philosophy, and political culture.

titles.title: Gravity
titles.author: Timothy Clifton
titles.subject: Physics
titles.abstract: Gravity is one of the four fundamental interactions that exist in nature and is essential for understanding the behaviour of the Universe, and all astrophysical bodies within it. Yet it remains puzzling. Gravity: A Very Short Introduction looks at the development of our understanding of gravity since the early observations of Kepler, Newtonian theory, and Einstein’s theory of gravity. It also discusses the recent detection of waves of gravitational radiation that were predicted by Einstein. This VSI concludes by considering the testing and application of General Relativity in astrophysics and cosmology, and looks at dark energy and efforts such as string theory to combine gravity with quantum mechanics.
titles.summaryAI: Gravitational force as one of four fundamental interactions in nature, essential for understanding astrophysical and cosmological phenomena. Covers the historical development from Kepler's laws through Newtonian gravity to Einstein's general relativity. Examines the recent detection of gravitational waves, testing and application of general relativity in astrophysics and cosmology, and the mystery of dark energy. Addresses efforts to unify gravity with quantum mechanics through string theory and other approaches. Connects classical mechanics, relativistic physics, and quantum theory to observational cosmology and fundamental physics.

titles.title: Animal Behaviour
titles.author: Tristram D. Wyatt
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: How animals behave is crucial to their survival and reproduction. Animal Behaviour: A Very Short Introduction discusses how animal behaviour has evolved, how behaviours develop in each individual (considering the interplay of genes, epigenetics, and experience), how we can understand animal societies, and how we can explain collective behaviour such as swirling flocks of starlings. The application of new molecular tools, such as DNA fingerprinting and genomics, and developments in computing and image analysis are causing a revolution in the study of animal behaviour. Combining these methods with field studies, it looks at mammals, butterflies, honeybees, fish, and birds, analysing what drives behaviour, and exploring instinct, learning, and culture.
titles.summaryAI: Scientific study of how animal behavior evolves, develops, and functions in relation to survival and reproduction. Covers the interplay of genes, epigenetics, and experience in behavioral development, examining instinct, learning, and cultural transmission. Analyzes social behavior, kin selection, parental care, alarm calls, and collective phenomena such as flocking. Examines diverse taxa including mammals, butterflies, honeybees, fish, and birds. Addresses the revolution in methodology through DNA fingerprinting, genomics, and computational image analysis. Connects evolutionary biology, ecology, genetics, and neuroscience to field and laboratory behavioral research.

titles.title: Navigation
titles.author: Jim Bennett
titles.subject: Science/History
titles.abstract: Navigation: A Very Short Introduction looks at the history of navigation, including the distinctive cultures of early navigation. It shows how the adoption of mathematical methods, use of instruments, writing of textbooks, and publication of charts combined to create a more standardized practice. Methods such as longitude-finding by chronometer and lunar distance were complemented by recording courses and reckoning position ‘by account’. This VSI also introduces the incredible array of instruments relied on by sailors, from astrolabes, sextants, and chronometers, to more modern radio receivers and electronic equipment, highlighting the crucial role played by the endeavour and resourcefulness of mathematicians, scientists, and seamen in finding their way at sea.
titles.summaryAI: History and science of finding position and course at sea, from early cultural navigation practices through the mathematization of the discipline during the Renaissance. Covers the development of navigational instruments including magnetic compasses, astrolabes, sextants, chronometers, and modern radio and electronic equipment. Examines longitude-finding methods including chronometer and lunar distance techniques, dead reckoning, chart production, and textbook standardization. Connects mathematics, astronomy, physics, and practical seamanship to the broader history of exploration, scientific instrumentation, and technological innovation in maritime commerce and discovery.

titles.title: The Habsburg Empire
titles.author: Martyn Rady
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: From the 13th to the 20th centuries, Habsburgs ruled much of Central Europe, and for two centuries were rulers of Spain. Through Spain, they acquired lands around the Mediterranean and part of the New World, spreading eastwards to include the Philippines. Reaching from South-East Asia to what is now Ukraine, the Habsburg Empire was truly global. The Habsburg Empire: A Very Short Introduction looks at the history of the Habsburgs from their 10th-century origins in Switzerland, to the dissolution of the Habsburg Empire in 1918. It introduces the pantheon of Habsburg rulers and discusses the lands and kingdoms that made up the Empire and the moments that shaped their history.
titles.summaryAI: Habsburg dynasty and its territories from tenth-century Swiss origins through dissolution in 1918, spanning Central Europe, Spain, the Mediterranean, the Americas, and Southeast Asia including the Philippines. Covers key rulers including Maximilian I and Charles II, major territorial acquisitions and losses, and defining historical moments. Examines the multi-ethnic, multi-linguistic character of Habsburg governance across diverse kingdoms and lands. Connects European dynastic politics, imperial administration, religious conflict, and national identity to the broader history of European state formation and global empire from the medieval through modern periods.

titles.title: The Future
titles.author: Jennifer Gidley
titles.subject: Futures Studies
titles.abstract: Humans have always been driven by both a fear of the unknown and a curiosity to know. They have prophesied, foretold, predicted, and tried to control the future. The Future: A Very Short Introduction considers some of our most burning questions: What is ‘the future’? Is there only one future or are there many possible futures? It introduces the exciting field of future studies, spanning social, cultural, and environmental innovations, as well as technological advances. It asks if the future can ever be truly predicted or if we create our own futures by our thoughts, feelings, and actions. The book concludes by exploring the grand global futures challenges.
titles.summaryAI: Interdisciplinary field of futures studies examining how humans envision, predict, and create possible futures. Covers the historical evolution of prophecy, prediction, and futurism from early human societies to modern systematic approaches. Examines social, cultural, environmental, and technological dimensions of future-oriented thinking. Addresses questions of agency and determinism, whether futures can be predicted or are shaped by human thought and action, and grand global challenges including ecology, migration, population, and evolution. Connects philosophy, social science, technology assessment, and strategic planning to the study of alternative future scenarios.

titles.title: Circadian Rhythms
titles.author: Russell Foster, Leon Kreitzman
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: The Earth’s daily rotation affects almost every living creature. From dawn through to dusk, there are changes in light, temperature, humidity, and rainfall. However, these changes are regular, rhythmic, and therefore predictable. Thus, the near 24-hour circadian rhythm is innate: a genetically programmed clock. Circadian Rhythms: A Very Short Introduction explains how organisms can ‘know’ the time and reveals what we now understand of the nature and operation of chronobiological processes. Covering variables such as light, the metabolism, human health, and the seasons, it illustrates how jet lag and shift work can impact on human well-being, and considers circadian rhythms alongside a wide range of disorders, from schizophrenia to obesity.
titles.summaryAI: Biological timekeeping systems governing near-24-hour cycles in living organisms, driven by genetically programmed molecular clocks. Covers the entrainment of circadian rhythms by light and other environmental cues, the molecular mechanisms of biological oscillators, and the relationship between circadian timing and metabolism, sleep, and seasonal adaptation. Examines health consequences of circadian disruption including jet lag, shift work, and associations with disorders such as schizophrenia and obesity. Connects chronobiology, genetics, neuroscience, and evolutionary biology to medical understanding of human health and disease.

titles.title: The Atmosphere
titles.author: Paul Palmer
titles.subject: Science
titles.abstract: The atmosphere is the thin, diffuse fluid that envelops the Earth’s surface. Despite its apparent fragility, the existence of this fluid is vital for human and other life on Earth. The Atmosphere: A Very Short Introduction describes the physical and chemical characteristics of different layers in the atmosphere, and shows how the atmosphere’s interactions with land, ocean, and ice affect these properties. It also looks at how movement in the atmosphere, driven by heat from the Sun, transports heat from lower latitudes to higher latitudes. Finally, it presents an overview of the types of measurements used to understand different parts of the atmosphere, and identifies future challenges in the light of climate change.
titles.summaryAI: Physical and chemical properties of Earth's atmospheric layers and their interactions with land, ocean, and ice surfaces. Covers atmospheric composition, the greenhouse effect, electromagnetic radiation absorption and emission, and heat transport from lower to higher latitudes driven by solar energy. Examines the relationship between atmospheric dynamics and weather and climate systems. Addresses measurement techniques including remote sensing and in situ observations used to monitor atmospheric properties. Discusses the tragedy of the commons as applied to atmospheric pollution and identifies future challenges posed by climate change.

titles.title: Infinity
titles.author: Ian Stewart
titles.subject: Mathematics
titles.abstract: Infinity has connections to philosophy, religion, and physics as well as mathematics. The infinitely large (infinite) is intimately related to the infinitely small (infinitesimal). Infinity: A Very Short Introduction explains the mathematical concept of infinity, its different forms, and its uses in calculus, Fourier analysis, and fractals, and also describes the philosophical aspects and debates involving infinity. It argues that working with infinity is not just an abstract, intellectual exercise, but that it is instead a concept with important practical everyday applications, and considers how mathematicians use infinity and infinitesimals to answer questions or supply techniques that do not appear to involve the infinite.
titles.summaryAI: Mathematical concept of infinity in its various forms and its relationship to the infinitely small or infinitesimal. Covers the use of infinite and infinitesimal quantities in calculus, Fourier analysis, and fractal geometry. Examines philosophical debates about the nature of infinity including paradoxes and connections to cosmology and physics. Addresses how mathematicians rigorously work with infinite sets, sequences, and processes to solve practical problems that do not explicitly involve infinity. Connects pure mathematics, philosophy of mathematics, and mathematical physics to demonstrate the practical significance of infinite reasoning.

titles.title: Organic Chemistry
titles.author: Graham Patrick
titles.subject: Chemistry
titles.abstract: Organic chemistry is the chemistry of compounds of carbon. As well as being central to life, in the form of large molecules such as nucleic acids and proteins, organic compounds are essential to many areas of industry. Organic Chemistry: A Very Short Introduction covers the whole range of organic compounds and their roles. Beginning with the structures and properties of the basic groups of organic compounds, it goes on to consider organic compounds in the areas of pharmaceuticals, polymers, food and drink, petrochemicals, and nanotechnology. It explores how new materials, such as graphene, are opening up exciting new possibilities for applications, and also discusses the particular challenges of working with carbon compounds, many of which are colourless.
titles.summaryAI: Chemistry of carbon-containing compounds, fundamental to both living systems and industrial applications. Covers the structures and properties of major organic compound classes, bonding theory, functional groups, and reaction mechanisms. Examines applications in pharmaceuticals, polymer science, food and beverage chemistry, petrochemicals, and nanotechnology including graphene. Addresses analytical and synthetic techniques for working with carbon compounds, including challenges posed by the colorless nature of many organic substances. Connects biochemistry, materials science, medicinal chemistry, and industrial chemistry through the central role of carbon in molecular diversity.

titles.title: Clinical Psychology
titles.author: Susan Llewellyn, Katie Aafjes-van Doorn
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: Clinical psychology makes a significant contribution to mental healthcare worldwide. Clinical Psychology: A Very Short Introduction provides insights into the world of clinical psychologists and their clients or patients, and covers the range of domains of practice, difficulties tackled, and approaches and models used. It considers the challenges and controversies facing the profession today, and also how it varies across the globe. Finally, it discusses key questions surrounding clinical psychology, such as whether it should compete or collaborate with psychiatry; how far it is yet another instrument of social control; what new technology can offer in the future; and whether clinical psychology can ever really be considered a science.
titles.summaryAI: Clinical psychology as a discipline addressing mental health assessment, diagnosis, and treatment across diverse populations worldwide. Covers major therapeutic approaches including cognitive therapy, behaviour therapy, family therapy, and psychoanalytic methods rooted in Freudian theory. Addresses conditions such as post-traumatic stress disorder and brain damage rehabilitation. Examines the professional relationship between clinical psychology and psychiatry, debates over clinical psychology as scientific practice versus social control, cross-cultural variations in practice, and emerging roles of technology in mental healthcare delivery and research methodology.

titles.title: Shakespeare's Tragedies
titles.author: Stanley Wells
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract: Tragedy, including grief, pain, and suffering, is a common theme in Shakespeare’s plays, often leading to the death of at least one character. Yet such themes can also be found in Shakespearian plays which are classed as comedies or histories. What is it that makes a Shakespearian tragedy, and what dramatic themes and conventions did the bard draw upon when writing them? Shakespeare’s Tragedies: A Very Short Introduction considers what is meant by the word ‘tragedy’, offering a critical analysis of nine of Shakespeare’s iconic tragic plays. It concludes by discussing why tragedy is regarded as a fit subject for entertainment, and why audiences find tragic plays so enjoyable.
titles.summaryAI: Critical analysis of nine Shakespearean tragedies including Romeo and Juliet, Julius Caesar, King Lear, Antony and Cleopatra, Titus Andronicus, Timon of Athens, and Troilus and Cressida. Examines dramatic conventions, themes of grief, suffering, and death that define tragic genre versus comedy and history plays. Considers theatrical performance traditions through actors such as Laurence Olivier and Ian McKellen. Explores classical and Renaissance dramatic sources, the aesthetics of tragic entertainment, and why audiences derive pleasure from witnessing tragic narratives on stage.

titles.title: Military Strategy
titles.author: Antulio Joseph Echevarria
titles.subject: Military
titles.abstract: Military strategy plays a vital role in every conflict. Inspired by the great military theoreticians Sun Tzu, Niccolò Machiavelli, and Carl von Clausewitz, Military Strategy: A Very Short Introduction highlights the dynamic relationship among the principal components of strategy: purpose, method, and means. It draws on examples such as Hannibal’s war against Rome, Napoleon’s victory at Austerlitz, the Allies’ campaign to overwhelm Hitler’s Fortress Europe, and the terror attacks of September 11, 2001, to illustrate the factors contributing to the success or failure of military strategy. The major types of military strategy and their advantages and disadvantages are described: annihilation and dislocation, attrition and exhaustion, deterrence and coercion, terror and terrorism, targeted killing, and cyber power.
titles.summaryAI: Military strategy across historical periods from ancient to contemporary warfare. Examines the interplay of purpose, method, and means drawing on theorists Sun Tzu, Machiavelli, and Clausewitz. Covers major strategy types: annihilation, dislocation, attrition, exhaustion, deterrence, coercion, terror, targeted killing, and cyber warfare. Illustrates principles through cases including Hannibal against Rome, Napoleon at Austerlitz, Allied campaigns against Nazi-held Europe, and the September 11 attacks. Distinguishes conventional military strategy from asymmetric and terrorist approaches, addressing factors determining strategic success or failure across different conflict environments.

titles.title: European Union Law
titles.author: Anthony Arnull
titles.subject: Law
titles.abstract: European Law: A Very Short Introduction considers the laws and legal system of the European Union and discusses the range of issues that the European Union has been given the power to regulate, including the free movement of goods and people. It considers why an organization based on international treaties has proved capable of having far-reaching effects on both its Member States and on countries that lie beyond its borders, and discusses the effectiveness of its law and legal system in ensuring that Member States respect the commitments they made when they signed the Treaties.
titles.summaryAI: Legal framework of the European Union, grounded in international treaties that established a supranational legal system with far-reaching effects on member states and beyond. Covers free movement of goods and people, the scope of EU regulatory power, and enforcement mechanisms ensuring treaty compliance. Examines why treaty-based law has achieved such deep penetration into domestic legal systems, the role of EU courts in interpreting and applying law, and the relationship between EU law and national legal orders. Addresses the effectiveness of the EU legal system in holding member states accountable.

titles.title: Multilingualism
titles.author: John C. Maher
titles.subject: Linguistics
titles.abstract: Multilingualism—language diversity in society—is a perfect expression of human plurality. About 6,500–7,000 languages are spoken, written, and signed, throughout the linguistic landscape of the world, by people who communicate in more than one language. Languages are instruments for interacting with the cultural environment and their ecology is complex. Multilingualism: A Very Short Introduction shows how multilingualism offers cultural diversity and complex identities, and plays a part in issues of nationalism and regional rights. It considers multilingualism in the context of globalization and the fate of many endangered languages as they disappear from the world. Why do some languages thrive, but others decline?
titles.summaryAI: Language diversity in societies worldwide, encompassing approximately 6,500 to 7,000 spoken, written, and signed languages. Covers how multilingual individuals navigate cultural environments, the complex ecology of language contact and coexistence, and the relationship between multilingualism and identity formation. Addresses nationalism, regional language rights, and globalization pressures on linguistic diversity. Examines endangered language decline and extinction, factors determining why some languages thrive while others disappear, and the sociolinguistic landscape shaped by migration, colonialism, and political boundaries. Engages with theoretical perspectives from Habermas, Jakobson, and Wittgenstein.

titles.title: Jewish History
titles.author: David N. Myers
titles.subject: History
keywords: Protocols of the Elders of Zion
titles.abstract: For millennia, Jews have defied odds by overcoming the travails of exile, persecution, and recurring plans for their annihilation. Jewish History: A Very Short Introduction points to two unlikely factors to explain the survival of the Jews: antisemitism and assimilation. Usually regarded as grave dangers, these two factors have continually interacted with one other to enable the persistence of the Jews. At every turn in their history, Jews have adapted to new environments, cultures, languages, and social norms through a process of assimilation, but full assimilation has been checked by the nearly constant presence of hatred toward the Jew. Antisemitism has affirmed the Jews’ sense of difference from the host society.
titles.summaryAI: Jewish historical experience spanning millennia, from ancient origins through exile, persecution, and survival in diaspora communities. Central argument that antisemitism and assimilation paradoxically interacted to sustain Jewish identity: assimilation enabled adaptation to new cultures, languages, and social norms, while persistent hatred reinforced distinctiveness from host societies. Covers key episodes including biblical foundations, the Ark of the Covenant, blood libel accusations, the Protocols of the Elders of Zion, and the emergence of Zionism through Theodor Herzl. Addresses historiographic approaches including Nachman Krochmal and evolving interpretations of Jewish continuity.

titles.title: Branding
titles.author: Robert Jones
titles.subject: Business
titles.abstract: Branding: A Very Short Introduction discusses the rising omnipresence of brands, and analyses how they work their magic. It considers the incredible potency of brands as a commercial, social, and cultural force, and looks at the many different kinds of brands that exist—from products, services, and artistic properties, to companies, charities, sports clubs, and political parties. Defining what we mean by the word ‘brand’, this VSI explores both the positive and negative aspects of brands. Finally, it considers the business of branding, and asks whether the idea of brands and branding is starting to decline, or whether it has a long future ahead.
titles.summaryAI: Brands as commercial, social, and cultural forces encompassing products, services, companies, charities, sports clubs, political parties, and artistic properties. Examines how brands function psychologically and culturally, their growing omnipresence in contemporary life, and the mechanisms through which brand identity and loyalty are constructed. Covers positive and negative dimensions including consumer empowerment, ethical concerns, data-driven marketing, and internet-era brand dynamics. Addresses the business practices of brand management, ownership, and media strategy, and evaluates whether branding as a concept faces decline or continued expansion in the digital marketplace.

titles.title: Pain
titles.author: Rob Boddice
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: What is pain? How is pain related to emotions, culture, and pleasure? What happens to us when we feel pain? Pain: A Very Short Introduction explores the history, culture, and medical science of pain. It charts the shifting meanings of pain across time and space, with the changing experience and treatment of pain. It describes historical hierarchies of pain experience related to social class and race, and the privileging of human pain over that of other animals. This VSI weaves a multifaceted account of pain, from pain concepts of classical antiquity to expressions of pain in contemporary art, and modern medical approaches to understanding, treating, and managing pain.
titles.summaryAI: Pain examined through intersecting lenses of history, culture, medical science, and art from classical antiquity to the present. Covers the neuroscience and physiology of pain perception alongside shifting cultural meanings across time and geography. Addresses historical hierarchies linking pain experience to social class and race, the privileging of human pain over animal suffering, and relationships between pain and emotion, pain and pleasure. Examines evolving treatment approaches including modern pain management, pharmacology, and clinical practice. Explores pain representation in contemporary art and philosophical questions about subjective pain experience.

titles.title: Oceans
titles.author: Dorrik Stow
titles.subject: Geography
titles.abstract: The oceans are critical for life on Earth. They are vital for the regulation of climate, and with global warming and decreasing land area they have become increasingly important as a source of food and of energy in the form of oil and gas, and for their mineral wealth. Oceans also form a key part of the biogeochemical cycles of carbon, nitrogen, and other elements essential to life. Oceans: A Very Short Introduction analyses these important components and considers their relationship with, and exploitation by, humans. It also explores how oceans originate, evolve, and change, including the physical processes that are active within their waters, and the shape and nature of the seafloor.
titles.summaryAI: Ocean science covering physical, chemical, biological, and geological dimensions of marine systems. Addresses ocean origins, evolution, and structural change including seafloor topography and tectonic processes. Covers ocean roles in climate regulation, biogeochemical cycling of carbon and nitrogen, and their importance as sources of food, oil, gas, and mineral resources. Examines physical processes within ocean waters including currents, tides, and wave dynamics. Addresses human exploitation of marine environments, the impacts of global warming on ocean systems, and the relationship between decreasing land area and increasing oceanic resource dependence.

titles.title: Utilitarianism
titles.author: Katarzyna de Lazari-Radek, Peter Singer
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Utilitarianism: A Very Short Introduction describes the nature, varieties, and justifications of utilitarianism, from its origins to arguments that draw on current research in neuroscience. Utilitarianism remains as influential—and controversial—as it was when Mill drew on it to argue for equality for women. It is often rejected because it seems to clash with widely accepted moral views. Could utilitarianism justify torture? Is only pleasure or happiness of intrinsic value? This VSI presents the strengths of utilitarian ethics and its challenge to our thinking on such issues as global poverty, speciesism, climate change, reducing the risk of human extinction, and end-of-life decisions for terminally ill patients.
titles.summaryAI: Utilitarian ethical theory from its classical foundations through contemporary developments informed by neuroscience research. Covers varieties of utilitarianism including act and rule-based approaches, the role of pleasure and happiness as intrinsic values, and justifications for utilitarian reasoning. Examines persistent controversies including whether utilitarianism could justify torture, and applications to global poverty, speciesism, climate change, human extinction risk, and end-of-life medical decisions. Addresses Mill's arguments for gender equality as historically grounded in utilitarian logic. Distinguishes utilitarianism from competing moral frameworks and evaluates common objections.

titles.title: Freemasonry
titles.author: Andreas Önnerfors
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: Freemasonry is one of the world’s oldest, most widespread voluntary organizations. With a strong sense of liberation, moral enlightenment, cosmopolitan openness, and forward-looking philanthropy, freemasonry has attracted some of the sharpest minds in history and created a strong platform for nascent civil societies worldwide. With the secrecy of internally communicated knowledge, its clandestine character, the enactment of rituals, and elaborate use of symbols, freemasonry has also opened up feelings of distrust, along with allegations of secretiveness and conspiracy. Freemasonry: A Very Short Introduction introduces the organization, rituals, and symbols of freemasonry, navigating through the prevalent fictions and conspiracy theories. It also sheds light on the participation of women in freemasonry.
titles.summaryAI: Freemasonry as a worldwide voluntary organization from its origins to the present, covering its rituals, symbols, and internally communicated knowledge systems. Examines its Enlightenment-era values of moral improvement, cosmopolitan openness, and philanthropy, attracting influential historical figures. Addresses the clandestine character generating distrust, conspiracy theories, and allegations of secretiveness. Covers freemasonry's interaction with Christianity, its role in nascent civil societies, its relationship to fascism and World War I era politics, its presence in Africa and global contexts, women's participation, and its representation in popular culture.

titles.title: Heredity
titles.author: John Waller
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: The idea of heredity—that qualities of body and mind are somehow inherited from one’s parents—has profoundly shaped many aspects of the human experience: from our attempts to understand variation in personality and intelligence, to popular attitudes about gender, race, and social hierarchy, to the methods employed to increase crop yields and the value of horses and cattle. Heredity: A Very Short Introduction traces both the technical study of biological inheritance and the ideological use of the concept of heredity from antiquity into a modern age of molecular biology in which a brave new prospect is emerging: the capacity to manipulate the human genome itself.
titles.summaryAI: Biological inheritance from ancient conceptions of heredity through modern molecular biology and genomics. Traces how ideas about inherited traits shaped understandings of personality, intelligence, gender, race, and social hierarchy across cultures and historical periods. Covers selective breeding of crops, horses, and cattle as practical applications. Examines the transition from pre-scientific seed theories to Mendelian genetics, chromosomal theory, and contemporary genome manipulation. Addresses ideological uses of heredity concepts to justify social stratification and racial categories, alongside emerging ethical questions about human genetic engineering capabilities.

titles.title: Thinking and Reasoning
titles.author: Jonathan Evans
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: Thinking and Reasoning: A Very Short Introduction explores cognitive psychological approaches to understanding the nature of thinking and reasoning, problem solving, and decision making. It shows how humans’ unique problem-solving capabilities are hugely dependent on having the imagination to ask the right questions, and the ability to see things from a completely new perspective. Beginning by considering the approaches of the behaviourists and the gestalt psychologists, it moves on to modern explorations of thinking, including hypothetical thinking, conditionals, deduction, rationality, and intuition. Covering the role of past learning, IQ, and cognitive biases, this VSI also discusses the idea that there may be two different ways of thinking, arising from our evolutionary history.
titles.summaryAI: Cognitive psychology of human thinking, reasoning, problem solving, and decision making. Covers historical approaches from behaviourism and gestalt psychology to modern cognitive science. Examines hypothetical thinking, conditional reasoning, deductive logic, rationality, and the role of intuition. Addresses dual-process theory proposing two distinct thinking systems shaped by evolutionary history. Covers cognitive biases, the influence of prior learning on judgment, the relationship between intelligence (IQ) and reasoning ability, and how imagination and perspective-shifting enable creative problem solving. Distinguishes analytical from heuristic processing in human cognition.

titles.title: Shakespeare's Sonnets and Poems
titles.author: Jonathan F. S. Post
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract: Shakespeare’s Sonnets and Poems: A Very Short Introduction introduces all of Shakespeare’s poetry: the Sonnets; the two great narrative poems, Venus and Adonis and The Rape of Lucrece; A Lover’s Complaint; and ‘The Phoenix and Turtle’. Describing Shakespeare’s double identity as both poet and playwright, in conjunction with several of his contemporaries, it evaluates the reciprocal advantages as well as the different strategies and strains that came with writing for the stage and the page. Exploring their reception, both with contemporary audiences and through the ages until today, this VSI explores the core themes of love and lust, and analyses how the sonnets compare with other great love poetry of the English Renaissance.
titles.summaryAI: Shakespeare's complete poetic works including the Sonnets, Venus and Adonis, The Rape of Lucrece, A Lover's Complaint, and The Phoenix and Turtle. Examines Shakespeare's dual identity as poet and playwright, analyzing reciprocal influences between stage and page composition. Situates the poetry among English Renaissance contemporaries including Samuel Daniel and Michael Drayton. Covers core themes of love, lust, desire, and their treatment in sonnet form compared to other Renaissance love poetry. Traces reception history from Elizabethan audiences through later critical traditions including Emerson's readings.

titles.title: Mammals
titles.author: T. S. Kemp
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: Mammals: A Very Short Introduction explores the nature, evolutionary history, and modern diversity of mammals. From a little shrew-like, nocturnal, insect-eating ancestor living 200 million years ago (mya), mammals have evolved into a huge variety of different kinds of animals. This VSI explains how it is endothermy—‘warm-bloodedness’—enabling high levels of activity and the relatively large brain associated with complex, adaptable behaviour that epitomizes mammals. It describes their remarkable fossil record, revealing how and when the mammals gained their characteristics, and the tortuous course of their evolution. It reveals the adaptations mammals evolved to suit their varied modes of life, including those of mainly arboreal primates culminating in Homo sapiens.
titles.summaryAI: Mammalian biology, evolutionary history, and modern diversity from a 200-million-year-old insectivorous ancestor to present-day variety. Covers endothermy as the defining adaptation enabling sustained activity and large brain development supporting complex adaptive behaviour. Examines the mammalian fossil record revealing acquisition of key characteristics and tortuous evolutionary pathways. Addresses major adaptive radiations and ecological specializations including terrestrial, aquatic, aerial, and arboreal modes of life. Traces primate evolution culminating in Homo sapiens. Covers mammalian taxonomy, reproductive strategies, and the relationship between morphological diversity and environmental adaptation.

titles.title: Environmental Law
titles.author: Elizabeth Fisher
titles.subject: Law
titles.abstract: Environmental law is the law concerned with environmental problems. It is a vast area of law that operates from the local to the global, involving a range of different legal and regulatory techniques. In theory, environmental protection is obvious and ethically desirable. Yet, in practice, environmental law is a messy and complex business fraught with conflict. Environmental Law: A Very Short Introduction discusses the nature and practice of environmental law, and explores the role of lawmakers, courts, and regulators. It analyses why environmental law is both a fundamental and controversial area of law, dealing with multiple interests, socio-political conflicts, and the limits of knowledge about the environment, using examples from across the globe.
titles.summaryAI: Environmental law operating from local to global scales, addressing regulatory frameworks for environmental protection and resource management. Covers the roles of lawmakers, courts, and regulators in developing and enforcing environmental standards. Examines legal and regulatory techniques including command-and-control regulation, market-based instruments, and international environmental agreements. Addresses conflicts between economic development and environmental protection, the tragedy of the commons, and challenges posed by scientific uncertainty about environmental impacts. Uses global case studies to illustrate how environmental law navigates competing stakeholder interests and socio-political disputes in the Anthropocene era.

titles.title: Projects
titles.author: Andrew Davies
titles.subject: Business
titles.abstract: What is a project? How is it organized? Projects: A Very Short Introduction looks at how projects have developed since the industrial revolution to create the human-built world in which we live, work, and play. Considering some of our greatest endeavours—such as the Erie Canal, Apollo Moon landing, and Chinese eco-city projects—it identifies how projects are organized and managed to design and produce large and complex systems, cope with fast changing conditions, and deal with the immense uncertainties required to create breakthrough innovations in products and services. It concludes by considering how projects could be organized to address the challenges facing the post-industrial society of the 21st century.
titles.summaryAI: Project organization and management from the industrial revolution to contemporary practice.
