The late 20th century saw a cultural explosion and the emergence of distinct nationalisms within Canada, particularly Quebec. Canada’s proximity to America has necessitated a diplomatic approach but Canada has used its peacekeeping strengths to further its position as a world power. Canada’s lack of a single identity is arguably a strength, not a weakness.
titles.summaryAI: Canadian history from early European settlement to the contemporary era, covering political development, cultural diversity, and national identity formation. Examines linguistic, ethnic, and religious pluralism as defining features. Addresses the impact of industrialization and development on indigenous peoples, the late twentieth-century cultural explosion, and distinct nationalisms including Quebec separatism. Covers Canada's diplomatic relationship with the United States, its peacekeeping role in international affairs, and its emergence as a world power. Argues that the absence of a single national identity constitutes a strength.

titles.title: French Philosophy
titles.author: Stephen Gaukroger, Knox Peden
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: French Philosophy: A Very Short Introduction outlines the history of French philosophy, summarising important thinkers and texts. Philosophy has played a significant role in French culture since the early modern period, and French philosophy has influenced the Western world for centuries. The Enlightenment was central to the relationship between philosophy and politics. Nineteenth-century French philosophy was focused on ideas of science and progress. In the twentieth century, existentialist ideas took hold, followed by an embrace of structuralism which led to post-structuralism and deconstruction. French philosophy is interwoven with political, literary, and scientific debates of the time, and ongoing conflicts between religious and secular beliefs.
titles.summaryAI: History of French philosophical thought from the early modern period through the twentieth century. Covers Montaigne, Enlightenment thinkers, and the relationship between philosophy and politics. Examines nineteenth-century focus on science and progress, twentieth-century existentialism, structuralism, post-structuralism, and deconstruction. Addresses how French philosophy interweaves with political, literary, and scientific debates and ongoing tensions between religious and secular worldviews. Highlights France's distinctive philosophical contributions to Western intellectual culture and the interplay between philosophical movements and broader cultural transformations.

titles.title: The Russian Economy
titles.author: Richard Connolly
titles.subject: Economics
titles.abstract: The Russian Economy: A Very Short Introduction looks at the historical roots of an economy dominated by the state and shaped by a need for security. The Soviet Union’s centrally planned economic system enabled industrialization, urbanization, and military success, but at what human cost? The transition to a market-based system in the late 20th century was difficult, and only partially successful. From the millennium onwards, Vladimir Putin’s economic policy emerged as a hybrid of state- and market-controlled approaches. Russia has been criticized for overdependence on natural resources and armaments. However, if some world powers can combine state control and a profile in the global markets, why not Russia?
titles.summaryAI: Russian economic development from the Soviet centrally planned system through post-Soviet transition to the contemporary hybrid economy under Vladimir Putin. Covers industrialization, urbanization, and military-industrial growth under Soviet planning, including human costs. Examines the difficult and partial transition to market economics in the 1990s. Addresses Russia's dependence on natural resources and armaments, state-controlled versus market-driven approaches, and Russia's position among world powers. Explores whether combining state economic control with global market participation represents a viable development model.

titles.title: Biogeography
titles.author: Mark V. Lomolino
titles.subject: Geography
titles.abstract: Biogeography: A Very Short Introduction explains how our ability to place life in an explicit geographic context is key to understanding our natural world. The geological evolution of Earth has fundamentally influenced its life forms. Biogeography brings together insights from the fields of genetics, geology, paleontology, geography, anthropology, meteorology, oceanography, and ecology to demonstrate how the geological and evolutionary histories of our planet are interconnected. The tools of biogeography are essential for us to develop effective strategies for conserving life across our planet. Through examining the diversity and locations of life forms, biogeography provides an unparalleled opportunity to explore the “great lessons of the earth.”
titles.summaryAI: The study of geographic distribution of life forms across Earth, integrating genetics, geology, paleontology, geography, anthropology, meteorology, oceanography, and ecology. Examines how geological evolution has fundamentally shaped biodiversity patterns. Covers island biogeography, continental drift effects on species distribution, and interconnections between evolutionary and geological histories. Addresses conservation applications, demonstrating how biogeographic tools inform strategies for preserving biodiversity. Explores the diversity and spatial patterns of organisms to understand ecological and evolutionary processes at planetary scales.

titles.title: The Ghetto
titles.author: Bryan Cheyette
titles.subject: History/Human Geography
titles.abstract: The Ghetto: A Very Short Introduction provides an overview of the history of the ghetto, focusing on specific times and places throughout history. Is the ghetto real or imagined? The word ‘ghetto’ would not have existed without 16th-century Italian economic interests, which led to the policy of placing the Jewish population in enclosed enclaves in around twenty-four Italian towns and cities. During the Holocaust, there were many hundreds of Nazi ghettos in Eastern Europe varying in size, duration, and purpose. The history of the ghetto was adopted by African-Americans in the mid-20th century and is still used to describe both the concrete and abstract qualities of segregated urban life. There is no single idea or place which encompasses ‘the ghetto’.
titles.summaryAI: History of the ghetto as both a physical space and a conceptual category from sixteenth-century Italy to the present. Covers the origins in Italian economic policies confining Jewish populations to enclosed enclaves in approximately twenty-four towns. Examines the hundreds of Nazi ghettos in Eastern Europe during the Holocaust, varying in size, duration, and purpose. Addresses the adoption of ghetto terminology by African Americans in the mid-twentieth century to describe segregated urban conditions. Explores the ghetto as simultaneously real and imagined, encompassing concrete and abstract dimensions of urban segregation.

titles.title: Ecology
titles.author: Jaboury Ghazoul
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: Ecology is the science of how organisms interact with each other and their environment to form communities and ecosystems. Ecology: A Very Short Introduction explains the history of ecology, the principles of ecological thinking, how ecology affects our everyday lives, and how it guides environmental decisions, especially in the light of current and future environmental challenges. What are the factors behind ‘boom and bust’ cycles in species populations? How and why do two species cooperate? Do humans need so many species? The cultural significance of ecology is also explored, with examples of different schools of thought that envisage ecology as a science and a worldview.
titles.summaryAI: The science of organism interactions with each other and their environments, forming communities and ecosystems. Covers ecological principles including population dynamics, boom-and-bust cycles, interspecies cooperation, and biodiversity value. Examines the history of ecological thought, how ecology informs environmental policy and everyday decisions, and current and future environmental challenges. Addresses the cultural significance of ecology as both a scientific discipline and a worldview, including different schools of ecological thought. Bridges organismal biology, community ecology, ecosystem science, and conservation biology.

titles.title: George Bernard Shaw
titles.author: Christopher Wixson
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: George Bernard Shaw: A Very Short Introduction provides an accessible foundation for those discovering George Bernard Shaw’s writing for the first time. The verbosity and scope of Shaw’s writing can at times feel daunting. With a canon of plays astonishing in size, breadth, and ambition, Shaw is considered the second greatest playwright in English behind William Shakespeare. The range and popularity of his work as journalist, essayist, and polemicist as well as playwright made him one of the world’s most recognizable public intellectuals and literary figures. Charting the contours of what constitutes ‘Shavian’, this VSI traces the creative evolution of core themes and styles over Shaw’s long career.
titles.summaryAI: George Bernard Shaw as playwright, journalist, essayist, and public intellectual in the late nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Covers the breadth and evolution of his dramatic canon, considered second only to Shakespeare in English-language theatre. Examines core Shavian themes and stylistic development across his long career. Addresses Shaw's roles as social critic and polemicist, his influence on modern drama, and his status as one of the world's most recognizable literary figures. Situates his work within British and Irish theatrical and intellectual traditions.

titles.title: Philosophical Method
titles.author: Timothy Williamson
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: What is philosophy and what are philosophers trying to achieve? Philosophical Method: A Very Short Introduction looks at the history of philosophy, including examples from history charting the successes and failures of philosophical thinking. Themes explored in detail include philosophy’s relationship to mathematics and science, common sense and its misinterpretations, the role of debate in the search for truth, and the importance of thought experiments to philosophical arguments. This VSI provides a contemporary look at philosophical methodology, asking if philosophy is always an ‘armchair-based’ discipline or if real-life thought experiments can help us solve philosophical problems.
titles.summaryAI: The methodology of philosophical inquiry, examining how philosophers approach problems and what constitutes philosophical progress. Covers the history of philosophical methods, including successes and failures. Examines philosophy's relationship to mathematics and science, the role of common sense and its misinterpretations, debate as a truth-seeking mechanism, and thought experiments in philosophical argumentation. Addresses whether philosophy is purely theoretical or whether empirical and real-life thought experiments contribute to solving philosophical problems. Bridges epistemology, philosophy of science, and metaPhilosophy.

titles.title: Soft Matter
titles.author: Tom McLeish
titles.subject: Physics
titles.abstract: Soft Matter: A Very Short Introduction explores the field of soft matter, looking beneath the appearances of matter into its inner structure. Drawing on physics, chemistry, mathematics, and engineering, soft matter science links fundamental scientific ideas to everyday phenomena such as ‘inkiness’ and ‘stickiness’, with a rich history and philosophy. It studies materials such as polymers, colloids, liquid crystals, and foams. This VSI shows how Brownian Motion—the random molecular motion underlying ‘heat’—is an underpinning principle of soft matter. From hair conditioners to honey, it discusses how common characteristics of these materials shape their behaviour and applications.
titles.summaryAI: The physics of soft matter, studying materials including polymers, colloids, liquid crystals, and foams. Covers how Brownian motion serves as an underpinning principle, linking molecular-scale thermal motion to macroscopic material behavior. Draws on physics, chemistry, mathematics, and engineering to connect fundamental concepts to everyday phenomena like viscosity and adhesion. Examines the inner structure beneath material appearances, the historical contributions of Boltzmann and Einstein, and applications ranging from consumer products to industrial materials. Bridges statistical mechanics, condensed matter physics, and materials science.

titles.title: Ovid
titles.author: Llewelyn Morgan
titles.subject: Classical Studies/Biography
titles.abstract: Ovid: A Very Short Introduction discusses Ovid’s poetry, and the social and cultural context in which it was written. No poet of the Graeco-Roman world has had a deeper impact on subsequent literature and art than Ovid. But he was also a man of his time, and while the poetry he wrote still speaks to us today, it channels the cultural and political upheavals that Rome in his day was experiencing: its public life under Rome’s first emperor Augustus, changing sexual mores, religion, literary debt to Greece, and urban landscape. This VSI introduces Ovid’s poetry on love, heroic women, metamorphosis, Roman festivals, and his own exile by Augustus. It also explores his immense influence on later literature and art, an uninterrupted popularity through the Middle Ages and into modern times. Artists as diverse as Chaucer, Goethe, and Dali are all his heirs. But it focuses on his own poetry. Ovid was the wittiest, most inventive, and least deferential of Roman poets, his poetry a scintillating combination of high intellect and mischief.
titles.summaryAI: The Greco-Roman poet Ovid and his literary works within the cultural context of Augustan Rome. Covers poetry on love, heroic women, metamorphosis, Roman festivals, and exile. Examines connections to Roman public life under Emperor Augustus, changing sexual mores, religion, literary debt to Greece, and Roman urban landscape. Addresses Ovid's immense influence on subsequent Western literature and art from the Middle Ages through modernity, including Chaucer, Goethe, and Dali. Highlights his wit, inventiveness, and irreverence as distinctive qualities within Roman literary tradition.

titles.title: Modern Brazil
titles.author: Anthony W. Pereira
titles.subject: Human Geography
titles.abstract: Modern Brazil: A Very Short Introduction provides an account of modern Brazil, covering some of the major features of the country’s transformation. Brazil is alternately praised as a rising power or written off as a country forever failing to reach its potential. This VSI discusses the rise of the modern state in the mid-20th century, and the domestic economic, political, and social challenges faced by the country today. Highlighting some of the peculiar features of Brazil’s development, it also examines how its political leaders engage in informal political deals. Moreover, it looks at the state’s welfare institutions and Brazil’s long history of peaceful relations with neighbours.
titles.summaryAI: Brazil's political, economic, and social transformation from the mid-twentieth century to the present. Covers the rise of the modern Brazilian state, democratic development, and challenges including poverty, racism, and citizenship. Examines informal political deal-making, welfare institutions, and economic development patterns. Addresses Brazil's long history of peaceful relations with neighboring countries and its international positioning. Explores the tension between assessments of Brazil as a rising power versus a country failing to meet its potential. Connects domestic social challenges with foreign policy and regional diplomacy.

titles.title: City Planning
titles.author: Carl Abbott
titles.subject: Architecture
titles.abstract: City Planning: A Very Short Introduction gives an international overview of progress in city planning over the last century. City planning explores the tension between the idea of cities as individually held land-parcels and as representations of community and identity. It has inevitable political and ethical dimensions. Over time, cities have grown and merged, leading to larger-scale thinking about planning, but it remains a regional discipline. Part of city planning involves making cities more resilient to natural disasters and civil conflict. Data, technological developments, commerce, and efficient functioning are important, but human connection is necessary for cities to survive.
titles.summaryAI: Urban planning as an international discipline over the past century, examining tensions between private land ownership and community identity in cities. Covers the political and ethical dimensions of planning, metropolitan growth and regional consolidation, and resilience to natural disasters and civil conflict. Addresses how data, technology, and commerce intersect with human connection in urban environments. Examines the evolution from city-scale to regional-scale planning approaches. Bridges architecture, public policy, transportation, infrastructure design, and social considerations in shaping livable urban communities.

titles.title: The Maya
titles.author: Matthew Restall, Amara Solari
titles.subject: History/Archaeology
titles.abstract: The Maya: A Very Short Introduction examines the history and evolution of Maya civilization, explaining Maya polities or city-states, artistic expression, and ways of understanding the universe. Study of the Maya has tended to focus on the 2,000 years of history prior to contact with Europeans, and romantic ideas of discovery and disappearance have shaped popular myths about the Maya. However, they neither disappeared at the close of the Classic era nor were completely conquered by Europeans. Independent Maya kingdoms continued until the seventeenth century, and while none exists today, it is still possible to talk about a Maya world and Maya civilization in the twenty-first century.
titles.summaryAI: Maya civilization from its ancient origins through the Classic era, European contact, and into the twenty-first century. Covers Maya city-states, political organization, artistic expression, and cosmological understanding. Challenges popular myths about Maya disappearance, demonstrating continuity through independent kingdoms surviving until the seventeenth century. Examines the persistence of Maya cultural identity in the modern world. Addresses over two thousand years of pre-contact history and the impact of European colonization. Covers archaeological evidence, hieroglyphic writing, architecture, and ritual practices across Mesoamerican geography.

titles.title: American Military History
titles.author: Joseph T. Glatthaar
titles.subject: History – U.S./Military
titles.abstract: American Military History: A Very Short Introduction outlines the forces shaping the American military for the past 400 years. Since the colonial period, the United States has struggled to balance standing armed forces with citizen soldiers and sailors. Technological developments and two world wars forced the military to embrace professionalism and its increased obligations. The United States emerged from World War II in a strong position but failed to recognize the limits of its power, a legacy that some might say continues. Recent wars highlight some of the problems of a volunteer-dominated force. To succeed, the American military needs improved communication, understanding, and support.
titles.summaryAI: Four hundred years of American military development from the colonial period through modern conflicts. Covers the tension between standing professional forces and citizen soldiers, the impact of technological developments on military organization, and transformations driven by two world wars. Examines American emergence as a global military power after World War II and failures to recognize the limits of that power. Addresses challenges of volunteer-dominated forces, the need for improved civil-military communication, and the evolving relationship between military professionalism and democratic governance.

titles.title: Montaigne
titles.author: William M. Hamlin
titles.subject: Literature/Philosophy/Biography
titles.abstract: Montaigne: A Very Short Introduction provides an overview of Michel de Montaigne’s life, thought, and writing. This VSI situates Montaigne’s influential Essays within his lived experience. Montaigne was a member of the minor aristocracy, a judicial investigator in his youth, and a traveler in later life. He also held the office of mayor and acted as a diplomatic link in war-torn France. Montaigne explored debates and dualities that would inspire future French philosophers, comparing marriage with friendship, solitude with sociability, and the Old World with the New. As a philosopher, he is seen as an early modern skeptic, but his broad-minded approach continues to inspire writers today.
titles.summaryAI: Michel de Montaigne's life, thought, and literary output in sixteenth-century France. Covers the Essays as a genre-defining work situated within his experience as minor aristocrat, judicial investigator, mayor, diplomat, and traveler during France's religious wars. Examines philosophical dualities including marriage versus friendship, solitude versus sociability, and Old World versus New World encounters. Addresses Montaigne's role as an early modern skeptic and his influence on subsequent French philosophical tradition. Bridges Renaissance humanism, moral philosophy, political engagement, and autobiographical literary innovation.

titles.title: Global Islam
titles.author: Nile Green
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Global Islam—A Very Short Introduction looks at the methods used by individuals, organizations, and states to spread multiple versions of Islam around the world. Since the late nineteenth century, publications, missions, congresses, and pilgrimages have contributed to the communication and evolution of Islam. At the start of the twentieth century, the infrastructure of European empire allowed for the widespread communication of Islamic beliefs. During a period of secularism in the mid-twentieth century, global Islam became more accessible and, in some cases, more political. How have today’s broadcasting and smartphone technologies changed the face of global Islam? Will communication technologies reconcile the contradictions between variations of the faith, or will they create new ones?
titles.summaryAI: The global spread and diversification of Islam from the late nineteenth century to the present. Covers methods used by individuals, organizations, and states to disseminate multiple versions of Islam worldwide through publications, missions, congresses, and pilgrimages. Examines how European imperial infrastructure facilitated Islamic communication, mid-twentieth-century interactions between Islam and secularism, and the politicization of global Islam. Addresses the impact of broadcasting and smartphone technologies on contemporary Islamic practice and identity. Explores tensions between unifying and fragmenting tendencies within global Muslim communities.

titles.title: Volcanoes
titles.author: Michael J. Branney, Jan Zalasiewicz
titles.subject: Geology
titles.abstract: Volcanoes: A Very Short Introduction explains how volcanoes work and how volcanologists forensically decipher the processes involved. Volcanic eruptions are amongst the most dramatic expressions of the powerful tectonic forces at work in the Earth beneath our feet. However, volcanism encompasses much more than just volcanoes themselves. On a planetary scale, it is an indispensable heat release mechanism; shaping landscapes, releasing gases into the atmosphere, and allowing the conditions for life on Earth. Thus, this VSI also considers how volcanoes interact with other physical processes on the Earth, with life, and with human society.
titles.summaryAI: Volcanic processes including eruption mechanisms, magma dynamics, lava flows, pyroclastic deposits, and explosive eruptions. Covers how volcanologists forensically reconstruct eruptive events. Examines volcanism as a planetary-scale heat release mechanism that shapes landscapes, releases atmospheric gases, and enables conditions for life on Earth. Addresses volcanic geohazards and interactions with human society, climate, and ecosystems. Spans tectonic forces driving volcanism, volcanic rock types, and the relationship between volcanism and broader Earth system processes including atmospheric chemistry and landscape evolution.

titles.title: Enzymes
titles.author: Paul Engel
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: Enzymes: A Very Short Introduction explores enzymes, the tiny molecular machines that make life possible. These proteins speed up chemical reactions inside a living organism many millionfold. Working together, teams of enzymes carry out all the processes that can be collectively recognized as life, from making DNA to digesting food. This VSI explains how this works, before going on to reveal how these catalysts of such extraordinary power and exquisite selectivity have evolved. It also examines the many varied ways in which individual enzymes are used nowadays as tools—in medical diagnosis and therapy, washing powders, food production, waste treatment, and chemical synthesis. New vistas have opened up through application of molecular genetics, not only allowing cheap, large-scale production of pure enzymes but also making possible new, tailor-made enzymes.
titles.summaryAI: Biological catalysts that accelerate chemical reactions within living organisms by many orders of magnitude. Covers enzyme structure, function, evolution, and specificity as proteins central to all life processes from DNA replication to digestion. Examines practical applications including medical diagnosis, therapy, washing powders, food production, waste treatment, and chemical synthesis. Addresses how molecular genetics enables large-scale production of pure enzymes and design of tailor-made enzymes. Bridges biochemistry, molecular biology, and biotechnology to explain catalytic mechanisms and industrial enzyme engineering.

titles.title: Islamic Law
titles.author: Mashood A. Baderin
titles.subject: Religion/Law
titles.abstract: Islamic Law: A Very Short Introduction examines the evolution, nature, and theory of Islamic law, incorporating both classical and modern scholarly perspectives. Islamic law is one of the world’s major legal systems, yet it is often misunderstood, particularly in the West. It is applicable in different forms as part of state law in countries across the Middle East, Asia, and Africa, and has a strong influence on Muslim communities throughout the Western world. This VSI traces the history of Islamic jurisprudence. It covers its scope—including family law, inheritance law, financial law, penal law, and international law—and its practice, providing an overview of this key legal system.
titles.summaryAI: Islamic jurisprudence as one of the world's major legal systems, tracing its evolution from classical foundations to modern applications. Covers family law, inheritance law, financial law, penal law, and international law within Islamic legal frameworks. Examines the practice of Islamic law across the Middle East, Asia, Africa, and Muslim communities in the Western world. Addresses common misunderstandings, particularly in Western contexts, and incorporates both classical and contemporary scholarly perspectives on Islamic legal theory, sources of law, and judicial methodology.

titles.title: Marketing
titles.author: Kenneth Le Meunier-FitzHugh
titles.subject: Business
titles.abstract: Marketing: A Very Short Introduction provides a general overview of the function and importance of marketing to modern organizations. Marketing is pivotal in today’s world as it stands at the interface between customers and businesses, determining and responding to customer needs, while also providing competitor and customer information to an organization. This VSI discusses how marketing remains central to creating competitive advantage, and why it needs to be forward looking and constantly reinventing itself in line with new developments in the marketplace, such as the growth of social media. It also considers the importance of ethics and responsible marketing in a globalized world.
titles.summaryAI: Marketing as an organizational function operating at the interface between businesses and customers. Covers market analysis, competitive advantage creation, customer needs identification, and competitor intelligence. Examines how marketing adapts to marketplace developments including social media growth and digital transformation. Addresses marketing ethics, responsible practices in a globalized economy, and the strategic importance of forward-looking marketing reinvention. Bridges consumer behavior, brand management, market research, pricing strategy, and communications to explain marketing's central role in modern organizational success.

titles.title: The Virtues
titles.author: Craig A. Boyd, Kevin Timpe
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: The Virtues: A Very Short Introduction explores both the nature of virtue in general and specific kinds of virtues. These include the moral virtues, the intellectual virtues, and the theological virtues, as well as the capital vices. From the philosophy of Aristotle and Confucius, to the paintings of Raphael, Botticelli, and many more, fascination with the virtues has endured and evolved to fit a wide range of cultural, religious, and philosophical contexts through the centuries. This VSI examines the role of the virtues in the moral life, their cultivation, and how they offer ways of thinking and acting that are alternatives to mere rule-following. It also considers the relationship of the virtues to one’s own emotions, desires, and rational capacities.
titles.summaryAI: Virtue theory examining moral virtues, intellectual virtues, theological virtues, and capital vices across philosophical and cultural traditions. Covers Aristotelian and Confucian virtue frameworks and their evolution through centuries of religious, philosophical, and artistic expression including Renaissance painting. Examines how virtues function in moral life as alternatives to rule-based ethics, their cultivation through practice, and their relationship to emotions, desires, and rational capacities. Addresses the integration and disintegration of virtuous character and connections between ancient ethical frameworks and contemporary moral philosophy.

titles.title: War & Religion
titles.author: Jolyon Mitchell, Joshua Rey
titles.subject: Religion/Military/History
titles.abstract: War and Religion: A Very Short Introduction traces the history of religion and war. Is religion a force for war or a force for peace? From the crusades to Sri Lanka's civil war, religion has been involved in some of the most terrible wars in history. Yet from the Mahabharata to just war theory, religion has also provided ethical frameworks to moderate war, while some of the bravest pacifists have been deeply religious people. Ranging from ancient history to modern day conflicts, this VSI offers a nuanced view on these issues that have had such weight in the past, and which continue to shape the present and future.
titles.summaryAI: The relationship between religious traditions and armed conflict from ancient history to modern warfare. Covers how religion has motivated and justified wars including the Crusades and Sri Lanka's civil war, while also providing ethical frameworks to moderate violence through just war theory and the Mahabharata. Examines religious pacifism and peace-building efforts by deeply religious individuals and communities. Addresses the dual role of religion as both catalyst for conflict and force for reconciliation. Spans multiple faith traditions and historical periods to analyze ongoing tensions between sacred violence and sacred peace.

titles.title: The American South
titles.author: Charles Reagan Wilson
titles.subject: History - U.S./Human Geography
titles.abstract: The American South: A Very Short Introduction explores the American South, a distinctive place with a dramatic history. It is a cultural crossroads, where Western Europe met West Africa in a colonial slave society. The Civil War and civil rights movement transformed the South and remain a part of a vibrant and contested public memory. Moreover, the South's pronounced traditionalism in customs and values have always contended with the forces of modernization and the continuing challenges of racial tension. This VSI looks at Southerners' diverse creative responses to these experiences, in literature, film, music, and cuisine, which have had worldwide influence.
titles.summaryAI: The American South as a distinctive cultural and historical region shaped by the intersection of Western European and West African peoples in a colonial slave society. Covers the Civil War, civil rights movement, and their contested public memory. Examines Southern traditionalism in customs and values alongside forces of modernization and continuing racial tensions. Addresses creative cultural responses in literature, film, music, and cuisine that have achieved worldwide influence. Explores Southern regionalism, identity formation, and the ongoing negotiation between heritage and social change.

titles.title: Arbitration
titles.author: Thomas Schultz, Thomas Grant
titles.subject: Law
titles.abstract: Arbitration: A Very Short Introduction explains what arbitration is, how it works, what parties who have agreed to go to arbitration should expect, the relationship between arbitration and the law, and the politics of arbitration. Arbitration is a legal dispute resolution mechanism, providing binding decisions which are enforceable around the world. It is where parties take their disputes when they have agreed that courts, for one reason or another, do not suit them. Some of the most politically sensitive disputes on the largest scale go to arbitration. But also much smaller, everyday cases are routinely dealt with by arbitration. This VSI considers where the global system of arbitration is headed.
titles.summaryAI: Arbitration as a legal dispute resolution mechanism providing binding, internationally enforceable decisions outside traditional court systems. Covers the procedural mechanics of arbitration, the relationship between arbitration and formal law, and the politics surrounding its use. Examines both large-scale politically sensitive disputes and routine everyday cases. Addresses why parties choose arbitration over courts, what participants should expect, and the future direction of the global arbitration system. Bridges private law, international law, commercial dispute resolution, and alternative justice mechanisms.

titles.title: Philosophy of Physics
titles.author: David Wallace
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Philosophy of Physics: A Very Short Introduction explores the core topics of philosophy of physics through three key themes: the nature of space and time; the origin of irreversibility and probability in the physics of large systems; how we can make sense of quantum mechanics. Central issues discussed include: the scientific method as it applies in modern physics; the distinction between absolute and relative motion; the way that distinction changes between Newton’s physics and special relativity; what spacetime is and how it relates to the laws of physics; how fundamental physics can make no distinction between past and future and yet a clear distinction exists in the world we see around us; why it is so difficult to understand quantum mechanics, and why doing so might push us to change our fundamental physics, to rethink the nature of science, or even to accept the existence of parallel universes.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophical foundations of modern physics organized around three themes: the nature of space and time, the origin of irreversibility and probability in large systems, and interpretation of quantum mechanics. Covers absolute versus relative motion in Newtonian physics and special relativity, the nature of spacetime and its relation to physical laws, and statistical mechanics. Examines why fundamental physics makes no past-future distinction despite observed temporal asymmetry. Addresses interpretive challenges of quantum theory including parallel universe hypotheses. Bridges philosophy of science, metaphysics, and theoretical physics methodology.

titles.title: Religion
titles.author: Thomas A. Tweed
titles.subject: Religion/Philosophy
titles.abstract: Religion: A Very Short Introduction offers a concise and fair account of the vast topic of religion, incorporating insights from different scholarly fields while also respectfully representing diverse religions and varying viewpoints. Everyone who aspires to be an informed global citizen needs to understand religion, since it affects how billions around the world conduct their lives. But most overviews are too wordy or too partisan. This one focuses on the key questions: What is religion? What does it do? How is religion expressed and how has it changed? That story of change begins with the first signs of religion among ancient humans using stone tools, and it ends with modern adherents using computer technology. Religion continues to evolve, and it continues to play an important role in how people deal with recent trends and contemporary problems, from climate change to armed conflict. Religion, the author shows, is both intensifying and alleviating those problems. Religion is pulling us apart and bringing us together.
titles.summaryAI: Religion as a global phenomenon from earliest human spiritual practices using stone tools to modern expressions using digital technology. Covers fundamental questions of definition, function, expression, and historical change across diverse traditions. Examines religion's role in how billions conduct their lives and its dual capacity to intensify and alleviate contemporary problems including climate change and armed conflict. Addresses the continuing evolution of religious practice, the relationship between religion and sustainability, and different scholarly approaches to studying religion across anthropological, sociological, and philosophical perspectives.

titles.title: Amphibians
titles.author: T. S. Kemp
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: Amphibians: A Very Short Introduction discusses amphibian evolution, adaptations, and biology. From frogs, toads, newts, and salamanders, to the lesser-known caecilians, there are over 8,000 species of amphibians alive today. Characterized by their moist, naked skin and the tadpole phase of their lives, they are uniquely adapted to occupy the interphase habitat between freshwater and land. This VSI explores topics from their complex courtship behaviour to how their permeable skin enables them to thrive in their habitat and it covers the whole history of amphibians, from their origins 360 million years ago, to the extinction threat they face from humans today.
titles.summaryAI: Amphibian biology covering over eight thousand living species including frogs, toads, newts, salamanders, and caecilians. Traces evolutionary origins from 360 million years ago to present-day extinction threats from human activity. Examines adaptations to the freshwater-land interphase habitat, the role of permeable skin in physiological function, and the tadpole developmental phase. Covers courtship behavior, reproductive strategies, and ecological roles. Addresses amphibian classification, morphological diversity, and conservation challenges facing these uniquely adapted vertebrates bridging aquatic and terrestrial environments.

titles.title: Silent Film
titles.author: Donna Kornhaber
titles.subject: Cinema
titles.abstract: Silent Film: A Very Short Introduction covers the full span of the silent era, touching on films and filmmakers from every corner of the globe and focusing on how the public experienced these films. Silent film evolved during three main periods: early, transitional, and classical. First seen as a technological attraction, it rapidly grew into a medium for telling longer stories. Silent film was genuinely global, with countries around the world using cinema to tell stories and develop their own industries. Although sound was introduced to cinema in the late 1920s, elements of silent film persist even into the twenty-first century.
titles.summaryAI: The history of silent cinema across three developmental periods: early, transitional, and classical, spanning from the late nineteenth century to the late 1920s introduction of sound. Covers global filmmaking traditions, examining how countries worldwide used cinema to tell stories and develop national industries. Examines the evolution from technological novelty to narrative storytelling medium and the audience experience of silent films. Addresses the persistence of silent film techniques into the twenty-first century. Bridges film history, cultural studies, and the technology of early motion picture production and exhibition.

titles.title: Creativity
titles.author: Vlad Petre Glăveanu
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: Creativity: A Very Short Introduction explores the history, theory, and practice of creativity from a psychological perspective. For thousands of years humanity has engaged in creative expression, allowing us to relate to other people, build an identity, and give meaning to our existence. From the painted caves in Lascaux and the invention of the first tools to modern day advertising campaigns and inventors’ labs, creativity has a long past. This VSI discusses how one can understand creative people and their creations within the social, material, and historical context that made them possible. It addresses the meaning and value of creativity beyond its contribution to economic growth and personal well-being.
titles.summaryAI: Psychological perspectives on creativity covering its history, theory, and practice from prehistoric cave paintings at Lascaux and early tool invention to modern advertising and technological innovation. Examines creative people and their works within social, material, and historical contexts. Addresses the meaning and value of creativity beyond economic growth and personal well-being, including identity formation and existential meaning-making. Covers psychological models of creative expression, the relationship between individual genius and cultural environment, and how creativity enables human connection across cultures and time periods.

titles.title: Refugees
titles.author: Gil Loescher
titles.subject: Human Geography/Politics
titles.abstract: Refugees: A Very Short Introduction explores the causes and impact of today's refugee crises for receiving states and societies, for global order, and for refugees themselves. Refugees and other forced migrants are one of the great challenges in the world today. All over the globe people leave their home countries to escape war, natural disasters, and cultural and political oppression. Unfortunately, despite many years of experience, the international community struggles to provide an adequate response to this vast population in need. This VSI discusses the need to understand the realities of the contemporary refugee situation in order to best respond to current and future challenges.
titles.summaryAI: Forced migration and refugee crises examining causes including war, natural disasters, and political and cultural oppression. Covers the impact of refugee movements on receiving states, societies, global order, and displaced populations themselves. Examines the international community's struggles to provide adequate responses despite decades of experience. Addresses the contemporary refugee situation, the gap between humanitarian needs and institutional capacity, and strategies for responding to current and future displacement challenges. Bridges international relations, humanitarian law, human rights, and development policy perspectives on forced migration.

titles.title: Biochemistry
titles.author: Mark Lorch
titles.subject: Chemistry
titles.abstract: Biochemistry: A Very Short Introduction discusses the key concepts of biochemistry, as well as the historical figures in the field and the molecules they studied. From bacteria to humans, all living things are composed of cells of one type or another, all of which have fundamentally the same chemistry. Biochemistry is the study of the chemical reactions within these cells; the molecules that are created, manipulated, and destroyed as a result of them; and the chemical structures such as DNA on which these biochemical reactions take place. This VSI considers the current science and innovations in the field. It also looks at the interaction between biochemistry, biotechnology, and synthetic biology.
titles.summaryAI: The chemistry of living cells, covering chemical reactions, molecular structures, and key biomolecules including DNA and proteins across all life forms from bacteria to humans. Examines how biochemical reactions are organized within cells, the historical development of the field and its key figures, and current scientific innovations. Addresses intersections with biotechnology and synthetic biology. Covers enzymatic processes, metabolic pathways, and the chemical foundations common to all cellular life. Bridges organic chemistry, molecular biology, and applied biosciences to explain the molecular basis of living systems.

titles.title: Samurai
titles.author: Michael Wert
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: A book about the samurai from their origins in the eighth and ninth centuries until their demise in the mid-nineteenth century. It describes samurai life, work, philosophy, and warfare as it changed over time and covers what samurai were doing when they weren’t fighting. The first half of the book tends to focus on warriors who were essentially aristocrats; warrior families who looked to non-warrior nobles for models of behaviour, lifestyle, and politics. It traces the early formation of a warrior regime, how it interacted with an emperor-centered noble court located permanently in Kyoto, and the political and cultural struggles within the warrior class. The second half of the book zeroes in on the details of warlord families, the struggles of “rank-and-file” samurai typically depicted in popular culture—warriors from the seventeenth through nineteenth centuries. It also shows how samurai history, culture, and values were consumed by non-samurai and, in so doing, contributed to an idealized warrior image that even samurai themselves tried to emulate.
titles.summaryAI: Japanese warrior class from eighth-century origins through mid-nineteenth-century dissolution. Covers samurai life, philosophy, warfare, and non-combat activities across distinct historical periods. Examines early aristocratic warriors who modeled behavior on court nobles, the formation of warrior regimes (shogunates), and interactions with the imperial court in Kyoto. Addresses warlord families, rank-and-file samurai of the seventeenth through nineteenth centuries, bushido ideology, and the daimyo-shogun political hierarchy. Explores how non-samurai consumed and idealized warrior culture, contributing to mythologized images that samurai themselves attempted to emulate.

titles.title: Horror
titles.author: Darryl Jones
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract:
Horror: A Very Short Introduction argues that horror, while it is often understood as a disreputable popular cultural form, is in fact a major component of human civilization and culture. It is central to many of our foundational ideas, in art, literature, religion, and ritual. Horror is an important part of what it means to be human, and an important means of understanding the world.
There are a number of major recurring themes within horror: monsters, the occult and the supernatural, the body, the mind, and scientific horrors and fears. What does horror look like now, since the millennium? Its preoccupations turn out very much to be our own: corporate control, the online life and its discontents, racial tensions, environmental fears, and the world under plague conditions.
titles.summaryAI: Horror as a cultural form spanning literature, film, religion, ritual, and popular culture from ancient foundational ideas to contemporary expressions. Covers major recurring themes including monsters, the occult and supernatural, body horror, psychological terror, and scientific fears including technophobia. Examines horror's role in human civilization as a means of understanding the world beyond mere entertainment. Addresses post-millennial preoccupations including corporate control, online life, racial tensions, environmental anxieties, and pandemic conditions. Bridges Gothic literary tradition, cinema studies, and cultural criticism.

titles.title: Henry James
titles.author: Susan L. Mizruchi
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: Henry James: A Very Short Introduction places Henry James’s notoriously difficult writings in their historical and biographical context. Henry James had a major impact on the literary culture of his time and ever since. From his childhood through his mature life, James created a unique body of fiction, travel writing, reviews, and theory that represents one of the greatest achievements in America’s literary history. This VSI studies various fictional and nonfictional works by James. These complex accounts of human experience engage with the vital issues of both James’s era and our own, including gender relations, sexuality, the nature of modernity, the threat of relativism, and the role of art.
titles.summaryAI: Henry James's fiction, travel writing, reviews, and literary theory from the mid-nineteenth to early twentieth century, situated within American and transatlantic literary culture. Covers major novels including The Portrait of a Lady, The Golden Bowl, and The Turn of the Screw. Examines gender relations, sexuality, modernity, moral relativism, and the social function of art. Addresses James's biographical development, his narrative techniques, and his lasting influence on the modern novel as a literary form and vehicle for psychological realism.

titles.title: Human Physiology
titles.author: Jamie A. Davies
titles.subject: Medicine
titles.abstract: Human Physiology: A Very Short Introduction explores how the human body works, senses, reacts, and defends itself. Physiology is the science of life. It considers how human bodies are supplied with energy, how they maintain their internal parameters, the ways in which they gather and process information or take action, and the creation of new generations. This VSI examines the experiments undertaken to understand the interplay of the vast variety of physiological mechanisms and principles within us, and analyses the ethical issues involved. It also looks at how enhanced understandings of physiological knowledge can help inform medical research and care.
titles.summaryAI: Physiological mechanisms governing the human body, including energy supply, homeostasis, sensory perception, motor responses, and immune defense. Covers bioenergetics, internal parameter regulation, information processing by the nervous system, and reproductive physiology. Examines experimental methods used to investigate physiological principles and their interplay across organ systems. Addresses ethical dimensions of physiological research and the application of physiological knowledge to medical research and clinical care, bridging basic biological science with health and medicine.

titles.title: Competition and Antitrust Law
titles.author: Ariel Ezrachi
titles.subject: Law
titles.abstract: Competition and Antitrust Law: A Very Short Introduction draws on case studies from across the EU and USA to examine the benefits of competition and the laws which safeguard competitive markets. Competitive markets deliver lower prices, better quality, abundance of choice, and increased innovation. But while competition benefits consumers, it can prove challenging for sellers and producers who may try to dampen the competitive process. This VSI elucidates the key challenges to competition — cartels and anti-competitive agreements, monopolies, and mergers — and looks at the policy considerations which affect competition law enforcement. There exists a delicate relationship between a free market economy and government intervention.
titles.summaryAI: Competition and antitrust law in the European Union and United States, examining the legal frameworks that protect competitive markets. Covers cartels, anti-competitive agreements, monopoly power, and merger regulation. Analyzes how competitive markets produce lower prices, higher quality, consumer choice, and innovation. Explores policy tensions between free market economics and government intervention, the role of enforcement agencies, and international cooperation in competition law. Draws on transatlantic case studies to illustrate legal doctrines and regulatory challenges.

titles.title: Habeas corpus
titles.author: Amanda Tyler
titles.subject: Law
titles.abstract: For nearly eight hundred years, the writ of habeas corpus has limited the executive in the Anglo-American legal tradition from imprisoning persons with impunity. Writing in the eighteenth century, William Blackstone declared the writ a “bulwark” of personal liberty. Across the Atlantic, in the lead up to the American Revolution, the Continental Congress declared that the habeas privilege and the right to jury trial were among the most important rights in a free society. This Very Short Introduction chronicles the storied writ of habeas corpus and how it spread from England throughout the British Empire and beyond, witnessing its use today all around the world. Beginning with the English origins of the writ, the book traces its historical development as a part of the common law and as grounded in the English Habeas Corpus Act of 1679, a statute that dramatically limited the executive's power to detain and that Blackstone called no less than a “second Magna Carta.” The book then takes the story forward to explore how the writ has functioned in the centuries since, including its controversial suspension by President Abraham Lincoln during the Civil War. It also explores the role of habeas corpus during World War II and the War on Terror. The story told in these pages reveals the immense challenges that the habeas privilege faces today and suggests that in confronting them, we would do well to remember how the habeas privilege brought even the king of England to his knees before the law.
titles.summaryAI: Historical development of habeas corpus from its medieval English common law origins through the Habeas Corpus Act of 1679 to modern global application. Traces the writ's role as a safeguard against executive detention across the Anglo-American legal tradition, including its spread throughout the British Empire. Examines controversial suspensions during the American Civil War under Abraham Lincoln, its invocation during World War II, and its significance in War on Terror detention disputes. Addresses the writ's relationship to personal liberty, due process, and constitutional limits on government power.

titles.title: Blasphemy
titles.author: Yvonne Sherwood
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Blasphemy: A Very Short Introduction studies the concept of blasphemy. In an increasingly secular world, ‘blasphemy’ is surely a defunct concept; and yet blasphemy (like God and religion) seems to be on the rise. This VSI asks why this should be the case, looking at factors such as the increased visibility of religious and racial minorities, new media, and the legacies of colonial blasphemy laws. Throughout, it uncovers new histories, from the story of accidentally blasphemous cartoons to the close associations between blasphemy, sex, and birth control. This VSI also asks why some ‘blasphemies’ have become infamous, while others have disappeared.
titles.summaryAI: Blasphemy as a religious, legal, and cultural concept, examining why accusations of blasphemy persist and intensify in an increasingly secular world. Covers colonial blasphemy laws and their modern legacies, the role of new media in amplifying blasphemy controversies, and connections between blasphemy accusations and the visibility of religious and racial minorities. Explores historical intersections of blasphemy with sexuality and birth control. Analyzes how certain blasphemous acts become culturally infamous while others are forgotten, including cases of unintentionally blasphemous cartoons.

titles.title: Jewish Literature
titles.author: Ilan Stavans
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Jewish Literature: A Very Short Introduction explores modern Jewish literature from 1492 to the early twenty-first century, rotating around the concept of aterritoriality to appreciate the diasporic journey Jews have embarked on across geographic and linguistic spheres to the present day. At the center are canonical figures like Franz Kafka, Isaac Babel, Bruno Schulz, Anne Frank, Martin Buber, Hannah Arendt, Isaac Bashevis Singer, Saul Bellow, Philip Roth, Grace Paley, Jacobo Timerman, Moacyr Scliar, and Susan Sontag. Unlike the output of other national literatures, Jewish literature does not have a fixed address. As a result, its practitioners are at once insiders and outsiders.
titles.summaryAI: Modern Jewish literature from 1492 to the early twenty-first century, organized around the concept of aterritoriality and diasporic identity. Covers canonical authors including Kafka, Babel, Schulz, Anne Frank, Buber, Arendt, Singer, Bellow, Roth, Paley, Timerman, Scliar, and Sontag. Examines multilingualism and transnationalism as defining features, with Jewish writers operating across geographic and linguistic boundaries. Explores how the absence of a fixed national homeland distinguishes Jewish literary production from other national literatures, creating a tradition defined by insider-outsider perspectives and modernity.

titles.title: American Intellectual History
titles.author: Jennifer Ratner-Rosenhagen
titles.subject: History – U.S.
titles.abstract: American Intellectual History: A Very Short Introduction provides an introduction to the history of American thought from the sixteenth century up until the present. Long before the United States was a nation, it was a set of ideas, projected onto the New World by European commentators and explorers. American thought grew from this foundation of expectation and experience, both enriched and challenged over the centuries by developments including the Revolutionary War, westward expansion, the rise of capitalism, the proliferation of diverse religions, immigration, industrialization, and the emergence of the United States as a superpower. This introduction provides an overview of some of the most compelling episodes and abiding preoccupations in American thought, while showing how ideas have been major forces driving the course of American history.
titles.summaryAI: American intellectual history from sixteenth-century European projections onto the New World through the present. Covers Enlightenment thought, transcendentalism, pragmatism, progressivism, liberalism, conservatism, and postmodernism as major currents in American ideas. Examines how the Revolutionary War, westward expansion, capitalism, religious pluralism, immigration, industrialization, and superpower emergence shaped American thought. Addresses the interplay between ideas and historical change, tracing how intellectual movements drove political, social, and cultural developments across the full arc of American history.

titles.title: Time
titles.author: Jenann Ismael
titles.subject: Science
titles.abstract: Time: A Very Short Introduction explores questions about the nature of time that have been at the heart of philosophical thinking since its beginnings: questions like whether time has a beginning or end, whether and in what sense time passes, how time is different from space, whether time has a direction, and whether it is possible to travel in time. These questions passed into the hands of scientists with the work of Isaac Newton when the structure of space and time became connected to motion and included the subject matter of physics. This VSI charts the way that the history of physics, from Isaac Newton through Albert Einstein’s two revolutions, wrought changes to the conception of time. There are parts of physics that are in a state of confusion, but this strand of development is a story of philosophical illumination and conceptual beauty. The discussion here provides an opportunity to see what distinguishes the methods of physics from those of philosophy. It brings together physics, cognitive science, and phenomenology in the service of reconciling what modern theories tell us about the nature of time with the everyday living experience of time.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophical and scientific inquiry into the nature of time, spanning questions about temporal direction, passage, beginning and end of time, distinction from space, and time travel. Traces the evolution of temporal concepts from philosophy through Newtonian mechanics and Einstein's two revolutions in relativity. Integrates physics, cognitive science, and phenomenology to reconcile theoretical accounts of time with lived temporal experience. Examines statistical mechanics and its implications for time's directionality, while distinguishing philosophical from physical methods of investigating temporality.

titles.title: James Joyce
titles.author: Colin MacCabe
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: James Joyce: A Very Short Introduction highlights one of the most influential writers of the 20th century: James Joyce. He is best known for his complex style, reinvention of language, and depiction of contemporary Ireland. Yet at the time of writing his work faced intense criticism, and his modernist epic Ulysses was banned for over a decade in Britain and America for obscenity. This VSI explores Joyce’s major works including Ulysses, Dubliners, and Finnegans Wake. It considers the contemporary significance of Joyce’s examination of sexuality and nationalism, and places Joyce’s works in the context of his life as well as the historical moment in which they were written.
titles.summaryAI: James Joyce's major literary works including Ulysses, Dubliners, and Finnegans Wake, situated within early twentieth-century modernism and Irish cultural history. Covers Joyce's innovative narrative techniques, linguistic experimentation, and complex literary style. Examines his treatment of sexuality and Irish nationalism as central thematic concerns. Addresses the censorship history of Ulysses in Britain and America and Joyce's critical reception. Places the works within biographical context and contemporary Ireland, exploring their lasting significance for modern literature and cultural representation.

titles.title: The Virgin Mary
titles.author: Mary Joan Winn Leith
titles.subject: Religion/Biography
titles.abstract: The Virgin Mary: A Very Short Introduction describes the evolution of Marian thought from early Christianity to the present day. Readers will learn what Catholic, Orthodox, and other Christian denominations believe about Mary as well as the place of Mary in Islam Taking an interdisciplinary approach that includes art history, archaeology, gender studies, and doctrinal history, this VSI also corrects common misunderstandings and unquestioned assumptions about the Virgin Mary. The apparitions of Mary and representations of Mary in contemporary popular culture are also discussed.
titles.summaryAI: Evolution of Marian thought and devotion from early Christianity to the present across Catholic, Orthodox, and other Christian denominations. Covers the Virgin Mary's role in Christian doctrine, Reformation and Counter-Reformation debates over Marian theology, feminist theological interpretations, and Mary's place in Islam. Draws on art history, archaeology, gender studies, and doctrinal history. Examines Marian apparitions, representations in contemporary popular culture, and connections to New Age religion. Corrects common misunderstandings about the Virgin Mary's theological and cultural significance.

titles.title: The Arctic
titles.author: Klaus Dodds, Jamie Woodward
titles.subject: Geography
titles.abstract: The Arctic: A Very Short Introduction provides an account of the Arctic, its physical environment, and its people. The Arctic is demanding global attention as it warms, melts, and thaws in a manner that threatens not just its 4 million inhabitants, but the whole planet. The reduction of the Arctic to its changing environment would ignore the complexities of the region and its potential. This VSI explores key issues facing the region today, from geopolitics to global warming. It examines the causes and effects of cultural, physical, ecological, political, and economic change in the Arctic, and considers its uncertain future.
titles.summaryAI: Physical geography, environment, and human dimensions of the Arctic region. Covers the cryosphere, including ice masses, permafrost, and their responses to global warming. Examines Arctic geopolitics, territorial sovereignty disputes, and resource competition among Arctic nations. Addresses the region's approximately four million inhabitants and their cultural adaptations. Analyzes ecological change, economic development pressures, and the Arctic's role in global climate systems. Explores how environmental transformation in the Arctic carries planetary consequences, linking regional physical processes to worldwide atmospheric and oceanic dynamics.

titles.title: The Short Story
titles.author: Andrew Kahn
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract: The Short Story: A Very Short Introduction charts the rise of the short story from its original appearance in magazines and newspapers. For much of the 19th century, tales were written for the press, and the form’s history is marked by engagement with popular fiction. The short story then earned a reputation for its skilful use of plot design and character study distinct from the novel. This VSI considers the continuity and variation in key structures and techniques such as the beginning, the creation of voice, the ironic turn or plot twist, and how writers manage endings. Throughout, it draws on examples from an international and flourishing corpus of work.
titles.summaryAI: Development and formal characteristics of the short story as a literary genre from its nineteenth-century origins in magazines and newspapers to the present. Covers the genre's historical relationship to popular fiction and periodical publishing, and its subsequent distinction from the novel. Examines key narrative techniques including plot design, character study, voice creation, beginnings and endings, ironic turns, and plot twists. Draws on an international corpus of examples to analyze continuity and variation in short story structure across different literary traditions and periods.

titles.title: Pakistan
titles.author: Pippa Virdee
titles.subject: Geography
titles.abstract: Pakistan: A Very Short Introduction describes Pakistan as one of the two-nation-states of the Indian subcontinent that emerged in 1947. It looks at the ancient past to understand the complex tapestry of linguistic, ethnic, political, and cultural identities and tensions of the region today. The region of the Indus valley has a 4,000-year-old history and is considered the site of one of the earliest riverine civilizations in the world. The modern nation of Pakistan was created as a postcolonial homeland for the Muslims of British India. This VSI also considers the challenges of the 21st century and the future of Pakistan.
titles.summaryAI: Pakistan as a postcolonial nation-state created in 1947 from British India as a homeland for Muslims of the Indian subcontinent. Covers the Indus valley civilization spanning 4,000 years, linguistic and ethnic diversity, cultural and political identities, and partition-era tensions. Examines the complex interplay of Islam, regional identity, and colonial legacy shaping modern Pakistan. Addresses 21st-century challenges including governance, security, and national cohesion. Connects ancient riverine civilizations, Islamic cultural heritage, British imperial rule, decolonization, and contemporary geopolitics of South Asia.

titles.title: American poetry
titles.author: David Caplan
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract: American Poetry: A Very Short Introduction proposes a new theory of American poetry showing that two characteristics mark the vast, contentious literature. On the one hand, several of its major poets and critics claim that America needs a poetry equal to the country’s own distinctiveness. On the other hand, American poetry welcomes techniques, styles, and traditions that originate from outside the country. Its influences range far beyond America’s borders. The force of these two competing characteristics drives both individual accomplishment and the broader field. The story moves through historical periods and honors the poets’ artistry by paying close attention to the verse forms, meters, and styles they employ. Its examples range from Anne Bradstreet, writing a century before America’s establishment, to the poets of the Black Lives Matter movement. Individual chapters consider how other major figures such as T. S. Eliot, Phillis Wheatley, Walt Whitman, and Emily Dickinson emphasize convention or idiosyncrasy and turn to American English as an important artistic resource.
titles.summaryAI: American poetry from the colonial era through the 21st century, tracing tensions between national distinctiveness and transnational influence. Covers verse forms, meters, and stylistic evolution across historical periods. Examines major figures including Anne Bradstreet, Phillis Wheatley, Walt Whitman, Emily Dickinson, and T. S. Eliot. Addresses American English as artistic resource, the interplay of convention and idiosyncrasy, and poetry's relationship to national identity. Extends to contemporary movements including Black Lives Matter poetry, connecting literary aesthetics to cultural and political expression in the United States.

titles.title: Philosophy of Mind
titles.author: Barbara Gail Montero
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Highlighting key philosophical thought experiments, Philosophy of Mind: A Very Short Introduction considers the strengths and weaknesses of physicalism, dualism, and behaviourism and explores the philosophical conundrums associated with sensation, perception, cognition, and emotion. It probes some of the great philosophical questions about the mind: What is the relationship between mind and body? Can science unravel the mystery of consciousness? How can our thoughts represent things in the world? Are computers genuinely intelligent? And it also touches on philosophy of mind’s cross-cultural roots and connections to psychology, neuroscience, and physics.
titles.summaryAI: Core problems in philosophy of mind including the mind-body relationship, consciousness, perception, cognition, and emotion. Evaluates physicalism, dualism, and behaviourism as competing frameworks. Addresses whether science can explain consciousness, how thoughts represent external reality, and whether computers possess genuine intelligence. Explores cross-cultural philosophical roots and interdisciplinary connections to psychology, neuroscience, and physics. Uses thought experiments to probe philosophical conundrums about sensation and mental representation, bridging analytic philosophy with empirical sciences of the mind.

titles.title: The History of Political Thought
titles.author: Richard Whatmore
titles.subject: Politics/History
titles.abstract: ‘A History of Political Thought: A Very Short Introduction’ explores the core concerns and questions in the history of political thought, considering the field as a branch of political philosophy and political science. The approaches of core theorists, such as Reinhart Koselleck, Leo Strauss, Michel Foucault, and the so-called Cambridge School of Quentin Skinner and John Pocock are important to this topic. There is ongoing relevance for current politics which can be seen by assessing the current relationship between political history, theory, and action. There are some areas of political thinking that tend to draw on history because of the comparisons and contrasts that the past can offer to contemporary dilemmas.
titles.summaryAI: The history of political thought as a discipline bridging political philosophy and political science. Examines methodological approaches of key theorists including Reinhart Koselleck, Leo Strauss, Michel Foucault, and the Cambridge School of Quentin Skinner and John Pocock. Explores how historical study of political ideas informs contemporary political dilemmas through comparisons and contrasts with the past. Addresses the relationship between political history, theory, and action, and the ongoing relevance of intellectual history for understanding current governance, legitimacy, and political reasoning.

titles.title: Planetary Systems
titles.author: Raymond T. Pierrehumbert
titles.subject: Astronomy
titles.abstract: Planetary Systems: A Very Short Introduction takes the reader on a journey through time and space, exploring how planetary systems such as ours form and evolve, and the conditions under which life may arise. Not long ago, the Solar System was the only example of a planetary system that we knew. Now, we know of thousands of planetary systems, and have even been able to observe the moment of their birth. This VSI reveals the astonishing variety of planetary systems out there. It explores the insights gained about these other worlds from a new generation of telescopes.
titles.summaryAI: Formation and evolution of planetary systems, including the Solar System and thousands of exoplanetary systems discovered through modern telescopes. Covers planetary formation processes, orbital dynamics, atmospheric composition, and conditions for the emergence of life. Examines the astonishing variety of planetary architectures observed beyond our solar system and how new observational technologies have enabled direct observation of planetary system birth. Connects astrophysics, astrobiology, and space exploration, addressing how comparative planetology deepens understanding of planetary habitability and cosmic diversity.

titles.title: Jane Austen
titles.author: Tom Keymer
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: Jane Austen: A Very Short Introduction combines critical introductions to each of Jane Austen’s major novels with an exploration of the themes of Austen’s writing. Austen wrote six of the best-loved novels in the English language, as well as a smaller corpus of works unpublished in her day. She pioneered new techniques for representing voices, minds, and hearts in narrative prose, and was a penetrating satirist of social tensions and trends. This VSI considers how Austen reveals the literary, social, and political tensions from which the novels emerge. It also analyses how her writing continues to charm and impact readers to the present day.
titles.summaryAI: Jane Austen's six major novels and smaller unpublished corpus, examining her innovations in narrative prose technique for representing voices, minds, and interior states. Covers her role as satirist of social tensions and trends in late 18th and early 19th-century England. Explores literary, social, and political contexts from which the novels emerged, including class dynamics, gender roles, and moral philosophy. Addresses Austen's enduring cultural impact and her pioneering contributions to the English novel tradition and techniques of free indirect discourse.

titles.title: Geometry
titles.author: Maciej Dunajski
titles.subject: Mathematics
titles.abstract: Geometry: A Very Short Introduction discusses the fundaments of Euclidean and non-Euclidean geometries. This topic includes curved spaces, projective geometry in Renaissance art, and the geometry of spacetime inside a black hole. The study of geometry is at least 2,500 years old, and within it is the concept of mathematical proof or deductive reasoning from a set of axioms. Geometry remained a very active area of research in mathematics, with links to science and art. The subject of geometry includes examples of mathematical objects, such as Platonic solids, or theorems like the Pythagorean theorem, as well as general principles.
titles.summaryAI: Euclidean and non-Euclidean geometries spanning 2,500 years of mathematical development. Covers curved spaces, projective geometry and its applications in Renaissance art, and the geometry of spacetime including black hole interiors. Examines the concept of mathematical proof and deductive reasoning from axioms. Addresses specific mathematical objects such as Platonic solids and the Pythagorean theorem alongside general geometric principles. Connects geometry to physics through spacetime curvature, linking pure mathematical abstraction with visual art and relativistic cosmology.

titles.title: John Stuart Mill
titles.author: Gregory Claeys
titles.subject: Politics/Biography
titles.abstract: John Stuart Mill: A Very Short Introduction surveys the life and key ideas of the influential writer John Stuart Mill. He is widely known as the leading philosopher, economist, and political theorist of Victorian Britain and is chiefly identified with the piece On Liberty (1859). This philosophical essay is considered the definitive statement of liberal attitudes and an important work of modern political thought. This VSI outlines the personal and wider philosophical background out of which Mill developed. It also covers the role of his childhood and marriage in his intellectual development, and explores his lasting impact on political philosophy.
titles.summaryAI: Life and key ideas of John Stuart Mill, the leading philosopher, economist, and political theorist of Victorian Britain. Centers on On Liberty (1859) as a definitive statement of liberal political philosophy. Examines Mill's intellectual development through his unusual childhood education and the influence of his marriage. Covers his contributions to utilitarianism, political economy, individual freedom, representative government, and the philosophy of logic. Addresses the broader philosophical context of 19th-century British liberalism and Mill's lasting impact on modern political thought.

titles.title: Polygamy
titles.author: Sarah Pearsall
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Polygamy: A Very Short Introduction offers a broad global and temporal history of polygamy and its importance in a range of settings. Polygamy, or plural marriage, has been an accepted form of union in the majority of human societies. People living on every continent have practiced this form of marriage; some still do.
