They examine the alternative methodologies employed for comparative inquiries, explaining how these choices contribute in assessing differences and similarities. Finally, they discuss the practical uses of comparative law, describing how legal comparison is employed in both law-making and adjudication.
titles.summaryAI: Comparative law as a discipline examining the world's legal systems through systematic comparison. Covers fundamental definitions, key concepts, and methodologies for mapping legal systems and explaining legal change and diffusion. Examines major legal families and traditions including common law, civil law, and others. Addresses techniques for assessing legal differences and similarities across jurisdictions. Explores practical applications of comparative law in both lawmaking and judicial adjudication. Connects jurisprudence, legal history, and cross-jurisdictional analysis in understanding how legal systems evolve and interact.

titles.title: Simone Weil
titles.author: A. Rebecca Rozelle-Stone
titles.subject: Literature/Philosophy/Biography
titles.abstract: This volume provides an overview of the provocative life and ideas of 20th-century French philosopher, mystic, and social activist Simone Weil. Although she died at 34 years of age, the breadth, poignancy, and prescience of Weil’s philosophy—on topics such as dignified work, the importance of attention, authentic religiosity, the meaning of justice, the self’s tendency to ‘fill the void’ in times of suffering, the contagious nature of violence, and the need to respect the world’s limits—has much to offer us today. In keeping with her spirit to consider and address lay people, this volume takes readers, including those who have had little or no previous exposure to Weil or philosophy, on an accessible journey of Weil’s major philosophical impacts. This exploration consists of seven chapters highlighting: her life and manner of death, both characterized by attention; the influence of ancient Greek ideas on her philosophy; her thoughts on labour and politics; her unique and ecumenical religious inspirations, stemming from Christianity, Hinduism, Buddhism, and Taoism; her ethical philosophy centred on a specific notion of attentiveness; her understanding of beauty as connected to fragility but also eternity; and finally, her legacy and influence on contemporary writers and issues, particularly as she may help us navigate and critically assess the growing convergence between religious fervour, late capitalist and corporate values, and authoritarian politics.
titles.summaryAI: Simone Weil's philosophy as a 20th-century French thinker, mystic, and social activist who died at age 34. Covers her ideas on dignified labor, the concept of attention, authentic religiosity, justice, suffering and the void, the contagious nature of violence, and respecting the world's limits. Examines her ecumenical religious inspirations drawing from Christianity, Hinduism, Buddhism, and Taoism. Addresses the influence of ancient Greek philosophy on her thought and her legacy in contemporary discussions of politics, ethics, beauty, and the convergence of religious fervor with capitalism and authoritarianism.

titles.title: The Gulag
titles.author: Alan Barenberg
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: A vast system of prisons, camps, and exile settlements, the Gulag was one of the defining institutions of the Stalinist Soviet Union and one of the most heinous examples of mass incarceration in the twentieth century. Combining the functions of a standard prison system with the goal of isolating and punishing alleged enemies of the Soviet regime, it stretched throughout the Soviet Union, from central Moscow to the farthest reaches of Siberia. From its creation in 1930 to its partial dismantling in the mid-1950s, approximately 25 million people passed through the Gulag. Prisoners and exiles were forced to work in brutal conditions, and millions perished. Although the majority of prisoners and exiles were released after Stalin’s death, this was not an end to their struggles. Survivors attempted to reintegrate themselves into a Soviet political, social, and economic system that was hardly welcoming. Although some former prisoners wrote or spoke about their experiences in the years and decades after release, it was not until after the collapse of the Soviet Union that a full reckoning became possible. The Gulag: A Very Short Introduction examines the Gulag and its legacies based on prisoner testimony, archival sources, and the very latest scholarship. It follows three themes: the close connections between the world of the Gulag and the Soviet Union at large; the diverse identities of prisoners and exiles and how this affected their experiences; and the long-term legacies of the Gulag.
titles.summaryAI: Soviet forced labor and mass incarceration system operating 1930 to mid-1950s across the USSR, from Moscow to Siberia. Encompasses prison camps, exile settlements, and their role in Stalinist political repression. Approximately 25 million prisoners endured brutal conditions with millions perishing. Covers prisoner diversity, connections between camp life and broader Soviet society, post-Stalin release and reintegration challenges, survivor testimony, and long-term cultural and political legacies. Examines archival sources and scholarship on memory, justice, and reckoning after the Soviet Union's collapse.

titles.title: Gender History
titles.author: Antoinette Burton
titles.subject: History/Gender/Sexuality
titles.abstract: This volume is designed to introduce readers to the scholarly field of gender history: its origins, development, reception, recalibrations, and frictions. It offers a set of working definitions of gender as a descriptive category and as a category of historical analysis, tracing the emergence, usage, and applicability of these entwined subjects across a range of times and places in the scholarship of the last five decades. Inevitably political, gender history has taken aim at the broader field of historical narrative by asking who counts as a historical subject and what difference gender difference makes. The book explores how gender history as a practice subverts reigning assumptions of what power, culture, economics, and identity have been in the past—with ramifications, of course, for what they are today.
titles.summaryAI: Scholarly field examining gender as both descriptive category and analytical framework for historical inquiry over the past five decades. Traces origins, development, and debates within gender history across diverse times and places globally. Addresses intersectionality of gender with class, race, and sexuality. Challenges assumptions about historical subjecthood, power, culture, economics, and identity. Explores how gender analysis subverts dominant historical narratives and questions who counts as a legitimate subject of history, with implications for contemporary understandings of difference and inequality.

titles.title: Dostoevsky
titles.author: Deborah Martinsen
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: Fyodor Dostoevsky: A Very Short Introduction showcases the life story and works of the great Russian novelist. Fyodor Dostoevsky is read worldwide for his gripping plots and distinctive treatment of ethics, psychology, religion, and philosophy. His insights into human nature and society draw upon his eventful life experiences, notably his political imprisonment, Siberian exile, gambling addiction, romances, and marriages. Key themes of his major works include doubling, freedom, shame, social justice, scandal, aesthetics, ethics, faith, and the eternal questions.
titles.summaryAI: Fyodor Dostoevsky's literary works and biography as a nineteenth-century Russian novelist read worldwide. Covers his treatment of ethics, psychology, religion, and philosophy across major novels. Examines biographical influences including political imprisonment, Siberian exile, gambling addiction, and personal relationships. Key thematic concerns include doubling, freedom, shame, social justice, scandal, aesthetics, faith, and existential questions about human nature and society in nineteenth-century Russia.

titles.title: Surveillance
titles.author: David Lyon
titles.subject: Law/Technology
titles.abstract: Surveillance: A Very Short Introduction examines the key processors of and concepts surrounding surveillance. It explains that surveillance is central to doing business, meeting friends, organizing governance, maintaining security, and being entertained. Surveillance makes people visible to organizations. However, the processes and involved parties behind the free-flowing data provided by surveillance and its subsequent social and personal consequences raise criticisms. Additionally, Surveillance: A Very Short Introduction details how surveillance depends on technologies to process data, while also considering its current global growth and prevalence. It then discusses ethics of surveillance and its development of cultural features before considering its potential to evoke political struggles as well as everyday limitation strategies.
titles.summaryAI: Contemporary surveillance as a pervasive social phenomenon spanning business, governance, security, and entertainment. Examines how surveillance renders individuals visible to organizations through data collection and processing technologies. Covers the global growth of surveillance systems, their dependence on digital technologies, and the social and personal consequences of free-flowing data. Addresses ethical dimensions, cultural features of surveillance societies, privacy concerns, and political struggles surrounding monitoring. Includes everyday strategies individuals employ to limit surveillance exposure and the tension between security and civil liberties.

titles.title: British Architecture
titles.author: Dana Arnold
titles.subject: Architecture
titles.abstract: British Architecture: A Very Short Introduction presents an original and engaging introduction to the architecture of the British Isles from medieval times to the present. Avoiding the traditional approach of a chronological survey of architects and architectural style, the chapters present thematic explorations of key aspects of British architecture that still have relevance today. The illustrated chapters demonstrate the cultural significance of British architecture, how it reflects social trends, and the ways in which it can project identity. Written in an accessible jargon-free style, this VSI is a refreshing and sophisticated consideration of British architecture that is essential reading for students, professionals, and those wishing to know more about this fascinating topic.
titles.summaryAI: Architecture of the British Isles from medieval times to the present, examined thematically rather than chronologically. Explores how British buildings reflect social trends, project cultural identity, and carry political significance. Covers domestic architecture, the architectural profession, and contributions of women architects. Addresses the relationship between built environment and British cultural and social history, including how architectural forms express power, class, and national identity across centuries of changing styles and building practices.

titles.title: The Self
titles.author: Marya Schechtman
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: The Self: A Very Short Introduction uses insights from philosophy, psychology, psychiatry, and popular thought to consider some of the most compelling and puzzling questions about the self, including questions about what kind of object a self is if it is an object at all, what it means to be oneself and why it is important, what kinds of changes the self can and cannot survive, whether a self can be separated from its body, whether more than one self can exist in a single body, and what role engagement with the environment and with other selves plays in constituting and maintaining the self. These investigations yield a complex, multi-dimensional picture of the self as a subject and agent embedded in and interacting with a natural and social world.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophical and psychological inquiry into the nature of selfhood, drawing on philosophy, psychology, and psychiatry. Examines whether the self is an object, what self-identity and self-knowledge entail, and what changes a self can survive. Explores mind-body relations, the possibility of multiple selves in one body, and whether a self can be separated from its body. Addresses agency, subjectivity, self-constitution, and how engagement with the natural and social environment shapes and maintains the self as an embedded subject.

titles.title: Faith
titles.author: Roger Trigg
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: What is faith? It usually means religious belief, and sometimes diverse religions are grouped together as faiths, with reference to ‘faith leaders’ or ‘faith schools’. What we have faith in matters, and that involves our reason, and claims to truth to that affect everyone. Faith is not just a personal attribute, like taste, but should be open to public examination and debate. Science in the modern age appears to many to provide a more certain basis for life together, particularly in the face of the competing beliefs of different religions. Science needs as much rational justification is any religion. This book argues that all faith needs reason. It puts contemporary discussions into historical perspective, particularly in the context of Christianity. Faith also involves a commitment to action. It matters for all social life. Because religion is typically directed at what is seen as of crucial importance for human life, faith should not be marginalized or privatized. It will permeate every idea of how people should behave, and has a role in the public sphere, however respectful it should be to competing views within democracy. It could have relevance for law, and Islam in particular, tends to emphasize obedience to divine law, instead of faith. A belief that does not result in appropriate action is empty. Acting without belief is unguided. All faith needs both.
titles.summaryAI: Religious faith examined through its relationship to reason, truth claims, morality, and public life, primarily within the Christian tradition with broader application. Argues faith requires rational justification comparable to that demanded of science. Addresses faith's role in social life, democratic governance, and law, including contrasts with Islam's emphasis on divine law and obedience. Explores tensions between privatizing religion and its public relevance, the connection between belief and action, faith communities, individual versus collective dimensions, and historical debates about the relationship between religious conviction and secular authority.

titles.title: Authoritarianism
titles.author: James Loxton
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: Authoritarianism is one of the buzzwords of our age. But what is it exactly? This book provides a clear and engaging introduction to the world of authoritarian regimes, including military, single-party, and personalist regimes. It asks why authoritarian regimes are born, why they sometimes die, and what legacies they leave behind. It shows that while many authoritarian regimes broke down in the final decades of the 20th century, about half of all countries remained authoritarian. Drawing on wide-ranging examples including Argentina, China, Spain, Saudi Arabia, and South Africa, the book asks what citizens can do to prevent their countries from sliding into authoritarianism—and what can be done once those regimes are in place. It also considers the disturbing fact that voters in many new democracies use their newfound rights to send former authoritarian officials back to power.
titles.summaryAI: Authoritarian political regimes including military, single-party, and personalist types examined through their origins, persistence, breakdown, and legacies. Covers democratization processes and democratic backsliding across global examples including Argentina, China, Spain, Saudi Arabia, and South Africa. Addresses why approximately half of all countries remained authoritarian despite late twentieth-century democratic transitions. Examines citizen responses to authoritarianism, prevention strategies, and the paradox of voters in new democracies returning former authoritarian officials to power through democratic elections.

titles.title: The Epic
titles.author: Anthony Welch
titles.subject: Classical Studies/History/Literature
titles.abstract: The Epic: A Very Short Introduction approaches the epic as a diverse global art form. Storytellers around the world have long told of heroes who are touched by greatness and win lasting fame. Surveying the Western epic canon alongside traditional heroic poetry from Asia, Africa, and the Near East, this VSI highlights shared themes and practices that unite the world’s epic literature. It describes the important roles that epic poets and performers serve for their societies, and explores how their stories portray heroism, warfare, family ties, nature and the supernatural, and the trauma of death. It also considers how the traditional epic has evolved to meet the challenges of our modern world. Analysing a wide range of epic narratives from the Bronze Age to the present day, it explains why this ancient form of artistic expression continues to fascinate and inspire us.
titles.summaryAI: Epic as a global literary art form spanning from the Bronze Age to the present, encompassing Western canonical works alongside traditional heroic poetry from Asia, Africa, and the Near East. Examines shared themes across cultures including heroism, warfare, family ties, nature, the supernatural, and death. Explores the social roles of epic poets and oral performers, the relationship between epic and myth, saga traditions, and oral storytelling practices. Addresses how the traditional epic form has evolved and adapted to modern contexts while maintaining cross-cultural narrative patterns.

titles.title: Marcel Proust
titles.author: Joshua Landy
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: This book is a brief guide to the thought and work of Marcel Proust, arguably France’s most famous literary writer. It explores a series of questions raised in Proust’s monumental novel, In Search of Lost Time: how we can feel at home in the world; how we can make genuine contact with other human minds; how we can find enchantment in a world without God; how art can transform our lives; whether an artist’s life can shed light on their work; what we can know about the world, other people, and ourselves; when not knowing is better than knowing; how sexual orientation affects questions of connection and identity; who we are, deep down; what memory tells us about our inner world; why it might be good to think of our life as a story; and how we can feel like a single, unified person when we are torn apart by change and competing desires. And it asks why Proust wrote about all this in the form of an intricate 3,000-page novel, rather than producing a philosophical treatise. Proust’s novel isn’t just rich in theoretical interest, thanks to its reflections on memory, society, art, same-sex desire, knowledge, self-understanding, self-fashioning, and the unconscious mind. Rather, it offers an experience—a long, demanding, but joyous experience—which can itself be transformative for minds and souls.
titles.summaryAI: Marcel Proust's monumental novel In Search of Lost Time and its philosophical dimensions. Covers memory, identity, self-knowledge, enchantment in a secular world, art's transformative power, and the relationship between an artist's life and work. Examines questions of sexual orientation, connection, self-unity amid change, and the unconscious mind. Addresses why Proust chose the novel form over philosophical treatise for exploring epistemology, society, same-sex desire, and self-fashioning. Situates Proust within French literary tradition and broader questions of aesthetics, narrative, and human experience.

titles.title: Thucydides
titles.author: Jennifer T. Roberts
titles.subject: History/Classical Studies/Biography
titles.abstract: Thucydides: A Very Short Introduction analyses the remarkable history of the war between Athens and Sparta composed by the Athenian Thucydides, who fought in it. In 432 bce, Sparta declared war on Athens, a war that would last until Athens finally fell in 404. A highly educated Athenian aristocrat, Thucydides suspected the magnitude of the conflict he saw unfolding and recorded its course while exploring the causes of the war in the context of human nature. Placing Thucydides within the intellectual traditions of his day unveils his connections to thinkers like Herodotus, Homer, and the tragedians. This study examines not only Thucydides’ thought and writing but the complex dynamics of the war that changed the Greek world forever.
titles.summaryAI: Thucydides' historical account of the Peloponnesian War between Athens and Sparta from 432 to 404 BCE. Examines his methodology, exploration of human nature as a causal force in warfare, and his connections to Greek intellectual traditions including Herodotus, Homer, and the tragedians. Covers the political and military dynamics that reshaped the ancient Greek world, Thucydides' identity as an Athenian aristocrat and combatant, and his pioneering approach to historiography as both participant and analyst of conflict.

titles.title: Elections
titles.author: L. Sandy Maisel, Jennifer A. Yoder
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: Elections: A Very Short Introduction focuses on the key concepts surrounding elections that occur in all democracies and nondemocratic regimes. Elections, essentially, determine who will hold public office and who will have the power to govern. Moreover, not all democracies function in the same way, nor do their respective election systems. The VSI then highlights how elections connect citizens to those whom they choose to make decisions on their behalf and who regulate their behavior. It examines a variety of voting and electoral systems before providing an overview of reforms under consideration in a number of countries.
titles.summaryAI: Electoral systems and voting processes across democracies and nondemocratic regimes worldwide. Examines how elections determine public officeholders and connect citizens to their representatives. Covers comparative analysis of different electoral systems, voting mechanisms, and how democratic governance structures vary across countries. Addresses the relationship between electoral design and political representation, citizen participation in governance, and contemporary reform proposals being considered in multiple countries to improve electoral fairness and democratic accountability.

titles.title: J.R.R. Tolkien
titles.author: Matthew Townend
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: J. R. R Tolkien: A Very Short Introduction provides a compact overview and analysis of the life and works of J. R. R. Tolkien, who wrote two of the most extraordinary and influential books of the twentieth century. For millions of readers in the modern world, The Hobbit and The Lord of the Rings have offered a form of re-enchantment, and have opened a door to myth and fairy tales, and to the discovery of fantasy literature. This VSI offers an accessible and authoritative guide to the full range of Tolkien’s extensive writings, including his posthumously published work. It examines the writings of Tolkien in the time and context of their composition, and explores Tolkien’s characteristic ideas, preoccupations, and artistry.
titles.summaryAI: J. R. R. Tolkien's literary works including The Hobbit, The Lord of the Rings, The Silmarillion, and posthumously published writings. Examines Tolkien's engagement with medievalism, myth, fairy tales, and the development of fantasy literature as a genre. Covers his characteristic ideas, preoccupations, and artistry within the historical and intellectual context of their composition. Addresses how Tolkien's fiction offered readers re-enchantment and opened pathways to mythological and fantastic literary traditions in twentieth-century English literature.

titles.title: Feminist Philosophy
titles.author: Katharine Jenkins
titles.subject: Gender/Philosophy
titles.abstract: Feminist Philosophy: A Very Short Introduction explains some key ideas and debates in feminist philosophy. It provides an overview of the notion of gender oppression and asks what it is to be a woman and whether there is a distinctive kind of women’s knowledge. The VSI adopts an intersectional approach focused on the combined impact of several factors like race and class alongside gender. It discusses feminist views on the relationship between the personal and the political, including accounts of beauty, objectification, and sexuality. It also considers the extent to which broader aspects of the world, such as justice, work, and the environment, can be considered feminist issues.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophical examination of gender oppression, the category of woman, and distinctive women's knowledge through an intersectional lens incorporating race and class alongside gender. Explores the relationship between personal experience and political structures, including analyses of beauty standards, objectification, and sexuality. Extends feminist philosophical inquiry to broader domains such as justice, labor, and environmental concerns. Addresses how feminist perspectives reshape foundational philosophical questions about knowledge, identity, power, and social organization across multiple dimensions of inequality.

titles.title: Sylvia Plath
titles.author: Heather Clark
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: Sylvia Plath: A Very Short Introduction provides an overview of the interplay of Sylvia Plath’s life and work. Plath is one of the most influential and iconic American writers of the 20th century. Moreover, Plath is simultaneously popular with academic and general audiences alike. The VSI then discusses the key themes in Plath’s poetry collections The Colossus and Ariel, her novel The Bell Jar, and her short stories. It also reframes her works into the broader context of poetic confessionalism, biography, feminism, politics, and mental illness before examining the ways in which Plath and her works were interpreted by successive generations.
titles.summaryAI: Sylvia Plath's life and literary works as one of the most influential twentieth-century American writers. Examines her poetry collections The Colossus and Ariel, her novel The Bell Jar, and short stories. Situates her work within poetic confessionalism, feminism, and Cold War-era American politics. Addresses themes of mental illness, identity, and artistic expression. Covers her reception across successive generations and the interplay between biographical narrative and literary interpretation that has shaped understanding of her cultural significance in both academic and popular contexts.

titles.title: Civil Wars
titles.author: Monica Duffy Toft
titles.subject: History/Politics/Military
titles.abstract: Prior to the 1990s most scholars approached civil wars as one-off events, with little general theorizing across them. That is no longer the case. In fact, in the past 30 years, the study of civil wars has been one of the largest growing segments of the international relations field. Civil wars are nasty, brutish and long. Their causes are complex; ranging from fights over access to housing, jobs, access to arable land or other resources, to political contests over offices, rights, and representation. Because civil wars tend to drag on, motives and relevant actors shift. Groups form, collapse, coalesce, align and realign, and then fight among themselves. Governments themselves change through elections, coups, military defeats, or revolutions. Understanding the origins of civil wars and their trajectories therefore demands some appreciation of the economic, political, social-cultural, and geographic order of societies. If there is one factor that best predicts why a civil war erupts, it is a prior civil war. That’s why knowledge of a country’s history of political violence, and associated narratives about who is to blame and why, are critical to understanding where a civil war might next occur. Moreover, once we have an understanding for why civil wars happen where and when they did, we have a much better sense for how a civil war might cross borders or eventually end.
titles.summaryAI: Intrastate armed conflicts examined through their causes, trajectories, and consequences across economic, political, social-cultural, and geographic dimensions. Covers how civil wars emerge from disputes over resources, political representation, and rights, with shifting actors and alliances over time. Addresses transnational dimensions, border-crossing effects, and why prior civil war is the strongest predictor of future conflict. Examines negotiation, settlement, and victory as war-ending mechanisms. Draws on international relations scholarship to analyze patterns of political violence and peacebuilding strategies.

titles.title: Anthony Trollope
titles.author: Dinah Birch
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: The Victorian novelist Anthony Trollope is among the most celebrated and engaging writers in the English language. His strongly drawn characters and skilful plots are compelling, while his moral judgements are often subtly challenging. He is an entertainer, but his power to make his readers both think and feel is unrivalled. This Very Short Introduction places Trollope’s work in the context of his life and times, drawing on recent scholarship to illuminate his central concerns and literary strategies, including his interests in work, money, marriage, gender, and the law, and his changing reputation in the 20th and 21st centuries. The major series of novels (the six novels set in the fictional Barsetshire, and the six Palliser novels) are explored alongside his autobiography, the Irish novels, his extensive travel writing, and examples of his less well-known fiction.
titles.summaryAI: Victorian novelist Anthony Trollope's literary output including the Barsetshire and Palliser novel series, Irish novels, autobiography, travel writing, and lesser-known fiction. Examines his treatment of work, money, marriage, gender, and law within nineteenth-century British society. Covers his moral complexity, character development, plotting techniques, and literary strategies. Addresses the interplay between entertainment and social commentary in his fiction, his engagement with Irish themes, and his changing critical reputation from the Victorian era through the twentieth and twenty-first centuries.

titles.title: Russian Politics
titles.author: Brian D. Taylor
titles.subject: Politics - Russia
titles.abstract: This book provides a concise and accessible overview that places Russia in a global context while explaining its internal political development. Russian politics is powerfully shaped by large, impersonal forces such as geography and Russia’s place in the international political and economic system. At the same time, its formal political institutions, such as its constitution and electoral procedures, are relatively weak and manipulable compared to those of stable, established democracies. Under these circumstances, powerful leaders such as Mikhail Gorbachev, Boris Yeltsin, and Vladimir Putin can represent a source of potential dynamism and change. To make sense of Russian politics, Brian Taylor grounds his discussion in an appreciation of some enduring influences, such as the primacy of the state over society and the role of the West, which has represented a source of inspiration, of threat, and of competition for generations of Russians. At the same time, most of the focus is on the period since the collapse of the Soviet Union in 1991. That revolutionary event brought about dramatic change across multiple spheres, from the nature of the political and economic system to the country’s borders and who counts as a citizen. More than three decades later, Russia is still working its way through the consequences of these transformations.
titles.summaryAI: Russian political development from the Soviet Union's collapse in 1991 through the post-Soviet era, emphasizing leaders Gorbachev, Yeltsin, and Putin. Examines how geography, international positioning, and weak formal institutions shape Russian governance. Covers the primacy of state over society, constitutional structures, electoral procedures, and their manipulation. Addresses Russia's relationship with the West as inspiration, threat, and competitor. Analyzes dramatic post-Soviet transformations in political systems, economic structures, borders, and citizenship that continue shaping contemporary Russian domestic and foreign politics.

titles.title: The Rule of Law
titles.author: Aziz Z. Huq
titles.subject: Law
titles.abstract: The Rule of Law: A Very Short Introduction sets out the origins and development of an English-language debate centered around the phrase “rule of law.” It aims is to explore the distinctive ethical contributions offered by various thinkers to this specific phrase, first theorized by the English scholar A. V. Dicey, while largely setting aside how the same questions are framed and resolved in other traditions. The book opens by canvassing the classical and early modern sources upon which Dicey and his successors explicitly drew. It then explores the ideas of Dicey, which it flags as the first self-conscious theorist of the rule of law. It then recounts his immediate successors. These include a heterogeneous range of thinkers, such as Friedrich Hayek, Lon Fuller, Ronald Dworkin, and Tom Bingham. With this genealogy in hand, the book then reflects the important question of why the rule of law (in some version or other) persists—that is, why do actors with the power to cast aside the rule of law not do so? The book next turns to the ways in which the phrase “rule of law “has diffused across borders, making it a geopolitical phenomenon. Specifically, the phrase was taken across borders toward the end of the twentieth century by actors as diverse as the World Bank, Singapore, and the Chinese Communist Party. Finally, the book closes by examining the way that the rule-of-law tradition can be challenged both as a matter of theory and practice.
titles.summaryAI: English-language intellectual tradition of the rule of law from classical and early modern sources through A. V. Dicey's foundational theorization and successors including Hayek, Fuller, Dworkin, and Bingham. Examines why powerful actors maintain rather than discard legal constraints. Covers the concept's global diffusion as a geopolitical phenomenon adopted by the World Bank, Singapore, and the Chinese Communist Party. Addresses theoretical and practical challenges to rule-of-law traditions, due process, legality, and the relationship between legal philosophy and political governance across jurisdictions.

titles.title: Social Science
titles.author: Alexander Betts
titles.subject: Social Science
titles.abstract: Social Science: A Very Short Introduction examines social science as the study of human behaviour. Social science provides tools to understand and explain people’s choices and actions while also considering how they live together in communities. The VSI explains how methods and theories from different disciplines can be applied and combined to address major global challenges. It further highlights how social scientists can have real-world impact and change the world for the better. It examines cutting-edge themes such as behavioural science, human data science, and the ways social science can work in collaboration with natural sciences and humanities.
titles.summaryAI: Social science as the systematic study of human behavior, examining how people make choices, act, and live together in communities. Covers research methods, theoretical frameworks, and interdisciplinary approaches combining insights across social science disciplines. Addresses applications to major global challenges and real-world policy impact. Explores emerging fields including behavioral science and human data science, and collaborative approaches integrating social science with natural sciences and humanities for addressing contemporary problems of governance, inequality, and collective action.

titles.title: Sustainability
titles.author: Saleem H. Ali
titles.subject: Environment/Environmental Science
titles.abstract: Sustainability: A Very Short Introduction explores the concept of sustainability and its development. The growing concern about global environmental change and human impacts on the planet has led to the emergence of a broad field of study on the sustainability of human societies. The VSI then highlights the key concepts crucial to sustainability, such as the material and energy flows in consumption and production and the technological interventions for a sustainable society. Some of the important concepts include tipping points, resilience in natural and social systems, and renewability and circularity in the economy. The VSI also discusses the political means of managing anthropogenic change for a more sustainable society.
titles.summaryAI: Sustainability as a field addressing global environmental change and human impacts on planetary systems. Covers material and energy flows in consumption and production, technological interventions for sustainable societies, and economic concepts of renewability and circularity. Examines tipping points and resilience in natural and social systems. Addresses political mechanisms for managing anthropogenic change, the relationship between development and environmental limits, and strategies for transitioning to sustainable production and consumption patterns across economic, technological, and governance dimensions.

titles.title: Symbiosis
titles.author: Nancy A. Moran
titles.subject: Biological Sciences
titles.abstract: Symbiosis—the close association of unlike organisms—was long considered a curiosity, interesting but confined to a small proportion of species in nature and of little consequence. But discoveries over the past 25 years have made it clear that symbiosis has shaped evolution and has affected all species and ecosystems. Symbiosis enabled plants to colonize land, termites to eat wood, and corals to build reefs. At the other end of the evolutionary timescale, symbioses arise constantly and can play an active role in the ecology of populations, giving plants and animals the ability to overcome disease, heat stress, and nutritional deficiencies. Human cells are themselves amalgamations of organisms with different evolutionary roots, and gut symbionts play a central role in human health. Drawing examples from diverse species and ecosystems, Nancy Moran explains the main drivers that fuel the origins of symbiotic unions as well as the obstacles to their stable persistence. The roles of symbioses in shaping Earth and life are explored, along with their potential as tools in efforts to reduce negative effects of global change.
titles.summaryAI: Symbiosis as the close association of unlike organisms, its role in shaping evolution, biodiversity, and ecosystems across the tree of life. Covers landmark symbiotic events including plant colonization of land, coral reef building, and the endosymbiotic origins of eukaryotic cells. Examines the microbiome's role in human health, horizontal gene transfer, insect-microbe partnerships, and plant root associations. Addresses drivers fueling symbiotic unions, obstacles to their persistence, and symbiosis as a tool for mitigating negative effects of global environmental change across diverse species and ecosystems.

titles.title: Meaning
titles.author: Emma Borg and Sarah A. Fisher
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Our ability to see meaning in things, and to recognize the meaning those things have for others, is one of the most important aspects (indeed, perhaps the most important aspect) of human life. However, it is also one of the most mysterious. Where does meaning come from? Can we find a place for it in a naturalistic, scientific description of our world? What sorts of things can be the bearers of meaning? And how do we grasp the meanings others want to convey? This book explores these issues, focusing on the ways in which meaning arises in language, and showing how the decisions we make about meaning can have profound practical consequences.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophy of meaning focused on how meaning arises in language, including word meaning, reference, truth, and concepts. Examines the origins and nature of linguistic meaning, what kinds of entities bear meaning, and how speakers and listeners grasp intended meanings. Covers formal semantics, speech act theory, and the practical consequences of decisions about meaning including toxic speech. Addresses whether meaning can be accommodated within a naturalistic scientific worldview and how philosophical analysis of meaning connects to broader questions about human communication and cognition.

titles.title: The Catholic Reformation
titles.author: James E. Kelly
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: The Catholic Reformation unleashed bursts of creativity that changed the spiritual and, indeed, sensory experience of everywhere it touched. While on the one hand it offered a universality of experience, differences in interpretation and implementation as it adapted to cultural circumstance, particularly on its furthest geographical edges, created a two-way relationship between the Catholic world’s traditional centres and its new peripheries. The Catholic Reformation: A Very Short Introduction places this movement within its global context. It considers the expected, such as spiritual and religious matters, and how they entwined with the political, but also covers the full gamut of experience of the Catholic Reformation, particularly its deliberately sensory approach in terms of art, architecture, and music. The book traces the Catholic Reformation from its beginnings in the first half of the 16th century, pre-dating the Council of Trent (which is frequently viewed as its starting point), through the years of unrest in Europe, to its global expansion in the Americas, Asia, and Africa, and its continued influence and legacy into the 20th century. This book not only introduces the reader to the breadth of the Catholic Reformation experience in concise form, but it also sets it within important recent debates within the scholarship, thus presenting a provocative discussion of the topic’s central issues. Combining broad overviews and focused examples, The Catholic Reformation: A Very Short Introduction offers a concise introduction to a global movement.
titles.summaryAI: Catholic Reformation from its origins in the early sixteenth century through its global expansion and legacy into the twentieth century. Covers the Council of Trent, spiritual renewal, missionary activity, and martyrdom. Examines the movement's sensory dimensions in art, architecture, and music, and its entanglement with political power. Addresses global reach across the Americas, Asia, and Africa, highlighting how cultural adaptation at geographical peripheries created a two-way relationship with traditional Catholic centers. Distinguishes from Protestant Reformation while examining their interconnected dynamics.

titles.title: Sophocles
titles.author: Edith Hall
titles.subject: Classical Drama/Biography
titles.abstract: Sophocles: A Very Short Introduction presents the life, work, and influence of Sophocles the Athenian, who is known to be one of the greatest dramatists of all time. It provides an overview of the analysis concerned with the conventions of ancient Greek tragic theatre while also considering the historical context of Sophoclean drama plays. Sophoclean dramas survived to be read and widely performed into the 21st century. The VSI then highlights the distinctive features―tight plots, titanic personalities, and lucid style―of the plays. It also looks into the key concepts of sympathetic women, exquisite poetry, and stagecraft featured within Sophocles’ works.
titles.summaryAI: Sophocles as an Athenian dramatist of ancient Greek tragic theatre, examining his surviving plays and their enduring performance tradition into the twenty-first century. Covers conventions of Greek tragedy, historical context of fifth-century BCE Athens, and distinctive Sophoclean features including tight plots, powerful personalities, and lucid poetic style. Addresses his treatment of sympathetic women characters, stagecraft innovations, and the interplay between dramatic poetry and theatrical performance. Examines his lasting influence on Western dramatic and literary traditions.

titles.title: Toleration
titles.author: Andrew R. Murphy
titles.subject: Philosophy/Law
titles.abstract: Toleration is one of the foundational concepts of modern political life: both praised as an important first step toward securing rights and liberties and criticized as a half-measure that fails to secure equal respect and mutual concern. This Very Short Introduction canvasses the history, development, and contemporary status of toleration, laying out its conceptual foundations; exploring arguments advanced in its favor as well as those offered by its critics; and surveying the eras and regions where rulers and regimes have attempted to implement it as a way of dealing with various types of difference. Finally, it reflects on the prospects of toleration in a globalized, highly polarized, and ever more diverse world.
titles.summaryAI: Toleration as a foundational concept in modern political philosophy, examining its history, conceptual foundations, and contemporary status. Covers arguments favoring toleration and criticisms that it fails to secure equal respect. Surveys historical eras and regions where rulers implemented toleration to manage religious, cultural, and political difference. Addresses connections to liberalism, pluralism, diversity, and identity politics. Examines prospects for toleration in a globalized, polarized world and its relationship to broader frameworks of rights, liberties, and mutual recognition across competing worldviews.

titles.title: Futurism
titles.author: Ara H. Merjian
titles.subject: Art
titles.abstract: Futurism: A Very Short Introduction presents the ideas, works, key figures, and development of the Futurist movement in Italy and beyond. As one of Western Europe’s least industrialized countries, Italy appeared impervious to developments of modern technology like motorcars and radio as it radically transformed urban life in the twentieth century. Moreover, Futurism holds significance in the pioneering development of new media. The VSI provides an overview of a nuanced account revolving around the relationship between Futurism and Mussolini’s Fascist regime. It also discusses the influence of Futurism on the art world, politics, and culture, and its subsequent continuing legacy.
titles.summaryAI: Italian Futurist movement examining its ideas, key figures, artworks, and development within early twentieth-century Italy and internationally. Covers Futurism's embrace of modern technology, speed, and urbanization in a relatively unindustrialized country. Addresses the movement's pioneering role in new media development and its complex relationship with Mussolini's Fascist regime. Examines Futurism's influence on visual arts, politics, and culture, its manifestos and aesthetic principles, and its continuing legacy in avant-garde artistic movements and contemporary cultural production.

titles.title: Mary Wollstonecraft
titles.author: E. J. Clery
titles.subject: Gender/Biography
keywords: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
titles.abstract: Mary Wollstonecraft: A Very Short Introduction discusses the life and legacy of Mary Wollstonecraft, who was widely hailed as the mother of modern feminism. It highlights the significance of her work A Vindication of the Rights of Woman. However, Wollstonecraft’s achievement as a thinker and writer reaches far beyond the text as she wrote about different topics and in a range of literary forms, some of which she created herself. The VSI then provides an overview of the conditions for Wollstonecraft’s emergence as a feminist and her subsequent reception. It also elaborates on Wollstonecraft’s status as an educator, a political thinker, and a romantic.
titles.summaryAI: Mary Wollstonecraft's life, writings, and legacy as a foundational figure in modern feminism, best known for A Vindication of the Rights of Woman. Examines her broader intellectual contributions across diverse literary forms, some self-invented, spanning political thought, education, and romanticism. Covers the historical conditions enabling her emergence as a feminist thinker in late eighteenth-century Britain. Addresses her reception across subsequent generations and her multifaceted identity as educator, political philosopher, and literary innovator within Enlightenment intellectual traditions.

titles.title: Psycholinguistics
titles.author: Fernanda Ferreira
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: Psycholinguistics: A Very Short Introduction discusses psycholinguistics as an accessible and engaging description of how people use language. Psycholinguistics refers to the study of how humans process language and how they store and retrieve linguistic information. On the surface, talking and understanding language seemed simple and straightforward. However, according to research in psycholinguistics, complex computations occur behind the scenes when people communicate with other people. This Very Short Introduction also provides an overview of recent debates revolving around how AI tools, such as ChatGPT, underscore some of the core questions about the language faculty and how humans comprehend, produce, and learn language.
titles.summaryAI: Psycholinguistics as the study of how humans process, comprehend, produce, store, retrieve, and learn language. Examines the complex cognitive computations underlying seemingly effortless communication. Covers language processing mechanisms, linguistic information storage and retrieval, and the relationship between language production and comprehension. Addresses contemporary debates about artificial intelligence language tools and what they reveal about the human language faculty. Explores how computational approaches and experimental research illuminate core questions about linguistic cognition and human communicative capacity.

titles.title: George Eliot
titles.author: Juliette Atkinson
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: George Eliot: A Very Short Introduction examines how Eliot pushed the boundaries of English fiction. An extraordinary woman whose unconventional life meant that she was often judged harshly, Eliot wanted to capture the feelings and motivations of ordinary people leading ordinary lives, and hoped that this might expand her readers’ powers of compassion and generosity. She believed her work might do good, but she was also clear-eyed about the limits of human sympathy and fiction. To convey the complexity of human behaviour, Eliot drew on an astonishing range of philosophical and scientific ideas and experimented with narrative voice, genre, imagery, structure, and syntax. This VSI considers how Eliot handled many of the recurrent preoccupations of her novels, such as the stifling nature of gossip, the constraints of provincial life, the challenges of practising fellow-feeling, the difficulty of transcending our limited perspectives, and how attending to individual and local needs might enable the gradual improvement of society. The book traces the ways in which a social outcast became a sage through the creation of probing and innovative novels that remain influential today.
titles.summaryAI: George Eliot's contributions to English fiction through innovative narrative techniques, realism, and engagement with philosophical and scientific ideas. Examines her treatment of sympathy, morality, provincial life, gossip, and the limits of human perspective. Covers her experimentation with narrative voice, genre, imagery, structure, and syntax. Addresses the relationship between her unconventional personal life and her literary ambitions to expand readers' compassion. Traces how she moved from social outsider to moral authority through probing novels that influenced the development of English literary realism.

titles.title: Agatha Christie
titles.author: Gill Plain
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: Agatha Christie: A Very Short Introduction explores the enduring appeal of the world’s bestselling novelist, whose works have been translated into over 100 languages and adapted for stage and screen. Christie’s prolific career, spanning 1920 to the 1970s, includes plays, thrillers, middlebrow melodramas, domestic noir, and the clue-puzzle detective fiction that made her name. This VSI explores how Christie’s fiction works, and considers the curious alchemy of her style, characterization, and plotting, while also noting her playfulness and satirical edge. The book also examines Christie in context, looking at how her fiction refracted contemporary concerns and mapped changing tensions, anxieties, and desires across the century. It considers the success of her detectives, their ethical preoccupations and the themes of power, hypocrisy, deceit, and duality that underpin her narratives. It recognizes Christie as a psychologically complex writer, but also a woman of her time, and the book explores some of the challenges her writing presents. Yet, changing cultural attitudes have not diminished Christie’s phenomenal popularity, and the VSI examines the reinvention of Christie and the reimagination of her characters across screen and stage adaptations. There are good reasons for her global popularity and the book explores why Christie continues to captivate a diverse readership and inspire screenwriters worldwide.
titles.summaryAI: Agatha Christie's literary career spanning 1920 to the 1970s across detective fiction, thrillers, plays, and domestic noir. Examines her narrative techniques, plotting, characterization, and satirical edge. Covers how her fiction reflected twentieth-century British cultural anxieties, tensions around Englishness, power, hypocrisy, and social change. Addresses the psychology underlying her detective characters' ethical preoccupations. Examines her global popularity across over one hundred languages and the reinvention of her characters through stage and screen adaptations, demonstrating the adaptability sustaining her enduring cultural significance.

titles.title: Henry David Thoreau
titles.author: Lawrence Buell
titles.subject: Literature/Philosophy/Biography
titles.abstract: Henry David Thoreau: A Very Short Introduction highlights Thoreau as a key figure in the American Transcendentalist movement and an influential thinker whose work continues to resonate globally. His masterpiece Walden, which chronicles his two-year experiment in simple living, blending nature writing with philosophical reflection, has inspired many similar experiments. His essay “Civil Disobedience,” recounting his arrest and incarceration for tax refusal, has inspired modern nonviolence resistance movements worldwide. Thoreau’s work provokes polarized reactions, however, from admiration for his environmental foresight to criticism of his alleged willful idiosyncrasy and self-focus. This VSI explores key themes in Thoreau’s writings, including self-reliance, nonconformity, political radicalism, environmental consciousness, scientific curiosity, and spiritual seeking, while acknowledging the controversies surrounding his life and legacy. It situates Thoreau within both his historical moment and broader literary, cultural, and intellectual traditions.
titles.summaryAI: Henry David Thoreau as a central figure in American Transcendentalism whose writings on nature, philosophy, and political resistance maintain global influence. Covers Walden as an experiment in simple living blending nature writing with philosophical reflection, and Civil Disobedience as inspiration for nonviolent resistance movements worldwide. Examines themes of self-reliance, nonconformity, political radicalism, environmental consciousness, scientific curiosity, and spiritual seeking. Addresses controversies surrounding his individualism, his position within broader American literary and intellectual traditions, and his legacy in environmental thought.

titles.title: Administrative Law
titles.author: Stephen Thomson
titles.subject: Law
titles.abstract: Administrative Law: A Very Short Introduction explores how administrative law shapes decision making across the public sector, from immigration and social welfare to taxation and public health. It highlights the mechanisms available to challenge decisions, including ombudsman complaints, tribunal appeals, and judicial review. This VSI examines core elements such as internal review, freedom of information, public inquiries, and anti-corruption measures. Drawing on examples from major common law jurisdictions—including the UK, Australia, Canada, the USA, India, and Hong Kong—it reveals both shared foundations and jurisdictional variations. Thomson offers a concise, comparative overview that illuminates the essential role of administrative law in modern governance.
titles.summaryAI: Administrative law governing public sector decision making across immigration, social welfare, taxation, and public health. Examines mechanisms for challenging governmental decisions including ombudsman complaints, tribunal appeals, and judicial review. Covers internal review processes, freedom of information, public inquiries, and anti-corruption measures. Draws comparative examples from major common law jurisdictions including the United Kingdom, Australia, Canada, the United States, India, and Hong Kong, revealing shared legal foundations and jurisdictional variations in administrative governance structures.

titles.title: Alexander Hamilton
titles.author: R B Bernstein
titles.subject: History/Politics/Biography
titles.abstract: Alexander Hamilton: A Very Short Introduction examines the life of a revolutionary leader, constitutional thinker, and the nation’s first secretary of the treasury. He led the effort to author The Federalist, offering a brilliant defense of the Constitution, and championed its broad interpretation. This VSI presents his drive to build a strong national government capable of preserving the Revolution’s achievements and securing the nation’s survival. It captures his role as a soldier, legal expert, and political strategist who shaped American governance. Through relentless ambition and action, Hamilton embodied the energy essential for the country’s success.
titles.summaryAI: Alexander Hamilton's role as a founding figure of American governance, encompassing his contributions as revolutionary soldier, constitutional thinker, co-author of The Federalist, and first Secretary of the Treasury. Examines his advocacy for a strong national government, broad constitutional interpretation, and efforts to secure the American Revolution's achievements. Covers his legal expertise, political strategy, and vision for national economic and institutional development in the early American republic during the late eighteenth century.

titles.title: Black Narratives of Slavery
titles.author: Robert J Patterson
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: Black Narratives of Slavery: A Very Short Introduction shows how slavery and its afterlife have shaped Black history, Black culture, Black politics, and Black freedom aspirations. It connects the pre-Emancipation era of slavery to forms of disenfranchisement, including Jim Crow segregation, to argue that American chattel slavery legally ends in 1865, but its legacies persist. This VSI brings together a wide range of sources, including oral, visual, and written, from the pre-Emancipation era to the twenty-first century to reveal how Black people and Black culture have used an array of strategies to respond to and fight against slavery and then other forms of discrimination. Finally, it insists that they do so not only to emphasize slavery’s enduring impact, significance, and meaning into the twenty-first century but also to imagine a world in which Black people possess their full citizenship rights and the power of anti-Black racism and white supremacy becomes less forceful.
titles.summaryAI: Black cultural and historical narratives about American chattel slavery and its enduring afterlife from the pre-Emancipation era through the twenty-first century. Examines how slavery's legacies persisted through Black Codes, Jim Crow segregation, and ongoing disenfranchisement after formal abolition in 1865. Covers oral, visual, and written sources documenting Black resistance strategies against slavery and subsequent discrimination. Addresses how Black culture has used narrative to fight anti-Black racism and white supremacy while imagining full citizenship rights and freedom from structural racial inequality in American society.

titles.title: Cicero
titles.author: Yelena Baraz
titles.subject: Classical Studies/Biography
titles.abstract: Marcus Tullius Cicero shaped late republican Rome as an orator, statesman, and philosopher, defining public speaking and adapting Greek thought for Roman audiences. He rose to the highest political office, engaged in power struggles, and faced exile before his assassination. This Very Short Introduction explores Cicero's role as a mediator between theory and practice, philosophy and politics, Greek ideas and Roman tradition. His speeches influenced oratory for centuries, while his philosophical works established key foundations for later thinkers. By examining his intellectual and political life, this book reveals how overarching ideas connected his diverse contributions.
titles.summaryAI: Marcus Tullius Cicero's career as orator, statesman, and philosopher in late republican Rome. Examines his adaptation of Greek philosophical thought for Roman audiences, his rise to the consulship, political power struggles, exile, and assassination. Covers his lasting influence on oratory and rhetoric, his philosophical works establishing foundations for later Western thinkers, and his role as mediator between theory and practice, philosophy and politics, and Greek and Roman intellectual traditions during the first century BCE.

titles.title: Entropy
titles.author: James Binney
titles.subject: Physics
titles.abstract: Entropy is as important a concept as energy, but less well known. The two concepts were first distinguished in the middle of the 19th century. By 1900 entropy was understood to be a measure of disorder and been widely applied in physics and chemistry, but in 1947 it was discovered to be the correct measure of ignorance. In an increasingly data-driven world the ‘principle of maximum entropy’ is playing a key role in data analysis and decision making. Quantum mechanics is a scheme for computing probability distributions, which are quantified by entropy. ‘entanglement entropy’, which plays a key role in cutting-edge quantum technologies, is revealing aspects of entropy that are not evident in the classical world. The thermodynamics of gravitating systems such as star clusters profoundly differs from that of the every-day systems from which entropy emerged. From the thermodynamics of black holes, which involves relativity and quantum mechanics in addition to gravity, an idea, the ‘holographic principle’, emerges that calls into question our current model of material reality. This book traces the emergence of entropy and energy as key concepts and illustrates their practical importance by discussing aspects of the energy transition such as heat pumps, solar cells, carbon capture, and liquified natural gas. Then it explains how entropy quantifies ignorance and illustrates the resulting principle of maximum entropy by reference to image reconstruction. Chapters on the role of entropy in quantum mechanics and gravity follow.
titles.summaryAI: Entropy as a fundamental physical concept encompassing thermodynamics, information theory, quantum mechanics, and gravity. Traces its nineteenth-century origins as a measure of disorder through its reinterpretation as a measure of ignorance. Covers the Carnot cycle, the arrow of time, entanglement entropy in quantum technologies, and black hole thermodynamics including the holographic principle. Addresses practical applications in the energy transition including heat pumps, solar cells, carbon capture, and liquefied natural gas. Examines the principle of maximum entropy in data analysis and decision making.

titles.title: Environmental Justice
titles.author: Pamela Hill
titles.subject: Environmental Science/Politics
titles.abstract: This book examines what environmental justice is, the emergence of the environmental justice movement, governmental and international responses to environmental injustices, and the critical problem of climate justice. Environmental justice concerns the disproportionate environmental burdens suffered by underserved and marginalized people and the unfair distribution of environmental benefits to those with the influence and financial resources to enjoy them. It recognizes the responsibilities of empowered groups, large corporations, and industrialized countries to correct environmental harms, such as pollution and rising seas from climate change, that they have, unwittingly or not, inflicted on those who are less powerful. In the last several years, this important idea has gained center stage globally: the UN, governments, nongovernmental organizations, and academia now regularly address it. Drawing on the latest scholarship and highlighting examples from around the world, the book identifies the many challenges to environmental justice and reflects on how we might achieve it. It explores root causes of environmental injustice such as racism, capitalism, and colonialism, and suggests that environmental justice requires fundamental changes to our economic and governmental structures and to societal values that promote consumerism and profitmaking rather than the common good.
titles.summaryAI: Environmental justice examining disproportionate environmental burdens on marginalized communities and the unfair distribution of environmental benefits globally. Covers the environmental justice movement's emergence, governmental and international responses, and the critical problem of climate justice. Addresses root causes including racism, capitalism, colonialism, and neocolonialism. Examines the roles of multinational corporations, the UN, NGOs, and academia. Explores pollution, climate change impacts on vulnerable populations, and arguments for fundamental changes to economic structures, governance systems, and societal values prioritizing common good over consumerism.

titles.title: Herman Melville
titles.author: Maurice S Lee
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: Herman Melville: A Very Short Introduction explores the life and work of one of America’s greatest writers. Best known for Moby-Dick, Melville crafted novels, experimental fiction, and poetry that confront economic inequality, slavery, science, and democracy’s fragility. This VSI traces his dramatic journey from privilege to poverty, his adventures at sea, his meteoric rise to fame, and his fall into obscurity after challenging social and artistic conventions. It examines how he reinvents himself as a poet and how his genius gains recognition only after his death. Melville’s restless ambition and bold imagination shape a literary legacy that remains vital today.
titles.summaryAI: Herman Melville's literary career spanning novels, experimental fiction, and poetry in nineteenth-century America, centered on Moby-Dick. Examines themes of economic inequality, slavery, science, democracy's fragility, philosophy, and religion. Covers his biographical trajectory from privilege to poverty, sea adventures, rapid fame, and fall into obscurity after challenging social and artistic conventions. Addresses his reinvention as a poet and posthumous recognition as one of America's greatest writers, situating his work within broader questions of American identity and literary innovation.

titles.title: Human Geography
titles.author: Patricia Daley, Ian Klinke
titles.subject: Geography
titles.abstract: Human Geography: A Very Short Introduction explores the dynamic ways in which space shapes global struggles over inequality, migration, racial and gender justice, and the climate crisis. It does so by examining spaces central to the functioning of the contemporary world, from the colony to the pipeline, and from the workplace to the conservation area. The authors even take their readers to outer space. In doing so, their book critically engages with the discipline’s history while emphasizing its ongoing relevance and transformative potential. By linking pressing global issues to spatial processes, this VSI provides a framework to understand and challenge existing power structures. The authors position human geography as vital to addressing the complexities of the modern world.
titles.summaryAI: Human geography as a discipline examining how spatial processes shape global struggles over inequality, migration, racial and gender justice, and the climate crisis. Covers diverse spaces from colonies and pipelines to workplaces, conservation areas, and outer space. Critically engages with the discipline's colonial history while emphasizing its transformative potential. Provides frameworks linking pressing global issues to spatial analysis of power structures, territorial governance, and the production of place across scales from local communities to planetary systems.

titles.title: Humanitarianism
titles.author: Julia F Irwin
titles.subject: Politics/History
titles.abstract: This book traces the events and trends that defined modern humanitarianism, from the eighteenth century up to the present day. It describes the origins of international humanitarian action, the development of major humanitarian organizations and movements, the evolution of international humanitarian law, and the expansion of the international humanitarian sector. It examines organized efforts to save lives, alleviate suffering, and maintain human dignity in the face of major conflicts, disasters, refugee crises, and other humanitarian emergencies. At the same time, it shows how beliefs about race, gender, religion, and nationality have historically influenced humanitarian sentiments and actions. Attuned to the distinctions between wartime, postwar, and nonconflict situations, it assesses criticisms waged against humanitarian actions and debates over the legitimacy of humanitarian interventions. It also teases out the complex relationship between humanitarianism, human rights, and international development. Finally, this book surveys diverse and competing humanitarian traditions throughout the world, placing humanitarian ideals and practices in a global context.
titles.summaryAI: Modern humanitarianism from the eighteenth century to the present, covering the development of major humanitarian organizations, international humanitarian law, and the expansion of the humanitarian sector. Examines organized efforts to save lives and alleviate suffering during conflicts, disasters, and refugee crises. Addresses how race, gender, religion, and nationality have influenced humanitarian action. Covers distinctions between wartime and nonconflict humanitarian work, criticisms of humanitarian interventions, and the complex relationships between humanitarianism, human rights, international development, and peacebuilding across diverse global traditions.

titles.title: The Iraq Wars
titles.author: Samuel Helfont
titles.subject: History/Politics
titles.abstract: American wars in Iraq dominated post-Cold War international relations for almost thirty years and will continue to shape global politics for the foreseeable future. The Gulf War of 1991, the American invasion of Iraq in 2003, and the campaign against the Islamic State beginning in 2014 each had its own logic. In many ways, they were distinct conflicts. However, something is lost by looking at them in isolation. Each war was fought to finish the previous conflict. The Iraq Wars, therefore, are perhaps best understood as a chain of events, one leading to the next. They swerved in many directions but are ultimately tied together by their closely intertwined history. This book relies of American and Iraqi sources, in both English and Arabic, to tie these wars together in a short, easily digestible volume. In doing so, it draws out the underlying causes of each war as well as the continuity between them.
titles.summaryAI: Three interconnected American wars in Iraq: the 1991 Gulf War, the 2003 invasion, and the anti-Islamic State campaign beginning in 2014, examined as a chain of causally linked conflicts. Covers post-Cold War international relations, United States foreign policy, Saddam Hussein's regime, sanctions, and the rise of ISIS. Draws on both American and Iraqi sources in English and Arabic. Addresses the underlying causes, continuities, and consequences of each conflict and their collective impact on Middle Eastern and global geopolitics.

titles.title: Jorge Luis Borges
titles.author: Ilan Stavans
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: A probing, engaging examination of the work of Jorge Luis Borges (1899–1986) in the context of Argentinian, Latin American, and world literature, from his beginnings as an avant-gardist to his consecration as one of the most influential literary figures of the twentieth century. In essays, stories, and poems, Borges developed a unique voice that allowed him to explore favorite intellectual motifs: labyrinths, the infinite, the universe as a book, memory, and storytelling as fate. Just as he renewed the Spanish language by means of a tight, almost mathematical style, he redefined Argentine literature by inviting its practitioners to tackle universal themes, and he embraced translation as a strategy for reconceptualizing the original text. Ilan Stavans, a world authority on Borges and an internationally celebrated essayist and translator, reflects on what the term Borgesian means in an age of instant information, consumerism, and apathy toward reading.
titles.summaryAI: Jorge Luis Borges' literary works spanning essays, stories, and poems within Argentine, Latin American, and world literature from the early to late twentieth century. Examines his intellectual motifs including labyrinths, infinity, the universe as text, memory, and storytelling as fate. Covers his avant-garde origins, his renewal of Spanish-language prose through mathematical stylistic precision, and his embrace of translation as creative reconceptualization. Addresses his influence on postmodernism, his redefinition of Argentine literature toward universal themes, and his cultural relevance in the contemporary information age.

titles.title: Mathematical Biology
titles.author: Philip K Maini
titles.subject: Biology/Mathematics
titles.abstract: Mathematical Biology: A Very Short Introduction explores how mathematical models uncover patterns in nature, such as animal coat markings (and their remarkable similarity to football shirt designs). It shows how biological ideas can be translated into the language of mathematics, where they can be integrated and studied in detail, generating results which can then be interpreted biologically. It introduces the key basic mathematical concepts used to analyse the models that arise from this procedure. It explains how the same mathematical principles apply across biological systems, from invasive species to cancer cells. This VSI illustrates how abstract mathematical thinking transfers knowledge between disciplines, improving scientific understanding. It demonstrates why mathematics is the language of science, challenging traditional approaches that rely solely on verbal reasoning. It shows that mathematical models can be used to test existing biological hypotheses and generate new hypotheses. Through numerous examples, it shows how modelling refines research in biology, medicine, ecology, and epidemiology, revealing insights that conventional methods often overlook. It also highlights what are the key future challenges in the field.
titles.summaryAI: Mathematical modeling applied to biological systems, demonstrating how mathematical frameworks uncover patterns in nature. Covers epidemiology, ecology, pattern formation including Turing patterns, logistic growth models, and Lotka-Volterra dynamics. Examines how biological phenomena translate into mathematical language for analysis, with applications spanning invasive species, cancer biology, and animal morphology. Addresses the transfer of mathematical principles across biological disciplines, the generation and testing of hypotheses through modeling, and the complementary relationship between mathematical and verbal reasoning in advancing biological understanding.

titles.title: Moses Maimonides
titles.author: Ross Brann
titles.subject: Philosophy/Religion/Biography
titles.abstract: Moses Maimonides: A Very Short Introduction highlights a philosopher, physician, scientist, rabbinic scholar, and community leader who transforms the pre-modern age. He reshapes Jewish thought and law with clarity and precision in The Guide for the Perplexed, his Commentary on the Mishnah, and the seminal Mishneh Torah. This VSI examines how Maimonides integrates a rationalist's methodology, inspired by his medical and scientific pursuits, into his philosophical and legal works. It explores his profound influence on Jewish and Western intellectual traditions, bridging reason and faith. Scholars and leaders draw enduring inspiration from Maimonides’ intellectual rigor and moral vision, securing his legacy as a timeless thinker.
titles.summaryAI: Moses Maimonides as a medieval Jewish philosopher, physician, scientist, rabbinic scholar, and community leader who transformed Jewish thought and law. Examines his major works including The Guide for the Perplexed, Commentary on the Mishnah, and the Mishneh Torah. Covers his integration of rationalist methodology from medical and scientific pursuits into philosophical and legal scholarship. Addresses his profound influence on both Jewish and Western intellectual traditions, bridging reason and faith, and his enduring legacy in religious philosophy, legal codification, and ethical thought.

titles.title: Neurodiversity
titles.author: Robert Chapman, Sue Fletcher-Watson
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: Neurodiversity: A Very Short Introduction explores the diverse ways individuals perceive, process, and respond to the world around them, shaping how they experience life and build relationships. It traces the development of the neurodiversity movement and situates it within a wider politics of collective liberation. This VSI examines neurodiversity across education, the workplace, and health and social care, highlighting both challenges and opportunities. The authors draw on current research and practice to advocate for more inclusive and equitable systems.
